## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Classify material handling equipment based on the type of material handled. Give two examples for each category.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Material handling equipment can be classified by material type into three categories:

1. **Solid Material Handling Equipment** (2 marks for naming and exemplifying):
   - **Unit load equipment:** Overhead cranes, forklifts, hoists.
   - **Bulk solid equipment:** Belt conveyors, bucket elevators, screw conveyors, pneumatic conveyors.

2. **Liquid Material Handling Equipment** (½ mark):
   - Pumps (centrifugal, positive displacement), pipelines, IBC tilters, drum handlers.

3. **Gaseous Material Handling Equipment** (½ mark):
   - Compressors, cryogenic tanks, pressure pipelines, gas cylinders with regulators.

**Key Point to Mention:** The classification determines equipment sizing, containment requirements, and safety protocols.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Distinguish between mechanized and automated material handling. Provide one suitable example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Mechanized Handling (1½ marks) | Automated Handling (1½ marks) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Operator Role | Operator controls machine manually | Minimal/no human intervention |
| Control System | Mechanical / hydraulic | Computer, sensor, PLC-based |
| Flexibility | High — easily reconfigured | Low — fixed paths/programmed |
| Cost | Lower capital cost | Higher capital cost |
| Example | Forklift driven by an operator | AGV following a magnetic strip |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of material handling equipment based on **equipment type**, with suitable examples and diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory needs to handle **50 tonnes of bulk grain per day** across a distance of **200 meters**. Compare the suitability of **(i) a belt conveyor** and **(ii) a series of forklift trips** for this application. Justify your recommendation with quantitative reasoning. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification by Equipment Type (7 Marks)

Material handling equipment can be classified into **four major categories** based on equipment type:

**1. Cranes and Hoists (2 marks):**
- Used for **vertical lifting and horizontal movement** of heavy unit loads.
- Types: Overhead bridge crane, gantry crane, jib crane, monorail hoist, mobile crane.
- **Application:** Heavy machinery assembly, steel mills, shipyards, construction.

**2. Conveyors (2 marks):**
- Provide **continuous transport** of materials over fixed paths.
- Types: Belt conveyor, roller conveyor, chain conveyor, screw conveyor, bucket elevator, pneumatic conveyor, vibratory conveyor.
- **Application:** Bulk material transport, assembly lines, packaging lines.

**3. Industrial Trucks (1½ marks):**
- Provide **flexible, short-distance horizontal movement**.
- Types: Forklifts, pallet jacks, hand trucks, order pickers.
- **Application:** Warehouses, loading docks, intra-plant movement.

**4. Specialized Equipment (1½ marks):**
- **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles):** Battery-powered, laser/wire-guided.
- **Robotic Arms:** 6-axis articulated for pick-and-place, palletizing, welding.
- **Industrial Manipulators:** Pneumatic balancers for ergonomic handling.
- **Hoppers, Chutes, Gravity Systems:** Gravity-driven bulk flow.

---

#### Part (b) — Belt Conveyor vs. Forklift Comparison (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Daily throughput: $Q_d = 50$ tonnes/day
- Distance: $D = 200$ meters
- Operating hours assumed: $H_{op} = 8$ hours/day
- Required hourly throughput: $Q = \dfrac{50}{8} = 6.25$ t/hr

**Option (i): Belt Conveyor**

**Step 1:** Calculate required belt speed assuming a belt width of 600 mm and bulk density of grain $\rho \approx 0.75$ t/m³.

$$Q = 3600 \times A \times v \times \rho$$

For a troughed belt, cross-sectional area $A \approx 0.05$ m² (typical for 600 mm width).

$$6.25 = 3600 \times 0.05 \times v \times 0.75$$

$$v = \dfrac{6.25}{3600 \times 0.05 \times 0.75} = \dfrac{6.25}{135} \approx 0.046 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** This speed (~ 2.76 m/min) is **well within standard belt speeds (0.5–3 m/s)**. Belt conveyor is **highly suitable**.

**Option (ii): Forklift Trips**

**Step 1:** Assume a forklift carries **1.5 tonnes per trip** at an average speed (loaded + return) of **10 km/hr = 2.78 m/s** with a round-trip distance of **400 m**.

$$T_{cycle} = \dfrac{400}{2.78} + t_{load} + t_{unload} \approx 144 + 60 + 60 = 264 \text{ seconds}$$

$$Q_{forklift} = \dfrac{1.5 \text{ t}}{264 \text{ s}} \times 3600 = 20.45 \text{ t/hr}$$

**Step 2:** To meet 6.25 t/hr, **one forklift suffices**, but it requires **continuous operation by a driver** for 8 hours — **labor-intensive and fatigue-prone**.

**Recommendation (2 marks):**
**Belt conveyor is superior** for this application because:
- Continuous, labor-free operation.
- Lower long-term operating cost per tonne-meter.
- Predictable throughput and lower product damage.
- Higher safety (no operator fatigue).

**Verdict:** Belt conveyor → **Recommended for distances > 50 m with bulk solids**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the classification of material handling **by method**, explaining the four main approaches with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An overhead crane operates for **6 hours per shift** in a machine shop. Each lift cycle takes **90 seconds** with a load of **1.5 tonnes**. Calculate: **(i)** the number of cycles per shift, **(ii)** the total tonnage handled per shift, and **(iii)** the throughput in tonnes/hour. If the demand increases to **15 tonnes/hour**, determine the **number of cranes** required. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Classification by Method (7 Marks)

The four methods of material handling are:

**1. Manual Handling (2 marks):**
- Movement by **human physical effort**, with simple aids (hand truck, pallet jack, wheelbarrow).
- Suitable for **light loads (< 25 kg)** and **short distances (< 10 m)**.
- Governed by **NIOSH Lifting Equation** for safety.

**2. Mechanized Handling (2 marks):**
- Movement by **powered, operator-controlled machinery** (forklifts, cranes, hoists, conveyors).
- Suitable for **medium-to-high volume** and **medium distances**.
- Requires **trained operators** and **safety protocols**.

**3. Automated Handling (1½ marks):**
- Movement by **computer-controlled systems** (AGVs, AS/RS, robotic arms, automated conveyors).
- Suitable for **high-volume, repetitive, hazardous, or precision environments**.
- High capital cost but **low operating cost and high accuracy**.

**4. Semi-Automated Handling (1½ marks):**
- **Hybrid mode** where the operator initiates the motion but the machine completes it (e.g., electric pallet jacks, robotic-assisted lifts).
- Balances **flexibility and automation**.

---

#### Part (b) — Crane Throughput Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Shift duration: $T_{shift} = 6$ hours $= 6 \times 3600 = 21,600$ seconds
- Cycle time: $T_c = 90$ seconds
- Load per cycle: $W = 1.5$ tonnes

**Step (i): Number of cycles per shift**

$$N = \dfrac{T_{shift}}{T_c} = \dfrac{21,600}{90} = 240 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step (ii): Total tonnage per shift**

$$T_{total} = N \times W = 240 \times 1.5 = 360 \text{ tonnes/shift}$$

**Step (iii): Throughput in tonnes/hour**

$$Q = \dfrac{T_{total}}{T_{shift,hr}} = \dfrac{360 \text{ t}}{6 \text{ hr}} = 60 \text{ t/hr}$$

**Step (iv): Number of cranes for 15 t/hr demand**

$$n = \dfrac{Q_{demand}}{Q_{per\,crane}} = \dfrac{15}{60} = 0.25$$

Since a fractional crane is infeasible, we round up to the next whole number:

$$n = 1 \text{ crane}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **single crane is sufficient** to meet the 15 t/hr demand, with a utilization of only **25%**. This indicates **substantial spare capacity** — the engineer may justify using the crane for **secondary tasks** like maintenance lifting.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** (seconds ↔ hours) are the **#1 cause of mark loss** in numerical questions. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Rounding rule:** Crane/equipment count must always be **rounded UP** to the next integer — never down. Partial equipment does not exist.
> 3. **Missing the "Justification" step** in comparative questions (like Q-A part b) costs **2–3 marks**. KTU examiners expect a clear **recommendation with engineering reasoning**, not just a calculation.
> 4. **Confusing "equipment type" with "method"** is a recurring conceptual error. Remember: **Method = HOW** (manual/mech/auto); **Equipment = WHAT TOOL** (crane/conveyor/forklift).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Material Handling Classification**

- ☐ **Three classification pillars:** **(1) By Material Type, (2) By Method, (3) By Equipment Type.**
- ☐ **By Material:** Solids (unit, packaged, bulk, sheet, fragmented), Liquids (drums, tanks, pipelines), Gases (compressed, cryogenic, pipelines).
- ☐ **By Method:** Manual → Mechanized → Semi-Automated → Automated (in increasing order of capital cost and consistency).
- ☐ **By Equipment:** Four families — **Cranes, Conveyors, Industrial Trucks, Specialized** (AGVs, robots, manipulators).
- ☐ **NIOSH limit:** 23 kg max recommended manual lift under ideal conditions.
- ☐ **Crane throughput formula:** $Q = \dfrac{W}{T_c}$ where $T_c = t_{load} + t_{travel} + t_{unload} + t_{return}$.
- ☐ **Belt conveyor capacity formula:** $Q = 3600 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho$.
- ☐ **Decision logic:** Material State → Volume → Distance → Hazard → Cost → Method → Equipment.
- ☐ **Golden rule:** *Belt conveyors excel for bulk solids over long distances; cranes excel for unit loads with vertical lift; AGVs excel for repetitive horizontal pallet movement.*
- ☐ **Always round equipment count UP** to the next whole number.
- ☐ **Cost metric:** Compare alternatives using **cost per tonne-meter (₹/t·m)**.
- ☐ **Standards to remember:** ISO 3691-4 (driverless trucks), NIOSH Lifting Equation, MHI's 6 R's.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 Module 1 focus:** Classification forms the **foundation for subsequent modules** on plant layout, warehouse design, and automation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stages in Material Handling System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stages in Material Handling System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Handling System Design (MHSD)** is a systematic, multi-stage engineering methodology used to plan, organize, and control the movement, storage, protection, and processing of materials within a facility so as to maximize overall productivity, minimize cost, ensure safety, and maintain product integrity throughout the production lifecycle.

According to the **American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME)**, material handling is defined as *"the art and science of moving, storing, protecting, and controlling materials."* Designing such a system requires a structured sequence of analytical and synthetic stages — moving from problem identification to final implementation and continuous improvement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Board Terminology):**
> - **Material Handling (MH):** The *physical movement* and storage of goods.
> - **Material Management (MM):** The *strategic planning* and control of material flow (procurement, inventory, distribution).
> - The **system design** bridges these two — it is the engineering blueprint that turns management intent into operational reality.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of designing a **hospital emergency room (ER)**. You don't just randomly place equipment and tell doctors to "figure it out." Instead, you:

1. **Identify** what kinds of patients (materials) come in — broken bones, heart attacks, fevers (raw materials, WIP, finished goods).
2. **Trace** the path each patient type takes — entry → triage → X-ray → surgery → recovery (this is the **material flow analysis**).
3. **Decide** what equipment is needed at each step — wheelchairs, stretchers, IV stands, monitors (**handling equipment selection**).
4. **Arrange** rooms, corridors, and signage so that movement is smooth (**layout design**).
5. **Estimate** the cost of beds, staff, and electricity (**economic analysis**).
6. **Train** staff, run the system, and periodically improve it (**implementation and feedback**).

A **material handling system** is the ER of a factory — every stage of design corresponds to a logical step that ensures patients (materials) are handled efficiently, safely, and economically.

### 1.3 Why the "Stages" Matter (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 10 Principles of Material Handling (Immer, 1956)** form the philosophical backbone of every design stage. The most exam-critical ones are:
> 1. **Orientation Principle** — Best results occur when the *direction of material flow* aligns with the *production flow*.
> 2. **Planning Principle** — All material handling should be *planned* before any layout decision is finalized.
> 3. **Systems Principle** — A handling system is a network of integrated operations; one change affects the whole.
> 4. **Unit Load Principle** — Combining items into *unit loads* increases efficiency (e.g., pallets, containers).
> 5. **Gravity Principle** — Use gravity whenever practical to reduce energy cost.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants (KTU-Grade Bold)

- **Unit Load**: A consolidated group of items moved, stored, and handled as a single entity. Standard sizes follow **ISO 6780** (pallet dimensions: 1200 × 800 mm EUR-pallet, 1219 × 1016 mm GMA-pallet).
- **Material Handling Cost (MHC)**: Typically ranges between **15% to 70%** of total manufacturing cost in discrete industries.
- **Target Value (TV)** in handling: A **dimensionless ratio** indicating handling efficiency.
- **Throughput Time (TPT)**: Time elapsed between release of an order and its completion, including all queue and handling delays.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Canonical Stages of Material Handling System Design

The structured engineering methodology for MH system design is conventionally broken down into **six sequential stages**. Each stage builds upon the outputs of the previous one, making this a *closed-loop iterative process*.

---

#### **Stage 1 — Problem Definition and Data Collection**

**Why:** Before designing anything, the engineer must precisely understand the *current state* and the *desired state* of material movement.

**How:**
- Identify the **scope** of the study (entire plant / single department / single work cell).
- Collect historical data on:
  - Type, quantity, and physical characteristics of materials.
  - Throughput rates (units/day).
  - Existing flow paths and bottlenecks.
  - Cost data (labour, equipment depreciation, energy).
- Conduct **management interviews, operator surveys, and time studies**.

> [!NOTE]
> A common student error is to *skip this stage* and jump directly to equipment selection. KTU board examiners consistently penalize answers that omit the "problem definition" preamble.

---

#### **Stage 2 — Analysis of Material Flow**

**Why:** You cannot optimize what you cannot visualize. Material flow analysis converts raw data into actionable flow patterns.

**How (Tools used):**
- **From-To Chart (Flow Diagram):** Tabulates the number of trips between every pair of work centers.
- **Activity Relationship Chart (ARC) / Relationship Diagramming:** Developed by Richard Muther, it prioritizes adjacency based on a closeness rating (A = Absolutely Necessary, E = Especially Important, I = Important, O = Ordinary, U = Unimportant, X = Not Desirable).
- **Flow Process Chart:** Records sequence of operations, inspections, transport, delays, and storage.
- **String Diagram / Travel Chart:** Spatial visualization of movement in 2D factory layout.

The output of this stage is a quantified **"Where does what go, how often, by whom?"** map.

---

#### **Stage 3 — Determination of Material Handling Requirements**

**Why:** Different materials demand different handling strategies. Generic equipment selection is a recipe for failure.

**How:**
For each material class identified, quantify the **5-P Framework**:
- **P1 — Product**: Size, weight, fragility, hazard level.
- **P2 — Path**: Distance, fixed vs. variable, vertical vs. horizontal.
- **P3 — Position**: Pickup and drop-off geometry (floor level, conveyor height, rack tier).
- **P4 — Place**: Receiving, production, storage, shipping, packing.
- **P5 — Power**: Manual, mechanical, gravity, automated (AGV, robot).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output of Stage 3 is a **Handling Requirements Specification (HRS)** document — a structured matrix that drives all downstream equipment decisions.

---

#### **Stage 4 — Selection of Material Handling Methods and Equipment**

**Why:** With requirements clearly defined, the engineer now matches needs to available technology.

**How (Two-step sub-process):**
1. **Selection of Handling Method (macroscopic decision):**
   - Manual vs. Mechanized vs. Automated.
   - Continuous (conveyors, pipelines) vs. Cyclical (forklifts, AGVs).
   - Fixed-path vs. Variable-path.
   - Horizontal vs. Vertical vs. Combined.
2. **Selection of Specific Equipment (microscopic decision):**
   - Conveyors (belt, roller, chain, screw, overhead).
   - Industrial trucks (forklift, pallet jack, stacker).
   - Cranes (jib, bridge, gantry, monorail).
   - Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (**AS/RS**).
   - Industrial robots (articulated, SCARA, gantry).
   - AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles).

**Decision Criterion:** Use the *Equipment Selection Matrix* — score candidate equipment against cost, capacity, reliability, flexibility, and safety.

---

#### **Stage 5 — Layout Design and Integration**

**Why:** Equipment is useless if it is poorly placed. The layout determines the spatial reality of material flow.

**How:**
- Develop alternative layout proposals using **Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** by Muther.
- Apply **Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique (CRAFT)** or **ALDEP** for quantitative comparison.
- Verify each layout against the Flow Analysis data from Stage 2.
- Ensure compliance with **5S** principles and ergonomic standards (e.g., **ISO 11228**, **NIOSH lifting equation**).

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between **Layout Design** and **Material Handling Design** is bidirectional — the layout dictates handling needs, and handling equipment constrains layout possibilities. KTU board questions often probe this **coupling**.

---

#### **Stage 6 — Economic Analysis, Implementation, and Control**

**Why:** A technically excellent system that is economically unviable is a failed system.

**How:**
- Compute **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)**:
  - Initial investment.
  - Operating cost (labour, energy, maintenance).
  - Depreciation and salvage value.
  - Downtime cost.
- Apply **Return on Investment (ROI)** and **Payback Period** metrics.
- **Implement** in phases (pilot → full deployment) to minimize disruption.
- **Control** via KPIs:
  - Material Handling Cost per unit produced.
  - Equipment Utilization Rate (**EUR**).
  - Mean Time Between Failures (**MTBF**).
  - Accident frequency rate.
- Initiate **Kaizen / continuous improvement** cycles.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every formula a student is likely to need in a 14-mark KTU board question on MH system design. **Memorize these — they form the analytical core of any numerical problem.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Unit Load Factor (ULF)** | $ULF = \dfrac{\text{Mass of consolidated load}}{\text{Mass of single item}}$ | Dimensionless | Measures consolidation benefit |
| 2 | **Throughput Time (TPT)** | $TPT = T_{process} + T_{queue} + T_{transport} + T_{storage}$ | minutes / hours | Reduces bottlenecks |
| 3 | **Handling Cost per Unit** | $HCU = \dfrac{C_{equipment} + C_{labour} + C_{energy}}{N_{units}}$ | ₹ / unit | Benchmarks efficiency |
| 4 | **Equipment Utilization Rate (EUR)** | $EUR = \dfrac{T_{actual\text{-}use}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$ | % | Justifies capacity |
| 5 | **Conveyor Capacity** | $Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot k$ | kg/hr or units/hr | Sizing conveyors |
| 6 | **Number of Forklifts Required** | $N = \dfrac{\sum (T_i \cdot F_i)}{H \cdot U}$ | Integer (rounded up) | Fleet planning |
| 7 | **AGV Travel Time** | $t = \dfrac{d}{v_{avg}}$ | minutes | Cycle time analysis |
| 8 | **Investment Payback Period** | $PB = \dfrac{I_{initial}}{C_{annual\text{-}saving}}$ | years | Economic justification |
| 9 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | ₹ | Long-term investment |
| 10 | **Simple Material Handling Index (MHI)** | $MHI = \dfrac{\sum (F_{ij} \cdot D_{ij})}{N_{products}}$ | trips·m | Flow complexity proxy |

> Where:
> - $A$ = cross-sectional area of load on conveyor (m²)
> - $v$ = conveyor speed (m/min)
> - $\rho$ = bulk density of material (kg/m³)
> - $k$ = loading efficiency factor ($0 < k \le 1$)
> - $T_i$ = average handling time for task $i$, $F_i$ = frequency of task $i$, $H$ = hours per shift, $U$ = utilization factor
> - $CF_t$ = cash flow in year $t$, $r$ = discount rate, $I_0$ = initial investment

### 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility

These six stages are deployed in every major industrial setting:

- **Automotive assembly (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki):** Used to design *just-in-time (JIT)* lines where material flow synchronization is paramount.
- **E-commerce fulfillment (Amazon, Flipkart Warehouses):** Used for AGV-grid layouts (**Kiva Systems**).
- **Pharmaceutical cleanrooms:** Used to integrate aseptic handling with strict environmental control.
- **Port logistics (Cochin Port, JNPT):** Used to coordinate cranes, conveyors, AGVs, and reach stackers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Number of Forklifts (N) for a Plant

This derivation shows how the **Stage 4 (Equipment Selection)** and **Stage 6 (Economic Analysis)** intersect mathematically.

**Given:**
- A plant operates $H$ hours per day.
- There are $m$ distinct handling tasks.
- For task $i$: $T_i$ = average time per trip (hours), $F_i$ = number of trips per day.

**Step 1: Total handling demand per day.**

The total time required to perform all trips of task $i$ in one day is:

$$
D_i = T_i \cdot F_i
$$

The total handling demand across all tasks is:

$$
D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} T_i \cdot F_i
$$

This represents the **sum of "time × frequency"** for every trip the forklifts must perform.

**Step 2: Effective working time per forklift per day.**

A forklift cannot operate at 100% due to breaks, refueling, maintenance, and idle time. We introduce a *utilization factor* $U$ (typically $0.75$ to $0.90$).

$$
T_{effective} = H \cdot U
$$

**Step 3: Compute theoretical fleet size.**

$$
N_{theory} = \dfrac{D_{total}}{T_{effective}} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} T_i \cdot F_i}{H \cdot U}
$$

**Step 4: Round up to the next whole integer.**

Because a fractional forklift is physically meaningless:

$$
N = \left\lceil N_{theory} \right\rceil
$$

**Worked Example (KTU Board Style):**

> A manufacturing plant operates **8 hours/day**. Three material handling tasks exist:
> - Task 1: $T_1 = 0.25$ hr/trip, $F_1 = 60$ trips/day
> - Task 2: $T_2 = 0.50$ hr/trip, $F_2 = 24$ trips/day
> - Task 3: $T_3 = 0.75$ hr/trip, $F_3 = 16$ trips/day
>
> Utilization factor $U = 0.80$. Find the number of forklifts required.

**Solution:**

$$
D_{total} = (0.25 \times 60) + (0.50 \times 24) + (0.75 \times 16)
$$

$$
D_{total} = 15 + 12 + 12 = 39 \text{ hr/day}
$$

$$
N_{theory} = \dfrac{39}{8 \times 0.80} = \dfrac{39}{6.4} = 6.09375
$$

$$
N = \lceil 6.09375 \rceil = 7 \text{ forklifts}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Six forklifts would be technically sufficient *if* they could operate at 100% efficiency. Accounting for realistic utilization, **one extra forklift** is needed as a buffer. KTU examiners love this type of question because it tests both the formula and engineering judgment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conveyor Capacity (Q)

**Given:**
- Belt conveyor with width $w$ (m).
- Material cross-section forms a trough of average depth $h$ (m).
- Belt speed $v$ (m/min).
- Bulk density of material $\rho$ (kg/m³).
- Loading efficiency $k$.

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of material on belt.**

For a typical troughed belt with three idlers, the area is approximated as:

$$
A = w \cdot h \quad (\text{m}^2, \text{ simplified flat-top assumption})
$$

**Step 2: Volumetric flow per minute.**

$$
Q_{volume} = A \cdot v \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{min})
$$

**Step 3: Convert to mass flow per hour.**

$$
Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot k \quad (\text{kg/hr})
$$

**Step 4: Annual throughput.**

$$
Q_{annual} = Q \cdot H_{annual} \quad (\text{kg/yr})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **unitized loads (e.g., boxes)** rather than bulk material, replace $\rho$ with the count density (boxes/m³) to yield **boxes/hour**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Handling Cost Reduction via Unit Load

**Given:**
- 50 individual units per batch, each weighing 5 kg.
- Without unit load: each unit handled separately.
- With unit load: 5 units strapped on a pallet (10 batches of 5 units per pallet).

**Step 1: Number of handling operations without unit load.**

$$
N_{no\text{-}UL} = 50 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2: Number of handling operations with unit load.**

$$
N_{UL} = 10 \text{ pallets}
$$

**Step 3: Cost per handling operation.**

Let cost per individual movement be $c$. Then:

$$
C_{no\text{-}UL} = 50c
$$

$$
C_{UL} = 10c + C_{pallet}
$$

where $C_{pallet}$ is the pallet/strapping cost.

**Step 4: Cost saving condition.**

$$
\Delta C = 50c - (10c + C_{pallet}) = 40c - C_{pallet}
$$

The unit-load principle is justified when $\Delta C > 0$, i.e.:

$$
C_{pallet} < 40c
$$

This is the **Break-Even Condition for Unit Load Investment** — a recurring 7-mark exam question.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Scoring of Handling Equipment

Use this matrix in the **Equipment Selection (Stage 4)** step to justify choices in board answers.

| Equipment Type | Cost (₹) | Flexibility | Capacity | Automation Potential | Best Application |
|----------------|----------|-------------|----------|----------------------|------------------|
| Hand Truck | Very Low | Very High | Low | None | Small workshops |
| Pallet Jack | Low | High | Medium | None | Warehouses |
| Forklift | Medium | High | High | Limited (semi-auto) | Loading docks |
| Belt Conveyor | Medium | Low | Very High | High | Assembly lines |
| Overhead Crane | High | Medium | Very High | High | Heavy machinery |
| AGV | Very High | High | Medium | Fully Auto | Modern warehouses |
| AS/RS | Very High | Low | Very High | Fully Auto | High-density storage |
| Industrial Robot | Very High | Medium | High | Fully Auto | Pick-and-place, welding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU answers, you may be asked to *"justify your choice."* Always reference at least **two criteria** from this matrix. A single-criterion justification attracts partial credit only.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — The Six Stages of MH System Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Problem Definition and Data Collection] --> B[Stage 2: Material Flow Analysis]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Determination of Handling Requirements]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Selection of Methods and Equipment]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Layout Design and Integration]
    E --> F[Stage 6: Economic Analysis Implementation and Control]
    F --> G{Continuous Improvement Loop}
    G -- Feedback KPIs --> B
    G -- New Requirements --> A
```

**Interpretation of the Loop:**
The output of **Stage 6** does not terminate the project — it generates *performance KPIs* and *new requirements* that flow back into Stages 1 and 2, making the methodology a **closed-loop iterative system**.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Inputs, Processes, and Outputs of Each Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Plant Layout Drawings]
        I2[Bill of Materials]
        I3[Production Schedule]
        I4[Cost Data]
    end
    subgraph Process
        P1[Data Collection]
        P2[Flow Mapping]
        P3[Requirement Spec]
        P4[Equipment Matrix]
        P5[Layout Optimization]
        P6[Cost Benefit Analysis]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        O1[Baseline Report]
        O2[From To Chart ARC]
        O3[5 P Specification]
        O4[Selected Equipment List]
        O5[Proposed Layout]
        O6[Investment Proposal]
    end
    I1 --> P1 --> O1
    I2 --> P2 --> O2
    I3 --> P3 --> O3
    I4 --> P4 --> O4
    O1 --> P5 --> O5
    O2 --> P5
    O3 --> P5
    O4 --> P6 --> O6
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Stage 4 Decision Logic for Equipment Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Handling Requirement Spec] --> Q1{Material Weight}
    Q1 -- Less than 25 kg --> M1[Manual Cart or Hand Truck]
    Q1 -- 25 to 500 kg --> Q2{Distance}
    Q1 -- Greater than 500 kg --> C1[Crane or Heavy Forklift]
    Q2 -- Short fixed path --> M2[Conveyor System]
    Q2 -- Long variable path --> M3[Industrial Truck or Forklift]
    M3 --> Q3{Automation Budget}
    Q3 -- Yes --> AGV[AGV or AMR]
    Q3 -- No --> M4[Manual or Semi Auto Forklift]
    C1 --> Q4{Storage Type}
    Q4 -- High Density --> ASRS[AS RS System]
    Q4 -- Open Yard --> R1[Mobile Crane or Reach Stacker]
```

**How to read this diagram for exam answers:**
- Begin at the **top node (S)**.
- Answer each decision diamond using the *5-P data* from Stage 3.
- The leaf nodes (M1, M2, AGV, etc.) are your *equipment recommendations* — defend them with cost & flexibility data from the **Analytical Matrix** in Section 3.

---

### 4.4 Feedback Loop Architecture — Closed-Loop Control of MH System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plant Floor] -->|Sensor Data| M[Monitoring Layer]
    M -->|KPIs and Dashboards| A[Analysis Engine]
    A -->|Corrective Action| D[Decision Maker]
    D -->|Reconfigure Routes| P
    A -->|Trigger Redesign| S1[Stage 1 Revisited]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram mirrors the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle of Deming and is the conceptual basis for **Industry 4.0** material handling systems. KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes digital integration, so this diagram is high-yield for board answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List and briefly explain any three stages involved in the design of a material handling system.**

**Model Answer:**

The six canonical stages of material handling system design are:

1. **Problem Definition and Data Collection:** The first stage establishes the scope of the study and gathers baseline data — material types, throughput rates, current flow paths, and cost figures. Tools include operator interviews, time studies, and process observation. Without accurate data, all subsequent stages are compromised.

2. **Material Flow Analysis:** The collected data is converted into visual and quantitative flow patterns using tools like **From-To charts, Activity Relationship Charts (ARC), Flow Process Charts, and String Diagrams**. This stage answers the question: *"Where does each material class move, and how often?"*

3. **Layout Design and Integration:** Using the flow data, alternative factory layouts are generated using techniques like **Systematic Layout Planning (SLP)** and **CRAFT algorithm**. The chosen layout must minimize handling distance, respect handling equipment constraints, and comply with ergonomic standards.

> [!NOTE]
> Any three of the six stages may be listed. Marks are awarded as: 1 mark per stage name + 2 marks for explanation. Board checkers often reward diagrams — include a small flowchart for full marks.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the "Unit Load Principle" in material handling. How does it influence equipment selection?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Unit Load Principle** states that *it is faster and cheaper to move a consolidated group of items as a single entity than to move each item individually*. Standardized unit loads use pallets, containers, totes, or bins conforming to **ISO 6780** dimensions.

**Influence on equipment selection:**
- Equipment must be sized to accommodate the **standard unit load dimensions** (e.g., 1200 × 800 mm EUR-pallet).
- Forklifts are selected based on **pallet count per trip** rather than per-piece handling.
- Conveyor widths are designed as multiples of unit-load width.
- **AGV and AS/RS systems** are essentially unit-load handling systems — they cannot efficiently process loose items.

This principle reduces the number of handling operations, lowers labour cost, decreases product damage, and improves inventory accuracy through barcode/RFID tagging at the unit-load level.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulations**, every Module in the question paper offers an **internal choice**. You must answer **either Question A or Question B** below. Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) Describe in detail the six stages involved in the design of a material handling system. Highlight the tools used in each stage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The six stages are:

| Stage | Name | Key Tools Used |
|-------|------|----------------|
| 1 | Problem Definition & Data Collection | Time studies, operator interviews, historical records |
| 2 | Material Flow Analysis | From-To chart, ARC, Flow Process Chart, String Diagram |
| 3 | Handling Requirements Determination | 5-P Framework, Handling Requirements Specification (HRS) |
| 4 | Method & Equipment Selection | Equipment Selection Matrix, Make-or-Buy Analysis |
| 5 | Layout Design & Integration | SLP, CRAFT, ALDEP, 3D simulation |
| 6 | Economic Analysis, Implementation & Control | TCO, ROI, Payback Period, KPI dashboards |

**Detailed expansion:**

- **Stage 1** establishes the *baseline*. Engineers collect data on material weight, dimensions, frequency of movement, distances, and existing bottlenecks. This stage defines the *As-Is* state.
- **Stage 2** converts raw data into visual flow maps. A **From-To chart** tabulates trips between every pair of work centers; an **Activity Relationship Chart** scores the importance of adjacency between departments.
- **Stage 3** uses the **5-P Framework** (Product, Path, Position, Place, Power) to generate an **HRS document** — a tabular specification of every handling need.
- **Stage 4** involves a two-step selection: first the *method* (manual vs. mechanized, continuous vs. cyclical), then the *specific equipment* (conveyor, forklift, AGV, crane, robot).
- **Stage 5** generates layout alternatives using **Systematic Layout Planning** (Muther), evaluates them using **CRAFT**, and refines the chosen layout with 3D simulation tools (e.g., FlexSim, Plant Simulation).
- **Stage 6** performs **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** analysis, computes **payback period**, deploys the system in phases, and monitors **KPIs** (EUR, MTBF, accident rate).

The entire process is iterative — Stage 6 outputs feed back into Stages 1 and 2, forming a closed-loop PDCA cycle.

---

**(b) A manufacturing plant operates 8 hours per day. There are four material handling tasks: Task 1 (T = 0.20 hr/trip, F = 80 trips/day), Task 2 (T = 0.40 hr/trip, F = 30 trips/day), Task 3 (T = 0.60 hr/trip, F = 20 trips/day), Task 4 (T = 0.30 hr/trip, F = 40 trips/day). Utilization factor = 0.75. Determine the number of forklifts required. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute total daily handling demand.**

$$
D_{total} = (0.20 \times 80) + (0.40 \times 30) + (0.60 \times 20) + (0.30 \times 40)
$$

$$
D_{total} = 16 + 12 + 12 + 12 = 52 \text{ hr/day}
$$

**Step 2: Effective working time per forklift per day.**

$$
T_{effective} = H \cdot U = 8 \times 0.75 = 6 \text{ hr/day}
$$

**Step 3: Theoretical number of forklifts.**

$$
N_{theory} = \dfrac{D_{total}}{T_{effective}} = \dfrac{52}{6} = 8.667
$$

**Step 4: Round up to the nearest integer.**

$$
N = \lceil 8.667 \rceil = 9 \text{ forklifts}
$$

**Interpretation:**
The plant requires **9 forklifts**. A fleet of 8 forklifts would result in approximately **0.67 hours** of unmet demand daily, leading to cascading delays. The 9th forklift acts as the buffer recommended by the utilization factor.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) Discuss the role of the "5-P Framework" in the determination of material handling requirements. Illustrate with an example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **5-P Framework** is a structured decision-making tool used in **Stage 3** of material handling system design. It categorizes every handling requirement along five dimensions:

1. **Product (P):** Physical and chemical characteristics — size, weight, shape, fragility, hazardous nature, stackability. *Example:* A glass bottle is fragile; an iron casting is heavy and non-fragile.

2. **Path (P):** The route the material travels — horizontal distance, vertical lift, fixed vs. variable, indoor vs. outdoor, cleanroom vs. general. *Example:* Movement from a dock to a third-floor mezzanine involves both horizontal and vertical motion.

3. **Position (P):** The pickup and drop-off geometry — floor level, conveyor height, rack tier, robotic cell. *Example:* A pallet loaded onto a high-bay rack requires vertical reach capability.

4. **Place (P):** The functional area of activity — receiving, production, storage, assembly, packing, shipping. *Example:* "Place" determines whether the handling equipment must interface with a dock door, a conveyor, or a truck.

5. **Power (P):** The energy source — manual, mechanical, gravity, automated (electric, hydraulic, pneumatic). *Example:* A 50-ton forging can only be moved using powered cranes.

**Illustrative Example — Shipping a 25 kg box of electronic components from receiving dock to the storage rack:**
- Product: 25 kg, fragile electronics, anti-static packaging.
- Path: 30 m horizontal, indoor, smooth concrete floor.
- Position: Floor-level pickup, rack at 1.5 m height.
- Place: Receiving → Storage.
- Power: Mechanized (battery pallet jack) due to weight and repeated cycles.

This HRS guides the engineer to select a **battery-powered electric pallet jack** rather than a manual jack (which would strain operators) or a forklift (which is overkill).

---

**(b) Explain the relationship between layout design and material handling system design. How does the SLP method integrate material handling considerations into facility planning? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**The Bidirectional Relationship:**
Layout design and material handling design are **inseparable** and influence each other iteratively:

- The **layout dictates the distances** materials must travel, the **bottlenecks** they encounter, and the **handling equipment** that can physically fit in the available space.
- Conversely, the **material handling equipment** (e.g., crane span, conveyor length, AGV aisle width) imposes **physical constraints** on the layout — minimum aisle widths, ceiling heights, and turning radii.

A layout optimized for one handling method may be infeasible for another. Therefore, both must be designed in **parallel, iterative loops**.

**SLP (Systematic Layout Planning) by Richard Muther:**

SLP integrates material handling through a four-phase methodology:

1. **Phase I — Location:** Determine *where* the facility should be (regional, market, supply considerations).
2. **Phase II — Overall Layout:** Decide on the **relative positions** of major departments (receiving, production, storage, shipping). Material flow data from **Stage 2** (From-To charts) is used to compute the **Closeness Ratings** in the Activity Relationship Diagram.
3. **Phase III — Detailed Layout:** Plan the *exact placement* of equipment within each department. Material handling dictates **aisle widths, column spacing, and door locations**.
4. **Phase IV — Installation:** Implement the chosen layout, ensuring material flow paths are physically realized as designed.

**Quantitative Integration via CRAFT:**
The **Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique (CRAFT)** algorithm uses the From-To data and material handling cost per unit distance to **iteratively swap department positions** until the total handling cost is minimized. This makes the material handling cost an **objective function** of the layout optimization — a powerful quantitative link.

**Conclusion:**
SLP provides the *qualitative* framework, while CRAFT provides the *quantitative* optimization. Together, they ensure that the final layout is the **minimum-cost arrangement** for the chosen material handling strategy.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Skipping Stage 1:** Many students jump directly to "select equipment." Examiners allocate up to **2 marks** for problem definition. Starting at Stage 4 will cap your answer at ~10/14.
>
> 2. **Confusing MH with MM:** Writing about procurement, inventory EOQ, or vendor selection is **Material Management**, not Material Handling. This is a top reason for losing 2–3 marks in 14-mark answers.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Round Up:** In forklift calculations, fractional answers (e.g., 8.667) must be **rounded up** to 9. Writing "8 forklifts" loses 1 mark.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Units:** Always write units with numerical answers (hr/day, kg/hr, ₹/unit). A correct number without units is treated as incomplete.
>
> 5. **Not Justifying Equipment Choice:** Simply listing equipment without referencing the **5-P Framework** or **Equipment Selection Matrix** yields partial credit only.
>
> 6. **Diagram-Free Answers:** A well-labelled flowchart or block diagram in a 14-mark answer typically fetches **+1 to +2 marks** beyond the textual content.
>
> 7. **One-Word Part A Answers:** Three-mark questions require *at least three sentences or one diagram*. One-line answers fetch at most 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stages in Material Handling System Design**

- [x] MH system design follows **six sequential stages** — Problem Definition → Flow Analysis → Requirements → Equipment Selection → Layout → Economic Analysis & Control.
- [x] The process is **iterative**, not linear — Stage 6 feeds back to Stages 1 and 2.
- [x] **Stage 1** establishes the *As-Is baseline* through data collection.
- [x] **Stage 2** uses **From-To charts, ARCs, Flow Process Charts, and String Diagrams**.
- [x] **Stage 3** uses the **5-P Framework** (Product, Path, Position, Place, Power).
- [x] **Stage 4** involves *method selection* first, then *equipment selection*.
- [x] **Stage 5** integrates the layout using **SLP** (qualitative) and **CRAFT** (quantitative).
- [x] **Stage 6** uses **TCO, ROI, Payback Period, and KPIs** (EUR, MTBF, accident rate).
- [x] Forklift fleet sizing formula: $N = \lceil \frac{\sum T_i F_i}{H \cdot U} \rceil$ — **always round up**.
- [x] Conveyor capacity formula: $Q = A \cdot v \cdot \rho \cdot k$.
- [x] The **Unit Load Principle** (ISO 6780) is a foundational concept — pallets 1200 × 800 mm (EUR) or 1219 × 1016 mm (GMA).
- [x] **Immer's 10 Principles** of material handling are the philosophical foundation — especially Planning, Systems, Gravity, and Unit Load principles.
- [x] Material handling typically consumes **15–70%** of manufacturing cost — a major economic lever.
- [x] **PDCA / closed-loop feedback** is essential for continuous improvement (Industry 4.0 readiness).
- [x] Always **justify equipment choice** using at least two criteria from the comparative matrix.
- [x] In board answers: **define → explain → illustrate with example → conclude with industrial relevance.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Material Storage and Store Keeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Material Storage — Formal Academic Definition

**Material Storage** is the systematic function within an industrial or production engineering environment that involves the holding, safeguarding, and preservation of raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, finished goods, tools, and consumables in designated physical facilities (stores, warehouses, or yards) from the time of their receipt until their issue for consumption or dispatch.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, material storage is one of the three core sub-functions of the broader discipline of **Materials Management**, the other two being **Material Handling** and **Inventory Control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Material storage is NOT just "keeping things in a room." It is a *planned, controlled, and documented* engineering function that ensures **material availability, quality preservation, and operational continuity** of an industrial enterprise.

## 1.2 Store Keeping — Formal Academic Definition

**Store Keeping** is the operational execution of storage activities. It is the day-to-day administrative and physical management of stored materials, encompassing receiving, binning, recording, issuing, returning, and dispatching activities carried out by a designated department known as the **Stores Department**, under the supervision of a **Storekeeper**.

| Term | Scope | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Material Storage | Planning, policy, location, layout | Strategic |
| Store Keeping | Receiving, issuing, recording | Operational |
| Warehousing | Bulk storage with mechanical handling | Tactical |
| Inventory Control | Quantity and timing decisions | Managerial |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen Pantry" Model**
> 
> Imagine your home **kitchen pantry**. You don't just dump rice, dal, and spices anywhere. You:
> - Keep grains in **airtight containers** (preservation),
> - Place frequently-used items at **eye level** (accessibility),
> - Label every jar with **name and date of receipt** (identification & traceability),
> - Follow a **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) order so older items are used first (rotation),
> - Maintain a **shopping list** that triggers a refill when stocks dip below a threshold (reorder level),
> - Lock away cleaning acids away from food (segregation & safety).
> 
> A **factory store** works on the exact same principles — only the scale, value, and consequences of error are **a thousand times larger**. A mislabeled item in a pantry wastes ₹50; a misissued component on a Boeing assembly line can cost **millions of dollars** or, worse, human lives.

## 1.4 Objectives of Material Storage and Store Keeping

The principal objectives, as enumerated in standard KTU-recommended production engineering texts, are:

1. **Preservation** — to protect materials against deterioration, damage, pilferage, and obsolescence.
2. **Identification** — to ensure every item can be unambiguously located and traced.
3. **Availability** — to make the right material, in the right quantity, available at the right time.
4. **Documentation** — to maintain accurate, auditable records of receipts, issues, and balances.
5. **Cost Minimization** — to reduce carrying costs, obsolescence costs, and storage-space costs.
6. **Safety and Security** — to safeguard personnel, materials, and the environment.
7. **Space Optimization** — to achieve maximum storage density without compromising accessibility.
8. **Coordination** — to act as a service-oriented interface between procurement, production, and dispatch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Storage Systems

Material storage can be broadly classified along multiple dimensions.

### A. Based on Material Form

| Storage Type | Typical Materials | Engineering Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Bulk Storage** | Liquids, granules, powders, ore, coal | Silos, bunkers, tanks, hoppers |
| **Unit Storage** | Discrete items, boxes, pallets, components | Racks, bins, shelves, carousels |
| **Open-Yard Storage** | Heavy machinery, lumber, steel sections | Outdoor fenced yards with cranes |
| **Hazardous Storage** | Flammables, explosives, toxic chemicals | Flame-proof cabinets, isolated bays |

### B. Based on Accessibility

| Storage Type | Characteristic | Access Time | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Random Storage** | Any free slot is used | Higher | Lower |
| **Dedicated Storage** | Fixed slot for each SKU | Lower | Higher |
| **Class-Based Storage** | SKUs grouped by class turnover | Medium | Medium |
| **Volume-Based Storage** | Fast-movers near I/O points | Optimized | Variable |

### C. Based on Function

- **Raw Material Store (RMS)** — receives inputs from suppliers.
- **Work-In-Progress Store (WIP)** — holds semi-finished items between operations.
- **Finished Goods Store (FGS)** — holds products ready for dispatch.
- **Tool Store** — holds jigs, fixtures, gauges, and cutting tools.
- **Consumable Store** — oils, fasteners, welding rods, lubricants.
- **Salvage/Scrap Store** — rejects, recyclable waste, obsolete items.

## 2.2 Functions of the Stores Department

The Stores Department performs **ten core functions**, often tested in KTU examinations:

1. **Receiving** — unloading, inspection, and acceptance of incoming materials against a Purchase Order (PO).
2. **Inspection Coordination** — routing materials to Quality Control before acceptance.
3. **Identification and Labeling** — assigning store codes, bin numbers, and material codes.
4. **Bin Allocation and Binning** — placing materials in designated locations.
5. **Recording** — updating the Bin Card, Stores Ledger, and Stock Register.
6. **Issuing** — releasing materials against authorized Material Requisition Notes (MRN).
7. **Stock Verification** — periodic physical stock-taking and reconciliation.
8. **Preservation** — applying anti-rust treatments, fumigation, climate control.
9. **Disposal** — releasing obsolete, surplus, or scrap items.
10. **Reporting** — preparing stock statements, aging analysis, and discrepancy reports.

## 2.3 Storage Equipment & Layout Principles

### 2.3.1 Storage Equipment Hierarchy

```
Level 1 — Pallets and Containers (Unit Load Devices)
        │
Level 2 — Shelving and Racks (Static Storage)
        │
Level 3 — Bins, Drawers, Cabinets (Fine Picking)
        │
Level 4 — Mezzanines (Vertical Space Utilization)
        │
Level 5 — Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)
```

### 2.3.2 Layout Principles (KTU Frequently Tested)

- **Fixed Location System** — Each item has a permanent, predefined slot.
- **Random Location System** — Items go to the nearest available slot.
- **Zone Pick** — Store divided into zones assigned to specific pickers.
- **ABC Class-Based Layout** — High-value 'A' items closest to the Issue Counter.
- **U-Shape Flow** — Receipt on one side, issue on the adjacent side (single operator covers both).

## 2.4 Storage-Related Cost Components

The total cost of storage can be decomposed into:

$$
C_{total} = C_{capital} + C_{carrying} + C_{ordering} + C_{stockout} + C_{obsolescence}
$$

Where each term is defined in the table below.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{capital}$ | Cost of capital tied up | $C_{capital} = i \cdot P \cdot Q_{avg}$ | ₹ / year |
| $C_{carrying}$ | Carrying cost | $C_{carrying} = c_c \cdot P \cdot Q_{avg}$ | ₹ / year |
| $C_{ordering}$ | Ordering cost per cycle | $C_{ordering} = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S$ | ₹ / year |
| $C_{stockout}$ | Shortage cost | $C_{stockout} = p_s \cdot E[shortage]$ | ₹ / year |
| $C_{obsolescence}$ | Obsolescence cost | $C_{obsolescence} = \alpha \cdot P \cdot Q_{obsolete}$ | ₹ / year |
| $i$ | Interest / opportunity rate | typically $\mathbf{0.10}$ to $\mathbf{0.20}$ | dimensionless |
| $P$ | Unit price | given | ₹ |
| $Q_{avg}$ | Average inventory | $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ (constant demand) | units |
| $D$ | Annual demand | given | units / year |
| $Q$ | Order quantity | decision variable | units |
| $S$ | Ordering cost / order | given | ₹ / order |
| $c_c$ | Carrying cost fraction | typically $\mathbf{0.15}$ to $\mathbf{0.25}$ | dimensionless |
| $\alpha$ | Obsolescence fraction | typically $\mathbf{0.02}$ to $\mathbf{0.10}$ | dimensionless |
| $EOQ$ | Economic Order Quantity | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{c_c P}}$ | units |
| $T$ | Cycle time | $T = \dfrac{Q}{D}$ | year |
| $N$ | Number of orders / year | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q}$ | orders / year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the bedrock of **warehouse economics**. A production engineer uses them to decide warehouse size, optimum re-order quantity, and total cost of holding stock — directly impacting the firm's working capital and profitability.

## 2.6 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of Store Keeping

For KTU analytical questions, remember these KPIs:

$$
\text{Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)} = \frac{\text{Cost of Goods Issued}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}}
$$

$$
\text{Carrying Cost \%} = \frac{\text{Total Carrying Cost}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Stockout Frequency} = \frac{\text{Number of Stockout Events}}{\text{Total Replenishment Cycles}}
$$

$$
\text{Storage Space Utilization} = \frac{\text{Volume Occupied}}{\text{Total Available Volume}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Pick Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct Picks}}{\text{Total Picks}} \times 100\%
$$

A **world-class** store typically targets: **ITR $\geq$ 8**, **Pick Accuracy $\geq$ 99.9%**, **Space Utilization $\geq$ 85%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Storage Cost Function

The derivation below establishes the **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** of an inventory system — a KTU-favorite derivation.

**Step 1 — Define the Average Inventory**

Assume demand is uniform over time and the inventory depletes linearly from $Q$ to $0$ before a new order arrives. The average inventory is the arithmetic mean of maximum and minimum:

$$
Q_{avg} = \frac{Q + 0}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Carrying Cost**

Carrying cost is the cost of holding one unit for one year, multiplied by average inventory:

$$
C_{carrying} = c_c \cdot P \cdot \frac{Q}{2}
$$

where $c_c$ is the carrying cost expressed as a fraction of unit price $P$.

**Step 3 — Annual Ordering Cost**

If annual demand is $D$ and each order is for $Q$ units, the number of orders per year is $\dfrac{D}{Q}$. Each order incurs a fixed administrative cost $S$:

$$
C_{ordering} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S
$$

**Step 4 — Total Relevant Cost**

$$
TRC(Q) = C_{carrying} + C_{ordering}
$$

Substituting:

$$
TRC(Q) = c_c \cdot P \cdot \frac{Q}{2} + \frac{D \cdot S}{Q}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimize TRC with respect to Q**

Take the first derivative and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d(TRC)}{dQ} = \frac{c_c P}{2} - \frac{D S}{Q^2} = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for EOQ**

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{c_c P}{2} = \frac{D S}{Q^2}
$$

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2 D S}{c_c P}
$$

$$
EOQ = Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{c_c P}}
$$

**Step 7 — Minimum Total Cost**

Substituting $Q^*$ back into the cost function:

$$
TRC_{min} = c_c P \cdot \frac{Q^*}{2} + \frac{D S}{Q^*}
$$

$$
TRC_{min} = \sqrt{2 D S c_c P}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At the optimum, **Ordering Cost = Carrying Cost**. This is a beautiful symmetry — the system is balanced; halving the order quantity would double the carrying cost but halve the ordering cost, leaving total cost unchanged to first order.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Orders and Cycle Time

Given $Q^*$:

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{D}{\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{c_c P}}} = \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot c_c P}{2 S}}
$$

$$
T = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\frac{2 S}{D \cdot c_c P}} \quad \text{[years]}
$$

In days:

$$
T_{days} = T \times 365 = 365 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 S}{D \cdot c_c P}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reorder Level (ROL) with Safety Stock

When demand fluctuates and lead time is variable, a safety stock $SS$ is maintained:

$$
ROL = (\text{Average Demand per day}) \times (\text{Average Lead Time in days}) + SS
$$

Letting $\bar{d}$ = average daily demand, $\bar{L}$ = average lead time in days, $\sigma_d$ = std. dev. of daily demand, $\sigma_L$ = std. dev. of lead time:

$$
ROL = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot \sigma_{dLT}
$$

where $\sigma_{dLT}$ is the standard deviation of demand during lead time:

$$
\sigma_{dLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^2 + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_L^2}
$$

and $z$ is the **service-level z-score** (e.g., $z = 1.65$ for **95%** service level, $z = 2.33$ for **99%**).

**Worked Numerical Example (for KTU-style practice):**

Given:
- $\bar{d} = 100$ units/day
- $\bar{L} = 6$ days
- $\sigma_d = 20$ units/day
- $\sigma_L = 1$ day
- Service level = 95%, hence $z = 1.65$

Step 1 — Compute $\sigma_{dLT}$:

$$
\sigma_{dLT} = \sqrt{(6)(20^2) + (100^2)(1^2)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{dLT} = \sqrt{2400 + 10000} = \sqrt{12400} \approx 111.36 \text{ units}
$$

Step 2 — Compute ROL:

$$
ROL = (100)(6) + (1.65)(111.36)
$$

$$
ROL = 600 + 183.74 \approx 783.74 \text{ units}
$$

Step 3 — Round up to next integer (units are indivisible):

$$
ROL \approx 784 \text{ units}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Storage Systems

| Criterion | Dedicated Storage | Random Storage | Class-Based | AS/RS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Travel Distance | Low | Medium | Low | Very Low |
| Space Utilization | Low | High | Medium | Very High |
| Labor Cost | High | Low | Medium | Very Low |
| Initial Investment | Low | Low | Medium | Very High |
| Best Suited For | Low-SKU, high-throughput | Many SKUs | Mixed | High-SKU, high-volume |
| Complexity | Very Low | Low | Medium | High |
| KTU Exam Frequency | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |

## 3.5 Analytical Decision — When to Use Which Store Type

```
Is the material hazardous?
├── YES → Isolated Hazardous Store with statutory compliance (Factories Act)
└── NO
    │
    Is it a liquid / gas / bulk solid?
    ├── YES → Bulk storage (tank / silo / bunker)
    └── NO
        │
        Is it a high-value, low-volume item?
        ├── YES → Lockable cabinet / safe (security-dominant)
        └── NO
            │
            Is the item fast-moving (A-class)?
            ├── YES → Pallet rack near issue counter
            └── NO → Bin shelving in remote zone (deep storage)
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Material Flow in a Typical Stores Department

The diagram below shows the sequential flow of materials from receipt to dispatch, with the Stores Department acting as the central hub.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supplier / Vendor] --> B[Receiving Bay]
    B --> C{Quality Inspection}
    C -->|Accepted| D[GRN Generated]
    C -->|Rejected| E[Rejection Note Returned]
    D --> F[Binning at Designated Location]
    F --> G[Bin Card Updated]
    G --> H[Stores Ledger Updated]
    H --> I{Material Requisition Received}
    I -->|Authorized| J[Issue Note Generated]
    I -->|Unauthorized| K[Request Returned]
    J --> L[Material Issued to Production]
    L --> M[Stores Ledger Reduced]
    M --> N{Stock Below Reorder Level?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Purchase Requisition to Buying Dept]
    N -->|No| P[Continue Monitoring]
    O --> A
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Store Keeping System

This block diagram maps the logical interactions between the key sub-systems that together constitute a store keeping function.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        I1[Goods Receipt Note]
        I2[Material Requisition Note]
        I3[Return Note]
        I4[Transfer Note]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Core Processing Subsystem]
        P1[Identification and Coding]
        P2[Binning and Location Allocation]
        P3[Bin Card Maintenance]
        P4[Stores Ledger Update]
        P5[Stock Verification Module]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Subsystem]
        O1[Issue Voucher]
        O2[Stock Statement]
        O3[Aging Analysis Report]
        O4[Disposal Note]
        O5[Reorder Alert]
    end

    subgraph SUPPORT[Support Subsystem]
        S1[Safety Module]
        S2[Preservation Module]
        S3[Security Module]
        S4[Space Management Module]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    I4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> O1
    P5 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
    P5 --> O4
    P5 --> O5
    S1 -.-> P2
    S2 -.-> P2
    S3 -.-> P2
    S4 -.-> P2
```

## 4.3 Storage Layout — U-Shape Floor Plan (Logical View)

The U-shape layout is one of the most efficient warehouse arrangements because a single storekeeper can supervise both receipt and issue points.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph U_LAYOUT[U-Shape Warehouse Layout]
        R1[Receiving Dock] --> R2[Inspection Area]
        R2 --> R3[Quarantine Zone]
        R3 --> R4[Bulk Storage Zone]
        R4 --> R5[Pallet Rack Zone]
        R5 --> R6[Bin Shelf Zone]
        R6 --> R7[Picking Area]
        R7 --> I1[Issue Counter]
        I1 --> D1[Dispatch Dock]
        D1 --> R1
    end
```

## 4.4 ABC Classification Logic — Decision Tree

The ABC classification is a direct consequence of the **Pareto Principle** (80/20 rule) and is extensively tested in KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Annual Consumption Value = P times D] --> B{Cumulative Value Percent}
    B -->|0 to 80 percent| C[A-Class Items 10 to 20 percent of SKUs]
    B -->|80 to 95 percent| D[B-Class Items 20 to 30 percent of SKUs]
    B -->|95 to 100 percent| E[C-Class Items 50 to 70 percent of SKUs]
    C --> F[Strict Control, Frequent Review, Tight Security]
    D --> G[Moderate Control, Periodic Review]
    E --> H[Loose Control, Bulk Systems, Two-Bin System]
```

## 4.5 Store Keeping Process — Swimlane Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BUYER[Buying Department]
        B1[Issue Purchase Order]
    end
    subgraph SUPPLIER[Vendor]
        S1[Deliver Material]
    end
    subgraph STORES[Stores Department]
        ST1[Receive and Unload]
        ST2[Inspect Quality]
        ST3[Bin and Label]
        ST4[Update Bin Card and Ledger]
        ST5[Issue on MRN]
    end
    subgraph PRODUCTION[Production Department]
        P1[Raise Material Requisition]
        P2[Receive Material]
    end
    subgraph ACCOUNTS[Accounts Department]
        A1[Verify and Value Stock]
    end
    B1 --> S1
    S1 --> ST1
    ST1 --> ST2
    ST2 --> ST3
    ST3 --> ST4
    P1 --> ST5
    ST5 --> P2
    ST4 --> A1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define material storage. List any four functions of the Stores Department.**

**Model Answer:**

**Material Storage** is the systematic holding, preservation, and safeguarding of raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, finished goods, and consumables in designated facilities from the time of receipt until issue for consumption or dispatch.

**Four functions of the Stores Department:**

1. **Receiving** — unloading and accepting incoming materials against the Purchase Order.
2. **Identification and Labeling** — assigning unique codes, bin numbers, and material codes to every item.
3. **Recording** — maintaining the Bin Card, Stores Ledger, and Stock Register.
4. **Issuing** — releasing materials against authorized Material Requisition Notes (MRN).

*(Any four from the ten functions listed in Section 2.2 are acceptable.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between 'Material Storage' and 'Store Keeping'.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Material Storage | Store Keeping |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Strategic — involves policy, location, and layout decisions | Operational — day-to-day receipt, issue, and recording |
| **Performed by** | Materials Manager / Plant Layout Engineer | Storekeeper and Stores Staff |
| **Time Horizon** | Long-term planning | Real-time execution |
| **Decision Type** | Where and how much to store | When and to whom to issue |
| **KTU Terminology** | Part of the Materials Management function | Sub-function of Storage |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **functions of the Stores Department** with a neat flowchart of material flow. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm uses **15,000 units** of a component annually. The unit price is **₹40**, the ordering cost is **₹250 per order**, and the carrying cost fraction is **20% per year**. Compute the **EOQ**, the **number of orders per year**, the **cycle time in days**, and the **minimum total annual cost**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functions of the Stores Department and Flow Chart:**

The Stores Department performs the following ten functions:

1. **Receiving Materials** — Unloading goods from the carrier, verifying quantity and condition against the Purchase Order and Goods Receipt Note.
2. **Inspection Coordination** — Routing material to Quality Control for acceptance testing before binning.
3. **Identification** — Tagging each item with a unique store code, part number, and bin location.
4. **Binning** — Placing the material in its designated bin/rack/shelf.
5. **Recording** — Updating the Bin Card (item-level) and Stores Ledger (accounting-level).
6. **Issuing** — Releasing material against a properly authorized Material Requisition Note (MRN).
7. **Stock Verification** — Conducting periodic physical stock-takes and reconciling with book stock.
8. **Preservation** — Applying protective measures: anti-rust oil, humidity control, fumigation, climate control.
9. **Reporting** — Generating stock statements, aging reports, slow-moving analysis, and ABC reports.
10. **Disposal** — Identifying and disposing of obsolete, surplus, or scrap items through approved channels.

**Material Flow Chart:**

```
Supplier → Receiving Bay → QC Inspection → (if accepted) → GRN → 
Binning → Bin Card Updated → Stores Ledger Updated → 
(against MRN) → Issue to Production → Ledger Reduced → 
(Reorder Triggered) → Purchase Requisition → Buying Dept → Supplier
```

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:**
- $D = 15{,}000$ units/year
- $P = ₹40$ per unit
- $S = ₹250$ per order
- $c_c = 0.20$ per year

**Step 1 — EOQ**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{c_c P}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 15{,}000 \times 250}{0.20 \times 40}}
$$

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{7{,}500{,}000}{8}} = \sqrt{937{,}500}
$$

$$
EOQ \approx 968.25 \text{ units} \approx 968 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Orders per Year**

$$
N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{15{,}000}{968.25} \approx 15.49 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Cycle Time in Days**

$$
T_{days} = \frac{365}{N} = \frac{365}{15.49} \approx 23.56 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Total Annual Cost**

$$
TRC_{min} = \sqrt{2 D S c_c P}
$$

$$
TRC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 15{,}000 \times 250 \times 0.20 \times 40}
$$

$$
TRC_{min} = \sqrt{60{,}000{,}000} = 7746 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Verification by splitting:**

$$
C_{carrying} = c_c \cdot P \cdot \frac{EOQ}{2} = 0.20 \times 40 \times 484.125 = ₹3{,}873
$$

$$
C_{ordering} = \frac{D \cdot S}{EOQ} = \frac{15{,}000 \times 250}{968.25} = ₹3{,}873
$$

The two halves are **equal**, confirming the optimum. ✓

**Final Answers:** $EOQ \approx 968$ units, $N \approx 15.49$ orders/year, $T \approx 23.56$ days, $TRC_{min} \approx ₹7{,}746$/year.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of storage systems** based on material form and accessibility. Explain **ABC classification** in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An item has average daily demand of **80 units**, average lead time of **8 days**, standard deviation of daily demand of **15 units**, and standard deviation of lead time of **2 days**. The store wishes to maintain a **97.5% service level** ($z = 1.96$). Calculate the **Reorder Level (ROL)** and the **Safety Stock**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Storage Systems:**

**Based on Material Form:**

1. **Bulk Storage** — Used for liquids, gases, granules, and powders. Examples: silos, bunkers, tanks, hoppers.
2. **Unit Storage** — Used for discrete items in boxes, cartons, or pallets. Examples: pallet racks, bin shelving, carousels.
3. **Open-Yard Storage** — Used for heavy machinery, steel sections, lumber, and items unaffected by weather.
4. **Hazardous Storage** — Specialized flame-proof, isolated bays for flammable, explosive, or toxic materials.

**Based on Accessibility:**

1. **Dedicated Storage** — Each SKU has a fixed, permanent slot. Faster retrieval but lower space utilization.
2. **Random Storage** — Any free slot is used; the WMS records the location. Higher space utilization but longer retrieval.
3. **Class-Based Storage** — SKUs are grouped into classes (A, B, C) and each class has a designated zone.
4. **Volume-Based Storage** — Fast-movers are placed near I/O docks; slow-movers in remote deep-storage zones.

**ABC Classification (Detailed):**

The ABC classification is based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** applied to inventory.

**Procedure:**

1. Compute the **Annual Consumption Value** for each SKU: $ACV = P \times D$.
2. Sort SKUs in **descending order** of ACV.
3. Compute **cumulative ACV** and **cumulative %** of total ACV.
4. Classify:
   - **A-items:** Top **10–20%** of SKUs contributing to **~80%** of ACV.
   - **B-items:** Next **20–30%** of SKUs contributing to **~15%** of ACV.
   - **C-items:** Bottom **50–70%** of SKUs contributing to **~5%** of ACV.

**Control Policy:**

| Class | Control | Review Frequency | Safety Stock |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Tight | Continuous / Weekly | High |
| B | Moderate | Monthly | Medium |
| C | Loose | Quarterly / Two-bin | Low |

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:**
- $\bar{d} = 80$ units/day
- $\bar{L} = 8$ days
- $\sigma_d = 15$ units/day
- $\sigma_L = 2$ days
- $z = 1.96$ (97.5% service level)

**Step 1 — Standard Deviation of Demand During Lead Time**

$$
\sigma_{dLT} = \sqrt{\bar{L} \cdot \sigma_d^2 + \bar{d}^{\,2} \cdot \sigma_L^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{dLT} = \sqrt{(8)(15^2) + (80^2)(2^2)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{dLT} = \sqrt{(8)(225) + (6400)(4)} = \sqrt{1800 + 25600} = \sqrt{27400}
$$

$$
\sigma_{dLT} \approx 165.53 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Safety Stock**

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_{dLT} = 1.96 \times 165.53 \approx 324.44 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Reorder Level**

$$
ROL = \bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + SS
$$

$$
ROL = (80)(8) + 324.44 = 640 + 324.44 = 964.44 \text{ units}
$$

**Final Answers:** $SS \approx 324$ units, $ROL \approx 965$ units.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Safety Stock with ROL:** Many students write $ROL = SS$ alone. This is **wrong**. ROL always = **average demand during lead time + safety stock**. The safety stock is *only the buffer*; the base demand must still be added.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units consistency:** Lead time and demand daily rates must be in matching units. If lead time is given in weeks, convert to days before multiplying.
> 
> 3. **Mis-applying EOQ when demand is uncertain:** EOQ assumes **deterministic** demand. When demand is stochastic (as in part B above), only ROL and SS are computed — EOQ is not the right tool.
> 
> 4. **Decimal units:** Always round ROL **up**, not down. Better to over-stock by 1 unit than to lose production.
> 
> 5. **Z-value table errors:** For 95% service level use $z = 1.65$; for 97.5% use $z = 1.96$; for 99% use $z = 2.33$. Mixing these values is a common 1-mark loss.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Material Storage** is the strategic function; **Store Keeping** is its operational execution.
- The **Stores Department** acts as the service interface between procurement, production, and dispatch.
- The **ten core functions** of the Stores Department are: Receive, Inspect, Identify, Bin, Record, Issue, Verify, Preserve, Report, Dispose.
- The **cost of storage** has five components: capital, carrying, ordering, stockout, and obsolescence.
- The **EOQ formula** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS / (c_c P)}$ minimizes the sum of ordering and carrying costs.
- At optimum, **Ordering Cost = Carrying Cost** — this is the equilibrium condition.
- The **Reorder Level (ROL)** under uncertainty is $\bar{d} \cdot \bar{L} + z \cdot \sigma_{dLT}$.
- The **ABC classification** applies the Pareto principle: ~20% of SKUs account for ~80% of value.
- The **U-shape layout** allows one storekeeper to supervise both receipt and issue.
- **AS/RS** offers the highest space utilization and lowest labor cost but requires the largest capital investment.
- **Key KPIs** to remember: Inventory Turnover Ratio, Pick Accuracy, Space Utilization, Carrying Cost %.
- **Safety Stock** is *not* ROL — it is only the buffer part of the ROL.
- Always **match units** (days/weeks/months) before any computation; round ROL **up**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scope and Objectives of Store Keeping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Scope and Objectives of Store Keeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Store Keeping** is the systematic, scientific, and administrative function within an organization that encompasses the receipt, storage, preservation, issue, and accounting of materials (raw materials, components, semi-finished goods, tools, spares, and finished goods) in a manner that ensures their availability, usability, and traceability at the lowest possible cost.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, store keeping is treated as the **operational backbone** of the broader discipline of *Materials Management*. It forms the **physical and informational bridge** between the purchasing function (procurement) and the production/operations function (consumption).

> [!NOTE]
> A *Store* refers to the physical location (warehouse, godown, bin, rack) where materials are held, while *Store Keeping* refers to the entire set of policies, procedures, personnel, and systems that govern the custody and movement of those materials.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **large hospital pharmacy** that must dispense thousands of medicines daily:
- The pharmacist (storekeeper) does not just hand out medicine; he must **receive** shipments, **verify** quality and quantity, **store** them under proper conditions (refrigerated, dry, locked), **track** expiry dates, **issue** them in correct doses, and **maintain** a register of every movement.
- If the pharmacy is disorganized, critical life-saving drugs might be unavailable when needed, or expired stock might be dispensed.

The industrial store is the **"pharmacy of the factory"** — a controlled, accountable custody zone that guarantees the right material, in the right quantity, of the right quality, at the right time, and at the right cost — commonly abbreviated as the **5R Principle of Store Keeping**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Bin** | A designated storage location identified by a unique code |
| **Stock** | The physical quantity of material held in store |
| **Inventory** | The total value or list of all stocked items |
| **Issue** | The act of releasing material from store to user department |
| **Receipt** | The act of accepting incoming material into the store |
| **Stock-Keeping Unit (SKU)** | A unique code identifying each distinct item |
| **Stores Ledger** | The formal accounting record of every receipt, issue, and balance |
| **Bin Card** | A location-wise card showing running balance of an item |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scope of Store Keeping

The **scope** of store keeping is wide and touches every functional area of an industrial enterprise. It is not limited to "putting things in a room" — it is a strategic, cross-functional discipline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU valuation, the **scope** of a topic is the *range of activities, functions, and boundaries* within which that discipline operates. Examiners frequently ask: *"Discuss the scope of store keeping"* (14-mark type).

The scope can be classified under **eight broad dimensions**:

#### A. Receiving Function
- Acceptance of incoming materials from suppliers.
- Physical verification of quantity, quality, and specifications against the **Purchase Order (PO)** and **Goods Received Note (GRN)**.
- Inspection coordination with the Quality Assurance (QA) department.
- Documentation of shortages, damages, or rejections.

#### B. Storage and Preservation Function
- Allocation of **bins, racks, shelves, and zones** to different categories of materials.
- Application of preservation techniques: anti-rust coating, desiccants, humidity control, oiling, wrapping, FIFO/FEFO rotation.
- Segregation of hazardous, flammable, fragile, and perishable items.
- Ensuring compliance with safety norms (Factories Act, OSHA standards).

#### C. Issuing Function
- Release of materials against authorized **Material Requisition Notes (MRN)** or **Issue Notes**.
- Verification of authorization, indent details, and project/work-order codes.
- First-In-First-Out (FIFO) or First-Expired-First-Out (FEFO) issue policy enforcement.

#### D. Stock Recording and Accounting Function
- Maintenance of the **Stores Ledger**, **Bin Cards**, and **Stock Registers**.
- Reconciliation of physical stock with book stock.
- Periodic stock valuation: **LIFO, FIFO, Weighted Average, Standard Cost**.

#### E. Inventory Control Function
- Setting of **re-order levels (ROL)**, **re-order quantities (ROQ)**, **safety stock**, and **maximum/minimum stock levels**.
- Classification of inventory using **ABC Analysis**, **VED Analysis**, **FSN Analysis**, **SDE Analysis**, **HML Analysis**.
