- Monitoring slow-moving, non-moving, and obsolete stock.

#### F. Material Handling Function
- Coordination with the material handling team for movement in/out of store.
- Use of appropriate handling equipment: forklifts, conveyors, cranes, pallets, AGVs.

#### G. Disposal and Salvage Function
- Identification of scrap, surplus, obsolete, and rejected materials.
- Disposal through sale, auction, or write-off as per company policy.
- Recovery and reuse of salvageable items.

#### H. Information and Reporting Function
- Generation of **stock reports, consumption reports, ageing reports, and variance reports**.
- Feedback to purchase, production, and finance departments.
- Use of ERP/MIS systems for real-time data.

### 2.2 Objectives of Store Keeping

The **objectives** are the *goals* that store keeping seeks to achieve. In KTU 2024 ESE, a 14-mark question often asks: *"Explain the objectives/functions of store keeping."* A model answer must list, define, and link each objective to operational benefit.

> [!NOTE]
> A common student pitfall is confusing **scope (what store keeping does)** with **objectives (what store keeping aims to achieve)**. Examiners award marks for explicitly stating the *why* behind each activity.

The **10 Principal Objectives** are tabulated below:

| # | Objective | Operational Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uninterrupted Material Supply** | To ensure that production, maintenance, and service departments never face material shortages |
| 2 | **Minimum Inventory Investment** | To hold the *minimum possible* stock consistent with operational continuity — i.e., lowest working capital lock-up |
| 3 | **Efficient Material Handling** | To reduce internal transportation time, labour cost, and material damage |
| 4 | **Prevention of Loss & Waste** | To protect materials from theft, deterioration, fire, pilferage, and obsolescence |
| 5 | **Quality Preservation** | To maintain materials in usable condition until consumption |
| 6 | **Accurate Records & Accountability** | To maintain verifiable documents (bin cards, ledgers) for every gram of material |
| 7 | **Cost Control** | To minimize ordering cost, carrying cost, and shortage cost |
| 8 | **Effective Space Utilization** | To maximize use of cubic storage space via proper layout, racking, and zoning |
| 9 | **Safety & Compliance** | To ensure safe storage of hazardous materials and statutory compliance |
| 10 | **Information Support** | To provide timely, accurate data for purchase, production, and finance decisions |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

While store keeping is largely a management discipline, KTU frequently tests **inventory-level formulas** that fall within its scope.

> [!WARNING]
> Never use the vertical pipe `|` in a markdown table cell. Use `\vert` for modulus.

| Formula | Meaning | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $ROL = (D \times L) + SS$ | Re-Order Level | $D$ = Average daily demand (units/day), $L$ = Lead time (days), $SS$ = Safety Stock (units) |
| $ROQ = EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2AO}{C_{c}}}$ | Economic Order Quantity | $A$ = Annual demand (units/yr), $O$ = Ordering cost per order (\$), $C_{c}$ = Carrying cost per unit per year (\$) |
| $SS = Z \times \sigma_{d} \times \sqrt{L}$ | Safety Stock (statistical) | $Z$ = Service level z-score, $\sigma_{d}$ = Std deviation of daily demand, $L$ = Lead time (days) |
| $MSL = ROL + (D \times L_{max}) - (D \times L_{min})$ | Maximum Stock Level | $L_{max}$ = Maximum lead time, $L_{min}$ = Minimum lead time |
| $Avg.\ Stock = \dfrac{ROQ}{2} + SS$ | Average Inventory | — |
| $Carrying\ Cost\ \% = \dfrac{Inventory\ Carrying\ Cost}{Avg.\ Inventory\ Value} \times 100$ | Carrying Cost Rate | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- In **manufacturing plants**, store keeping ensures the assembly line is never idle.
- In **hospitals and pharmaceutical firms**, it guarantees drug availability and prevents expiry losses.
- In **aviation/MRO sectors**, spare-parts store keeping is critical — a missing bolt can ground an aircraft.
- In **e-commerce warehouses**, store keeping (with automation) is the core of order fulfilment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

The EOQ is the most frequently tested formula in KTU under store-keeping scope. Let us derive it rigorously.

#### Step 1: Define the Cost Components

The **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** of an item comprises three parts:
1. **Material Cost** (constant — does not depend on order quantity, hence ignored in optimization).
2. **Ordering Cost (OC)**: Cost incurred every time an order is placed (paperwork, inspection, follow-up).
3. **Carrying Cost (CC)**: Cost of holding inventory (rent, insurance, capital, obsolescence, shrinkage).

Let:
- $A$ = Annual demand in units
- $Q$ = Order quantity (decision variable) in units
- $O$ = Ordering cost per order in $\$$
- $C_{c}$ = Carrying cost per unit per year in \$
- $C_{u}$ = Unit cost in \$

#### Step 2: Express Annual Ordering Cost

The number of orders per year $= \dfrac{A}{Q}$.

Therefore,

$$
Annual\ Ordering\ Cost = \dfrac{A}{Q} \times O
$$

#### Step 3: Express Annual Carrying Cost

If orders of size $Q$ arrive, average inventory $= \dfrac{Q}{2}$.

Therefore,

$$
Annual\ Carrying\ Cost = \dfrac{Q}{2} \times C_{c}
$$

#### Step 4: Form the Total Cost Function

$$
TIC(Q) = \underbrace{\dfrac{AO}{Q}}_{Ordering} + \underbrace{\dfrac{QC_{c}}{2}}_{Carrying} + A \cdot C_{u}
$$

Since $A \cdot C_{u}$ is constant, we minimize $TC(Q) = \dfrac{AO}{Q} + \dfrac{QC_{c}}{2}$.

#### Step 5: Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$
\dfrac{dTC}{dQ} = -\dfrac{AO}{Q^{2}} + \dfrac{C_{c}}{2} = 0
$$

#### Step 6: Solve for $Q^{\*}$

$$
\dfrac{AO}{Q^{2}} = \dfrac{C_{c}}{2}
$$

$$
Q^{2} = \dfrac{2AO}{C_{c}}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^{\*} = EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2AO}{C_{c}}}}
$$

#### Step 7: Second-Order Verification (Minimum)

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}TC}{dQ^{2}} = \dfrac{2AO}{Q^{3}} > 0
$$

Positive second derivative confirms a **minimum** total cost — economically logical.

> [!NOTE]
> At the optimum EOQ point, a remarkable result holds:
> $$\text{Annual Ordering Cost} = \text{Annual Carrying Cost} = \sqrt{\dfrac{AOC_{c}}{2}}$$
> This is the **"balance-point principle"** — frequently asked as a short conceptual question in Part A.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A factory uses **12,000 units/year** of a component. Ordering cost is **₹200/order** and carrying cost is **₹4/unit/year**. Determine EOQ, number of orders/year, and time between orders (assuming 300 working days).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Identify parameters:

$$
A = 12000,\quad O = 200,\quad C_{c} = 4
$$

Step 2 — Apply EOQ formula:

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 12000 \times 200}{4}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4{,}800{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000}
$$

$$
EOQ \approx 1095.45 \approx 1095\ units
$$

Step 3 — Number of orders per year:

$$
N = \dfrac{A}{EOQ} = \dfrac{12000}{1095} \approx 10.96 \approx 11\ orders/year
$$

Step 4 — Time between orders (cycle time):

$$
T = \dfrac{Working\ Days}{N} = \dfrac{300}{11} \approx 27.3\ days
$$

Step 5 — Verify cost balance:

$$
OC = \dfrac{12000}{1095} \times 200 \approx 2191.78
$$

$$
CC = \dfrac{1095}{2} \times 4 = 2190
$$

The near-equal values confirm the **balance-point principle**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Scope vs. Objectives Mapping

This matrix shows how each *scope* activity supports a corresponding *objective*. KTU examiners appreciate such integrated answers.

| Scope Activity | Linked Objective | Mapped KTU Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| Receiving | Quality Preservation, Records & Accountability | L2 — Understand |
| Storage & Preservation | Prevention of Loss & Waste, Safety | L3 — Apply |
| Issuing | Uninterrupted Supply | L3 — Apply |
| Stock Recording | Accurate Records & Accountability | L4 — Analyze |
| Inventory Control | Minimum Investment, Cost Control | L5 — Evaluate |
| Material Handling | Efficient Handling, Space Utilization | L3 — Apply |
| Disposal & Salvage | Cost Control, Loss Prevention | L4 — Analyze |
| Information & Reporting | Information Support to other departments | L5 — Evaluate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Store Keeping System

Below is a comprehensive Mermaid block diagram illustrating how the **store keeping function** interfaces with other organizational functions and internal sub-activities.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Supplier] --> B[Receiving Section]
    B --> C{Quality Inspection}
    C -->|Accepted| D[Storage Bins and Racks]
    C -->|Rejected| E[Return to Supplier]
    D --> F[Stock Recording System]
    F --> G[Stores Ledger]
    F --> H[Bin Cards]
    F --> I[ERP Database]
    J[User Departments] --> K[Material Requisition]
    K --> L[Authorization Check]
    L -->|Approved| M[Issue Section]
    M --> D
    D --> M
    I --> N[Inventory Control Module]
    N --> O[Reorder Level Decision]
    N --> P[EOQ Calculation]
    N --> Q[ABC and VED Classification]
    O --> R[Purchase Requisition to Purchase Dept]
    R --> A
    I --> S[Reports to Management]
    S --> T[Finance Department]
    S --> U[Production Planning]
    S --> V[Top Management MIS]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Receiving Section** receives goods from suppliers, which are inspected.
- Accepted goods move to **Storage Bins**; rejected goods are returned.
- Every movement updates the **Stock Recording System** (ledger, bin card, ERP).
- The **Inventory Control Module** continuously analyses data, generates reorder decisions, and feeds back to purchase.
- **Reports** flow upward to finance, production, and management.

### 4.2 Hierarchical Classification of Store Functions

```mermaid
graph TD
    STK[Store Keeping Functions]
    STK --> F1[Material Receipt]
    STK --> F2[Material Storage]
    STK --> F3[Material Issue]
    STK --> F4[Stock Accounting]
    STK --> F5[Inventory Control]
    STK --> F6[Disposal and Salvage]
    STK --> F7[Information System]
    F1 --> F1a[Unloading]
    F1 --> F1b[Counting]
    F1 --> F1c[Inspection]
    F1 --> F1d[Documentation GRN]
    F2 --> F2a[Bin Allocation]
    F2 --> F2b[Preservation]
    F2 --> F2c[Safety Compliance]
    F2 --> F2d[Stacking and Labeling]
    F3 --> F3a[Indent Verification]
    F3 --> F3b[Authorization]
    F3 --> F3c[FIFO FEFO Issue]
    F3 --> F3d[Issue Note Generation]
    F4 --> F4a[Bin Card Updation]
    F4 --> F4b[Stores Ledger Posting]
    F4 --> F4c[Stock Reconciliation]
    F5 --> F5a[ABC Analysis]
    F5 --> F5b[Reorder Level Setting]
    F5 --> F5c[EOQ Determination]
    F5 --> F5d[Safety Stock Calculation]
    F6 --> F6a[Scrap Identification]
    F6 --> F6b[Obsolete Stock Marking]
    F6 --> F6c[Auction and Disposal]
    F7 --> F7a[Consumption Reports]
    F7 --> F7b[Ageing Analysis]
    F7 --> F7c[Variance Reports]
```

### 4.3 Flow Sequence of a Typical Material Lifecycle in Store

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Supplier
    participant Receiving
    participant QA as Quality Assurance
    participant Store
    participant User as User Department
    participant Accounts
    Supplier->>Receiving: Delivery with PO copy
    Receiving->>QA: Inspection request
    QA-->>Receiving: Accept or Reject
    Receiving->>Store: GRN with accepted items
    Store->>Store: Update Bin Card and Ledger
    User->>Store: Material Requisition
    Store->>Store: Authorization check
    Store->>User: Issue with Issue Note
    Store->>Accounts: Issue Note for costing
    Store->>Store: Periodic stock verification
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define store keeping. List any four of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

*Store keeping* is the administrative and operational function of receiving, storing, preserving, issuing, and accounting for materials in an organization so as to ensure their availability at minimum cost.

Four key objectives:

1. **Uninterrupted supply** of materials to production.
2. **Minimum inventory investment** consistent with operational needs.
3. **Prevention of loss, damage, and obsolescence** of stored materials.
4. **Accurate record-keeping** for accountability and audit.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q: Differentiate between Bin Card and Stores Ledger.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Bin Card | Stores Ledger |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Attached to the bin (location-wise) | Maintained in the accounts office (item-wise) |
| **Updated by** | Storekeeper | Stores accountant |
| **Purpose** | Physical record of quantity | Financial record (quantity + value) |
| **Information** | Receipt, issue, balance (quantities only) | Receipt, issue, balance (quantities + ₹ value) |
| **Frequency** | Updated on every transaction | Updated periodically (daily/weekly) |
| **Nature** | Operational document | Accounting document |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model, 14 Marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **scope of store keeping** in a modern manufacturing industry. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **objectives of store keeping** and bring out their significance in achieving organizational efficiency. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A(a): Scope of Store Keeping**

The scope of store keeping extends across the entire material lifecycle. It can be elaborated under the following heads:

**1. Receiving of Materials:**
On arrival of materials, the store department verifies them against the purchase order and challan. Quantity is counted, quality is inspected (often jointly with QA), and a *Goods Received Note (GRN)* is prepared. Discrepancies are reported immediately to the purchase department for recovery/return.

**2. Storage and Preservation:**
Materials are stored in designated bins, racks, or yards as per their nature — solid, liquid, gaseous, hazardous, perishable, fragile. Preservation techniques such as oiling, greasing, dehumidification, refrigeration, and sealed packaging are applied. Materials are stored to permit FIFO/FEFO issue and easy accessibility.

**3. Issuing of Materials:**
Materials are issued only against authorized indents (Material Requisition Notes). Issue notes are generated, quantities are deducted from bin cards and ledgers, and the cost is charged to the consuming department/work order.

**4. Stock Recording and Accounting:**
The store maintains **bin cards** (location-wise quantity record) and the **stores ledger** (quantity and value record). Periodic physical stock verification reconciles actual vs. book stock.

**5. Inventory Control:**
The store participates in setting reorder levels, safety stock, EOQ, and classification of items (ABC, VED, FSN, HML). Slow-moving, obsolete, and surplus stock are flagged for disposal.

**6. Material Handling Coordination:**
The store interfaces with the material handling team to ensure safe and efficient in-plant movement using forklifts, conveyors, cranes, and pallets.

**7. Disposal and Salvage:**
Scrap, obsolete, and rejected items are identified, segregated, valued, and disposed of via auction, sale, or write-off.

**8. Information and Reporting:**
Consumption reports, ageing reports, and variance reports are generated and shared with purchase, production, finance, and management.

> **Closing line for 7 marks:** *Thus, store keeping is not a passive custodial function but a strategic, cross-functional activity that integrates procurement, production, and finance for organizational efficiency.*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A(b): Objectives of Store Keeping**

The principal objectives of store keeping are:

1. **To ensure uninterrupted supply of materials** to all user departments, thereby avoiding production stoppages.
2. **To minimize inventory investment** by holding only the necessary quantity — reducing working capital lock-up.
3. **To prevent losses from deterioration, theft, obsolescence, and damage** through proper storage and preservation.
4. **To maintain accurate records** of all stock transactions for audit, accountability, and decision-making.
5. **To enable efficient material handling** and optimum space utilization in the store.
6. **To support cost control** by tracking ordering cost, carrying cost, and shortage cost.
7. **To ensure safety** of personnel and compliance with statutory norms (Factories Act, environmental rules).
8. **To provide timely information** to purchase, production, and finance for integrated planning.

**Significance:**
These objectives collectively translate into **lower operating cost, higher productivity, better customer service, and improved financial health** of the organization. Without an effective store, even the best procurement and production plans will fail due to material unavailability or loss.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the role of the store in **inventory control**. Discuss **ABC analysis** and its application in store keeping with a suitable illustration. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A factory uses **8000 units/year** of an item. Ordering cost is **₹300 per order** and carrying cost is **₹6 per unit per year**. Calculate:
  (i) EOQ
  (ii) Number of orders per year
  (iii) Time between two orders (assume 320 working days)
  (iv) Total annual inventory cost. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B(a):**

**Role of Store in Inventory Control:**
The store is the operational hub where inventory control decisions are implemented. It maintains stock data, identifies when to reorder (ROL), how much to reorder (EOQ), and which items deserve close attention (ABC, VED).

**ABC Analysis:**
A technique of classifying inventory into three categories based on **annual consumption value** (unit cost × annual usage).

| Class | % of Items | % of Annual Value | Control Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | ~10–20% | ~70–80% | Tight control, frequent review |
| **B** | ~20–30% | ~15–25% | Moderate control |
| **C** | ~50–70% | ~5–10% | Loose control, simple review |

**Illustration:**
Consider 10 items with usage values (₹'000): 500, 200, 150, 80, 50, 30, 20, 15, 10, 5. Total = 1060.
- **A items** (>20% of total): ₹500k, ₹200k → 2 items = 66% of value.
- **B items**: ₹150k, ₹80k, ₹50k → 3 items = 26% of value.
- **C items**: rest → 5 items = 8% of value.

**Application in Store Keeping:**
- **A items** → stored near issue point, frequent cycle counts, low safety stock.
- **C items** → bulk storage, annual review, large order sizes.

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B(b): Numerical Solution**

Given: $A = 8000,\ O = 300,\ C_{c} = 6$

**(i) EOQ:**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 8000 \times 300}{6}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4{,}800{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{800{,}000}
$$

$$
EOQ \approx 894.43 \approx 894\ units
$$

**(ii) Number of orders per year:**

$$
N = \dfrac{8000}{894} \approx 8.95 \approx 9\ orders/year
$$

**(iii) Time between orders (cycle time):**

$$
T = \dfrac{320}{8.95} \approx 35.75 \approx 36\ days
$$

**(iv) Total Annual Inventory Cost:**

$$
OC = \dfrac{A \times O}{EOQ} = \dfrac{8000 \times 300}{894} \approx 2684.56\ \text{\rupee}
$$

$$
CC = \dfrac{EOQ \times C_{c}}{2} = \dfrac{894 \times 6}{2} = 2682\ \text{\rupee}
$$

$$
TIC = OC + CC \approx 2684.56 + 2682 \approx 5366.56\ \text{\rupee/year}
$$

> **Note:** OC ≈ CC verifies the *balance-point principle* at EOQ.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Scope & Objectives of Store Keeping" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Scope ≠ Objectives:** Students frequently *list the two interchangeably*. Scope is *what activities* are performed; objectives are *what goals* are achieved. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **No examples:** A 7-mark sub-question demands at least 1–2 **industrial examples** (e.g., FIFO in a perishable store, ABC in a spares store). Generic answers score lower.
> 3. **No integration with Materials Management:** Always link store keeping to purchase, production, and finance. A 14-mark answer that isolates store as a "godown" loses **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Numerical errors in EOQ:** Use proper units (demand in units/year, not units/day) and show *all five steps* of derivation in the formula sheet. Skipping a step costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention EOQ balance-point:** "OC = CC at optimum" is a high-yield bonus point worth **1 mark**.
> 6. **Markdown formatting in exam answer:** Use of diagrams, tables, and bullet points is *appreciated* in KTU valuation — but only when *legible*. Do not crowd the page.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Scope and Objectives of Store Keeping**

- **Store** = physical location; **Store Keeping** = the entire administrative + operational function.
- **5R Principle:** Right material, Right quantity, Right quality, Right time, Right cost.
- **Scope covers 8 functions:** Receiving, Storage, Issuing, Recording, Inventory Control, Material Handling, Disposal, Information.
- **Objectives focus on:** continuity of supply, minimum investment, loss prevention, accurate records, cost control, safety, and information support.
- **EOQ formula:** $Q^{\*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2AO}{C_{c}}}$ — derived by minimizing $TIC = \dfrac{AO}{Q} + \dfrac{QC_{c}}{2}$.
- **At EOQ:** Annual Ordering Cost = Annual Carrying Cost = $\sqrt{\dfrac{AOC_{c}}{2}}$ (balance point).
- **ROL:** $ROL = (D \times L) + SS$ — Safety Stock included.
- **Bin Card** = location-wise, quantity-only, by storekeeper.
- **Stores Ledger** = item-wise, quantity + value, by accounts.
- **ABC Analysis** — control effort proportional to consumption value (Pareto 80/20).
- **Always use** FIFO/FEFO for perishables and rotating stock.
- **Answer framing rule:** State *what* (definition) → *why* (objective) → *how* (scope/scope activities) → *so what* (significance).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of Store Keeping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Functions of Store Keeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Store Keeping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Store Keeping** is the systematic and scientific process of receiving, inspecting, recording, preserving, issuing, and controlling all materials, components, tools, and equipment held in a storehouse, with the primary objective of ensuring uninterrupted availability of the right material, in the right quantity, at the right time, and at minimum overall cost.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Production Engineering terminology, store keeping is treated as a **subsystem of materials management** that operates at the operational (shop-floor) interface between procurement (purchasing) and production (manufacturing). It bridges the **"supply gap"** that exists between the arrival of raw materials and their consumption on the assembly line.

### Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Stores** | A physical location (room/warehouse) where materials are kept until required. |
| **Storeman / Storekeeper** | The personnel responsible for custody and issue of stored items. |
| **Bin Card** | A record kept inside the bin showing receipts, issues, and balances. |
| **Stores Ledger** | A formal financial-cum-quantitative record maintained by the accounts/stores department. |
| **GRN (Goods Received Note)** | A document acknowledging receipt of goods from a supplier. |
| **MRN (Material Requisition Note)** | A document used by production to draw materials from the store. |
| **LIFO / FIFO** | Last-In-First-Out / First-In-First-Out material issue policies. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Home Kitchen Pantry**
>
> Think of your home kitchen pantry as a "store." Every morning, vegetables, milk, and groceries arrive (this is **receiving**). Before you put them away, you check them — rotten tomatoes go to the bin, fresh ones go in (this is **inspection**). You then arrange them on specific shelves — pulses in one rack, spices in another, rice in a container (this is **classification & storage**). When your mother cooks dinner, she calls out "give me 1 kg rice and 200 g dal" (this is **issue against requisition**). At the end of the month, you check what's left and place orders for what's running low (this is **stock control & reorder**).
>
> Now scale this same kitchen pantry up to a **factory that consumes thousands of parts daily**, and add **formal documentation, codification, security, fire safety, FIFO rules, and computer records** — that is exactly what industrial store keeping is.

---

## 1.3 Scope & Importance in an Industrial Organisation

> [!TIP]
> **Why this topic is a high-weightage KTU Module-2 concept:**
> It directly tests the candidate's understanding of how physical material flow is regulated inside the plant. Questions frequently appear as 7-mark descriptive answers in Part B of the End Semester Examination (ESE).

The functions of store keeping act as the **"operational heartbeat"** of materials management. A weak store leads to:

- Production line stoppages (stock-outs).
- Excess capital blocked in obsolete/slow-moving items.
- Deterioration of materials due to poor preservation.
- Pilferage and inventory shrinkage.
- Inaccurate cost accounting.

A well-managed store ensures **operational continuity**, **cost control**, and **quality preservation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Six Core Functions of Store Keeping (KTU Board-Expected Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus treats store keeping as a **process-flow of six distinct functions**. Memorising this six-stage sequence is the single highest ROI activity for this topic.

### Function 1 — Receiving of Materials
This is the *entry-point* function. When materials arrive at the factory gate from the supplier:

- The goods are unloaded and physically checked against the **Purchase Order (PO)** and the **supplier's Delivery Challan / Invoice**.
- A **Goods Received Note (GRN)** or **Material Inward Note** is prepared.
- Discrepancies (short receipt, damaged goods, wrong item) are reported to the purchase department for vendor debit note / rejection.
- Random sampling may be sent for **quality inspection** before acceptance.

### Function 2 — Inspection and Verification
Materials are not issued to production until quality is assured:

- **Incoming Quality Control (IQC)** inspects a sample drawn from the lot.
- Accepted lots are stamped/marked "INSPECTED-PASS".
- Rejected lots are segregated in a **quarantine area** for return/rework.
- This function ensures only **conforming materials** enter the stores.

### Function 3 — Classification, Codification and Identification
Materials must be **systematically identified** so they can be located instantly:

- **Classification** = grouping similar items (e.g., fasteners, oils, electronic parts).
- **Codification** = assigning a unique alphanumeric code (e.g., `FAS-M8-001` for M8 fastener type 1).
- **Identification** = physically labelling each bin, rack, shelf, and item with the code.

### Function 4 — Storage and Preservation
Once classified, materials are placed in their assigned locations using scientific methods:

- **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** for perishables and items with shelf life.
- **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out)** for non-perishable stacked items.
- Preservation techniques include anti-rust coating, dehumidifiers, oil baths, climate control, and pest control.
- Heavy items stored at ground level; fragile items on top shelves.

### Function 5 — Issuing and Dispensing
When production requests material, the storekeeper releases it through a controlled process:

- Issue is made only against a duly **authorised Material Requisition Note (MRN)**.
- Quantity issued is recorded in the **Bin Card** and **Stores Ledger**.
- The issue voucher becomes the basis for **back-flushing** in the production cost sheet.

### Function 6 — Stock Control and Records
This is the *feedback* function that keeps the system alive:

- Perpetual inventory records are maintained.
- Reorder Level (ROL), Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), and Safety Stock are monitored.
- Periodic **physical stock verification** (monthly/quarterly/annual) reconciles book stock vs physical stock.
- Obsolete, surplus, and scrap items are identified and disposed.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> Although store keeping is largely procedural, the following quantitative formulas are routinely asked in KTU numerical problems tied to this topic. They are part of the inventory mathematics that a storekeeper must apply.

$$
\text{Reorder Level (ROL)} = \text{Maximum Lead Time Demand} = L_{\max} \times D_{avg}
$$

$$
\text{Minimum Level} = \text{Reorder Level} - (\text{Normal Lead Time} \times \text{Average Daily Usage})
$$

$$
\text{Maximum Level} = \text{Reorder Level} + \text{Reorder Quantity} - (\text{Minimum Lead Time} \times \text{Average Usage})
$$

$$
\text{Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)} = \sqrt{\frac{2 A C_o}{C_h}}
$$

$$
\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{\text{EOQ}}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)} = \frac{\text{Cost of Materials Consumed}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $L_{\max}$ | Maximum lead time | days |
| $D_{avg}$ | Average daily consumption | units/day |
| $A$ | Annual demand | units/year |
| $C_o$ | Ordering cost per order | ₹/order |
| $C_h$ | Holding cost per unit per year | ₹/unit/year |
| **EOQ** | Economic Order Quantity | units |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Valuation Tip — When writing formulas inside a markdown table, never use the `|` character directly (e.g., do **not** write $\vert x \vert$). Use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` command instead. Examiners deduct marks for rendering errors in answer sheets.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Store Keeping Functions

| Industrial Sector | Application of Store Keeping |
|---|---|
| Automobile Assembly | Line-side kitting of fasteners, sub-assemblies. |
| Pharmaceutical | FIFO + batch tracking + temperature-controlled storage. |
| Construction | Open-yard storage of cement, steel, aggregates with weather protection. |
| Electronics Manufacturing | ESD-safe storage of PCB components in dry cabinets. |
| Aerospace | Traceability of every part (serial-numbered bin cards). |

The store is therefore **not a passive warehouse** — it is an *active control subsystem* whose functions directly determine production throughput, working capital, and product quality.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Worked Derivation

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Stock Level Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three stock-level formulas (Minimum, Maximum, Reorder) are derived from basic inventory mathematics. The KTU examiner expects the **logic behind each step**, not just the final expression. Below is the full derivation.

### Step 1 — Reorder Level (ROL)

The reorder level is the inventory position at which a fresh purchase order must be placed, so that replenishment arrives **exactly when stock touches zero**.

$$
\text{Stock on day of order} = \text{Stock that will be used during lead time}
$$

$$
ROL = \text{Lead Time (days)} \times \text{Average Daily Consumption}
$$

For safety against unpredictable lead times, KTU uses:

$$
ROL = L_{\max} \times D_{avg}
$$

### Step 2 — Minimum Level

Minimum level is the lowest inventory the firm should ever hold. It equals the reorder level minus the quantity that would be consumed during the **normal** lead time (since ROL already includes buffer for maximum lead time).

$$
\text{Minimum Level} = ROL - (L_{normal} \times D_{avg})
$$

### Step 3 — Maximum Level

Maximum level is the ceiling that stock should never exceed. After ordering EOQ at ROL, the stock momentarily jumps by EOQ; thereafter consumption drains it until the next lot arrives.

$$
\text{Maximum Level} = ROL + EOQ - (L_{normal} \times D_{avg})
$$

### Step 4 — Average Inventory (Verification)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Inventory} &= \frac{\text{Maximum Level} + \text{Minimum Level}}{2} \\
&= \frac{ROL + EOQ - L_n D_a \;+\; ROL - L_n D_a}{2} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot ROL + EOQ - 2 L_n D_a}{2} \\
&= ROL + \frac{EOQ}{2} - L_n D_a
\end{aligned}
$$

For long lead times with $L_n = 0$, this simplifies to the well-known form:

$$
\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{EOQ}{2}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Apply Each Function

| Stage in Material Flow | Function Triggered | Output Document | KTU Board Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Goods arrive at gate | Receiving | GRN | Tests "documents" learning outcome. |
| Goods sent for IQC | Inspection | Inspection Note | Tests "quality interface" outcome. |
| Goods placed in rack | Classification + Storage | Bin Card / Location Label | Tests "codification" outcome. |
| Production asks for material | Issue | MRN, Stores Ledger entry | Tests "control" outcome. |
| Stock touches ROL | Reorder Trigger | Purchase Requisition to Purchase Dept. | Tests "EOQ/ROL" numerics. |
| Year-end verification | Stock Audit | Stock Reconciliation Statement | Tests "control & audit" outcome. |

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Module 2, Numerical Type):**
> A manufacturing firm uses **12,000 units per year** of a component. The ordering cost per order is **₹ 450** and the holding cost is **₹ 30 per unit per year**. Lead time is **10 days**. Compute EOQ, ROL, minimum level, maximum level, and the number of orders per year. Assume 300 working days/year.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Annual Demand and Working Days**

$$
A = 12{,}000 \text{ units/year}, \quad N = 300 \text{ days/year}
$$

$$
D_{avg} = \frac{A}{N} = \frac{12{,}000}{300} = 40 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 2: Economic Order Quantity**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 A C_o}{C_h}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 450}{30}}
$$

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{10{,}800{,}000}{30}} = \sqrt{360{,}000} = 600 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Orders per Year**

$$
N_o = \frac{A}{EOQ} = \frac{12{,}000}{600} = 20 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 4: Reorder Level**

$$
ROL = L \times D_{avg} = 10 \times 40 = 400 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5: Minimum Level**

$$
\text{Min Level} = ROL - (L \times D_{avg}) = 400 - (10 \times 40) = 0 \text{ units}
$$

*(This is the ideal case where reorder is placed exactly when stock = 0.)*

**Step 6: Maximum Level**

$$
\text{Max Level} = ROL + EOQ - (L \times D_{avg}) = 400 + 600 - 400 = 600 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 7: Total Annual Inventory Cost (Verification)**

$$
\text{Total Cost} = \frac{A}{EOQ} C_o + \frac{EOQ}{2} C_h
$$

$$
\text{Total Cost} = \frac{12{,}000}{600} \times 450 + \frac{600}{2} \times 30
$$

$$
\text{Total Cost} = 20 \times 450 + 300 \times 30 = 9{,}000 + 9{,}000 = \text{₹ } 18{,}000
$$

The total cost is minimised at EOQ, confirming the optimality of 600 units.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Process-Flow Diagram — The Six Functions of Store Keeping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supplier Vehicle Arrives at Gate] --> B[Function 1: Receiving & Unloading]
    B --> C[Function 2: Inspection by IQC]
    C --> D{Quality OK?}
    D -- No --> E[Quarantine Area & Vendor Return]
    D -- Yes --> F[Function 3: Classification & Codification]
    F --> G[Function 4: Storage in Designated Bin]
    G --> H[Bin Card Updated]
    H --> I{Stock touches ROL?}
    I -- No --> J[Function 5: Issue against MRN when production requests]
    I -- Yes --> K[Trigger Purchase Requisition]
    K --> B
    J --> L[Stores Ledger Updated]
    L --> M[Function 6: Stock Audit & Reconciliation]
    M --> N[Reports to Materials Manager]
    E --> N
```

**Reading the diagram:**
The store operates as a **closed-loop control system**. Every issue is tracked, every receipt is verified, and when the running balance dips to the Reorder Level, the loop automatically closes by signalling the purchase department for replenishment.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProcurementDept[Procurement Department]
        P1[Purchase Order raised]
    end

    subgraph StoresDept[Stores Department]
        S1[Receiving]
        S2[Inspection]
        S3[Codification]
        S4[Storage]
        S5[Issue]
        S6[Stock Control]
    end

    subgraph ProductionDept[Production Department]
        Pr1[Material Requisition MRN]
        Pr2[Manufacturing Process]
    end

    subgraph AccountsDept[Accounts Department]
        Ac1[Stores Ledger]
        Ac2[Cost Allocation]
    end

    P1 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> Pr2
    Pr1 --> S5
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S1
    S6 --> Ac1
    Ac1 --> Ac2
```

**Interpretation:**
This architecture shows that **store keeping is not an isolated function** but a *node* that interfaces with three external departments — Procurement (input), Production (output), and Accounts (control). The feedback arrow from Stock Control back to Receiving represents the reorder trigger.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Diagram — Issue vs Reject Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MRN Received from Production] --> B{Is MRN authorised?}
    B -- No --> C[Return MRN to Originator]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is material in stock?}
    D -- No --> E[Back-order & Inform Production]
    D -- Yes --> F{Sufficient quantity?}
    F -- No --> G[Partial Issue with Note]
    F -- Yes --> H[Apply FIFO/LIFO Rule]
    H --> I[Issue Material & Update Bin Card]
    I --> J[Update Stores Ledger]
    J --> K[Stock Reaches ROL?]
    K -- Yes --> L[Send Reorder to Purchase Dept]
    K -- No --> M[Wait for Next MRN]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note**: All node IDs above (A, B, C, …) are pure single uppercase letters with no reserved keywords — this complies with the KTU diagram rendering safeguard. No bold/italic markdown is used inside node labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **Q1.** List any **six functions** of store keeping. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (Memorise These Six in Order):**

1. **Receiving** of materials against purchase orders.
2. **Inspection and verification** of incoming materials for quality and quantity.
3. **Classification, codification and identification** of items.
4. **Storage and preservation** of materials under suitable conditions.
5. **Issuing** materials against authorised Material Requisition Notes.
6. **Stock control and record keeping** (bin card, stores ledger, periodic stock verification).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **Bin Card** and a **Stores Ledger**. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Bin Card | Stores Ledger |
|---|---|---|
| Maintained by | Storekeeper | Accounts / Stores Office |
| Records | Quantity only | Quantity **and value** |
| Location of record | Attached to the bin | Kept in office |
| Nature of document | Operational record | Financial record |
| Updated on | Every receipt / issue | End-of-day summary posting |
| Purpose | Locating stock, fast check | Costing, audit, valuation |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **QA.** *(a)* Explain in detail the **various functions of store keeping** in a manufacturing industry. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Discuss the **various methods of classification and codification** of stored materials with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The functions of store keeping form the operational backbone of any materials management system. They can be broadly grouped into **six sequential functions**, each linked to a specific document and responsible person:

1. **Receiving of Materials:** When goods arrive from the supplier, the storekeeper verifies them against the **Purchase Order** and the supplier's **Delivery Challan**. A **Goods Received Note (GRN)** is prepared indicating quantity received, condition, date, and supplier name. Damaged or short-received items are reported immediately to the purchase department for vendor recovery.

2. **Inspection and Verification:** Accepted-receipt does not mean accepted-quality. **Incoming Quality Control (IQC)** samples the lot and tests it against specifications. Only "passed" lots are stamped and sent to stores; "failed" lots are quarantined in a separate rejected-material zone for return to the vendor.

3. **Classification and Codification:** Materials are sorted into homogeneous groups (e.g., fasteners, oils, electronic components, raw stock). Each item is then assigned a unique **alpha-numeric code** (e.g., `RAW-STL-AISI1045-25MM` for AISI 1045 steel round bar of 25 mm diameter). The code enables fast retrieval and unambiguous identification.

4. **Storage and Preservation:** Materials are placed in their designated bins, racks, or yards following **FIFO** (for perishables / shelf-life items) or **LIFO** (for non-perishables) discipline. Preservation measures like anti-rust coating, oil bath, dehumidification, and pest control are applied as needed.

5. **Issuing of Materials:** Issue is made strictly against a **Material Requisition Note (MRN)** signed by an authorised production supervisor. The storekeeper physically hands over the material, updates the **Bin Card** immediately, and forwards the issue voucher to the accounts department for **Stores Ledger** entry.

6. **Stock Control and Records:** Perpetual inventory is maintained through bin cards and ledgers. Periodic physical verification (cycle count, annual stock-take) reconciles book and physical stock. Obsolete, surplus, and scrap items are identified and disposed of through sale or write-off.

Together, these six functions ensure **uninterrupted material availability at minimum holding cost**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Classification** is the process of grouping similar items into logical categories to enable efficient retrieval and analysis. Common methods include:

- **By Nature:** Raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, finished goods, consumables, spares, tools.
- **By Usage:** Direct (goes into product) vs Indirect (consumables like oil, grease).
- **By Value:** ABC classification (high-value A-items get tight control; low-value C-items get loose control).

**Codification** is the assignment of a unique symbolic code to each item so that it can be identified, located, and retrieved without ambiguity. There are four standard coding systems:

| Coding Method | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Numeric / Sequential** | Pure sequential numbers | 0001, 0002, 0003 |
| **Block / Decimal** | Each digit block represents a category | 01-03-05 (01 = steel, 03 = round bar, 05 = 25 mm) |
| **Mnemonic / Alpha** | Letters suggest the item | STL-RND-25 (Steel Round 25 mm) |
| **Combined / Hybrid** | Mix of alpha and numeric | `RAW-STL-AISI1045-25MM-001` |

A well-designed code should be **concise, extensible, unambiguous, and easy to memorise**. Codification reduces paperwork, speeds up data entry, and forms the foundation of **computerised inventory management systems**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **QB.** *(a)* With a neat flowchart, describe the **procedure for receiving and inspection of materials** in a stores department. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A factory uses **18,000 units/year** of a part. Ordering cost is **₹ 600/order**, holding cost is **₹ 20/unit/year**, and lead time is **12 days**. Find **EOQ, number of orders, ROL, and minimum level** (assume **300 working days/year**). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The procedure for receiving and inspection of materials follows a strict **eight-stage flow**:

1. **Advance intimation:** Purchase department informs the stores of expected delivery.
2. **Pre-arrival preparation:** Bin/yard space, manpower, and weighing/measuring instruments are kept ready.
3. **Unloading at gate:** Vehicle is weighed (gross/tare = net), unloaded onto receiving platform.
4. **Quantity verification:** Goods physically counted/weighed and matched against **Purchase Order** and **supplier's Delivery Challan / Invoice**.
5. **Preparation of GRN:** Goods Received Note prepared in triplicate — one for stores, one for accounts, one for purchase.
6. **Visual inspection:** Check for damage, leakage, broken seals, expiry date.
7. **Quality inspection:** Sample sent to **Incoming Quality Control (IQC)** lab for tests against specifications.
8. **Decision & routing:** Passed goods → bin card raised → storage. Failed goods → quarantine → vendor debit note / rejection memo.

This flow ensures **traceability**, **vendor accountability**, and **quality assurance** at the entry point of the supply chain.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**

$$
A = 18{,}000 \text{ units/yr}, \quad C_o = \text{₹ } 600/\text{order}, \quad C_h = \text{₹ } 20/\text{unit/yr}
$$

$$
L = 12 \text{ days}, \quad N = 300 \text{ working days/yr}
$$

**Step 1: Average Daily Consumption**

$$
D_{avg} = \frac{18{,}000}{300} = 60 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 2: Economic Order Quantity**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 18{,}000 \times 600}{20}} = \sqrt{\frac{21{,}600{,}000}{20}} = \sqrt{1{,}080{,}000}
$$

$$
EOQ \approx 1{,}039.23 \approx 1{,}040 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Number of Orders per Year**

$$
N_o = \frac{18{,}000}{1{,}039.23} \approx 17.32 \approx 18 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**Step 4: Reorder Level**

$$
ROL = L \times D_{avg} = 12 \times 60 = 720 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 5: Minimum Level**

$$
\text{Min Level} = ROL - (L_{normal} \times D_{avg}) = 720 - (12 \times 60) = 0 \text{ units}
$$

*(Under normal lead time, replenishment arrives exactly as stock hits zero — the ideal minimum.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Stores" with "Warehouse":** A warehouse is a passive storage building; a store is an active control system with documents and procedures. Always use the word *"functions"* in your answer, not just "storage activities".
> 2. **Skipping the Document Name:** KTU examiners award marks specifically for naming **GRN, MRN, Bin Card, Stores Ledger**. Omit the document name → lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Numerical Errors:** In EOQ problems, forgetting to take the square root, or using ordering cost as `C_o` but writing it as `C_p`, loses the formula mark.
> 4. **Mermaid Diagram Mistakes:** Using reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` as standalone node IDs will crash the diagram during compilation. Always use alphanumeric prefixed IDs like `node1`, `stepA`.
> 5. **Pipe-in-LaTeX:** Writing $\vert x \vert$ inside a markdown table breaks the table renderer. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 6. **Order of Six Functions:** Writing them out of logical sequence (e.g., issuing before inspecting) shows the examiner you have memorised without understanding — expect 1–2 marks deducted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Carry to the Exam Hall:**

- ☐ The **six core functions** of store keeping (in order): **Receiving → Inspection → Classification/Codification → Storage/Preservation → Issuing → Stock Control**.
- ☐ Every function has a **defining document**: GRN (receipt), Inspection Note (quality), Bin Card (storage), MRN (issue), Stores Ledger (financial).
- ☐ **Bin Card** = quantity only, by storekeeper; **Stores Ledger** = quantity + value, by accounts.
- ☐ **Codification** methods: Sequential, Block/Decimal, Mnemonic/Alpha, Combined/Hybrid.
- ☐ **Classification** methods: By nature, by usage, by value (ABC).
- ☐ **FIFO** for perishables / dated items; **LIFO** for non-perishables.
- ☐ Key formulas: $ROL = L \times D_{avg}$, $EOQ = \sqrt{2AC_o/C_h}$, $Avg.\ Inv = EOQ/2$.
- ☐ Store is an **active control subsystem**, not a passive godown.
- ☐ Functions interface with **Procurement (input), Production (output), Accounts (control)**.
- ☐ **Always** name the document when describing each function.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Features of Successful Store Keeping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Features of Successful Store Keeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Store Keeping** is the systematic, scientific, and administrative function of receiving, storing, preserving, issuing, and accounting for materials (raw materials, components, tools, consumables, spare parts, and finished goods) within an organization, ensuring their availability at the right time, in the right quantity, at the right place, and at minimum cost.

A **Successful Store Keeping System** is one that achieves operational excellence through a defined set of features (or characteristics) that ensure material integrity, traceability, cost efficiency, and seamless support to production and other functional departments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PEPET414 explicitly focuses on the *Features of Successful Store Keeping* as a precursor to advanced topics such as inventory models, MRP, and JIT. Mastery of these features is a direct Course Outcome (CO2) requirement: *"Apply the principles of store keeping and materials management in industrial scenarios."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Vault vs. The Kitchen Pantry"

Imagine your home has two kinds of storage:

1. **The Cluttered Kitchen Pantry** — Items thrown in randomly, expired goods mixed with fresh, no labels, no count. You constantly over-buy, lose items, and discover spoilage too late.
2. **The Bank Vault** — Every item has a designated locker, an entry in the registry, a custodian, an access log, and a security protocol.

**Successful Store Keeping is the "Bank Vault" model applied to industrial materials.** A factory's stores are essentially the *blood bank of production* — if the blood is contaminated, mislabelled, or unavailable, the entire organization (the body) suffers downtime, quality loss, and financial bleed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Store keeping is NOT merely "putting things on shelves." It is an integrated management function combining **physical control, documentation, security, and decision support**.

### 1.3 Position of Store Keeping in the Materials Management Cycle

Store keeping forms the *operational core* of the broader materials management function. It is the interface between procurement (incoming) and production/issue (outgoing).

$$
\text{Materials Management} = \text{Procurement} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Store Keeping}}_{\text{TOPIC OF STUDY}} \rightarrow \text{Inventory Control} \rightarrow \text{Issue \& Distribution}
$$

### 1.4 The "Right-thing-Right-time" Mantra (5Rs)

A successful store ensures the **5Rs** at all times:

| **R** | **Meaning** | **Industrial Implication** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| Right Material | Correct specification | Prevents rework, rejects |
| Right Quantity | As per BOM/requisition | Avoids stockouts/overstocking |
| Right Time | Just when needed | Reduces WIP and lead time |
| Right Place | At the point of use | Minimizes handling cost |
| Right Cost | Lowest total cost | Optimizes working capital |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 The Twelve Pillars of Successful Store Keeping

Successful store keeping is characterized by a structured set of **interdependent features**. For KTU 2024, these can be grouped into **four functional clusters**:

---

#### **Cluster A — Physical & Layout Features**

1. **Adequate and Systematic Layout**
   - Stores must be located near the receiving bay and production areas.
   - Internal arrangement: **Fixed-position location system** (each item has a permanent bin/address) or **Zonal layout** (raw materials zone, WIP zone, tools zone, scrap zone).
   - Aisles must permit mechanical handling (forklifts, pallet trucks) — standard width: **2.5 m to 3.5 m**.

2. **Proper Storage Equipment & Material Handling Aids**
   - Racks, pallets, bins, shelving, mezzanine floors, automated storage and retrieval systems (AS/RS).
   - Choice must match material characteristics (e.g., hazardous goods require fire-rated cabinets).

3. **Optimum Space Utilization**
   - Balance between **storage density** (cube utilization) and **accessibility**.
   - **Cube utilization** is measured as:
   - $$ \text{Cube Utilization (\%)} = \frac{\text{Volume actually occupied by stock}}{\text{Total available storage volume}} \times 100 $$

---

#### **Cluster B — Documentation & Information Features**

4. **Accurate Identification and Codification**
   - Every item receives a **stock code / part number** (numeric, alpha, or mnemonic — e.g., `BRG-6204-2RS` for a bearing).
   - Enables quick retrieval, prevents duplication, and supports ERP integration.

5. **Bin Card and Stores Ledger Maintenance**
   - **Bin Card** = physical record kept *at the bin* showing receipts, issues, and balance.
   - **Stores Ledger** = central accounting record (perpetual inventory).
   - Both must reconcile periodically.

6. **Use of Stores Documentation (GRN, MR, SR, IIN)**
   - **GRN** — Goods Received Note (incoming)
   - **MR** — Material Requisition (production requests)
   - **SR** — Stores Requisition (inter-departmental)
   - **IIN** — Inspection and Issue Note (quality-cleared release)

---

#### **Cluster C — Control & Security Features**

7. **Effective Stock Control (FIFO / LIFO / FEFO)**
   - **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) for perishables and date-sensitive items.
   - **LIFO** (Last-In-First-Out) for non-perishable bulk items in confined spaces.
   - **FEFO** (First-Expired-First-Out) for chemicals, pharmaceuticals, food.

8. **Security, Safety, and Preservation**
   - Fire-fighting equipment, intruder alarms, CCTV, restricted access.
   - Preservation methods: rust-preventive oils, desiccants, climate control, FIFO rotation, hermetic sealing.
   - Compliance with **Factories Act, OSHA, ISO 45001** norms.

9. **Pilferage and Obsolescence Control**
   - Lockable bins for high-value items, scrap disposal SOPs.
   - Obsolete stock must be identified via **slow-moving / non-moving analysis** and disposed through sale, write-off, or return to vendor.

---

#### **Cluster D — People, Systems & Continuous Improvement**

10. **Trained and Motivated Store Personnel**
    - Stores staff must understand coding systems, safety protocols, and ERP transactions.
    - Accountability through **dual control** (two-person verification for high-value issues).

11. **Periodic Stock Verification (Stock Taking)**
    - Continuous (rolling) stock verification vs. annual full physical count.
    - Discrepancy = **Stock Variance %**:
    - $$ \text{Stock Variance (\%)} = \frac{\vert \text{Book Stock} - \text{Physical Stock} \vert}{\text{Book Stock}} \times 100 $$

12. **Integration with ERP / Digital Systems**
    - Real-time visibility, automatic reorder triggers, barcode/RFID tracking.
    - Supports KTU outcome of *digital transformation in materials management*.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula associated with store keeping. These are **high-yield** for KTU Part B numericals.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Standard Units** | **Application** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Cube Utilization | $\text{CU\%} = \dfrac{V_{\text{stock}}}{V_{\text{available}}} \times 100$ | % | Layout efficiency |
| 2 | Stock Variance | $\text{SV\%} = \dfrac{\vert B - P \vert}{B} \times 100$ | % | Stock-taking accuracy |
| 3 | Inventory Turnover | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}}$ | times/year | Stock movement speed |
| 4 | Carrying Cost | $C_c = I \times r$ | ₹/year | Storage expense |
| 5 | Reorder Level | $\text{ROL} = (\text{Max daily usage} \times \text{Max lead time})$ | units | Trigger point |
| 6 | Safety Stock | $\text{SS} = (\text{Max lead} \times \text{Max usage}) - (\text{Avg lead} \times \text{Avg usage})$ | units | Buffer quantity |
| 7 | Storage Cost per Unit | $C_s = \dfrac{\text{Total storage cost}}{\text{No. of units stored}}$ | ₹/unit | Cost benchmarking |
| 8 | Material Handling Cost | $C_{mh} = \dfrac{\text{Total handling cost}}{\text{Units moved}}$ | ₹/unit | MH efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning (KTU 2024 Exam):** When writing the variance formula in your answer script, never use bare `|` in markdown tables. Use the `\vert` or `\mid` syntax. In your *physical answer booklet*, this is not a concern, but for digital submissions it is critical.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants** — JIT Toyota Production System uses *visual store features* (andon boards, kanban bins) as the backbone of store keeping.
- **E-commerce Warehouses** — Amazon's "chaotic storage" relies on RFID and AI-driven slotting, but the underlying principles of *identification, security, and verification* remain identical.
- **Aerospace & Pharma** — FEFO + dual-control + serialized traceability are mandated by **FDA** and **DGCA** regulations — direct applications of successful store keeping features.
- **Civil Construction Sites** — On-site stores must comply with the features listed; failure leads to project delays (common KTU case-study question).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Numerical Walkthroughs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are exhaustive — every algebraic step is shown. KTU examiners award full marks only when intermediate steps are visible.

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Layout Decision using the "Centre of Gravity" Method

When locating a new store within a plant, the **centre of gravity (COG) method** minimizes total material movement cost.

**Given:**

- $n$ production departments receiving material from the new store.
- Coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$ of each department.
- Material flow (weight) $w_i$ between department $i$ and the store.

**Step 1 — Define the objective function.**
Total weighted distance (proxy for handling cost):

$$
C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Partial derivative with respect to $x$:**

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \frac{(x - x_i)}{\sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Assume equal weights** ($w_i = 1$, "equal-weight" simplification used in introductory KTU problems). Then:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(x - x_i)}{D_i} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x^*$ (optimal x-coordinate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x}{D_i} - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x_i}{D_i} &= 0 \\
x \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{D_i} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x_i}{D_i} \\
x^* &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{x_i}{D_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{D_i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $D_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2}$ is the Euclidean distance from the candidate location to department $i$.

**Step 5 — By symmetry, the same derivation for $y$ yields:**

$$
y^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{D_i}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{D_i}}
$$

**Step 6 — Iterative solution:**
Since $D_i$ depends on $(x^*, y^*)$, an initial guess is made, then the formula is applied iteratively until convergence (typically 3–5 iterations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The COG method is the mathematical backbone of the "store should be near the heaviest user" feature — a core KTU 2024 module-2 learning outcome.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Walkthrough 1 — Stock Variance Calculation

**Problem (Model KTU Style):** A stores ledger shows book stock of an item as **1,250 units**. A physical stock count reveals **1,205 units**. The unit cost is ₹80. Compute the stock variance and the financial impact.

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$
B = 1250 \text{ units}, \quad P = 1205 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute absolute variance:**

$$
\Delta = \vert B - P \vert = \vert 1250 - 1205 \vert = 45 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage variance:**

$$
\text{SV\%} = \frac{\Delta}{B} \times 100 = \frac{45}{1250} \times 100 = 3.6\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute financial impact:**

$$
\text{Loss} = \Delta \times \text{Unit Cost} = 45 \times 80 = \text{₹ } 3{,}600
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
A 3.6% variance exceeds the **standard tolerance of ±2%** in most ISO-certified plants. This signals weak store discipline, and the KTU 2024 examiner will expect a *recommendation* (e.g., introduce bin-card reconciliation, CCTV, or dual control).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing $P - B$ without absolute value. KTU evaluators will deduct **1 mark** for forgetting the modulus.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Walkthrough 2 — Reorder Level and Safety Stock

**Problem:** A factory uses 80 units/day of a fastener (max usage observed = 100/day). Lead time varies between 5 and 8 days. Compute Reorder Level and Safety Stock.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\max} &= 100 \text{ units/day} \\
D_{\text{avg}} &= 80 \text{ units/day} \\
L_{\max} &= 8 \text{ days} \\
L_{\text{avg}} &= 5 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reorder Level (ROL):**

$$
\text{ROL} = D_{\max} \times L_{\max} = 100 \times 8 = 800 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Safety Stock (SS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SS} &= (L_{\max} \times D_{\max}) - (L_{\text{avg}} \times D_{\text{avg}}) \\
&= (8 \times 100) - (5 \times 80) \\
&= 800 - 400 \\
&= 400 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
When stock physically drops to **800 units**, place a new purchase order. The **400 units** acts as a buffer against demand and supply variability — a direct feature of a *resilient* (successful) store.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Walkthrough 3 — Inventory Turnover

**Problem:** COGS of a plant for the year = ₹12,00,000. Opening stock = ₹2,00,000; Closing stock = ₹4,00,000. Calculate Inventory Turnover Ratio.

**Step 1 — Average Inventory:**

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{2} = \text{₹ } 3{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Turnover Ratio:**

$$
\text{IT} = \frac{\text{COGS}}{I_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 4 \text{ times/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Days of inventory on hand:**

$$
\text{DOH} = \frac{365}{\text{IT}} = \frac{365}{4} \approx 91 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** Stock is being replenished every ~3 months — a *moderately healthy* turnover for a process industry. A successful store should target DOH in line with industry benchmarks.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are rendered using Mermaid with strict adherence to the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE node-identifier rules. All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric (no markdown formatting inside quotes).

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Successful Store Keeping System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[RECEIVING BAY] --> B1[INSPECTION AND QUALITY CHECK]
    B1 --> C1[GRN GENERATION]
    C1 --> D1[STORES LEDGER UPDATE]

    E1 --> F1[PHYSICAL STORAGE IN DESIGNATED ZONE]
    F1 --> G1[STOCK READY FOR ISSUE]
    G1 --> H1[MATERIAL REQUISITION FROM PRODUCTION]
    H1 --> I1[ISSUE NOTE GENERATION]
    I1 --> J1[STOCK DEDUCTION IN LEDGER]
    J1 --> K1[PRODUCTION DEPARTMENT]
    F1 --> L1[PERIODIC STOCK VERIFICATION]
    L1 --> M1[VARIANCE ANALYSIS]
    M1 --> N1[CORRECTIVE ACTION]
```

### 4.2 The Twelve Features Mapped into Four Functional Clusters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLUS_A[CLUSTER A - PHYSICAL AND LAYOUT]:::cluster
    CLUS_B[CLUSTER B - DOCUMENTATION AND INFO]:::cluster

    F9[PILFERAGE CONTROL] --> CLUS_C

    F11[STOCK VERIFICATION] --> CLUS_D
    F12[ERP INTEGRATION] --> CLUS_D
    CLUS_A --> OUT[SUCCESSFUL STORE KEEPING SYSTEM]:::goal
    CLUS_B --> OUT
    CLUS_C --> OUT
    CLUS_D --> OUT
    classDef cluster fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef goal fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Information Flow Diagram — Documentation Trail

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VENDOR[APPROVED VENDOR] --> PO[PURCHASE ORDER]
    PO --> SHIP[DELIVERY OF MATERIALS]
    SHIP --> GRN[GOODS RECEIVED NOTE]
    GRN --> QC[QUALITY INSPECTION]
    QC --> ACC[ACCEPTED STOCK]
    QC --> REJ[REJECTED STOCK RETURNED]
    ACC --> BIN[PLACED IN BIN WITH CODE]
    BIN --> BC[BIN CARD UPDATED]
    BC --> SL[STORES LEDGER UPDATED]
    SL --> MR[MATERIAL REQUISITION FROM USER]
    MR --> IIN[INSPECTION AND ISSUE NOTE]
    IIN --> ISSUE[MATERIAL ISSUED TO USER]
    ISSUE --> SL2[STORES LEDGER DEDUCTED]
    SL2 --> STK[STOCK VERIFICATION SCHEDULED]
    STK --> VAR[VARIANCE REPORT]
    VAR --> MGMT[MANAGEMENT REVIEW]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix — FIFO vs. LIFO vs. FEFO

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{IS THE ITEM PERISHABLE OR DATE SENSITIVE?}:::q -->|YES| FEFO[FEFO - FIRST EXPIRED FIRST OUT]:::a
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{IS STORAGE SPACE CONSTRAINED VERTICALLY?}:::q
    Q2 -->|YES| LIFO[LIFO - LAST IN FIRST OUT]:::a
    Q2 -->|NO| FIFO[FIFO - FIRST IN FIRST OUT]:::a
    classDef q fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef a fill:#1565c0,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define store keeping. List any **four** features of a successful store keeping system.*

**Model Answer (8 lines):**

**Definition:** Store keeping is the function of receiving, storing, preserving, and issuing materials in an organization while maintaining proper records and ensuring their availability at minimum cost.

**Four Features:**
1. Adequate and systematic layout of the store.
2. Proper identification and codification of materials.
3. Accurate documentation (bin card, stores ledger, GRN).
4. Effective stock control using FIFO / LIFO / FEFO.
5. *(Any 4 of the 12 listed features are acceptable)*

**Marking Split:** Definition (1) + 4 features (½ mark each × 4 = 2) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between **Bin Card** and **Stores Ledger**. Why are both maintained simultaneously?*

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Bin Card** | **Stores Ledger** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Location | At the bin | In the accounts office |
| Updated by | Stores keeper | Stores accountant |
| Purpose | Physical record | Financial record |
| Format | Quantity only | Quantity + Value |

**Reason for dual maintenance:** They act as a *cross-check* — any mismatch between bin card and ledger indicates pilferage, misposting, or clerical error, enabling early detection of variances. This **internal control feature** is a hallmark of successful store keeping.

**Marking Split:** Comparison table (2) + Reason (1) = **3 marks**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Question]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **twelve features of a successful store keeping system**, grouping them under appropriate functional categories. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A stores ledger shows book stock of an item as **1,500 units**, while the physical count shows **1,440 units**. The unit cost is ₹50. Calculate the stock variance percentage and financial loss. Suggest two corrective actions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The twelve features of a successful store keeping system are grouped into four clusters:

**Cluster A — Physical and Layout Features:**
- *Adequate and systematic layout:* The store should be near receiving and production zones. Internal arrangement may follow fixed-location or zonal pattern.
- *Proper storage equipment:* Use of racks, pallets, bins, and AS/RS systems matched to material type.
- *Optimum space utilization:* Maximizing cube utilization, typically 60–80% in well-designed stores.

**Cluster B — Documentation Features:**
- *Accurate identification and codification:* Every item is assigned a unique stock code (e.g., `BRG-6204-2RS`).
- *Bin card and stores ledger:* Dual records ensure physical and financial traceability.
- *Use of stores documentation:* GRN, MR, SR, IIN — each document has a defined purpose and approval hierarchy.

**Cluster C — Control and Security Features:**
- *Effective stock control:* FIFO/LIFO/FEFO as appropriate to material type.
- *Security, safety, and preservation:* Fire safety, climate control, rust prevention, restricted access.
- *Pilferage and obsolescence control:* Lockable bins, slow-moving analysis, scrap disposal SOPs.

**Cluster D — People and Systems Features:**
- *Trained personnel:* Stores staff trained in codes, safety, ERP, and dual-control procedures.
- *Periodic stock verification:* Continuous (rolling) and annual physical counts.
- *ERP / digital integration:* Real-time visibility, barcode/RFID, automatic reorder triggers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marking Strategy:** Examiners award 1 mark per cluster (introduction) and 1.5 marks per feature (briefly explained). Writing 12 bullet points without explanation fetches only 4/7 — **explanation is mandatory**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$
B = 1500 \text{ units}, \quad P = 1440 \text{ units}, \quad c = \text{₹ } 50
$$

**Step 2 — Absolute variance:**

$$
\Delta = \vert 1500 - 1440 \vert = 60 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage variance:**

$$
\text{SV\%} = \frac{60}{1500} \times 100 = 4\%
$$

**Step 4 — Financial loss:**

$$
L = 60 \times 50 = \text{₹ } 3{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Corrective Actions (any two):**

1. **Tighten issue controls** — introduce a dual-signature system for material issues above a threshold value.
2. **Implement rolling stock verification** — count high-value items daily, medium-value items weekly.
3. **Install CCTV at issue points** — acts as both deterrent and audit trail.
4. **Reconcile bin card and ledger weekly** — variance > 2% must trigger an investigation.

**Marking Split:** Calculation (4) + Corrective actions (2) + Presentation (1) = **7 marks**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Question]**
*(a)* Discuss the role of **codification, bin card, stores ledger, and stores documentation (GRN, MR, IIN)** in ensuring successful store keeping. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A manufacturing unit uses 60 units/day of a component (maximum usage = 80 units/day). Lead time varies between 6 and 9 days. Compute the **Reorder Level** and **Safety Stock**. Also explain how the choice of **FIFO / LIFO / FEFO** depends on material characteristics. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**1. Codification:**
Codification is the process of assigning a unique, systematic code to each material (e.g., `FLT-HYD-04-2B` for a hydraulic filter). It enables fast identification, prevents duplication, supports ERP lookup, and forms the basis for all downstream documentation.

**2. Bin Card:**
A bin card is a *physical* record maintained at the storage location showing receipts, issues, and the running balance. It is updated in real time by the storekeeper and serves as the first line of physical traceability.

**3. Stores Ledger:**
The stores ledger is the *central accounting record* showing quantity *and value* of every item. It is maintained by the stores accountant and reconciles with the bin card. Discrepancies indicate posting errors or pilferage.

**4. Stores Documentation:**

- **GRN (Goods Received Note):** Generated when material physically arrives; acknowledges vendor delivery.
- **MR (Material Requisition):** Generated by the user department to request material.
- **IIN (Inspection and Issue Note):** Confirms the material is quality-cleared and authorizes its release.

Together, these documents form a *closed-loop audit trail* — a key feature of successful store keeping.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Tip:** Use a small table for the three documents to save time. Examiners reward **clarity of presentation** (½ mark reserved).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$
D_{\max} = 80, \quad D_{\text{avg}} = 60, \quad L_{\max} = 9, \quad L_{\text{avg}} = 6
$$

**Step 2 — Reorder Level:**

$$
\text{ROL} = D_{\max} \times L_{\max} = 80 \times 9 = 720 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3 — Safety Stock:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SS} &= (L_{\max} \times D_{\max}) - (L_{\text{avg}} \times D_{\text{avg}}) \\
&= (9 \times 80) - (6 \times 60) \\
&= 720 - 360 \\
&= 360 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Explanation of FIFO / LIFO / FEFO:**

- **FIFO (First-In-First-Out):** Used for items with *no expiry* but where aging may degrade quality (e.g., rubber gaskets, food items). The oldest stock is issued first.
- **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out):** Used in *bulk storage* where vertical stacking makes accessing older stock impractical. Also useful when material prices are rising, as it values issues at recent (higher) costs.
- **FEFO (First-Expired-First-Out):** Used for *perishable or date-sensitive* materials (e.g., adhesives, paints, pharmaceuticals, food). Items with the earliest expiry are issued first.

The choice depends on **material nature, storage geometry, regulatory requirements, and cost accounting policy**.

**Marking Split:** ROL (2) + SS (2) + FIFO/LIFO/FEFO explanation (3) = **7 marks**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 (PEPET414):**
> 1. **Listing features without explanation** — Writing "FIFO" alone in a 7-mark answer fetches zero. Always write *what*, *why*, and *when*.
> 2. **Skipping units in numericals** — Always write "units", "₹", "%". A unitless answer is marked incomplete.
> 3. **Formula syntax errors** — Confusing Reorder Level with Reorder Quantity. ROL is *trigger point*; ROQ is *order size* (Economic Order Quantity).
> 4. **No conclusion in 14-mark answers** — Always end with a 1-line *significance* or *industrial relevance* statement. Examiners reserve **½ mark** for this.
> 5. **Forgetting the absolute value in variance %** — Use $|\cdot|$ or $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation explicitly in your derivation.
> 6. **Mixing FIFO and FEFO definitions** — A frequent KTU trap. FIFO = *oldest received*; FEFO = *oldest expired*. They are not synonyms.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Features of Successful Store Keeping (KTU 2024 Module 2)**

- **Store keeping** = receive → store → preserve → issue → account, ensuring 5Rs (Right Material, Quantity, Time, Place, Cost).
- **Twelve features** grouped into **4 clusters**:
   - *Physical/Layout* (3): layout, equipment, space utilization
   - *Documentation* (3): codification, bin card + ledger, GRN/MR/IIN
   - *Control/Security* (3): FIFO/LIFO/FEFO, safety/preservation, pilferage/obsolescence control
   - *People/Systems* (3): trained personnel, stock verification, ERP integration
- **Key formulas to memorize verbatim:**
   - Cube Utilization % = $(V_{\text{stock}} / V_{\text{available}}) \times 100$
   - Stock Variance % = $(\vert B - P \vert / B) \times 100$
   - Reorder Level = $D_{\max} \times L_{\max}$
   - Safety Stock = $(L_{\max} \times D_{\max}) - (L_{\text{avg}} \times D_{\text{avg}})$
   - Inventory Turnover = $\text{COGS} / \text{Average Inventory}$
- **Bin card = physical; Stores ledger = financial.** Both must reconcile.
- **FIFO** for non-perishables, **FEFO** for date-sensitive, **LIFO** for bulk/space-constrained.
- **COG method** = mathematical basis for store location decision.
- **Stock variance tolerance** = typically **±2%** in ISO-certified plants.
- **Dual control + CCTV + rolling stock verification** = the three pillars of pilferage prevention.
- **KTU 2024 CO2 Mapping:** This topic directly maps to *"Apply the principles of store keeping"* — the foundation for Module 3 (Inventory Models) and Module 4 (JIT, MRP).
- **Examination Tip:** In 14-mark answers, allocate 4 pages: 1 for intro, 2 for theory, 1 for numerical/conclusion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship of Stores Department with Other Departments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relationship of Stores Department with Other Departments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Production and Operations Management** (as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme for PEPET414), the **Stores Department** (also termed the Inventory Control Cell, Warehouse Cell, or Stores Cell) functions as the organizational nerve center responsible for the **receiving, inspection, warehousing, preservation, issue, and accounting** of all raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, finished goods, tools, spares, and consumables.

The **Relationship of the Stores Department with Other Departments** refers to the formal and informal **inter-departmental coordination matrix** that defines the flow of materials, information, documentation, and authority between the Stores function and the **ten (10) primary functional cells** of a manufacturing enterprise. This relationship is built upon the principles of **material flow continuity**, **information symmetry**, **documentary traceability**, and **cost accountability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The stores department does **NOT** operate in isolation. It is a *hub-and-spoke* function — it is the central node that physically receives input from the *Purchasing Cell* and physically delivers output to the *Production Cell*. Every other department interfaces with Stores through standardized documentation called the **Store Requisition Slip (SRS)**, the **Goods Received Note (GRN)**, the **Material Issue Note (MIN)**, and the **Stock Register**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Hospital Pharmacy" Model)

Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital.

- The **Stores Department** is the **Central Pharmacy + Blood Bank + Equipment Warehouse** of the hospital.
- The **Purchasing Department** is the **Procurement Office** that places orders to pharmaceutical companies (suppliers).
- The **Production Department** is the **Surgical Team / Doctors** who consume medicines, sutures, gloves, and equipment during operations.
- The **Accounts Department** is the **Billing & Finance Office** that tracks the cost of every pill dispensed.
