\textbf{(7) Equipment Availability} \quad A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variable Legend (KTU Standard):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:----:|
| $A$ | Cross-sectional area of load on belt | $m^2$ |
| $V$ | Belt velocity | m/s |
| $\rho$ | Material bulk density | $t/m^3$ |
| $H$ | Vertical lift height | m |
| $L$ | Horizontal conveyor length | m |
| $K$ | Friction factor (typical 0.020–0.030) | — |
| $W$ | Load weight | N |
| $v$ | Hoisting velocity | m/s |
| $\eta$ | Mechanical efficiency (0.75–0.90) | — |
| $C_{\text{rated}}$ | Nameplate forklift capacity | kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The *Unit Handling Cost (UHC)* equation is the most important formula in KTU ESE Module 2 because it directly evaluates the **economic merit** of any MHE choice — examiners frequently give 5-mark problems on cost comparison between two equipment options.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 Comprehensive Merits-Demerits Comparative Matrix

The matrix below is the **highest-weightage analytical tool** for KTU ESE questions on this topic. It must be reproduced from memory.

### Detailed Comparison Table

| Equipment | Primary Merit | Primary Demerit | Best Application | Worst Application |
|:----------|:-------------|:---------------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| **Belt Conveyor** | High continuous throughput | Fixed path, high capex | Mining, grain, port loading | Unit-load manufacturing |
| **Roller Conveyor** | Low power, gravity option | Needs inclined/declined plane | Carton sorting, assembly lines | Heavy steel ingots |
| **Screw Conveyor** | Enclosed, dust-free | High power, wear on flights | Cement, flour, granular chemicals | Sticky/wet materials |
| **Pneumatic Conveyor** | 3-D pipe routing | High air-power cost | Plastics pellets, grain silos | Abrasive or fibrous materials |
| **Overhead Crane** | 3-D coverage of bay | Operator-skill dependent | Steel mills, heavy machinery | Small workshop tasks |
| **Jib Crane** | 360° in work cell radius | Limited reach radius | Machine-shop tending | Long-distance moves |
| **Mobile Crane** | Site portability | Setup time, road permits | Construction sites | Indoor warehouse |
| **Forklift Truck** | Maximum route flexibility | Accident risk, floor loading | Palletized warehouse goods | Fragile unit items |
| **Pallet Jack (Manual)** | Zero power cost | Operator fatigue | Short-distance flat moves | Long-distance transport |
| **AGV** | Unmanned 24/7 accuracy | Very high capex | Modern automated warehouses | Low-volume operations |
| **AS/RS** | Space density, accuracy | High structural cost | E-commerce, pharma | Small-scale stores |
| **Hoist (Electric)** | Vertical lift efficiency | Limited to 1-D motion | Workshop, garage | Horizontal transport |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Cost-Based Selection (14-Mark Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **canonical KTU ESE problem** combining merits/demerits with quantitative evaluation.

### **Problem Setup (Typical KTU Problem):**
> A factory must transport **500 tonnes/day** of coal over **120 m horizontal + 8 m vertical lift**, operating **8 hr/day, 300 days/yr**. Compare:
> **Option A:** Belt Conveyor — Capex ₹18,00,000, Life 10 yr, Op-cost ₹50/hr, Maintenance ₹30,000/yr.
> **Option B:** Forklift + 2 trips/day over equivalent distance — Capex ₹6,00,000, Life 7 yr, Op-cost ₹120/hr (driver+diesel), Maintenance ₹15,000/yr.
> Take interest rate **i = 10%** and salvage value **S = 10% of capex**.

### **Step 1: Annual Throughput**

$$
T_{\text{annual}} = 500 \times 300 = 150{,}000 \text{ tons/yr}
$$

### **Step 2: Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)**

The CRF converts initial capex into an equivalent annual cost, accounting for interest.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRF &= \frac{i \times (1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n} - 1} \\
CRF_A &= \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{10}}{(1.10)^{10} - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 2.5937}{2.5937 - 1} = \frac{0.25937}{1.5937} \\
CRF_A &= 0.16275 \\
CRF_B &= \frac{0.10 \times (1.10)^{7}}{(1.10)^{7} - 1} = \frac{0.10 \times 1.9487}{0.9487} \\
CRF_B &= 0.20541
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3: Annualized Capital Cost (ACC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACC_A &= (Capex_A - S_A) \times CRF_A + S_A \times i \\
ACC_A &= (18{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}80{,}000) \times 0.16275 + 1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.10 \\
ACC_A &= 16{,}20{,}000 \times 0.16275 + 18{,}000 \\
ACC_A &= 2{,}63{,}655 + 18{,}000 = ₹2{,}81{,}655/\text{yr} \\
\\
ACC_B &= (6{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}000) \times 0.20541 + 60{,}000 \times 0.10 \\
ACC_B &= 5{,}40{,}000 \times 0.20541 + 6{,}000 \\
ACC_B &= 1{,}10{,}921 + 6{,}000 = ₹1{,}16{,}921/\text{yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 4: Annual Operating + Maintenance Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{op},A} &= 50 \times 8 \times 300 = ₹1{,}20{,}000/\text{yr} \\
C_{\text{maint},A} &= ₹30{,}000/\text{yr} \\
\text{Total Operating}_A &= ₹1{,}50{,}000/\text{yr} \\
\\
C_{\text{op},B} &= 120 \times 8 \times 300 = ₹2{,}88{,}000/\text{yr} \\
C_{\text{maint},B} &= ₹15{,}000/\text{yr} \\
\text{Total Operating}_B &= ₹3{,}03{,}000/\text{yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5: Total Annual Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAC_A &= ACC_A + \text{Operating}_A = 2{,}81{,}655 + 1{,}50{,}000 = ₹4{,}31{,}655/\text{yr} \\
TAC_B &= ACC_B + \text{Operating}_B = 1{,}16{,}921 + 3{,}03{,}000 = ₹4{,}19{,}921/\text{yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 6: Unit Handling Cost (UHC)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UHC_A &= \frac{4{,}31{,}655}{1{,}50{,}000} = ₹2.88/\text{ton} \\
UHC_B &= \frac{4{,}19{,}921}{1{,}50{,}000} = ₹2.80/\text{ton}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 7: Engineering Decision**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although **Forklift (Option B) is marginally cheaper per ton (₹2.80 vs ₹2.88)**, the **merit-demerit qualitative analysis reverses this decision** for this scale of operation:
> - Forklifts are **intermittent, labour-intensive, and unsafe** at 500 TPD.
> - Belt conveyors offer **continuous, safer, automated operation** with **lower MDR**.
> - KTU verdict: **Option A (Belt Conveyor) is preferred** for high-volume, fixed-route, repetitive duty — economic advantage is marginal, but **operational merit dominates**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MHE Selection Decision Tree (KTU High-Yield Schematic)

The diagram below maps the **decision logic** for choosing MHE based on the merits/demerits framework. This is the standard KTU Module 2 cognitive map.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Handling Requirement] --> B{Load Type?}
    B -->|Bulk Continuous| C{Path Fixed?}
    B -->|Unit Load| D{Distance?}
    B -->|Heavy Intermittent| E[Overhead Crane]
    B -->|Mixed| F[Industrial Truck]

    C -->|Yes| G{Throughput?}
    C -->|No| H[Mobile Crane or AGV]

    G -->|High >100 TPH| I[Belt Conveyor]
    G -->|Medium| J[Screw or Chain Conveyor]
    G -->|Low| K[Roller Conveyor]

    D -->|Short <50m| L[Pallet Jack]
    D -->|Medium 50-200m| M[Forklift]
    D -->|Long >200m| N[AGV or Tractor-Trailer]

    F --> M

    I --> O[Merit: High throughput]
    I --> P[Demerit: High capex fixed]

    E --> Q[Merit: 3D heavy lift]
    E --> R[Demerit: Operator skill needed]

    M --> S[Merit: Flexible route]
    M --> T[Demerit: Accident risk]

    styleA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333

    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B

    style P fill:#FFE4E1
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Block Diagram — Cost-Based MHE Evaluation Engine

```mermaid

    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]
        A1[Load W tons/day]
        A2[Distance L meters]
        A3[Lift H meters]
        A4[Operating Hours]
    end

        C1[Best MHE Option]
        C2[Qualitative Score]
        C3[Justification Report]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1

    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3

    style INPUT fill:#FFFACD
    style ENGINE fill:#E0FFFF
    style OUTPUT fill:#98FB98
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any four merits and three demerits of belt conveyors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Merits:*
1. High continuous throughput — up to 10,000 TPH in mining.
2. Low labour requirement after installation.
3. Suitable for hazardous, abrasive, hot materials.
4. Predictable operating cost; long service life.

*Demerits:*
1. Fixed installation path — no route flexibility.
2. High capital cost (₹15–25 lakh per 100 m typical).
3. Belt wear and dust generation in abrasive service.

*(1 mark per correct point, max 3 = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Compare forklifts and overhead cranes on three merits and two demerits each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | Forklift | Overhead Crane |
|:----------|:---------|:---------------|
| Merit 1 | Route flexibility | 3-D bay coverage |
| Merit 2 | Low capital cost (₹5–10 lakh) | Handles very heavy loads (>50 T) |
| Merit 3 | Quick deployment | No floor space consumed |
| Demerit 1 | Limited lift height (≤ 8 m) | Operator-skill intensive |
| Demerit 2 | High accident risk | High unit cost per lift |

---

## **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the merits and demerits of **conveyors** (belt, roller, screw, pneumatic) as a class of material handling equipment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory handles **300 tonnes/day** of cement over **80 m horizontal + 5 m vertical lift** using a **screw conveyor**. Belt conveyor alternative has capex ₹12 lakh (life 8 yr, op-cost ₹40/hr) versus screw capex ₹7 lakh (life 6 yr, op-cost ₹55/hr). Take $i = 12\%$, salvage $S = 8\%$. Compare annual cost and recommend. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks — 1 mark per valid point, plus 1 for classification]**

*Belt Conveyor:*
- **Merits:** continuous high throughput, long-distance capability (km range).
- **Demerits:** high capex, dust emission, belt wear.

*Roller Conveyor:*
- **Merits:** gravity-driven option, zero power cost for downhill flow.
- **Demerits:** limited to flat/inclined surfaces, manual push-start.

*Screw Conveyor:*
- **Merits:** enclosed, dust-free, handles sticky/wet materials.
- **Demerits:** high power consumption, flights wear on abrasive material, short length (≤ 30 m).

*Pneumatic Conveyor:*
- **Merits:** 3-D pipe routing, fully enclosed.
- **Demerits:** high air-compression cost, limited to fine granular materials.

**Classification Mark:** Conveyors are **fixed-path, continuous-flow MHE**, suited for **bulk, repetitive, high-volume** applications.

**(b) [7 Marks — Step-by-Step Numerical Solution]**

**Step 1 — Annual throughput:**
$T = 300 \times 300 = 90{,}000 \text{ tons/yr}$

**Step 2 — Capital Recovery Factor:**
$CRF_A = \frac{0.12 \times (1.12)^8}{(1.12)^8 - 1} = \frac{0.12 \times 2.4760}{1.4760} = 0.20130$

$CRF_B = \frac{0.12 \times (1.12)^6}{(1.12)^6 - 1} = \frac{0.12 \times 1.9738}{0.9738} = 0.24323$

**Step 3 — Annualized Capital Cost:**
$ACC_A = (12{,}00{,}000 - 96{,}000) \times 0.20130 + 96{,}000 \times 0.12$
$ACC_A = 11{,}04{,}000 \times 0.20130 + 11{,}520 = ₹2{,}22{,}235 + 11{,}520 = ₹2{,}33{,}755/\text{yr}$

$ACC_B = (7{,}00{,}000 - 56{,}000) \times 0.24323 + 56{,}000 \times 0.12$
$ACC_B = 6{,}44{,}000 \times 0.24323 + 6{,}720 = ₹1{,}56{,}640 + 6{,}720 = ₹1{,}63{,}360/\text{yr}$

**Step 4 — Operating Cost:**
$Op_A = 40 \times 8 \times 300 = ₹96{,}000/\text{yr}$
$Op_B = 55 \times 8 \times 300 = ₹1{,}32{,}000/\text{yr}$

**Step 5 — Total Annual Cost:**
$TAC_A = 2{,}33{,}755 + 96{,}000 = ₹3{,}29{,}755/\text{yr}$
$TAC_B = 1{,}63{,}360 + 1{,}32{,}000 = ₹2{,}95{,}360/\text{yr}$

**Step 6 — Unit Handling Cost:**
$UHC_A = 3{,}29{,}755 \div 90{,}000 = ₹3.66/\text{ton}$
$UHC_B = 2{,}95{,}360 \div 90{,}000 = ₹3.28/\text{ton}$

**Recommendation:** Screw conveyor is **marginally cheaper (₹3.28/ton)** and is **enclosed (dust-free)** — preferred for **cement** because airborne cement dust is a health hazard. **Screw conveyor (Option B) is recommended.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the merits and demerits of **overhead travelling cranes** vs **mobile cranes** for a heavy engineering workshop. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial truck has **MTBF = 800 hr** and **MTTR = 12 hr**. Calculate availability. If availability falls below **95%**, recommend two engineering interventions. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

| Aspect | Overhead Travelling Crane | Mobile Crane |
|:-------|:------------------------|:-------------|
| **Merit 1** | 3-D bay coverage; full floor-to-ceiling access | Site portability |
| **Merit 2** | High capacity (50–500 T) | No track/rail infrastructure |
| **Merit 3** | Operator cabin or pendant — repeatable cycles | Lower capex (₹10–30 lakh) |
| **Demerit 1** | Requires heavy structural runway (₹) | Setup time, road permits |
| **Demerit 2** | Limited to one bay/building | Limited lift height |
| **Demerit 3** | High skill required | Weather-sensitive outdoors |

**Verdict:** **Overhead crane preferred** for fixed workshop; **mobile crane preferred** for construction/outdoor sites.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Availability formula:**
$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{800}{800 + 12} = \frac{800}{812} = 0.9852 = \mathbf{98.52\%}$

**Step 2 — Decision:**
Since **98.52% > 95%**, the truck **meets the availability target** — no intervention strictly required. However, for best-practice KTU answer:

**Two Engineering Interventions to improve availability further:**
1. **Predictive Maintenance Program** — Install vibration sensors on hydraulic pump and motor bearings to detect wear before failure (reduces MTTR by ~40%).
2. **Operator Skill Certification (KTU standard)** — Reduce MTBF-degrading operator errors by 30% through quarterly training; cross-train backup drivers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Formula Mis-quote:** Students often write $A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF+MTTR}$ (inverted!). Always remember — Availability = Uptime/(Uptime+Downtime) = MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR).
> 2. **Skipping CRF Derivation:** KTU awards 2 marks specifically for showing the CRF expansion step in numerical problems. Writing just the final CRF value = **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting Salvage Value:** Many students ignore the $S \times i$ term in annualized cost. This is a **−1 mark** deduction.
> 4. **Confusing "Merits" with "Selection Criteria":** Merits are inherent properties (e.g., high throughput). Selection criteria include site, cost, safety — these are *applications*, not merits.
> 5. **No Units in Numerical Answers:** Always write ₹/ton, tons/yr, etc. Missing units = **−0.5 to −1 mark** per answer.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Merits & Demerits of MHE (Module 2):**
>
> - **Conveyors:** Merits = continuous, high-volume, low labour. Demerits = fixed path, high capex, bulk-only.
> - **Cranes:** Merits = 3-D movement, very heavy loads. Demerits = intermittent, skilled operator, structural cost.
> - **Industrial Trucks:** Merits = flexible, cheap, quick. Demerits = accident-prone, lift-height-limited, floor-load.
> - **AGV/AS/RS:** Merits = unmanned, accurate, 24/7. Demerits = very high capex, structured environment needed.
> - **Master Formulae:** $Q = A V \rho$ (throughput), $P = Q H/367 + Q L K$ (belt power), $CRF = \dfrac{i(1+i)^n}{(1+i)^n-1}$ (cost economics), $A = MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR)$ (availability).
> - **Decision Rule:** Choose MHE by *intersection of throughput demand, route flexibility, and unit cost* — not by single merit alone.
> - **Always quantify:** every merits/demerits answer in KTU ESE should end with **either a numerical justification or a quantitative metric** (e.g., "₹/ton", "% availability").
> - **Salmon-pink callout:** When in doubt, KTU examiners prefer the **safer + continuous** equipment over cheaper + intermittent, for large-scale fixed operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Stores and Storage Equipment
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Stores and Storage Equipment

# 📘 TYPES OF STORES AND STORAGE EQUIPMENT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** This topic forms the foundational layer of *Industrial Stores Management*. It directly maps to **Course Outcome CO2** — *"Analyse the various types of stores, storage systems, and material handling equipment used in manufacturing and service industries."* Students must be prepared for a 7-mark sub-part question in Part B and 3-mark direct questions in Part A.

---

## 1.1 What is a "Store" in Industrial Engineering?

In the context of a manufacturing or service enterprise, a **store** (or **warehouse** / **stockroom**) is a planned physical space or facility that is used to **hold (stockpile), protect, preserve, and issue raw materials, work-in-progress (WIP), finished goods, tools, spare parts, and consumables** in a controlled and accountable manner.

A store is **not just a "godown"** — it is a *system* with a definite *layout, equipment, documentation, and operating procedures* designed to minimize the cost of material flow while maximizing **availability, traceability, and security**.

### 1.2 Formal KTU Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *A store is a physical location within or outside the plant premises, equipped with suitable storage equipment and managed by trained store-keepers, where materials are received, inspected, identified, classified, preserved, and issued against authorized documentation, in order to support continuous production at minimum total cost of inventory.*

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Hospital Pharmacy" Model

Think of a hospital pharmacy:
- The pharmacy **receives** medicines from suppliers (analogous to **goods received**).
- It **inspects** them (analogous to **incoming quality check**).
- It **stores** them in proper racks, refrigerators, and locked cabinets (analogous to **storage equipment**).
- It **issues** medicines to wards on a written indent (analogous to **material issue voucher**).
- The **chief pharmacist** maintains minimum stock and re-order levels (analogous to **inventory control**).

A factory store works on **exactly the same logic** — the *type* of store and the *kind* of storage equipment depend on *what* you are storing, *how often* you need it, and *how critical* it is for production.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever the examiner asks "Differentiate…", always use the analogy first to *frame* the answer, then drop the formal definition. This fetches the **impression marks (1–2 extra)** that decide between an S and A+ grade.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Pallet Size** | **1200 mm × 1000 mm** (ISO/EU) or **1219 mm × 1016 mm** (US) | Base unit for all rack/aisle design |
| **Standard Aisle Width** | **2.5 – 3.0 m** (counterbalance forklift) | Determines warehouse layout |
| **Narrow Aisle Width** | **1.6 – 1.9 m** (reach truck) | High-density storage |
| **Standard Ceiling Height Utilization** | **Up to 85%** of clear height | Vertical storage efficiency |
| **Standard Bin Height** | **≤ 1.8 m** for manual picking | Ergonomics limit |

---

## 1.5 Why Classify Stores? — The Core Engineering Logic

Different materials have **different physical, chemical, and economic characteristics**. A single, monolithic store cannot efficiently handle all of them. Hence, KTU expects every student to know **how to classify stores** based on:

1. **Location relative to the plant** → *Centralized, Decentralized, Combined*.
2. **Function served** → *Receipt, Issue, Buffer, Reserve*.
3. **Nature of material stored** → *Raw material, WIP, Finished Goods, Tools, Spares, Consumables*.
4. **Operational visibility** → *Open, Closed, Bonded (Excise/Customs), Quarantine*.
5. **Hazard classification** → *Hazardous, Non-hazardous, Perishable, Flammable, Radioactive*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Trade-off:** Centralization reduces *inventory carrying cost* but increases *internal transport cost*. Decentralization does the opposite. The **optimal store design** balances these two opposing costs — this is the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) logic** applied spatially.

---

## 1.6 The 3 Master Types of Stores (Board-Exam Focus)

| # | Type | One-line Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Centralized Store** | *One main store serves the entire plant* | Small to medium plants, low material variety |
| 2 | **Decentralized Store** | *Multiple sub-stores located near user departments* | Large plants, high material variety, hazardous items |
| 3 | **Combined Centralized & Decentralized** | *A main store + departmental sub-stores* | Most common in KTU-referenced industries |

> [!NOTE]
> **Further classification (frequently tested):** *Perpetual Store, Buffer Store, Transit Store, Bonded Store, Quarantine Store, Cold Store, Tool Store, Spares Store, Salvage Store, and Rejected Material Store.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 Type 1 — Centralized Stores

### Definition
A **centralized store** is a *single, large storehouse* usually located at the **center of the plant** (or in a corner with good road access), from which **all departments receive their material issues**.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. All incoming materials are **received at one dock** and routed to the central store.
2. The storekeeper **inspects, codes, bins, and stocks** them.
3. Departments raise **material requisitions (indents)** which are approved by the stores manager.
4. The storekeeper **issues** material against the approved indent, with a **Material Issue Voucher (MIV)**.
5. **Bin cards** and **stock registers** are updated at this single point.

### Advantages (8 Points — Board-Exam Favourite)
1. **Lower inventory value** — bulk purchasing & single-point control.
2. **Better control over pilferage, wastage, and obsolescence.**
3. **Economical in store-keeping staff** — one expert team handles all materials.
4. **Easier physical verification and stock-taking.**
5. **Better utilization of storage space and equipment** (racks, cranes, hoists).
6. **Centralized accountability** — single point of responsibility.
7. **Standardization of procedures** (FIFO, FEFO, LIFO can be enforced uniformly).
8. **Lower insurance and rental cost** (one large store vs. many small ones).

### Disadvantages (5 Points)
1. **Higher internal transport cost** — departmental sub-stores must send trucks to the main store.
2. **Production delays** if distance to the central store is large.
3. **Bottleneck risk** — if the store is shut, the whole plant stops.
4. **Not suitable for hazardous materials** that need isolation.
5. **Not suitable for very high-volume, low-value items** (nuts, bolts) that are consumed in thousands per day.

### Best Suited For
- Small and medium plants (< 500 employees).
- Low product variety.
- Single-product or job-shop production with limited material variety.

---

## 2.2 Type 2 — Decentralized Stores

### Definition
A **decentralized store** consists of **two or more sub-stores** located *near or within* the consuming departments (e.g., a **Tool Room** near the machine shop, a **Paint Store** near the paint shop, a **Spare Parts Store** near the maintenance workshop).

### Operational Logic
1. Each department has its **own sub-store and storekeeper**.
2. Central purchase still happens, but materials are **pre-allocated** to sub-stores.
3. **Inter-store transfers** happen when one sub-store has surplus and another has shortage.
4. Each sub-store maintains its own **bin card, ledger, and re-order level**.

### Advantages
1. **Minimum internal transport cost** — sub-store is *walking distance* from the user.
2. **Faster issue** → reduced production delay.
3. **Hazardous materials can be isolated** safely (e.g., LPG cylinders, acids).
4. **Department-specific storage** (e.g., temperature-controlled cold store for vaccines).
5. **Less congestion** at any single store.

### Disadvantages
1. **Higher total inventory value** — each sub-store maintains its own safety stock.
2. **Duplication of staff and equipment.**
3. **Difficult central control** — pilferage is harder to detect.
4. **Higher administrative cost.**
5. **More space occupied** in total (corridors, multiple receiving points).

### Best Suited For
- Large plants with **multi-product, high-variety** production.
- Process industries (chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical).
- Plants with **hazardous, perishable, or high-security** materials.

---

## 2.3 Type 3 — Combined Centralized & Decentralized Stores

### Definition
This is a **hybrid model** in which a **Central Main Store (CMS)** acts as the *parent* and **Departmental Sub-Stores (DSS)** act as *satellites*.

### Operational Logic
1. **Bulk receipt and inspection** happens at the Central Main Store.
2. Materials are **issued in bulk to sub-stores** based on a **periodic re-order level**.
3. Sub-stores **issue to operators** in small, controlled quantities against daily indents.
4. The CMS **replenishes** sub-stores using a **two-bin system** or **periodic review system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the MOST COMMON model** in real industries. The KTU examiner expects students to draw a *block diagram* showing the flow: **Supplier → Central Store → Sub-Store → User Department**.

### Why is it the Most Common? (The Engineering Trade-off)
It *balances* the **low inventory cost of centralization** with the **low transport cost of decentralization**. This is the *Pareto-optimal* solution for medium-to-large plants.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Store & Equipment Selection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this entire table — it is a 14-mark question's "free 4 marks" bank.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Logic | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units/order | Optimal replenishment qty |
| 2 | **Re-Order Level (ROL)** | $ROL = \text{Max. Lead Time Demand} + \text{Safety Stock}$ | units | When to raise a new PO |
| 3 | **Safety Stock (SS)** | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | units | Buffer against demand/lead-time variation |
| 4 | **Minimum Stock** | $Min = ROL - \text{Average Lead Time Usage}$ | units | Trigger for emergency purchase |
| 5 | **Maximum Stock** | $Max = ROL + EOQ - Min$ | units | Upper limit to avoid over-stocking |
| 6 | **Average Inventory** | $Avg = \dfrac{Min + Max}{2}$ | units | Insurance & carrying cost base |
| 7 | **Storage Capacity (Floor)** | $A = \dfrac{N \times P_s}{K_u \times S_a}$ | m² | Floor area of store |
| 8 | **Storage Capacity (Volume)** | $V = \dfrac{N \times V_u}{K_u}$ | m³ | Volumetric capacity |
| 9 | **Pallet Position Capacity** | $N_p = \dfrac{L \times W}{l_p \times w_p} \cdot \dfrac{H}{h_p}$ | pallets | Pallet rack design |
| 10 | **Aisle Width (min)** | $W_a = R_{tr} + L_{load} + 2 \times 150\text{ mm}$ | mm | Truck turning radius |
| 11 | **Carrying Cost Rate** | $C_c = i_c \times C \times \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | ₹/year | Holding cost of inventory |
| 12 | **Order Cost** | $C_o = \dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$ | ₹/year | Procurement cost |
| 13 | **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** | $TIC = C_c + C_o + \text{Shortage Cost}$ | ₹/year | Optimization objective |
| 14 | **Warehouse Utilization Index** | $UI = \dfrac{\text{Used Volume}}{\text{Total Volume}} \times 100$ | % | Storage efficiency KPI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend of Variables (used in the table above):**
> - $D$ = Annual demand (units/yr)
> - $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
> - $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit-yr)
> - $Z$ = Service-level Z-score (e.g., **1.65 for 95%**, **2.33 for 99%**)
> - $\sigma_{LT}$ = Standard deviation of lead-time demand
> - $L$ = Replenishment lead time
> - $K_u$ = Storage utilization factor (**typically 0.7 – 0.85**)
> - $S_a$ = Stacking height allowance
> - $N$ = Number of items to be stored
> - $P_s, V_u$ = Footprint & unit volume of single item
> - $R_{tr}$ = Turning radius of truck, $L_{load}$ = Load length

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

> [!TIP]
> The same formulas are used by:
> - **Amazon / Flipkart warehouse designers** for rack capacity planning.
> - **Reliance Fresh cold-chain designers** for perishable storage.
> - **ISRO** for spare-part management in satellite assembly.
> - **Hospitals** for pharmacy and blood-bank inventory.

---

## 2.6 Storage Equipment — The 8 Master Categories

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Storage Equipment):** *Storage equipment refers to the physical hardware — fixed or mobile — used to hold, organize, protect, and enable retrieval of stored materials inside a store or warehouse.*

The 8 master categories, each fully covered in 2.7 – 2.14:

1. **Racks and Shelving**
2. **Bins, Drawers, and Cabinets**
3. **Pallets and Pallet Accessories**
4. **Mezzanines (Floors)**
5. **Stacker Cranes & AS/RS**
6. **Carousels (Horizontal & Vertical)**
7. **Conveyor-Based Storage**
8. **Bulk Storage (Silos, Bunkers, Tanks)**

---

## 2.7 Category 1 — Racks and Shelving

### (a) Selective Pallet Rack
- **Most common** industrial rack.
- Each pallet is accessed **directly** from the aisle.
- **Pros:** 100% selectivity, easy access.
- **Cons:** Lowest space utilization (~35% of floor).
- **Use case:** FMCG, e-commerce.

### (b) Drive-In / Drive-Through Rack
- Forklift **drives into** the rack structure.
- **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** for drive-in, **FIFO** for drive-through.
- **Use case:** Cold stores, high-density same-product storage.

### (c) Cantilever Rack
- Has **arms projecting from a vertical column**; no front obstruction.
- **Use case:** Long items — pipes, timber, rods, steel bars.

### (d) Push-Back Rack
- Pallets are pushed back on **inclined rollers**; LIFO logic.
- **Use case:** Medium-density, multi-SKU pallet storage.

### (e) Flow Rack (Pallet Live Storage)**
- **Inclined roller lanes**; pallets roll forward by gravity.
- **FIFO logic** automatically.
- **Use case:** High-throughput, expiry-date-critical items.

### (f) Mobile (Sliding) Rack
- Entire rack structure **moves on rails** to open up an aisle only where needed.
- **Space saving: up to 50%.**
- **Use case:** Archives, slow-moving spares.

### (g) Shelving (Boltless / Rivet / Slotted Angle)
- For **hand-loaded items** (boxes, small parts).
- **Use case:** Spare-parts stores, tool rooms.

---

## 2.8 Category 2 — Bins, Drawers, and Cabinets

| Type | Material | Capacity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plastic Bins** | HDPE / PP | 1 – 60 L | Small parts, fasteners |
| **Metal Bins** | GI / SS | 5 – 100 L | Heavy, oily parts |
| **Drawer Cabinets** | CRCA steel | 25 – 75 drawers | Tools, gauges, dies |
| **Locker Cabinets** | CRCA steel | 4 – 12 lockers | Personal protective equipment (PPE), valuables |
| **Bin Boxes (Corrugated)** | Cardboard | 1 – 20 L | Dispatch packaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two-Bin System:** A KTU-favourite concept. Two bins of equal size are kept for each item. The storekeeper uses the *first* bin. When it empties, the second bin is opened, and an indent is raised for replenishment. The first bin is the *re-order trigger*.

---

## 2.9 Category 3 — Pallets and Pallet Accessories

### Pallet Types
| Type | Material | Load Capacity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wooden Pallets** | Hardwood / softwood | 1000 – 1500 kg | General purpose, export |
| **Plastic Pallets** | HDPE | 500 – 1500 kg | Pharma, food, hygienic |
| **Metal Pallets** | MS / SS / Al | 1500 – 3000 kg | Heavy engineering, aerospace |
| **Pallet Collars (Boxes)** | Wood / plastic | 600 – 1200 L | Bulk loose material |
| **Slip Sheets** | Plastic / kraft | 800 – 1500 kg | Export, one-way shipping |

### Pallet Accessories
- **Pallet Jacks** (manual / electric).
- **Pallet Inverters** (for empty pallet stacking).
- **Pallet Stretch Wrappers.**
- **Edge Protectors and Strapping.**

---

## 2.10 Category 4 — Mezzanines (Intermediate Floors)

A **mezzanine** is a **semi-permanent raised platform** built inside a warehouse, supported by **free-standing steel columns**, that *doubles or triples* the usable floor area.

- **Structural Mezzanines** — designed for **heavy loads (500 – 1000 kg/m²)**.
- **Rack-Supported Mezzanines** — shelves act as the *floor joists*.
- **Shelf-Supported Mezzanines** — shelving units support the floor above.

**Use case:** Spare-parts warehouses, e-commerce fulfilment, records archives.

---

## 2.11 Category 5 — Stacker Cranes & Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)

### Stacker Crane
A **mast-mounted crane** that travels along an aisle between two racks, picks/picks a pallet or tote, and delivers it to a station at the aisle end.

- **Height:** Up to **40 m**.
- **Speed:** Horizontal **3 m/s**, vertical **1.5 m/s** (typical).
- **Aisle width:** as low as **1.5 m**.

### AS/RS Variants
| Variant | Description | Throughput |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit-Load AS/RS** | Handles full pallets | 50 – 200 cycles/hr |
| **Mini-Load AS/RS** | Handles totes / bins | 100 – 500 cycles/hr |
| **Vertical Lift Module (VLM)** | Enclosed vertical cabinet | 50 – 150 cycles/hr |
| **Horizontal Carousel** | Rotating horizontal loop | 200 – 500 picks/hr |
| **Vertical Carousel** | Rotating vertical loop | 100 – 250 picks/hr |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Definition (AS/RS):** *An Automated Storage and Retrieval System is a computer-controlled combination of storage equipment (racks), handling equipment (cranes, conveyors), and management software (WMS) that automatically stores and retrieves loads with minimum human intervention.*

---

## 2.12 Category 6 — Carousels (Horizontal & Vertical)

### Horizontal Carousel
- A **series of bins mounted on a horizontal oval track**.
- The carousel **rotates** to bring the required bin to the operator.
- **Best for:** Small parts picking in assembly lines.

### Vertical Carousel
- A **series of shelves mounted on a vertical oval track**.
- The carousel **rotates vertically** to bring the required shelf to the picker at waist height.
- **Space saving: up to 75%** vs. static shelving.

---

## 2.13 Category 7 — Conveyor-Based Storage

- **Roller Conveyors** for carton/tote flow.
- **Chain Conveyors** for heavy pallet flow.
- **Belt Conveyors** for piece-part accumulation.
- **Powered vs. Gravity** — choice based on throughput and layout.

> [!TIP]
> **Conveyors are mostly Material Handling Equipment (MHE)**; they are covered in detail in **Module 3**. In Module 2, they are studied only as a *storage-feeder* component.

---

## 2.14 Category 8 — Bulk Storage Equipment (Silos, Bunkers, Tanks)

| Equipment | Material Stored | Construction |
|---|---|---|
| **Silo** | Grains, cement, fly-ash, plastic granules | Cylindrical steel / RCC |
| **Bunker** | Coal, ore, scrap | Trapezoidal steel / RCC |
| **Storage Tank** | Liquids (oil, chemicals, water) | Cylindrical, often pressurized |
| **Hopper** | Sand, sugar, fertilizer | Conical bottom for gravity discharge |
| **Bin** | Granular materials | Steel, rectangular |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIFO/LIFO logic in bulk storage:** Silos are *LIFO* (last filled is first discharged), while live-bottom bins with *first-in/first-out* screws are *FIFO*.

---

## 2.15 Selection Criteria for Storage Equipment (Decision Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 6-Factor Selection Model (frequently asked for 7 marks):**

1. **Material characteristics** — size, weight, shape, hazardous nature.
2. **Throughput requirement** — picks per hour, pallet movements per shift.
3. **Storage density** — pallets per m².
4. **Selectivity** — direct access vs. LIFO/FIFO.
5. **Capital and operating cost** — ROI, payback period.
6. **Compatibility with material handling equipment** — forklift, conveyor, crane.

---

## 2.16 Industry-Wise Application Map

| Industry | Preferred Store Type | Preferred Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Combined | Selective rack + AS/RS |
| **Pharma** | Centralized | VLM + cold rooms |
| **E-commerce** | Decentralized | Carousels + AGV-fed racks |
| **Cement** | Centralized | Silos + bucket elevators |
| **Steel Plant** | Decentralized | Cantilever + heavy-duty racks |
| **Hospital** | Centralized | Drawer cabinets + cold store |
| **Warehouse (3PL)** | Combined | Mezzanines + flow racks |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, COMPUTATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section turns the descriptive theory above into **executable numerical logic** — the kind of question that earns 7 marks in Part B. Every step is explicitly written out. **No shortcut, no skip.**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Floor Area of a Store (The "Storage Capacity Equation")

### Statement of the Problem
Given a store must hold **N** items per day (e.g., pallet positions), each pallet occupies a **footprint P_s** (m²) on the floor, and the **storage utilization factor is K_u** (because of aisles, doors, staging), **derive the floor area of the store**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the gross footprint of items.**
If we have N items, each occupying a footprint $P_s$, then the *theoretical* floor area required is:
$$A_{ideal} = N \times P_s$$

**Step 2 — Account for aisles, doors, columns, and safety margins.**
Real warehouses are not 100% packed. The actual usable area is the *gross area* multiplied by the *utilization factor* $K_u$ (typically **0.7 to 0.85**). Hence the *gross* area required is:
$$A = \dfrac{N \times P_s}{K_u}$$

**Step 3 — Account for vertical stacking.**  
If pallets can be stacked up to a height $S_a$ (m), and the items themselves have a unit height $h_p$, then the *stacking factor* is $\dfrac{S_a}{h_p}$. We can reduce the floor area by this factor:
$$A = \dfrac{N \times P_s \times h_p}{K_u \times S_a}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute standard values (worked example).**
Let $N = 1200$ pallets, $P_s = 1.2 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m} = 1.2 \text{ m}^2$, $h_p = 1.4 \text{ m}$, $S_a = 4.2 \text{ m}$, $K_u = 0.8$.

Plug in:
$$A = \dfrac{1200 \times 1.2 \times 1.4}{0.8 \times 4.2}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$1200 \times 1.2 = 1440$$
$$1440 \times 1.4 = 2016$$

Compute the denominator:
$$0.8 \times 4.2 = 3.36$$

Final division:
$$A = \dfrac{2016}{3.36} = 600 \text{ m}^2$$

**Interpretation:** The store needs a minimum floor area of **600 m²**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pallet Position Capacity of a Selective Rack

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Count positions per beam level.**
For a rack of length $L$ (m), with each pallet slot of length $l_p$ (m):
$$n_L = \dfrac{L}{l_p}$$

**Step 2 — Count positions per cross-bay.**
For a rack of width $W$ (m), with each pallet slot of width $w_p$ (m):
$$n_W = \dfrac{W}{w_p}$$

**Step 3 — Count beam levels (vertical).**
For a rack of height $H$ (m), with each pallet slot of height $h_p$ (m):
$$n_H = \dfrac{H}{h_p}$$

**Step 4 — Total pallet positions.**
$$N_p = n_L \times n_W \times n_H = \dfrac{L \times W \times H}{l_p \times w_p \times h_p}$$

**Step 5 — Worked example.**
Let $L = 30 \text{ m}$, $W = 2.4 \text{ m}$, $H = 6.0 \text{ m}$, pallet size = $1.2 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m} \times 1.4 \text{ m}$ (L × W × H).

$$N_p = \dfrac{30 \times 2.4 \times 6.0}{1.2 \times 1.0 \times 1.4} = \dfrac{432}{1.68} = 257.14$$

**Since the number of pallet positions must be an integer, we round down: $N_p = 257$ pallets.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Aisle Width for Forklift Operation

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the geometry of a 90° turn.**
When a forklift turns 90° into a rack, the *minimum aisle width* is the sum of:
- The **turning radius** of the truck $R_{tr}$ (mm).
- The **length of the load** $L_{load}$ (mm).
- **Two safety margins** of 150 mm each.

**Step 2 — The general formula:**
$$W_a = R_{tr} + L_{load} + 2 \times 150 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Worked example.**
Counterbalance forklift: $R_{tr} = 2000$ mm. Load: $L_{load} = 1200$ mm.

$$W_a = 2000 + 1200 + 300 = 3500 \text{ mm} = 3.5 \text{ m}$$

This is the **standard 3.5 m wide aisle** seen in conventional warehouses.

**Step 4 — Narrow-aisle variant.**
For a *reach truck* with $R_{tr} = 1400$ mm and the same load:
$$W_a = 1400 + 1200 + 300 = 2900 \text{ mm} = 2.9 \text{ m}$$

This explains why **reach trucks** are preferred for *narrow-aisle* (2.5 – 3.0 m) high-density storage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — The Wilson Formula

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the variables.**
- $D$ = Annual demand (units).
- $Q$ = Order quantity per order (units).
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹).
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹).

**Step 2 — Express the two cost components.**

Number of orders per year = $\dfrac{D}{Q}$.
Total ordering cost per year:
$$C_o = \dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$$

Average inventory = $\dfrac{Q}{2}$.
Total holding cost per year:
$$C_h = \dfrac{Q}{2} \times H$$

**Step 3 — Total annual inventory cost.**
$$TIC(Q) = C_o + C_h = \dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Minimize TIC with respect to Q (First derivative = 0).**
$$\dfrac{d(TIC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0$$

Rearrange:
$$\dfrac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} = \dfrac{H}{2}$$

$$Q^2 = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}$$

Take the square root:
$$EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Example.**
Annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. Ordering cost $S = ₹500$ per order. Holding cost $H = ₹20$ per unit per year.

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 500}{20}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10{,}000{,}000}{20}} = \sqrt{500{,}000} \approx 707 \text{ units}$$

**So the optimal order size is 707 units per order.**

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Safety Stock from Service Level

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Z-score for service level.**
For a 95% service level, $Z = 1.65$. For 99%, $Z = 2.33$. For 99.9%, $Z = 3.09$.

**Step 2 — Compute lead-time demand standard deviation.**
If daily demand has standard deviation $\sigma_d$ and lead time is $L$ days:
$$\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

**Step 3 — Safety Stock Formula.**
$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
Daily demand std. dev. $\sigma_d = 10$ units. Lead time $L = 9$ days. Service level = 95% ($Z = 1.65$).

$$\sigma_{LT} = 10 \times \sqrt{9} = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ units}$$

$$SS = 1.65 \times 30 = 49.5 \approx 50 \text{ units}$$

---

## 3.6 The KTU Analytical Matrix — Store Type Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **This 5-criterion decision matrix is the most important 7-mark "Part B (a)" answer format.**

| Decision Criterion | Centralized | Decentralized | Combined |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventory carrying cost** | ✅ Low | ❌ High | ✅ Medium |
| **Internal transport cost** | ❌ High | ✅ Low | ✅ Medium |
| **Control & accountability** | ✅ Strong | ❌ Weak | ✅ Strong |
| **Production delay risk** | ❌ High | ✅ Low | ✅ Low |
| **Hazardous material handling** | ❌ Poor | ✅ Excellent | ✅ Good |
| **Best plant size** | Small | Large | Medium–Large |
| **Capital investment** | ✅ Low | ❌ High | ✅ Medium |
| **Flexibility for expansion** | ❌ Low | ✅ High | ✅ High |

**Reading the matrix:** A ✅ means *advantage*; an ❌ means *disadvantage*. The *Combined* model wins on 6 of 8 criteria — this is the engineering proof that it is the most common model.

---

## 3.7 The KTU Analytical Matrix — Storage Equipment Selection

| Equipment | Space Utilization | Selectivity | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Selective Rack** | Low (35%) | 100% | Low | FMCG, e-commerce |
| **Drive-In Rack** | High (70%) | 33% | Low | Cold store, bulk |
| **Cantilever Rack** | Medium (50%) | 100% | Medium | Long items, pipes |
| **Push-Back Rack** | Medium (60%) | LIFO | Medium | Multi-SKU pallets |
| **Flow Rack** | High (65%) | FIFO | High | Perishables, dated items |
| **Mobile Rack** | Very High (80%) | 100% | High | Archives, slow movers |
| **AS/RS** | Very High (85%) | 100% | Very High | Pharma, e-commerce |
| **Mezzanine** | Doubles area | 100% | Medium | Spare parts, archives |
| **Carousel (H/V)** | Very High | 100% | High | Small parts picking |
| **Silo / Bunker** | Very High | LIFO | High | Cement, grain, coal |

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the matrix for an exam answer:** "If the question says *perishable goods, FIFO needed, high throughput* → the answer is **Flow Rack** or **AS/RS**."

```mermaid
    SUP[Supplier] -->|Goods Receipt Note| CMS[Central Main Store CMS]
    CMS -->|Inspection and GRN| INSP[Quality Inspection]
    INSP -->|Accept| CMS
    INSP -->|Reject| REJ[Rejected Material Store]
    CMS -->|Bulk Issue on Reorder| DSS1[Sub Store 1 Machine Shop]
    CMS -->|Bulk Issue on Reorder| DSS2[Sub Store 2 Assembly]
    CMS -->|Bulk Issue on Reorder| DSS3[Sub Store 3 Maintenance]
    DSS1 -->|Daily Issue on Indent| USR1[Operators]
    DSS2 -->|Daily Issue on Indent| USR2[Operators]
    DSS3 -->|Daily Issue on Indent| USR3[Maintenance Crew]
    USR1 -->|Return Scrap| SC[Salvage Store]
    USR2 -->|Return Scrap| SC
    USR3 -->|Return Scrap| SC
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Master Classification of Storage Equipment

```mermaid
graph TD
    SE[STORAGE EQUIPMENT] --> SE1[Racks and Shelving]
    SE --> SE2[Bins Drawers Cabinets]
    SE --> SE3[Pallets and Accessories]
    SE --> SE4[Mezzanines]
    SE --> SE5[Stacker Cranes and ASRS]
    SE --> SE6[Carousels]
    SE --> SE7[Conveyor Based Storage]
    SE --> SE8[Bulk Storage]

    SE1 --> SE1A[Selective Pallet Rack]
    SE1 --> SE1B[Drive In Drive Through Rack]
    SE1 --> SE1C[Cantilever Rack]
    SE1 --> SE1D[Push Back Rack]
    SE1 --> SE1E[Flow Rack FIFO]
    SE1 --> SE1F[Mobile Sliding Rack]
    SE1 --> SE1G[Shelving Boltless Rivet]

    SE2 --> SE2A[Plastic Bins]
    SE2 --> SE2B[Metal Bins]
    SE2 --> SE2C[Drawer Cabinets]
    SE2 --> SE2D[Locker Cabinets]

    SE3 --> SE3A[Wooden Pallets]
    SE3 --> SE3B[Plastic Pallets]
    SE3 --> SE3C[Metal Pallets]
    SE3 --> SE3D[Pallet Collars and Slip Sheets]

    SE5 --> SE5A[Unit Load ASRS]
    SE5 --> SE5B[Mini Load ASRS]
    SE5 --> SE5C[Vertical Lift Module VLM]

    SE6 --> SE6A[Horizontal Carousel]
    SE6 --> SE6B[Vertical Carousel]

    SE8 --> SE8A[Silos]
    SE8 --> SE8B[Bunkers]
    SE8 --> SE8C[Storage Tanks]
    SE8 --> SE8D[Hoppers]
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Two-Bin System Logic (Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Operator Needs Item X] --> Q1{Bin A Empty?}
    Q1 -->|No| USE1[Use from Bin A]
    USE1 --> STOP1[Stop]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Bin B Empty?}
    Q2 -->|No| USE2[Use from Bin B]
    USE2 --> RAISE[Raise Purchase Indent to Stores]
    RAISE --> REFILL[Refill Bin A from CMS]
    REFILL --> STOP2[Stop]
    Q2 -->|Yes| ALERT[Stock Out Emergency Action]
    ALERT --> STOP3[Stop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the *Two-Bin System* — a classic KTU 7-mark question.** It is the simplest, oldest, and most effective way to trigger a purchase order without any software.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Decision Tree for Choosing Store Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start New Plant Design] --> Q1{Plant Size?}
    Q1 -->|Small to Medium| Q2A{Item Variety High?}
    Q1 -->|Large| Q3{Hazardous Materials?}

    Q2A -->|No| CENT[Choose Centralized Store]
    Q2A -->|Yes| COMB1[Choose Combined Store]

    Q3 -->|Yes| DEC[Choose Decentralized Store]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Item Variety High?}
    Q4 -->|No| CENT
    Q4 -->|Yes| COMB2[Choose Combined Store]
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6 — Functional Architecture of an AS/RS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WMS[Warehouse Management System WMS] --> CTRL[ASRS Control Computer]
    CTRL --> CRANE[Stacker Crane]
    CRANE --> RACK1[Rack Bay A]
    CRANE --> RACK2[Rack Bay B]
    CRANE --> RACK3[Rack Bay C]
    CRANE --> IN[Inbound Station]
    CRANE --> OUT[Outbound Station]
    IN --> CONV1[Infeed Conveyor]
    OUT --> CONV2[Outfeed Conveyor]
    CONV1 --> PICK[Operator Pick Station]
    CONV2 --> DISP[Dispatch Bay]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For the board exam:** The KTU examiner will award full 7 marks if you draw a *block diagram* showing: **WMS → Controller → Crane → Racks → In/Out Stations → Operator**. You don't need the exact symbols — a clean, labelled block diagram is enough.

---

## 4.7 Diagram 7 — Block Diagram of Warehouse Layout Zones

```mermaid
graph LR
    Z1[Zone 1 Receiving Dock] --> Z2[Zone 2 Incoming Inspection]
    Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3 Quarantine Store]
    Z2 --> Z4[Zone 4 Accepted Material Central Store]
    Z4 --> Z5[Zone 5 Sub Stores Near User Departments]
    Z5 --> Z6[Zone 6 User Department Production Line]
    Z6 --> Z7[Zone 7 Finished Goods Store]
    Z7 --> Z8[Zone 8 Dispatch Bay]
    Z4 --> Z9[Zone 9 Rejected and Salvage Store]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This 9-zone warehouse layout is the KTU "model answer"** for any question that asks "Draw a block diagram of a typical industrial store."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are **simulated in the exact KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**:
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (no choice, answer all).
> - **Part B (Module 2):** 1 question × 14 marks with internal choice (a or b sub-parts of 7 marks each).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define a "centralized store". List any four advantages of a centralized store over a decentralized store.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A centralized store is a single, large storehouse usually located at the center of the plant, from which all departments receive their material issues.

**Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Lower inventory value** — bulk purchasing and single-point control.
2. **Better control over pilferage, wastage, and obsolescence.**
3. **Economical in store-keeping staff** — one expert team handles all materials.
4. **Easier physical verification and annual stock-taking.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *describe* the advantages without giving the *definition first*. Always structure: **Definition → Diagram (optional but impressive) → Bulleted advantages**. This fetches the 1 "impression" mark that decides between A and A+.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between "selective pallet rack" and "drive-in rack" on the basis of (a) space utilization, (b) selectivity, and (c) FIFO/LIFO capability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

| Parameter | Selective Pallet Rack | Drive-In Rack |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Space Utilization** | Low (~35% of floor) | High (~70% of floor) |
| **(b) Selectivity** | 100% — every pallet is directly accessible | Limited (~33%) — only front pallets accessible |
| **(c) FIFO/LIFO** | Both FIFO and LIFO possible | LIFO (Drive-In) or FIFO (Drive-Through) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "selective rack is *better* than drive-in rack". This is *not* always true — it depends on the application. The correct answer is "selective rack is better for *variety*, drive-in is better for *density of same SKU*."

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions for a 14-mark slot follow this exact internal-choice pattern:
> - **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks** = 14 marks
> - You must answer **either (a) OR (b)**, not both.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Question A (a): [7 Marks]**
*Explain the different types of stores based on **location**. Compare centralized and decentralized stores on the basis of any five parameters. Which type is most commonly used in medium-to-large industries and why?*

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Industrial stores are classified by location into three types: **Centralized**, **Decentralized**, and **Combined Centralized & Decentralized**.

**Type 1 — Centralized Store (1 mark):**
A single, large storehouse at the center of the plant, serving all departments.

**Type 2 — Decentralized Store (1 mark):**
Multiple sub-stores located near or within the consuming departments.

**Type 3 — Combined Store (1 mark):**
A main central store with departmental sub-stores — the most common model in real industries.

**Comparison Table (2 marks — 0.4 each):**

| Parameter | Centralized | Decentralized |
|---|---|---|
| Inventory carrying cost | Lower | Higher |
| Internal transport cost | Higher | Lower |
| Control & accountability | Strong | Weaker |
| Hazard handling | Poor | Excellent |
| Best plant size | Small | Large |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The **Combined Store** is most commonly used in medium-to-large industries because it *balances* the low inventory cost of centralization with the low transport cost of decentralization, while supporting hazardous material isolation in sub-stores.

**Diagram (1 mark — for impression):**
Draw the flow: **Supplier → Central Store → Sub-Store → User Department**.

---

**Question A (b): [7 Marks]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an Automated Storage and Retrieval System (AS/RS)**. List **four advantages** and **two limitations** of an AS/RS.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
An AS/RS is a computer-controlled combination of racks, stacker cranes, conveyors, and WMS software that automatically stores and retrieves loads with minimum human intervention.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
WMS → Control Computer → Stacker Crane → Racks (Bay A, B, C) → Inbound/Outbound Stations → Operator Pick/Dispatch.

**Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Maximum space utilization** (up to 85% of cube).
2. **Higher accuracy** (>99.9%) due to barcode/RFID integration.
3. **Higher throughput** (200 – 500 cycles/hr).
4. **Improved safety** — no forklifts inside aisles.

**Two Limitations (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Very high capital cost** (~₹ 5 – 50 crore depending on size).
2. **High maintenance cost and dependence on power** — any breakdown halts the system.

**Application (1 mark):**
Used in pharmaceutical warehouses, e-commerce fulfilment, cold-chain, and automotive spare-parts centres.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted — Alternative Choice]**

**Question B (a): [7 Marks]**
*Classify **storage equipment** into eight master categories with **two examples** under each category. Explain **cantilever rack** and **flow rack** with diagrams and applications.*

**Model Answer:**

**Eight Categories (4 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Racks and Shelving — Selective rack, Cantilever rack
2. Bins and Drawers — Plastic bin, Drawer cabinet
3. Pallets — Wooden pallet, Plastic pallet
4. Mezzanines — Structural, Rack-supported
5. Stacker Cranes / AS/RS — Unit-load, Mini-load
6. Carousels — Horizontal, Vertical
7. Conveyors — Roller, Chain
8. Bulk Storage — Silo, Bunker

**Cantilever Rack (1.5 marks):**
A rack with **arms projecting horizontally** from a vertical column. **No front vertical obstruction** — ideal for **long items** like pipes, timber, rods. Length of stored item can be 3 – 12 m.

**Flow Rack (1.5 marks):**
A rack with **inclined roller lanes**; pallets roll forward by **gravity** to the picking face. **FIFO logic** is automatically achieved. **Use case:** perishable goods, expiry-dated items, FMCG.

---

**Question B (b): [7 Marks]**
*A manufacturing company needs to store **1500 pallets** of raw material. The pallet size is **1.2 m × 1.0 m × 1.4 m**. The store has a **clear height of 7 m**, and the **utilization factor is 0.8**. Compute the **floor area** and **number of rack bays** required if selective pallet rack is used. Assume each bay holds **3 pallets per beam level** and **3 beam levels**.*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Footprint of one pallet (0.5 mark):**
$$P_s = 1.2 \times 1.0 = 1.2 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total footprint (0.5 mark):**
$$A_{total} = 1500 \times 1.2 = 1800 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Adjust for utilization factor (0.5 mark):**
$$A_{floor} = \dfrac{1800}{0.8} = 2250 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Account for vertical stacking (0.5 mark):**
Each pallet is 1.4 m high. Clear height 7 m allows **3 levels** (since $3 \times 1.4 = 4.2$ m ≤ 7 m, with 2.8 m spare for sprinklers/clearance).
Hence pallets per footprint position = 3.
$$A_{reduced} = \dfrac{2250}{3} = 750 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total rack positions (0.5 mark):**
If 3 beam levels × 3 pallets per level = **9 pallets per bay**.
$$\text{Bays} = \dfrac{1500}{9} = 166.67 \approx 167 \text{ bays}$$

**Step 6 — Rack length (0.5 mark):**
If each bay is 2.7 m long (standard):
$$L_{rack} = 167 \times 2.7 = 450.9 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Rack height (0.5 mark):**
$$H_{rack} = 3 \times 1.4 + \text{clearance} \approx 4.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Aisle allowance (0.5 mark):**
Assuming 2 aisles of 3.5 m each:
$$A_{aisle} = 2 \times 3.5 \times 30 \text{ (assumed rack depth)} = 210 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final Total Floor Area:**
$$A_{total,final} = 750 + 210 = 960 \text{ m}^2$$

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
A **167-bay selective pallet rack** of length **451 m** and total floor area **~960 m²** is required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the utilization factor $K_u$** — loses 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up the bay count** — must round *up* to the next integer, not down.
> 3. **Missing the unit conversion** (mm to m, m² to ft² if asked).
> 4. **Not showing all substitution steps** — even if the final answer is correct, *every step must be shown* for full marks.
> 5. **Mixing up FIFO and LIFO** — *Cantilever is FIFO; Drive-In is LIFO; Flow is FIFO; Push-Back is LIFO.*

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section 3 times the night before the exam — it is your "last 7 marks" insurance.**

- [ ] **A "store" is a system, not a room** — it includes layout, equipment, documentation, and people.
- [ ] **Three master types of stores:** Centralized, Decentralized, Combined (most common).
- [ ] **Centralized →** low inventory cost, high transport cost.
- [ ] **Decentralized →** high inventory cost, low transport cost, good for hazardous materials.
- [ ] **Combined →** balances both; the KTU model answer for medium-to-large plants.
- [ ] **Other store types:** Perpetual, Buffer, Transit, Quarantine, Bonded, Cold, Salvage, Rejected.
- [ ] **Standard pallet:** 1200 × 1000 mm (EU) or 1219 × 1016 mm (US).
- [ ] **Standard aisle:** 2.5 – 3.0 m (narrow), 3.5 m (counterbalance forklift).
- [ ] **8 categories of storage equipment:** Racks, Bins, Pallets, Mezzanines, AS/RS, Carousels, Conveyors, Bulk Storage.
- [ ] **Selective Rack** = 100% selectivity, low density.
- [ ] **Drive-In Rack** = high density, LIFO.
- [ ] **Cantilever Rack** = long items (pipes, timber, rods).
- [ ] **Flow Rack** = gravity-driven, automatic FIFO.
- [ ] **Mobile Rack** = rack moves on rails, 80% space utilization.
- [ ] **AS/RS** = highest space utilization (85%), highest cost, highest throughput.
- [ ] **Carousels:** Horizontal (for small parts), Vertical (for slow-moving items).
- [ ] **Bulk storage:** Silos (LIFO), Hoppers (FIFO via screw), Tanks (liquids), Bunkers (coal, ore).
- [ ] **EOQ formula:** $EOQ = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — the most-tested formula in this module.
- [ ] **Safety Stock:** $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$, with $Z = 1.65$ for 95% service.
- [ ] **ROL = Max Lead Time Demand + Safety Stock.**
- [ ] **Max Stock = ROL + EOQ − Min Stock.**
- [ ] **Floor area formula:** $A = (N \times P_s \times h_p) / (K_u \times S_a)$.
- [ ] **Aisle width formula:** $W_a = R_{tr} + L_{load} + 300$ mm.
- [ ] **Two-Bin System:** Use Bin A; when empty, use Bin B and raise an indent. The simplest, software-free reorder trigger.
- [ ] **Six-factor equipment selection:** Material, throughput, density, selectivity, cost, MHE compatibility.
- [ ] **Industry mapping:** Auto = Selective + AS/RS; Pharma = VLM + Cold; E-com = Carousels + AGV-fed racks; Steel = Cantilever + Heavy racks; Cement = Silo.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Day Mantra:**
> 1. *Always* start the 7-mark answer with a **definition** (1 mark free).
> 2. *Always* support the answer with a **block diagram or table** (1 mark free).
> 3. *Always* use **KTU 2024 terminology** — "AS/RS", "Combined Store", "Selective Pallet Rack", etc.
> 4. *Always* close with an **application or industry example** (1 mark free).
> 5. *Never* use the word *"godown"* — it is unprofessional and loses impression marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preservation of Stores and Stock Verification
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2 — PRESERVATION OF STORES & STOCK VERIFICATION

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Preservation of Stores and Stock Verification

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Preservation of Stores — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preservation of Stores** is defined as the systematic application of preventive, protective, and corrective measures undertaken to retain stores, materials, and components in a usable, serviceable, and saleable condition throughout their entire storage life-cycle, from the moment of receipt until final issue or disposal.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, preservation falls under the *Materials Management* function, specifically the **Storage and Warehousing** sub-function. It is a science-cum-art that combines **physics** (humidity, temperature), **chemistry** (corrosion, oxidation), and **management science** (procedural controls, audits).

### 1.2 Stock Verification — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stock Verification** (also called *Stock Taking* or *Inventory Audit*) is the formal physical or administrative examination of all recorded stock items against actual physical balances, conducted to confirm the existence, quantity, quality, and condition of materials held in stores, and to identify and report discrepancies.

It is a statutory and managerial control mechanism ensuring that the **bin card balance**, **store ledger balance**, and **physical stock** all reconcile to within acceptable tolerance limits.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Pharmacy (Preservation) and an Auditor (Stock Verification).**

Imagine you are running a small chemist shop at home. You stock paracetamol, syrups, bandages, and cotton. **Preservation** is what you do so that the syrup does not ferment in summer, the cotton does not absorb moisture, the tablets do not crumble, and rats do not chew through cardboard. You put silica gel packets, refrigerate insulin, keep tablets away from sunlight, and clean shelves monthly.

Now imagine every **quarter**, the Income Tax officer comes, opens every bottle, counts every strip, weighs bulk medicines, and checks whether your written inventory register matches the physical count. That independent count is **Stock Verification**. If you have written 100 strips but only 96 are physically present, that's a **shortage of 4**, and you must explain why.

- **Preservation = Protection (avoid loss)**
- **Stock Verification = Detection (record actual loss)**

Both functions are complementary halves of the same objective: **asset integrity**.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used in This Topic

The following bolded metrics and constants are universally applied in KTU examination answers and industrial practice:

- **Acceptable Shrinkage Limit:** generally **2% to 5%** depending on item class (lower for high-value items).
- **Standard Storage Temperature:** **15 °C to 25 °C** for general dry goods; **2 °C to 8 °C** for pharmaceuticals.
- **Relative Humidity (RH):** ideally **40% to 60%** for metal stores to arrest corrosion.
- **Standard Storage Life (Shelf Life):** specified by manufacturer on a Material Safety Data Sheet (**MSDS**).
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR):** **> 6 cycles/year** for healthy FMCG; **< 2** for slow-moving capital items.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Objectives of Preservation

Preservation is undertaken to fulfil **five primary objectives**, each of which has direct bearing on KTU module-level questions:

1. **Maintain functional usability** — the item must work when issued, not just exist on paper.
2. **Prevent deterioration** — arrest physical, chemical, and biological degradation.
3. **Avoid financial loss** — damaged stock = write-off = capital erosion.
4. **Ensure safety** — flammable, toxic, or radioactive items must never leak, combust, or escape containment.
5. **Comply with statutory norms** — Factories Act 1948, Indian Explosives Act, Environmental Protection Act, ISO 9001:2015, ISO 14001.

### 2.2 Classification of Preservation Techniques

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus groups preservation techniques into **three operational tiers** — Environmental, Chemical, and Procedural.

#### 2.2.1 Environmental Preservation (Level 1)
Controls the **storage micro-climate** itself:

| Parameter | Target Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | 15–25 °C (general) | All dry stores |
| **Relative Humidity** | 40–60% | Ferrous metals |
| **Light** | Dark / UV-filtered | Rubber, polymers, photo chemicals |
| **Ventilation** | 6–10 air changes/hr | Solvents, paints |

#### 2.2.2 Chemical Preservation (Level 2)
Active coatings or atmospheres applied to the item:

- **Temporary rust preventives:** grease, lanolin, petroleum jelly, VPI (Vapour Phase Inhibitor) paper.
- **Desiccants:** silica gel, activated alumina, molecular sieves.
- **Inert gas blanketing:** Nitrogen (N₂) or Argon (Ar) for reactive metals.
- **Fungicides / Bactericides** for leather, jute, textiles.
- **Cathodic protection** for underground metallic pipelines (sacrificial anode method).

#### 2.2.3 Procedural Preservation (Level 3)
Administrative and housekeeping controls:

- **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) and **FEFO** (First-Expired-First-Out) issue discipline.
- **Periodic inspection schedules** — daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly.
- **First-Aid and Fire-Fighting readiness.**
- **Pest control (fumigation)** — quarterly pest-control treatment.
- **5S / 7S methodology** for housekeeping.
- **Stacking rules** — height, spacing, FIFO lanes.

### 2.3 Causes of Store Deterioration

```
Physical  →  Impact, vibration, load, abrasion, fracture.
Chemical  →  Oxidation, corrosion, hydrolysis, polymerization.
Biological →  Fungi, bacteria, insects, rodents.
Thermal   →  Heat damage, cold embrittlement.
Radiation →  UV degradation, sunlight fading.
Electrical →  Static discharge, leakage currents, capacitor ageing.
```

### 2.4 Stock Verification — Detailed Taxonomy

#### 2.4.1 Types of Stock Verification (Periodic Basis)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the difference between **Periodic**, **Continuous**, and **Perpetual** verification. Memorise the table below verbatim for Part-A questions.

| Type | When Conducted | Who Conducts | Scope | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Periodic Verification** | Year-end / quarter-end | Internal storekeeper | All items once a year | Annual / Quarterly |
| **Continuous Verification** | Throughout the year | Internal storekeeper | Rolling sub-set of items | Daily / weekly cycle |
| **Perpetual Verification** | After every receipt/issue | Automated (ERP / WMS) | Real-time | Continuous |
| **Annual / Surprise Audit** | Announced / Unannounced | External auditor | Random statistical sample | Annual |

#### 2.4.2 Methods of Physical Counting

1. **Direct counting** — small, countable items (nuts, bolts, fuses).
2. **Weighing & conversion** — bulk items (coal, cement, grain).
3. **Measuring** — length, area, volume (cables, pipes, cloth).
4. **Tank dipping / calibration** — liquids (oil, fuel).
5. **Sampling inspection** — for huge lots, statistically valid samples (AQL tables).

#### 2.4.3 Discrepancy Classification

A discrepancy is the **variance between book stock and physical stock**:

- **Surplus (Excess):** Physical stock > Book stock.
- **Shortage (Deficit):** Physical stock < Book stock.
- **Unserviceable:** Physically present but unfit for use.
- **Obsolete:** Technically usable but superseded by new technology.
- **Deteriorated:** Quality degraded below specification.

#### 2.4.4 Board of Survey

A **Board of Survey** is a formal committee constituted under store rules (Government of India Stores Rules; defence rules; CVC guidelines) to:

- Examine discrepancies.
- Classify loss as *normal* (acceptable shrinkage), *unusual* (needs investigation), or *negligent* (employee liability).
- Recommend write-off, recovery, or disciplinary action.

The board typically comprises **3 members**: a Chairman (not from stores), one member from the user department, and one from the finance/accounts wing.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every numerical problem on stock verification will require one or more of the following six formulas. Tabulate them in your answer sheet during the exam.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shrinkage Percentage** | $S\% = \dfrac{\text{Book Stock} - \text{Physical Stock}}{\text{Book Stock}} \times 100$ | All quantities in same UoM | Detects loss between audits |
| 2 | **Accuracy Percentage** | $A\% = \dfrac{\text{Physical Stock}}{\text{Book Stock}} \times 100$ | % | Quality of record-keeping |
| 3 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** | $\text{ITR} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Materials Consumed}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}}$ | ratio / year | Stock velocity check |
| 4 | **Average Inventory** | $I_{avg} = \dfrac{I_{opening} + I_{closing}}{2}$ | ₹ or units | Used in ITR denominator |
| 5 | **Days of Stock (DOS)** | $\text{DOS} = \dfrac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{Daily Consumption}}$ | days | Stock-out prediction |
| 6 | **Sample size (statistical)** | $n = \dfrac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot q}{e^{2}}$ | Z=1.96, p≈0.5, e=tolerance | Audit sample design |
| 7 | **Obsolete Stock %** | $O\% = \dfrac{\text{Obsolete Value}}{\text{Total Stock Value}} \times 100$ | % | Dead-stock indicator |
| 8 | **Carrying Cost Ratio** | $C_c = \dfrac{\text{Total Carrying Cost}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}} \times 100$ | % | Storage burden |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a manufacturing enterprise, every **₹1** of inventory blocked represents **₹0.20 to ₹0.30 per year** of carrying cost (capital, insurance, depreciation, obsolescence). Poor preservation can convert a **₹100 crore** inventory into **₹70 crore** of usable stock in just 18 months. Similarly, a single unverified stock line worth **₹50 lakh** that is silently lost becomes a direct hit to profit. This is why **PEPET414** treats both topics as the *nerve-centre* of materials management.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Shrinkage Calculation (KTU Numerical Standard)

**Problem:**
A factory's bin card for Item X (M-12 bolts) records an opening balance of **12,500** units and receipts of **2,500** units during the quarter. Issues total **3,000** units. Physical stock verification at quarter-end reveals an actual count of **11,800** units.

**Find:**
- (a) Book closing stock
- (b) Shrinkage in units
- (c) Shrinkage percentage
- (d) Accuracy percentage
- (e) Comment on whether the loss falls in the acceptable band (assume acceptable = ≤ 2%).

#### Step (a) — Book Closing Stock

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book Closing Stock} &= \text{Opening Balance} + \text{Receipts} - \text{Issues} \\
&= 12{,}500 + 2{,}500 - 3{,}000 \\
&= 12{,}000 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (b) — Shrinkage in Units

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shrinkage}_{\text{units}} &= \text{Book Closing} - \text{Physical Closing} \\
&= 12{,}000 - 11{,}800 \\
&= 200 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (c) — Shrinkage Percentage

$$
\begin{aligned}
S\% &= \dfrac{\text{Book Stock} - \text{Physical Stock}}{\text{Book Stock}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{12{,}000 - 11{,}800}{12{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{200}{12{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 1.6667\% \approx 1.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (d) — Accuracy Percentage

$$
\begin{aligned}
A\% &= \dfrac{\text{Physical Stock}}{\text{Book Stock}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{11{,}800}{12{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 98.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (e) — Examiner's Verdict

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Calculated } S\% &= 1.67\% \\
\text{Acceptable limit} &= 2.00\% \\
\therefore \text{Loss is within tolerance — class as NORMAL shrinkage.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check logic:** $S\% + A\% = 1.67\% + 98.33\% = 100.00\%$ ✓ (This algebraic identity is a useful self-verification tool in the exam hall.)

---

### 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — Inventory Turnover & Days of Stock

**Problem:**
A manufacturing plant has the following data for FY 2023–24:
- Opening inventory value = **₹ 80 lakh**
- Closing inventory value = **₹ 120 lakh**
- Total cost of materials consumed during the year = **₹ 600 lakh**

**Find:** (a) Average inventory, (b) Inventory Turnover Ratio, (c) Days of Stock available.

#### Step (a) — Average Inventory

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= \dfrac{I_{opening} + I_{closing}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{80 + 120}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{200}{2} \\
&= \text{₹ } 100 \text{ lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (b) — Inventory Turnover Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ITR} &= \dfrac{\text{Cost of Materials Consumed}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}} \\
&= \dfrac{600}{100} \\
&= 6.0 \text{ turns per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (c) — Days of Stock

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily Consumption} &= \dfrac{\text{Annual Consumption}}{365} \\
&= \dfrac{600}{365} \\
&= \text{₹ } 1.6438 \text{ lakh / day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOS} &= \dfrac{I_{avg}}{\text{Daily Consumption}} \\
&= \dfrac{100}{1.6438} \\
&= 60.83 \text{ days} \\
&\approx 61 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A turnover of **6.0** with a **61-day DOS** is healthy for a mid-sized engineering industry. Anything below 4 turns/year or above 90 days DOS indicates accumulation, which is a *trigger for re-examining preservation needs and dead-stock write-off*.

---

### 3.3 Worked Problem 3 — Discrepancy Classification Decision Matrix

A Board of Survey is convened with the following five items of discrepancy:

| Item | Book Qty | Phys Qty | Item Value (₹) | Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1,000 | 980 | 10 | Standard nuts |
| B | 500 | 502 | 5,000 | Bearings |
| C | 200 | 150 | 8,000 | Hydraulic seals |
| D | 100 | 100 | 12,000 | PLC cards (damp) |
| E | 80 | 80 | 25,000 | Phased-out motors |

**Classify each** using a decision matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Surplus} &\Rightarrow \text{Phys} > \text{Book} \\
\text{Shortage} &\Rightarrow \text{Phys} < \text{Book} \\
\text{Unserviceable} &\Rightarrow \text{Quality fails} \\
\text{Obsolete} &\Rightarrow \text{Superseded technology}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Item | Result | Classification | Board Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | –20 units (2.0%) | Normal shortage | Write-off (within tolerance) |
| B | +2 units (0.4%) | Normal surplus | Adjust bin card upward |
| C | –50 units (25%) | **Unusual shortage** | Investigate, recover from custodian |
| D | 0 units but damp | **Unserviceable** | Write-off, root-cause humidity audit |
| E | 0 units but obsolete | **Obsolete** | Declare dead stock, scrap-sale |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU valuation point: Notice that **D** has zero variance in quantity, yet is still a discrepancy. Many students incorrectly classify "no-quantity-variance" items as "no discrepancy". **Quality** and **technology** obsolescence also constitute valid discrepancies — make sure to state this explicitly in your answer.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Preservation Decision Table

The following master matrix is the **single most useful exam artefact** for KTU Part-B answers on this topic. Memorise the structure:

| Item Category | Primary Threat | Preservation Method | Inspection Frequency | Verification Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ferrous metals | Rust / corrosion | Oil coating + VPI paper | Monthly | Visual + weight |
| Non-ferrous metals | Oxidation | Desiccant + sealed pack | Quarterly | Visual + chemical |
| Rubber goods | Hardening, UV damage | Talc dusting, dark storage | Monthly | Flexibility test |
| Electrical / Electronics | Humidity, ESD | Silica gel + ESD bag | Quarterly | Power-on test |
| Food / Pharma | Microbial, expiry | Cold chain (2–8 °C) | Daily | FIFO/FEFO + sensor |
| Lubricants | Contamination, water | Sealed drums, breather | Quarterly | Lab test |
| Textiles | Moth, fungi | Naphthalene, dry storage | Half-yearly | Visual |
| Chemicals / Solvents | Fire, evaporation | Bonded store, fire vault | Weekly | Dip test |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Preservation & Verification Loop

```mermaid
    T --> D
```

```mermaid
    S3 --> S4[Physical Count by Teams]

    S8 --> S15
    S15 --> S16[Submission to Auditor / Management]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Preservation Sub-System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENV[Environmental Control Layer]
        E1[Temperature 15-25 C]
        E2[Humidity 40-60 percent]

    ENV --> ITEM[Store Item]
```
