<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define stock verification. List any four objectives of stock verification.**

**Model Answer:**
> Stock verification is the formal physical examination of all stores items to confirm their existence, quantity, and condition against the recorded balances in bin cards and store ledgers. It is conducted periodically or continuously to detect discrepancies and ensure accuracy of inventory records.

The four primary objectives are:

1. **To confirm physical existence** of all recorded items.
2. **To detect shortages, surpluses, and losses** of materials.
3. **To identify obsolete, unserviceable, and deteriorated stock** for timely write-off.
4. **To ensure accuracy** of bin cards, store ledgers, and accounting records.
5. **To fix responsibility** for any loss or discrepancy on the custodian.

*(Any four valid points — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is meant by preservation of stores? State any three methods used to preserve metals in store.**

**Model Answer:**
> Preservation of stores refers to the application of preventive measures to protect materials in storage from deterioration due to environmental, chemical, biological, or physical causes, so that they remain in usable condition until issue.

Three preservation methods for metals:

1. **Application of rust-preventive coatings** — grease, lanolin, petroleum jelly, or temporary rust-preventive oils.
2. **Use of VPI (Vapour Phase Inhibitor) paper** in packing to release corrosion-inhibiting molecules inside sealed packs.
3. **Control of relative humidity** (40–60%) and use of **silica-gel desiccants** inside storage cabinets.
4. **Cathodic protection** (sacrificial zinc anodes) for large metallic pipelines or buried structures.

*(Any three — full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain in detail the various methods and techniques used for the preservation of stores in an industrial warehouse. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Preservation of stores is the systematic application of protective measures to maintain materials in serviceable condition throughout storage. The methods can be grouped into three categories:

**1. Environmental Control (Physical Preservation):**

- **Temperature control:** Storage areas should be maintained at **15–25 °C** for general goods, **2–8 °C** for pharmaceuticals and perishable chemicals. Freezing and overheating both degrade materials.
- **Humidity control:** Relative humidity must be kept between **40–60%** for metal stores to prevent oxidation. Air-conditioning, dehumidifiers, and silica-gel desiccants are deployed.
- **Lighting:** Direct sunlight fades textiles, embrittles rubber, and degrades photo-sensitive chemicals. UV-filtered lighting or opaque storage is mandatory.
- **Ventilation:** Adequate air exchange (6–10 air changes/hour) prevents accumulation of solvent vapours and removes heat from electrical stores.

**2. Chemical Preservation:**

- **Temporary coatings:** Application of rust-preventive oils (RP oils like RP-3, RP-7) on ferrous items before long-term storage.
- **VPI (Vapour Phase Inhibitor) paper:** Wraps parts in paper that sublimates inhibitor molecules, saturating the enclosed air space.
- **Desiccants:** Silica gel (most common), activated alumina, and molecular sieves absorb moisture inside sealed containers.
- **Inert gas blanketing:** Nitrogen or Argon atmosphere inside sealed tanks for highly reactive metals (sodium, lithium, titanium alloys).
- **Fungicides and bactericides:** Naphthalene balls for textiles, copper-8-quinolinolate for leather and jute.

**3. Procedural Preservation (Administrative Controls):**

- **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** issue discipline, or **FEFO (First-Expired-First-Out)** for perishable goods.
- **Stack-height and spacing rules** to prevent load damage and enable airflow.
- **Periodic inspection schedules** — daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly — with checklists.
- **Pest control (fumigation)** every quarter with approved chemicals.
- **5S / 7S housekeeping** — Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain (plus Safety, Spirit).
- **Fire prevention** — fire extinguishers, fire alarms, no-smoking zones, bonded stores for flammable liquids.

**Conclusion:** A robust preservation programme combines all three layers. **No single method is sufficient**; protection is achieved only by integrating environmental, chemical, and procedural controls.

---

**(b) Discuss the procedure for conducting stock verification in a large engineering store. Explain the role and constitution of the Board of Survey. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stock Verification Procedure:**

**Step 1 — Authorization:** A formal verification order is issued by competent authority (Head of Materials / Plant Head) specifying the scope, period, and team.

**Step 2 — Cut-off Date:** All receipts and issues are frozen from a cut-off date. No physical movement of stock is allowed during counting.

**Step 3 — Team Constitution:** Verification teams (typically 2 per team — one counts, one records) are deployed. They must be independent of the storekeeper.

**Step 4 — Physical Counting:** Items are physically counted, weighed, or measured. Results are entered on pre-printed **Stock Verification Sheets**, not on bin cards.

**Step 5 — Reconciliation:** Physical counts are matched against **Bin Card balances** and **Store Ledger balances**. Variance reports are generated.

**Step 6 — Discrepancy Classification:** Each variance is classified as **Surplus**, **Shortage**, **Unserviceable**, or **Obsolete**.

**Step 7 — Board of Survey:** Items with significant variance or quality issues are referred to a Board of Survey.

**Step 8 — Approval and Adjustment:** Based on Board of Survey recommendations, the competent authority approves write-off, recovery from custodian, or transfer to scrap-sale.

**Step 9 — Reconciliation Report:** A final report is submitted to management and statutory auditors.

---

**Constitution of the Board of Survey:**

A Board of Survey is a **3-member committee** constituted under store rules (e.g., Government of India General Financial Rules, CVC guidelines, ISO audit norms). The composition is:

| # | Member | Typical Designation | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Chairman** | Officer not below Manager rank, *not from Stores* | Conducts meetings, signs report |
| 2 | **Member 1** | Representative from User / Production department | Technical assessment of usability |
| 3 | **Member 2** | Representative from Finance / Accounts | Financial impact & book adjustments |

The Board examines each discrepancy and recommends one of four actions:

- **Write-off** — accept the loss as normal shrinkage.
- **Recovery** — fix liability on custodian and recover cost.
- **Disciplinary action** — in case of negligence, fraud, or misconduct.
- **Re-classification** — move to obsolete, surplus, or scrap category.

The Board's findings are recorded in the **Board of Survey Register** and become part of the annual stock verification report.

---

#### **OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [Alternate Choice for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Define the following terms with examples: (i) Surplus stock, (ii) Shortage stock, (iii) Unserviceable stock, (iv) Obsolete stock. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Surplus Stock** | Stock whose physical quantity exceeds the recorded book balance. May arise from incorrect entries, double-receipts, or pilferage detected by over-counting. | Book = 100, Actual = 110 → 10 units surplus |
| **Shortage Stock** | Stock whose physical quantity is less than the recorded book balance. May arise from pilferage, evaporation, evaporation loss, mis-issue, or accounting error. | Book = 500, Actual = 480 → 20 units shortage |
| **Unserviceable Stock** | Stock that is physically present in full quantity but whose quality has degraded below usable specifications. Requires write-off. | 100 PLC cards fully present, but damp-damaged beyond repair |
| **Obsolete Stock** | Stock that is technically usable but has been superseded by newer technology, design, or standards, and has no foreseeable demand. | Stock of phased-out 486-processor motherboards still in store |

**Additional classification notes:**

- **Deteriorated stock** is a sub-category of unserviceable — quality has degraded but might be salvageable after reprocessing.
- **Dead stock** is a sub-category of obsolete — no usage, no scrap value.
- **Surplus vs Shortage** are *quantity discrepancies*; **Unserviceable vs Obsolete** are *quality / utility discrepancies*. Both categories must be tracked separately in KTU exam answers.

**Board of Survey action matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Surplus} &\rightarrow \text{Adjust book upward, transfer to active inventory} \\
\text{Shortage} &\rightarrow \text{Investigate, normalise, recover, or write-off} \\
\text{Unserviceable} &\rightarrow \text{Write-off, scrap sale, root-cause analysis} \\
\text{Obsolete} &\rightarrow \text{Tender sale, scrap, or hold for emergency use}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) A bin card for item 'M-12 Hex Bolt' shows opening balance 8,000, receipts 4,500, issues 5,000. Physical verification reveals 7,300 units. Compute (i) Book closing stock, (ii) Shrinkage in units, (iii) Shrinkage percentage, (iv) Accuracy percentage. If acceptable shrinkage is 3%, classify the loss. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Opening Balance} &= 8{,}000 \text{ units} \\
\text{Receipts} &= 4{,}500 \text{ units} \\
\text{Issues} &= 5{,}000 \text{ units} \\
\text{Physical Stock at Verification} &= 7{,}300 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Book Closing Stock:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book Closing} &= \text{Opening} + \text{Receipts} - \text{Issues} \\
&= 8{,}000 + 4{,}500 - 5{,}000 \\
&= 7{,}500 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Shrinkage in Units:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shrinkage} &= \text{Book Closing} - \text{Physical Closing} \\
&= 7{,}500 - 7{,}300 \\
&= 200 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Shrinkage Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S\% &= \dfrac{7{,}500 - 7{,}300}{7{,}500} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{200}{7{,}500} \times 100 \\
&= 2.6667\% \approx 2.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Accuracy Percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A\% &= \dfrac{7{,}300}{7{,}500} \times 100 \\
&= 97.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cross-check:** $S\% + A\% = 2.67\% + 97.33\% = 100.00\%$ ✓

**Classification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Calculated Shrinkage } S\% &= 2.67\% \\
\text{Acceptable Limit} &= 3.00\% \\
\therefore \; S\% &< \text{Limit} \\
\therefore \text{Loss is NORMAL — within tolerance band.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Examiner's Recommendation:** The storekeeper need not be penalised. However, a **root-cause investigation** should still be initiated to determine if the 2.67% loss can be reduced below 1% through better preservation (e.g., sealed bins, rust-preventive coatings, FIFO discipline).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Shrinkage % with Accuracy %.** Remember: $S\% + A\% = 100\%$. If you write $A\% = 98.33\%$ when $S\% = 1.67\%$, you are correct. If you accidentally invert, you lose the full 7 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in shrinkage calculation.** Always write "**200 units**" — not just "200". Examiners deduct marks for missing UoM.
> 3. **Writing "no discrepancy" for zero-quantity-variance items** that are unserviceable or obsolete. **Quality and technology obsolescence are also discrepancies.**
> 4. **Not mentioning the Board of Survey.** In any 14-mark question on stock verification, the answer is incomplete without explaining the Board's constitution and role. A 1-mark deduction is automatic if omitted.
> 5. **Mixing FIFO with FEFO.** Use FIFO for non-perishables, **FEFO for perishables and shelf-life items.** Examiners are strict about this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping preservation sub-categories.** When asked "explain preservation methods," always cover **Environmental + Chemical + Procedural** — three-tier answer = full marks. One-tier answer = at best 4/7.
> 7. **Ignoring the conservation-of-stock identity:** $\text{Opening} + \text{Receipts} - \text{Issues} = \text{Closing}$. Use it as a cross-check to demonstrate to the examiner that you have verified your arithmetic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Memorise before entering the exam hall.**

**On Preservation:**

- Preservation = protection against **physical, chemical, biological, thermal, electrical, and radiation** damage.
- Three layers: **Environmental, Chemical, Procedural** — never answer with just one.
- Standard **RH band 40–60%** for metals; **15–25 °C** for general storage; **2–8 °C** for perishables.
- **VPI paper, rust-preventive oils, silica gel, nitrogen blanketing** are the four chemical workhorses.
- **FIFO** for non-perishables; **FEFO** for perishables.
- **5S** = Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain. (Add **Safety** and **Spirit** for 7S.)
- **Pest control + fire prevention + periodic inspection** are the three procedural must-haves.

**On Stock Verification:**

- **Periodic** = annual / quarterly full count.
- **Continuous** = rolling sub-set count throughout the year.
- **Perpetual** = ERP / WMS-based real-time reconciliation.
- **Discrepancy types:** Surplus, Shortage, Unserviceable, Obsolete.
- **Board of Survey = 3 members** (Chairman + User Dept + Finance).
- **Stock verification cycle ends with a Reconciliation Report** submitted to management and statutory auditor.

**Key Formulas (one-line revision):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book Closing} &= \text{Opening} + \text{Receipts} - \text{Issues} \\
\text{Shrinkage \%} &= \dfrac{\text{Book} - \text{Physical}}{\text{Book}} \times 100 \\
\text{Accuracy \%} &= \dfrac{\text{Physical}}{\text{Book}} \times 100 \\
\text{ITR} &= \dfrac{\text{Consumption}}{\text{Average Inventory}} \\
\text{DOS} &= \dfrac{I_{avg}}{\text{Daily Consumption}} \\
\text{Carrying Cost Ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Carrying Cost}}{I_{avg}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Identity to remember forever:**

$$
\boxed{\;S\% + A\% = 100\%\;}
$$

**Final Exam Mantra:**
> "Preservation prevents loss; Stock Verification detects it. The two together form the *integrity backbone* of any materials management system."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Materials Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Materials Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Materials Management (MM)** is the integrated, cross-functional discipline that governs the planning, sourcing, purchasing, storing, movement, control, and disposition of materials throughout the industrial value stream, with the primary objective of **maximizing efficiency in material flow** while **minimizing cost, wastage, and supply disruption**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Framework):**
> *Materials Management is the function responsible for the coordination of planning, sourcing, purchasing, moving, storing, and controlling materials in an optimum manner to provide a predetermined service to the organization at a minimum cost.*

Mathematically, the **Core Material Cost Equation** governing any industrial system is expressed as:

$$C_{M} = C_{DM} + C_{IM} + C_{OH}$$

Where:
- $C_{M}$ = Total Material Cost
- $C_{DM}$ = Cost of Direct Materials (raw materials that become part of the finished product)
- $C_{IM}$ = Cost of Indirect Materials (consumables, lubricants, tools)
- $C_{OH}$ = Material Overhead Cost (handling, insurance, depreciation)

In a typical manufacturing enterprise, **$C_{DM}$ alone constitutes 50% to 60%** of the total product cost — making Materials Management one of the highest-leverage cost control functions in engineering management.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hospital Analogy: Why Materials Management is the "Circulatory System" of an Organization**

Imagine a large, multi-specialty hospital:

- The **doctors and surgeons** are the *Production Department* — they treat patients.
- The **nurses and ICU units** are the *Operations Floor* — they execute treatments.
- The **pharmacy, blood bank, surgical supply warehouse, and ambulance dispatch** constitute the **Materials Management Department**.

If the pharmacy runs out of insulin, the diabetic patient cannot be treated — *production halts*. If surgical gloves expire in storage because of over-ordering, *capital is locked and wasted*. If a critical blood unit cannot be located during an emergency, *life is lost*.

Similarly, in a factory:
- If raw steel is unavailable → *production line stops* (Line Stoppage Cost).
- If excessive inventory is held → *capital is tied up* (Carrying Cost).
- If poor-quality material enters → *rejection and rework* (Quality Cost).

**Therefore, Materials Management acts as the *circulatory system* that pumps the right material, in the right quantity, of the right quality, to the right place, at the right time, at the right cost** — famously termed the **"5 Rs and 1 C" Principle** in KTU-approved Operations Management literature.

| The 5 Rs + 1 C | Factory Equivalent | Hospital Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Right Material** | Certified grade steel | Insulin of correct type |
| **Right Quantity** | EOQ-based batches | Blood units as per surgery |
| **Right Quality** | ISO 9001 conformance | Sterile surgical equipment |
| **Right Place** | Assembly workstation | ICU / Operation Theatre |
| **Right Time** | Just-in-Time delivery | During surgery |
| **Right Cost** | Minimum landed cost | Affordable patient care |

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Materials Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) You Must Memorize for KTU Exams:**

- **Material Cost as % of Sales** = $(C_M / \text{Total Sales}) \times 100$ — Industry benchmark: **55% to 65%**
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** = $\text{Cost of Goods Sold} / \text{Average Inventory}$
- **Lead Time (LT)** = Time from purchase order placement to material receipt (typically measured in **days**)
- **Stockout Probability** = Frequency of zero-stock events per planning horizon
- **Service Level (SL)** = $(1 - \text{Stockout Probability}) \times 100\%$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strategic Objectives of Materials Management

A KTU 14-mark answer is expected to list, explain, and rank the objectives. Here is the **exam-ready structured breakdown**:

### Objective 1: **Minimization of Material Cost**
- Achieve **economic order quantities (EOQ)** to balance ordering vs holding costs.
- Negotiate **bulk discount tiers** without breaching carrying cost thresholds.
- Substitute expensive materials with **functionally equivalent cost-effective alternatives** (Value Engineering).

### Objective 2: **Uninterrupted Supply Assurance**
- Maintain **safety stock buffers** above mean lead-time demand.
- Develop **dual-sourcing strategies** to mitigate vendor failure risk.
- Implement **MRP-I / MRP-II** (Material Requirements Planning) systems.

### Objective 3: **Quality Compliance**
- Enforce **Inward Quality Inspection (IQC)** protocols.
- Enforce vendor certifications: **ISO 9001**, **AS 9100**, etc.
- Reduce **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**.

### Objective 4: **Optimized Inventory Investment**
- Minimize the **Working Capital Lock-up** in raw materials, WIP, and finished goods.
- Apply **ABC Analysis (Always Better Control)** for inventory stratification.
- Apply **VED Analysis** (Vital, Essential, Desirable) for spare parts.

### Objective 5: **Efficient Storage and Handling**
- Design **ergonomic warehouse layouts** (U-flow, I-flow, L-flow).
- Deploy **MHE (Material Handling Equipment)** such as forklifts, conveyors, AGVs.
- Implement **FIFO / LIFO** dispatch rules.

### Objective 6: **Vendor Development and Relationship Management**
- Conduct **vendor rating** and **vendor rating indexing**.
- Establish **strategic partnerships** (long-term contracts, JIT supply).

### Objective 7: **Effective Inter-Departmental Coordination**
- Interface with **Production, Marketing, Finance, Quality, and R&D**.

---

## 2.2 Functional Scope of Materials Management (The 8 Pillars)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Sub-Heading:** "Discuss the various functions of Materials Management."

The **8 functional pillars** recognized in the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Material Planning and Budgeting** — Forecasting demand, raising PRs (Purchase Requisitions).
2. **Purchasing / Procurement** — Vendor selection, negotiation, PO (Purchase Order) issuance.
3. **Inventory Control** — Setting stock levels (Re-order, Min, Max, Safety, EOQ).
4. **Stores Management** — Receipt, inspection, binning, issue, recording.
5. **Material Handling** — Internal movement via MHE.
6. **Transportation and Inward-Outward Logistics** — Inbound and outbound freight.
7. **Salvage and Disposal Management** — Scrap handling, surplus disposal.
8. **Materials Information System (MIS)** — ERP, SAP-MM, digital records.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least 2 formulas appear in every KTU ESE paper.**

| S. No. | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Units / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Total Material Cost | $C_M = C_{DM} + C_{IM} + C_{OH}$ | Currency (₹) |
| 2 | Material Cost Ratio | $MCR = (C_M / C_P) \times 100$ | % ; $C_P$ = Product Cost |
| 3 | Inventory Turnover Ratio | $ITR = \text{COGS} / \bar{I}$ | Dimensionless ; $\bar{I}$ = Avg Inventory |
| 4 | Average Inventory | $\bar{I} = (I_{max} + I_{min}) / 2$ | Units / ₹ |
| 5 | Safety Stock | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{LT}$ | Units ; Z = Service-level constant |
| 6 | Reorder Level | $ROL = D_{avg} \cdot LT + SS$ | Units |
| 7 | Economic Order Quantity | $EOQ = \sqrt{2DS / H}$ | Units ; D=Demand, S=Order cost, H=Holding/unit/yr |
| 8 | Total Inventory Cost | $TIC = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | Currency / Year |
| 9 | Lead Time Demand | $LTD = D_{avg} \cdot LT$ | Units |
| 10 | Stockout Cost | $C_{SO} = P_s \cdot N_{SO}$ | Currency ; $P_s$ = penalty per unit, $N_{SO}$ = no. of stockout units |

**Where critical safety factor values are standardized:**
- For **90% Service Level** → $Z = 1.28$
- For **95% Service Level** → $Z = 1.65$
- For **97.5% Service Level** → $Z = 1.96$
- For **99% Service Level** → $Z = 2.33$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **Introduction to Materials Management** framework is the **foundation upon which all subsequent modules** (Purchasing, Inventory Control, Stores Layout, ERP Systems) are constructed. In real industrial practice:

- **Automotive Industry (Toyota Production System):** Materials management is implemented as *Just-in-Time (JIT)* to reduce inventory carrying cost to near zero.
- **Aerospace Industry:** Materials management is tightly coupled with **configuration control** due to traceability requirements.
- **E-commerce Warehousing (Amazon, Flipkart):** MM is the central nervous system, with WMS (Warehouse Management System) automating every function listed above.
- **Construction Industry:** MM governs the supply of cement, steel, aggregates — typically **60% to 70%** of project cost.

**Engineering Insight:** A 5% reduction in material cost (achieved via better materials management) translates to a **5% increase in operating profit** — a leverage that no other business function can match without proportionate capital investment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — The Wilson Formula

The **EOQ derivation is a KTU favorite** (asked almost every year in Part B). Let us derive it rigorously.

### Step 1: Define the Variables

Let:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $Q$ = Order quantity per cycle (units)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = Holding (carrying) cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $P$ = Purchase cost per unit (₹/unit)

### Step 2: Number of Orders per Year

If each order is of size $Q$, the number of orders placed annually is:

$$N = \frac{D}{Q}$$

### Step 3: Annual Ordering Cost (AOC)

Total ordering cost per year equals the number of orders multiplied by the per-order cost:

$$AOC = N \cdot S = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S = \frac{DS}{Q}$$

### Step 4: Annual Holding Cost (AHC)

Inventory level starts at $Q$ (just after receipt) and depletes to **0** just before the next receipt. Hence, the **average inventory** is $Q/2$. Total holding cost:

$$AHC = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H = \frac{QH}{2}$$

### Step 5: Total Annual Inventory Cost (TAIC)

The total cost of managing inventory (excluding the purchase cost $D \cdot P$, which is independent of $Q$) is:

$$TC(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$$

### Step 6: Minimize the Cost Function

To find the optimal $Q$, differentiate $TC(Q)$ with respect to $Q$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

### Step 7: Solve for Q

Rearranging the equation:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

Taking the square root yields the **Wilson EOQ formula**:

$$Q^* = EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

### Step 8: Verify Minimum (Second Derivative Test)

The second derivative is:

$$\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$$

Since the second derivative is **positive**, the critical point is a **minimum** — confirming that $EOQ$ is the cost-optimal order quantity.

### Step 9: Optimal Total Inventory Cost (TIC\*)

Substituting $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ back into the cost function:

$$TC^* = \frac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot H}{2}$$

Simplify the first term:

$$TC^* = \sqrt{\frac{DS \cdot H}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DS \cdot H}{2}}$$

$$TC^* = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$

$$TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

This is the **famous simplified minimum total inventory cost** expression.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Safety Stock Based on Service Level

### Step 1: Define Variability

Let the lead time $LT$ be **variable**, and let the standard deviation of lead time demand be $\sigma_{LT}$.

### Step 2: Apply the Z-Table

The required safety stock is the buffer above **mean lead time demand** such that the probability of stockout equals $(1 - SL)$:

$$SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

### Step 3: Full Reorder Level Formula

The reorder level is set at the point when inventory must be replenished:

$$ROL = (D_{avg} \cdot LT) + (Z \cdot \sigma_{LT})$$

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A factory uses **10,000 units/year** of a component. Lead time is **15 days** with a standard deviation of **3 days**. The management wants a **95% service level**. The factory operates **300 days/year**. Find the Reorder Level.

**Solution:**

$$D_{avg} = 10000 / 300 = 33.33 \text{ units/day}$$

$$LTD = 33.33 \times 15 = 500 \text{ units}$$

$$\sigma_{LT} = 33.33 \times 3 = 100 \text{ units}$$

For **95% SL**, $Z = 1.65$:

$$SS = 1.65 \times 100 = 165 \text{ units}$$

$$ROL = 500 + 165 = 665 \text{ units}$$

**Interpretation:** When the stock reaches **665 units**, a new purchase order must be raised.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Materials Management Across Industries

| Industry Sector | Material Cost as % of Product Cost | Primary MM Challenge | Dominant MM Strategy |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Heavy Engineering | 55% – 65% | Long lead time for imported parts | Strategic vendor partnerships |
| Automotive | 60% – 70% | High variety, low margin | JIT / Lean Supply |
| FMCG (Fast Moving Consumer Goods) | 40% – 55% | Demand variability, perishability | Quick response, FEFO |
| Pharmaceuticals | 25% – 40% | Regulatory, cold chain | Traceability + cold chain MM |
| Construction | 65% – 75% | Bulky, weather-sensitive | Just-in-Time + bulk logistics |
| Electronics / IT Hardware | 50% – 65% | Obsolescence, miniaturization | Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Scope of Materials Management

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **8 functional pillars** of Materials Management and their **interdependencies**. This diagram is a **guaranteed 4 to 6 mark visual** in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Material Planning and Budgeting] --> B[Purchasing and Procurement]
    B --> C[Vendor Management and Development]
    B --> D[Inventory Control]
    D --> E[Stores Management]
    E --> F[Inward Quality Inspection]
    E --> G[Material Handling]
    G --> H[Production Line Issue]
    H --> I[Finished Goods Stock]
    I --> J[Outward Dispatch and Logistics]
    E --> K[Salvage and Scrap Disposal]
    L[Materials Information System MIS] -.-> A
    L -.-> B
    L -.-> D
    L -.-> E
    M[Top Management] --> A
    M --> B
    M --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Solid arrows (`-->`) represent **sequential material flow**.
- Dotted arrows (`-.->`) represent **information flow** (MIS support).
- `M[Top Management]` provides strategic direction to the three macro-functions: Planning, Purchasing, and Inventory Control.

---

## 4.2 Organization Structure — Typical Materials Management Department

This Mermaid block diagram shows the **hierarchical reporting structure** of a Materials Management department in a medium-to-large manufacturing enterprise.

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[Materials Manager] --> P[Purchase Officer]
    M --> S[Stores Officer]
    M --> I[Inventory Controller]
    M --> L[Logistics Coordinator]
    M --> Q[Quality Inspection Officer]
    M --> V[Vendor Development Executive]
    P --> P1[Junior Purchase Executive]
    P --> P2[Vendor Liaison]
    S --> S1[Stores Keeper]
    S --> S2[Bin Card Clerk]
    I --> I1[MRP Planner]
    I --> I2[ABC Analyst]
    L --> L1[Transport In-charge]
    L --> L2[Traffic Clerk]
```

---

## 4.3 Input-Process-Output (IPO) Model of Materials Management

This diagram is useful when a KTU question asks: *"Explain the working of Materials Management as a system."*

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1[Demand Forecast] --> P1[MM Process]
    IN2[Production Plan] --> P1
    IN3[Budget Allocation] --> P1
    IN4[Vendor Database] --> P1
    P1 --> P2[Purchase Order Generation]
    P1 --> P3[Inventory Level Setting]
    P1 --> P4[Storage Layout Design]
    P1 --> P5[Transportation Scheduling]
    P2 --> OUT1[Uninterrupted Supply]
    P3 --> OUT2[Optimized Inventory]
    P4 --> OUT3[Efficient Material Flow]
    P5 --> OUT4[Cost Effective Logistics]
    OUT1 --> FB[Feedback Loop KPI Monitoring]
    OUT2 --> FB
    OUT3 --> FB
    OUT4 --> FB
    FB --> P1
```

**Key Insight from IPO Model:** The **Feedback Loop** is the most critical element — KPIs (Inventory Turnover, Stockout Rate, Carrying Cost %) flow back into the planning process, making MM a **self-correcting closed-loop system**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Materials Management. List any four of its objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Materials Management is the integrated planning, sourcing, purchasing, storage, movement, and control of materials with the aim of providing a pre-decided service to the organization at minimum cost.

**Any Four Objectives (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. Ensuring uninterrupted supply of materials.
2. Minimizing material cost and inventory carrying cost.
3. Maintaining desired quality levels of incoming materials.
4. Developing reliable vendor relationships.
5. Efficient storage and material handling.
6. Optimizing working capital investment in inventory.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the definition and forget to **list the objectives explicitly** — losing 1.5 marks. Always dedicate **half the answer word count** to the list/expansion.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is meant by the term "5 Rs and 1 C" in Materials Management?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **5 Rs and 1 C** is a guiding principle in Materials Management that mandates that the **right material**, in the **right quantity**, of the **right quality**, at the **right place**, at the **right time**, must be made available at the **right cost**.

| Element | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| Right Material | Correct specification and grade |
| Right Quantity | EOQ-based order size |
| Right Quality | Conformance to specifications |
| Right Place | Required workstation / storage bin |
| Right Time | Within the lead-time window |
| Right Cost | Minimum landed cost |

(1 mark for the principle statement + 1.5 marks for the **tabular explanation of all 5 Rs and 1 C** + 0.5 marks for relevance.)

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

### **QUESTION A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **functions/scope of Materials Management**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **importance of Materials Management** in a manufacturing organization with reference to material cost as a percentage of total product cost. (7 marks)

---

### **MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION A**

#### **Part (a) — Functions of Materials Management (7 marks)**

The **8 major functions** of Materials Management are:

**1. Material Planning and Budgeting (1 mark):**
Forecasting future material requirements based on production plans, sales forecasts, and lead times. Preparation of material budgets and procurement schedules.

**2. Purchasing / Procurement (1 mark):**
Sourcing of materials, vendor selection, price negotiation, and purchase order issuance. Objective: obtain materials of desired quality at the lowest total cost.

**3. Inventory Control (1 mark):**
Determining economic order quantities, safety stock, reorder levels, and minimum-maximum stock levels. Techniques include ABC analysis, VED analysis, EOQ, and MRP.

**4. Stores Management (1 mark):**
Receipt of materials, inward inspection, binning in warehouses, issue to production, and maintenance of stock records (bin cards, stores ledger).

**5. Material Handling (1 mark):**
Internal movement of materials using appropriate material handling equipment — forklifts, conveyors, cranes, AGVs — to minimize manual effort and damage.

**6. Transportation and Logistics (0.5 mark):**
Selection of transport mode, route planning, freight negotiation, and inbound-outbound coordination.

**7. Salvage and Disposal (0.5 mark):**
Disposal of obsolete, surplus, and scrap materials through auctions, tenders, or recycling routes — recovering residual value.

**8. Materials Information System (0.5 mark):**
ERP-based management of material data — supplier master, item master, stock levels, PO status — enabling real-time visibility.

> **Diagrammatic representation (0.5 mark):** Include a simple block diagram showing the 8 functions with arrows indicating inter-relationships.

---

#### **Part (b) — Importance of Materials Management (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
In any manufacturing organization, **materials constitute the single largest cost element**, typically accounting for **50% to 70%** of the total product cost depending on the industry.

**Importance Points (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

**1. Cost Leverage:** A 1% reduction in material cost can increase profit by 5% to 10% — much higher leverage than equivalent reductions in labor or overhead.

**2. Production Continuity:** MM ensures uninterrupted material supply, preventing costly line stoppages (each hour of downtime in a plant may cost ₹50,000 to ₹5,00,000).

**3. Quality Assurance:** By enforcing vendor quality standards and inward inspection, MM reduces **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — rejections, rework, warranty claims.

**4. Working Capital Optimization:** Efficient inventory control reduces capital locked in stocks, releasing cash for productive use.

**5. Customer Satisfaction:** Timely delivery of finished products depends on the timely availability of raw materials — a direct MM output.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Materials Management is therefore **not a clerical function** but a **strategic function** that directly influences profitability, competitiveness, and survival of the enterprise.

---

### **QUESTION B [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Define **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** and explain its significance. A factory has an average inventory of ₹25,00,000 and the cost of goods sold is ₹1,50,00,000. Calculate the ITR. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Input-Process-Output (IPO) model of Materials Management**. (7 marks)

---

### **MODEL ANSWER FOR QUESTION B**

#### **Part (a) — Inventory Turnover Ratio (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR) is the ratio of the Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) during a period to the Average Inventory held during that period. It indicates how many times the average inventory is "turned over" or consumed in a given period.

$$ITR = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\bar{I}}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- A **high ITR** indicates efficient inventory management, lower holding costs, and faster stock movement.
- A **low ITR** signals overstocking, obsolete inventory, or weak sales.
- **Typical benchmark** in manufacturing: 6 to 12 turns per year.

**Numerical Solution (3 marks):**

Given:
- Average Inventory, $\bar{I}$ = ₹25,00,000
- COGS = ₹1,50,00,000

$$ITR = \frac{1,50,00,000}{25,00,000}$$

$$ITR = 6 \text{ turns per year}$$

**Interpretation:** Inventory is replenished 6 times a year, which corresponds to a holding period of 365/6 ≈ 61 days — within acceptable manufacturing benchmarks.

---

#### **Part (b) — IPO Model of Materials Management (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Input-Process-Output (IPO) model views Materials Management as a **system** that converts organizational inputs into valuable outputs through a series of structured processes.

**Inputs (2 marks — 1 mark per input point):**
- Demand forecasts from marketing.
- Production plans and schedules.
- Budget allocations from finance.
- Vendor master data and external market intelligence.

**Processes (2 marks — 0.5 mark each for 4 processes):**
- Material planning and budgeting.
- Purchasing and procurement.
- Inventory control (EOQ, ABC, ROL setting).
- Stores management and material handling.
- Transportation and logistics.

**Outputs (1.5 marks — 0.5 mark each):**
- Uninterrupted supply of materials.
- Optimized inventory investment.
- Cost-effective logistics.

**Feedback Loop (0.5 mark):**
KPIs such as ITR, stockout rate, and carrying cost % are fed back to refine future planning — making the system **closed-loop and self-correcting**.

> **Block Diagram (additional credit — up to 1 mark):** Draw a clear IPO block diagram with arrows. The Mermaid version provided in **Section 4.3** of these notes can be replicated by hand in the exam.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT skip the numerical sub-part.** KTU examiners allocate **3 dedicated marks** for the calculation. Show all formula substitutions and units explicitly.
> 2. **Do NOT write a "wall of text"** in the IPO answer — a **neat block diagram** carries equal weight to the textual explanation. Always draw the diagram.
> 3. **Do NOT confuse ITR with Inventory Days.** ITR is dimensionless (turns/year); Inventory Days = 365/ITR.
> 4. **Do NOT forget the Feedback Loop** in the IPO model — it is the differentiator between a "good" and an "excellent" answer.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Last-Minute Exam Prep:**

- **Definition (must be verbatim or near-verbatim):** Materials Management is the *integrated planning, sourcing, purchasing, storage, movement, and control of materials at minimum cost and pre-decided service level*.

- **Golden Formula:** **5 Rs + 1 C** — *Right Material, Right Quantity, Right Quality, Right Place, Right Time, Right Cost*.

- **Material Cost is 50% to 70%** of total product cost in manufacturing.

- **EOQ Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — derived by minimizing $TC = DS/Q + QH/2$.

- **Minimum Total Cost:** $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$.

- **Reorder Level:** $ROL = D_{avg} \cdot LT + SS$.

- **Safety Stock:** $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ — use $Z = 1.65$ for 95% service level.

- **ITR Formula:** $ITR = \text{COGS} / \bar{I}$ — higher is better.

- **8 Functions of MM:** Planning, Purchasing, Inventory Control, Stores Management, Material Handling, Transportation, Salvage, MIS.

- **Difference to remember:** Materials Management is **broader** than *Inventory Management* — the latter is a *subset* (function) of the former.

- **Strategic Significance:** A 1% reduction in material cost often produces a **5% to 10% increase in profit** — the highest cost-control leverage in operations.

- **Always end 14-mark answers with a Conclusion** and **draw at least one diagram** (block diagram of 8 functions, IPO model, or organization chart) — diagrams fetch **1 to 2 bonus marks** consistently.

- **Mnemonic to remember the 8 functions:** **"P-P-I-S-H-T-S-M"** → *Planning, Purchasing, Inventory, Stores, Handling, Transport, Salvage, MIS*.

- **Don't confuse:** *Materials Management* (covers *raw, WIP, finished, MRO*) vs *Supply Chain Management* (broader, includes *suppliers, warehouses, distributors, retailers, customers*).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scope, Primary and Secondary Objectives of Materials Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Materials Management (MM)** is the integrated, systematic, and coordinated function of planning, sourcing, purchasing, storing, moving, controlling, and accounting for materials throughout their life cycle, from the point of requirement identification to the point of final consumption or disposal, in order to achieve optimal cost, quality, delivery, and service levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> *Materials Management is the branch of management that deals with the planning, organizing, and controlling of the activities concerned with the flow of materials from the initial purchase of raw materials to the final delivery of finished products to the consumer.*

The discipline encompasses both **physical materials handling** (movement, storage, packaging) and **materials management** (procurement, inventory control, warehousing, distribution, and value engineering). Together, they ensure uninterrupted production at minimum total cost.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" of Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Hospital Emergency Room**
> Imagine a large hospital's emergency room. For patients to be treated effectively, the hospital must:
> 1. **Forecast** when emergencies (demand) will arrive
> 2. **Procure** medicines, blood, oxygen, and equipment (purchasing)
> 3. **Store** them at proper temperatures in accessible locations (warehousing)
> 4. **Move** them quickly to where they are needed (handling)
> 5. **Track** usage to replenish at the right time (inventory control)
> 6. **Dispose** of expired items safely (scrap management)
>
> If any of these steps fail, lives are lost. In a factory, if any materials management step fails, **production stops, costs spiral, and customers leave**. Materials Management is the **circulatory system of an enterprise** — it does not generate revenue directly, but without it, every other organ (department) fails.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Materials Management

The following metrics are universally accepted benchmarks in industrial practice:

- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** — measures how often inventory is sold/replaced per year.
- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** — the optimal order size minimizing total cost.
- **Reorder Level (ROL)** — the stock level at which a new order must be placed.
- **Lead Time (LT)** — the time between order placement and receipt.
- **Carrying Cost (CC)** — typically **15 % to 30 %** of inventory value per year.
- **Service Level** — usually targeted at **95 % to 99 %** in modern supply chains.
- **Stock-out Probability** — kept below **5 %** for critical items.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote ranges and standards (e.g., "*ABC analysis — 10–20 % of items account for 70–80 % of value*") in your answers. Examiners reward precise numerical recall.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scope of Materials Management — Structured Logical Breakdown

The **scope** of materials management extends far beyond mere "buying and storing." It is a **horizontal function** cutting across every department of an organization. The scope is broadly classified into the following eight functional areas:

#### 2.1.1 Materials Planning and Forecasting
- Determining **what, when, and how much** to purchase or produce.
- Techniques: **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)**, **MRP II**, **ERP**, **JIT (Just-In-Time)**, demand forecasting models (moving average, exponential smoothing).
- Why it matters: Prevents both **stock-outs** and **over-stocking**, which are the two cardinal sins of materials management.

#### 2.1.2 Purchasing and Procurement
- **Purchasing** = the operational act of buying.
- **Procurement** = the *strategic* end-to-end process (vendor selection → negotiation → contract → payment).
- Key activities: *Vendor development, e-procurement, framework agreements, rate contracts*.

#### 2.1.3 Inventory Control and Storekeeping
- Maintenance of stock at optimal levels using techniques like **EOQ, ABC Analysis, FSN Analysis, VED Analysis, SDE Analysis, HML Analysis, GOLF Analysis**, and the **Min-Max System**.
- Functions: *Receipt, inspection, binning, issue, recording, physical verification*.

#### 2.1.4 Materials Handling
- The **physical movement** of materials within the store, warehouse, and production floor.
- Equipment: *Forklifts, conveyors, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), cranes, pallet jacks*.
- Objective: *Minimize handling cost, reduce damage, improve flow*.

#### 2.1.5 Warehousing and Storage
- Designing **layout, space utilization, racking systems, climate control**, and **safety norms**.
- Modern trends: *Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems (AS/RS), WMS (Warehouse Management Systems)*.

#### 2.1.6 Transportation and Distribution
- **Inbound logistics** (raw material movement from supplier) and **outbound logistics** (finished goods to customer).
- Mode selection: *Road, rail, air, sea, multimodal*.

#### 2.1.7 Quality Assurance and Inspection
- Incoming material inspection, **ISO 9001 / Six Sigma / TQM** compliance.
- Prevention of defective materials entering the production stream.

#### 2.1.8 Disposal, Scrap, and Salvage Management
- **Reverse logistics** — handling obsolete stock, scrap, and end-of-life products.
- Value recovery through *recycling, resale, or responsible disposal*.

---

### 2.2 Primary Objectives of Materials Management

The **primary objectives** are the *core, non-negotiable goals* that define the very purpose of the function. Failure in any one of these means the system has failed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Primary Objectives (The Golden Triangle of MM):**
> 1. **Right Quantity**
> 2. **Right Quality**
> 3. **Right Time**
> ...all delivered at the **Right Price** to the **Right Place**.

| # | Primary Objective | Definition | Practical Mechanism |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Right Quantity** | Procuring neither too much nor too little | EOQ, Min-Max, MRP, safety stock calculation |
| 2 | **Right Quality** | Materials meeting design specifications | Vendor qualification, incoming inspection, SPC |
| 3 | **Right Time** | Availability exactly when production needs it | Lead-time analysis, ROP scheduling, JIT |
| 4 | **Right Price** | Minimum total cost of ownership (TCO) | Negotiation, value analysis, bulk contracts |
| 5 | **Right Place** | Delivered to the correct production point | Internal logistics, bin allocation, WMS |
| 6 | **Right Source** | From reliable, ethical, long-term vendors | Vendor rating, single vs. multiple sourcing policy |

### 2.3 Secondary Objectives of Materials Management

The **secondary objectives** are the *supporting, performance-enhancing goals* that strengthen the primary ones. They are not optional in modern KTU-evaluated answers — they carry marks.

| # | Secondary Objective | Why It Matters | Key Metric |
|---|---------------------|----------------|------------|
| 1 | **Minimize Inventory Investment** | Frees working capital | Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR) |
| 2 | **Minimize Materials Handling Cost** | Up to 30 % of warehouse expense | Handling cost per unit |
| 3 | **Maintain Quality Standards** | Reduces rework and warranty cost | Defect rate (PPM) |
| 4 | **Develop Vendor Relationships** | Long-term price stability & quality | Vendor rating score |
| 5 | **Achieve Efficient Storekeeping** | Reduces obsolescence and pilferage | Shrinkage % |
| 6 | **Ensure Smooth Production Flow** | No stoppages due to material shortage | Stock-out incidents per quarter |
| 7 | **Promote Standardization** | Reduces SKUs, simplifies procurement | Number of active part numbers |
| 8 | **Value Engineering & Cost Reduction** | Continuous improvement (Kaizen) | Year-on-year cost savings % |
| 9 | **Employee Safety & Ergonomics** | Reduces accidents and insurance cost | Lost-time injury frequency rate (LTIFR) |
| 10 | **Environmental & Sustainability Compliance** | Green supply chain mandates | Carbon footprint per unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students list primary objectives only. Question banks repeatedly ask: *"Differentiate between primary and secondary objectives"* — losing 3 marks is common.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table. Every numerical problem in PEPET414 uses one or more of these formulas.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand (units), $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹), $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹) | Units |
| 2 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | $d$ = average daily demand, $L$ = lead time (days), $SS$ = safety stock (units) | Units |
| 3 | **Safety Stock (SS)** | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | $Z$ = service-level z-score (e.g., 1.65 for 95 %), $\sigma_{LT}$ = std. dev. of lead-time demand | Units |
| 4 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{Average\ Inventory}$ | COGS = Cost of Goods Sold, Average Inventory = (Opening + Closing)/2 | Times/year |
| 5 | **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** | $TIC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | $Q$ = order quantity, rest as above | ₹/year |
| 6 | **Number of Orders per Year** | $N = \dfrac{D}{EOQ}$ | $D$ = annual demand, $EOQ$ = from formula 1 | Orders/year |
| 7 | **Cycle Time** | $T = \dfrac{Working\ Days}{N}$ | $N$ = orders/year | Days/order |
| 8 | **Carrying Cost %** | $CC\% = \dfrac{Holding\ Cost\ per\ Unit}{Unit\ Price} \times 100$ | — | % |
| 9 | **Stock-out Cost per Cycle** | $SOC = C_s \cdot E[shortage]$ | $C_s$ = shortage cost/unit, $E[shortage]$ = expected units short | ₹ |
| 10 | **Pareto (ABC) Cut-offs** | Class A: 10 % items, 70 % value    Class B: 20 % items, 20 % value    Class C: 70 % items, 10 % value | — | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning Resolved:** All absolute-value bars in tables above use vertical spacing — the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` convention is preserved to keep the markdown table parser intact.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

- **Mechanical / Production Engineering:** EOQ decisions for cutting tools, lubricants, fasteners directly affect **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)**.
- **Civil Engineering:** Cement, steel, and aggregate inventory is the *largest working-capital block* on any construction site — ABC + safety stock analysis is routine.
- **Electrical / Electronics:** Component obsolescence (especially semiconductors) makes **disposal management** a critical secondary objective.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Materials handling is one of the **"7 wastes" (muda)** in Lean Manufacturing.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is **conceptual-managerial** in nature, the "derivations" below are *logical reasoning chains* and *analytical frameworks* — not calculus. This matches KTU 2024 Scheme expectations for PEPET414 (a non-mathematical management course).

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why EOQ Minimizes Total Cost (Logical Derivation)

We derive EOQ step-by-step, beginning from the cost functions.

**Step 1 — Annual Ordering Cost:**
If $D$ units are required per year and each order is of size $Q$, then the number of orders placed per year is $\dfrac{D}{Q}$.
Each order costs $S$ (paperwork, transport, inspection). Therefore:

$$
OC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S
$$

**Step 2 — Annual Holding (Carrying) Cost:**
Average inventory is $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ (linearly depletes from $Q$ to $0$).
Each unit costs $H$ per year to hold. Therefore:

$$
HC(Q) = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Inventory Cost:**

$$
TIC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimize by Differentiating w.r.t. $Q$ and Setting to Zero:**

$$
\frac{d(TIC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Q$ (which is the EOQ):**

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}}
$$

$$
Q^{2} = \frac{2 D S}{H}
$$

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Check:** At EOQ, *Ordering Cost = Holding Cost*. This is a **classic KTU exam line** — write it down verbatim in any derivation question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — ROP with Safety Stock (Reasoning Chain)

**Step 1 — During lead time $L$, demand is consumed at rate $d$.**
Expected demand during lead time = $d \cdot L$.

**Step 2 — Real demand fluctuates.** To avoid stock-out, we add a *buffer*:

$$
SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT}
$$

**Step 3 — Reorder Point = expected lead-time demand + safety stock:**

$$
ROP = (d \cdot L) + (Z \cdot \sigma_{LT})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $Z$ changes with service level:**
> - 90 % service → $Z = 1.28$
> - 95 % service → $Z = 1.65$
> - 99 % service → $Z = 2.33$
> Higher service = more safety stock = higher carrying cost. The **trade-off** is a primary objective in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Objectives to KPIs

This matrix is the **answer backbone** for a 14-mark question on "Discuss the primary and secondary objectives of materials management."

| Objective Category | Specific Objective | Key Performance Indicator (KPI) | Standard Benchmark | Trade-off / Conflict |
|--------------------|--------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Primary** | Right Quantity | EOQ accuracy, stock-out frequency | ≤ 2 stock-outs/year | High stock vs. low carrying cost |
| **Primary** | Right Quality | Incoming defect rate | ≤ 1000 PPM | Quality vs. supplier cost |
| **Primary** | Right Time | On-time-in-full (OTIF) | ≥ 98 % | Lead time vs. inventory |
| **Primary** | Right Price | Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) | Bottom decile in industry | Low price vs. high quality |
| **Primary** | Right Source | Vendor rating score | ≥ 80/100 | Single vs. multiple sourcing |
| **Secondary** | Minimize investment | Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR) | 6 – 12×/year (mfg.) | High turnover vs. stock-out |
| **Secondary** | Minimize handling cost | Cost per unit moved | 3 – 8 % of product cost | Mechanization vs. CapEx |
| **Secondary** | Vendor development | % of strategic partnerships | ≥ 60 % | Dependence risk |
| **Secondary** | Standardization | Number of active SKUs | Year-on-year ↓ 5 % | Standardization vs. customization |
| **Secondary** | Value engineering | Cost savings YoY | ≥ 3 % | Innovation cost |
| **Secondary** | Safety / Ergonomics | LTIFR | < 1.0 per million hours | Speed vs. safety |
| **Secondary** | Sustainability | CO₂ per unit | Year-on-year ↓ 2 % | Green cost premium |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Testing a Student's Understanding

**Question:** A factory uses 12,000 units/year of a component. Ordering cost = ₹300/order. Holding cost = ₹20/unit/year. Lead time = 6 days. Working days = 300. Service level = 95 % ($Z = 1.65$). Lead-time demand std. dev. = 50 units.

**Solution Outline:**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 12000 \cdot 300}{20}} = \sqrt{360000} = 600\ \text{units}
$$

$$
d = \frac{12000}{300} = 40\ \text{units/day}
$$

$$
SS = 1.65 \times 50 = 82.5 \approx 83\ \text{units}
$$

$$
ROP = 40 \times 6 + 83 = 240 + 83 = 323\ \text{units}
$$

$$
N = \frac{12000}{600} = 20\ \text{orders/year}
$$

$$
TIC = \frac{12000}{600}\cdot 300 + \frac{600}{2}\cdot 20 = 6000 + 6000 = \text{₹}12{,}000/\text{year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observe:** At EOQ, Ordering Cost (₹6,000) = Holding Cost (₹6,000). This confirms the **KTU favourite point**: *the optimum is where both costs are equal.*

```mermaid

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The diagram above is **not physical geometry** — it is a **trade-off representation**. You can optimize **only two** of the three corners simultaneously. This is the foundational principle behind JIT (favors B and C) and EOQ (favors A and B). KTU examiners reward this conceptual clarity.


> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Note:** The feedback loop is what makes materials management a *closed-loop* control system. In TQM and Six Sigma terminology, this is the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle applied to material flow.

**Definition (1 mark):** Materials Management is the integrated function of planning, procuring, storing, moving, and controlling the flow of materials from raw material stage to finished product delivery, with the objective of ensuring right quantity, right quality, at the right time, at the right price, from the right source, at the right place.

1. Materials planning and forecasting
2. Purchasing and procurement
3. Inventory control and storekeeping

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primary Objectives | Secondary Objectives |
