### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - ❌ Do **NOT** list only 2–3 objectives; minimum **six** are expected for full marks.
> - ❌ Do **NOT** write primary and secondary without giving a *clear differentiator* (the matrix-style table is the gold standard).
> - ❌ Avoid one-line answers. KTU 14-mark questions need **structure**: introduction → explanation → examples → conclusion.
> - ❌ Do not confuse **scope** (functional areas) with **objectives** (goals). Examiners mark this strictly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **scope of materials management**, covering all major functional areas. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Discuss the **primary objectives** of materials management with suitable examples and trade-offs. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Scope of Materials Management (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The scope of materials management is not limited to purchasing. It is a horizontal function spanning the entire material life cycle, from demand identification to final disposal.

**Eight Functional Areas (1 mark each, total 6 marks; 2 marks for explanation):**

1. **Materials Planning & Forecasting** — Techniques: MRP, MRP II, JIT, forecasting models. *Example:* A car manufacturer uses MRP to plan steel sheets 6 weeks in advance.

2. **Purchasing & Procurement** — Vendor selection, negotiation, contracts, e-procurement. *Example:* Tata Motors' centralized procurement portal.

3. **Inventory Control & Storekeeping** — ABC, EOQ, Min-Max, FSN. *Example:* A pharma company uses VED analysis to prioritize life-saving drugs.

4. **Materials Handling** — Equipment selection, layout, automation. *Example:* Amazon's Kiva robots in fulfillment centers.

5. **Warehousing & Storage** — Layout, racking, climate control, WMS. *Example:* Cold storage for vaccines in a hospital chain.

6. **Transportation & Distribution** — Mode selection, route optimization, third-party logistics. *Example:* Flipkart's hub-and-spoke delivery model.

7. **Quality Assurance & Inspection** — Incoming inspection, SPC, ISO certification. *Example:* Rejecting 5 % of an incoming steel batch based on hardness test results.

8. **Disposal, Scrap & Salvage** — Recycling, resale, reverse logistics. *Example:* Apple Inc.'s recycling program for old iPhones.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The eight areas function as a closed-loop, ensuring the right material reaches the right place, at the right time, at the right cost.

---

**(b) Primary Objectives of Materials Management (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The six primary objectives are universally known as the **"6 Rs of Materials Management."**

**The 6 Rs (1 mark each):**

1. **Right Quantity** — Avoid both stock-out and over-stocking. *Example:* Use EOQ to order 600 units instead of 5,000, reducing carrying cost by 80 %.

2. **Right Quality** — Conformance to specification. *Example:* ISO 9001-certified suppliers; incoming AQL sampling.

3. **Right Time** — Material available *just in time*. *Example:* Toyota's JIT system reduces WIP inventory by 90 %.

4. **Right Price** — Minimum total cost of ownership. *Trade-off:* Cheaper price may mean lower quality — focus is on *TCO*, not unit price.

5. **Right Place** — Material delivered to the correct production point. *Example:* Sub-assembly parts routed directly to line-side, not main stores.

6. **Right Source** — Reliable, ethical vendor. *Trade-off:* Single source (deep relationship) vs. multiple sources (risk diversification).

**Trade-offs and Conclusion (1 mark):** The primary objectives are mutually constrained — increasing one (e.g., service level) increases cost elsewhere. The role of the materials manager is to find the optimum.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Discuss the **secondary objectives** of materials management. Why are they important even though they are called "secondary"? *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** With the help of a **formula sheet and worked example**, explain how the primary objective of "right quantity" is achieved using **EOQ and ROP analysis**. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution

**(a) Secondary Objectives of Materials Management (7 marks):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Secondary objectives are *performance-enhancing* goals that reinforce the primary ones. They are called "secondary" not because they are unimportant, but because they are *enablers* of the primary six Rs.

**List with Examples (1 mark each, total 6 marks):**

1. **Minimize Inventory Investment** — KPI: Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR). *Example:* Increasing ITR from 4 to 10 frees ₹50 crore in working capital.

2. **Minimize Materials Handling Cost** — KPI: Cost per unit moved. *Example:* Replacing manual carts with conveyors reduces handling cost from 12 % to 4 % of product cost.

3. **Develop Vendor Relationships** — KPI: Vendor rating score, on-time delivery %. *Example:* Toyota's keiretsu system — 200 strategic partners co-developed over decades.

4. **Standardization & Variety Reduction** — KPI: Number of active SKUs. *Example:* Ford reducing fastener types from 1,200 to 400 in the Model T.

5. **Value Engineering** — KPI: YoY cost savings %. *Example:* Substituting aluminum for steel in a chassis, reducing weight by 30 % with no performance loss.

6. **Employee Safety & Environmental Compliance** — KPI: LTIFR, carbon footprint. *Example:* ISO 14001 certification for green supply chains.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Without these secondary objectives, the primary ones cannot be sustained long-term. They are the *quality multipliers* of the system.

---

**(b) Right Quantity via EOQ and ROP (7 marks):**

**Formula Box (2 marks — give all formulas neatly):**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
$$

$$
ROP = d \cdot L + SS
$$

$$
SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{LT}
$$

$$
TIC = \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}
$$

**Worked Example (3 marks):**

A factory needs $D = 12{,}000$ units/year, $S = \text{₹}300$/order, $H = \text{₹}20$/unit/year, $L = 6$ days, working days = 300, service level 95 % ($Z = 1.65$), $\sigma_{LT} = 50$.

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 12000 \cdot 300}{20}} = \sqrt{360000} = 600\ \text{units}
$$

$$
d = 40\ \text{units/day}, \quad SS = 1.65 \times 50 = 82.5 \approx 83
$$

$$
ROP = 40 \times 6 + 83 = 323\ \text{units}
$$

$$
TIC = \frac{12000}{600}\cdot 300 + \frac{600}{2}\cdot 20 = 6000 + 6000 = \text{₹}12{,}000/\text{year}
$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):**
- EOQ = 600 units minimizes the *sum* of ordering and holding costs.
- At EOQ, OC = HC = ₹6,000 — confirming the optimum.
- ROP = 323 units means: *when stock falls to 323, place a new order of 600 units*.
- Safety stock = 83 units provides a 95 % protection against lead-time variability.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip:** If the question gives you *carrying cost as a percentage*, compute $H$ first as $H = c \cdot i \cdot P$ where $c$ = holding cost factor (typically 0.15–0.30), $i$ = annual interest, $P$ = unit price.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin):**

- ✅ **Definition (must be memorized):** Materials Management = integrated planning, procurement, storage, handling, and control of material flow.
- ✅ **Scope = 8 areas:** Planning, Purchasing, Inventory Control, Handling, Warehousing, Transport, Quality, Disposal.
- ✅ **Primary Objectives = 6 Rs:** Right Quantity, Quality, Time, Price, Place, Source.
- ✅ **Secondary Objectives = at least 6 to be named:** Cost minimization, vendor development, standardization, value engineering, safety, sustainability, smooth flow.
- ✅ **EOQ formula:** $EOQ = \sqrt{2DS/H}$.
- ✅ **ROP formula:** $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$.
- ✅ **Key result:** At EOQ, **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** — *guaranteed 1-mark question*.
- ✅ **ABC Analysis:** 10–20–70 rule (items) → 70–20–10 rule (value).
- ✅ **Service Level ↔ Z-value:** 90 % → 1.28, 95 % → 1.65, 99 % → 2.33.
- ✅ **Trade-off principle:** Cannot maximize all of {Low cost, High service, Low lead time} simultaneously — only two.
- ✅ **Modern tools to name in answers:** MRP II, ERP, JIT, WMS, AS/RS, Six Sigma, TQM, e-procurement, Industry 4.0.
- ✅ **Common KTU question pattern:** "Differentiate primary and secondary objectives" — use a *table* to score full marks.
- ✅ **Diagram to memorize:** The 6-Rs hierarchy tree (Draw it! 2 marks guaranteed).
- ✅ **Common student mistake:** Confusing *scope* (functional areas) with *objectives* (goals) — read the question word carefully.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of Management as Applied to Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Functions of Management as Applied to Materials ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Functions of Management as Applied to Materials** refer to the systematic application of the classical managerial functions — **Planning, Organizing, Staffing, Directing, and Controlling** — to the integrated lifecycle of industrial materials, from forecasting demand and procurement through storage, handling, usage, and final disposal or recovery. In the context of KTU's PEPET414 syllabus, these functions govern how an organization ensures the **right material, in the right quantity, of the right quality, at the right time, at the right place, and at the right cost (the 6R principle)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Pantry Manager"** > Imagine a large restaurant kitchen. The Head Chef (Manager) must: > - **Plan** tomorrow's menu and ingredients needed (Planning) > - **Organize** the pantry shelves, freezers, and dry stores (Organizing) > - **Hire & train** storekeepers and porters (Staffing) > - **Direct** the team during a dinner rush — assigning, motivating (Directing) > - **Monitor** wastage, pilferage, stock levels, and costs (Controlling) > - **Coordinate** with purchasing, suppliers, and serving staff (Coordinating) This is *exactly* what materials management does in a factory — it is the "pantry management" of an entire industrial enterprise. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used The efficiency of management functions applied to materials is measured using: - **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** — bold standard metric - **Carrying Cost Percentage** — bold standard metric - **Order Lead Time (OLT)** — bold standard metric - **Stock-out Frequency** — bold standard metric - **Purchase Price Variance (PPV)** — bold standard metric > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The functions of management when applied to materials form the *backbone* of materials management, directly supporting Course Outcomes related to planning, procurement strategy, and cost optimization in industrial engineering. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Core Functions Applied to Materials #### 2.1.1 PLANNING (in Materials Context) Planning is the *foremost* function. In materials management, it involves: - **Materials Planning**: Determining types and quantities of materials required - **Forecasting Demand**: Using historical data, market trends, and production schedules - **Budgeting**: Allocating financial resources for procurement - **Make-or-Buy Decisions**: Whether to manufacture in-house or purchase - **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)**: Time-phased planning - **Sourcing Strategy**: Single vs. multiple suppliers, local vs. global **Why?** To avoid both stock-outs (lost production) and overstocking (blocked capital). #### 2.1.2 ORGANIZING (in Materials Context) Establishing a formal structure for materials flow: - **Departmental Structure**: Creating Purchase, Stores, and Inventory Control departments - **Authority & Responsibility**: Defining reporting hierarchies - **Workflow Design**: Establishing standard operating procedures (SOPs) - **Layout Planning**: Designing warehouses and storage facilities - **Material Classification**: ABC, FSN, HML, SDE analysis **Why?** To ensure seamless flow with clear accountability. #### 2.1.3 STAFFING (in Materials Context) Recruiting and developing the materials workforce: - **Manpower Planning**: Estimating required personnel - **Recruitment & Selection**: Hiring purchase officers, storekeepers - **Training & Development**: ERP systems, negotiation skills, safety - **Performance Appraisal**: KPIs for materials team #### 2.1.4 DIRECTING (in Materials Context) Activating the workforce to execute materials plans: - **Leadership**: Motivating the materials team - **Communication**: Clear instructions, briefings - **Motivation**: Incentives for cost savings - **Supervision**: Day-to-day oversight of stores operations #### 2.1.5 CONTROLLING (in Materials Context) The regulatory function — ensuring plans are executed as intended: - **Inventory Control**: Setting min, max, reorder levels - **Cost Control**: Monitoring material costs vs. budget - **Quality Control**: Incoming material inspection - **Variance Analysis**: Standard vs. actual consumption - **Audit**: Physical stock verification - **Performance Measurement**: KPIs and dashboards ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | D = Annual demand, S = Ordering cost/order, H = Holding cost/unit/year | Units | Minimizes total inventory cost | | Reorder Level (ROL) | $ROL = \text{Max. Lead Time Demand} = D_{\max} \times LT_{\max}$ | D = Daily demand, LT = Lead time in days | Units | Triggers fresh order | | Safety Stock (SS) | $SS = \text{Max. Usage} \times \text{Max. LT} - \text{Avg. Usage} \times \text{Avg. LT}$ | Usage rate, Lead time | Units | Buffer against uncertainty | | Inventory Turnover Ratio | $ITR = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | COGS = Cost of Goods Sold | Times/year | Efficiency measure | | Carrying Cost % | $CC\% = \dfrac{\text{Annual Holding Cost}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}} \times 100$ | Holding cost, Inventory value | % | Storage cost efficiency | | Total Inventory Cost (TIC) | $TIC = D \cdot P + \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$ | P = Unit price | Currency/year | Optimal ordering decision | | Material Cost Variance | $MCV = (SQ \times SP) - (AQ \times AP)$ | SQ/AQ = Standard/Actual Qty, SP/AP = Standard/Actual Price | Currency | Cost control | | Order Lead Time | $OLT = \text{Processing Time} + \text{Supplier Time} + \text{Transit Time}$ | Various | Days | Planning parameter | | Break-Even for Make-or-Buy | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Setup Cost}}{\text{Buy Price} - \text{Variable Make Cost}}$ | Fixed cost, prices | Units | Make/Buy decision | | ABC Classification | $\text{Cumulative \% Value} = \sum \frac{\text{Item Value}}{\text{Total Value}} \times 100$ | Item value, Total value | % | Stock prioritization | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These functions and formulas underpin **ERP systems** (SAP, Oracle, Tally), **Just-in-Time (JIT)** manufacturing, **Lean management**, and **Supply Chain Management (SCM)** — all critical in modern industries from automotive to pharmaceuticals. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) The EOQ model is fundamental in *controlling* function of materials management. Let us derive it exhaustively. **Step 1: Define the components of Total Annual Cost (TIC)** The total cost of inventory comprises three parts: - **Purchasing Cost** = $D \times P$ (constant for given D) - **Ordering Cost** = $\dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$ (decreases as Q increases) - **Holding Cost** = $\dfrac{Q}{2} \times H$ (increases as Q increases) **Step 2: Combine into Total Cost Equation** $$ TIC = D \cdot P + \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H $$ Since $D \cdot P$ is constant (independent of Q), we minimize the **Variable Total Cost**: $$ TVC = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2} $$ **Step 3: Differentiate TVC with respect to Q and set to zero** $$ \frac{d(TVC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0 $$ **Step 4: Solve for Q** $$ \frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} $$ $$ Q^2 = \frac{2 D S}{H} $$ $$ Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} $$ **Step 5: Verify with second derivative (minimum confirmation)** $$ \frac{d^2(TVC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D S}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{(confirms minimum)} $$ **Final EOQ Formula:** $$ EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} $$ ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example:** A manufacturing unit consumes **12,000 units/year** of a raw material. Ordering cost is **₹200/order** and holding cost is **₹10/unit/year**. Determine EOQ, number of orders, time between orders, and total cost (unit price ₹50). **Given:** - $D = 12{,}000$ units/year - $S = ₹200$ per order - $H = ₹10$ per unit/year - $P = ₹50$ per unit **Step 1: Calculate EOQ** $$ EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 200}{10}} $$ $$ EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}800{,}000}{10}} = \sqrt{480{,}000} $$ $$ EOQ \approx 692.82 \text{ units} \approx 693 \text{ units} $$ **Step 2: Number of orders per year** $$ N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{12{,}000}{693} \approx 17.32 \text{ orders/year} $$ **Step 3: Time between orders (assuming 365 working days)** $$ T_{BO} = \frac{365}{N} = \frac{365}{17.32} \approx 21.07 \text{ days} $$ **Step 4: Total Annual Inventory Cost** $$ TIC = D \cdot P + \frac{D \cdot S}{EOQ} + \frac{EOQ \cdot H}{2} $$ $$ TIC = (12{,}000 \times 50) + \frac{12{,}000 \times 200}{693} + \frac{693 \times 10}{2} $$ $$ TIC = 600{,}000 + 3{,}464.30 + 3{,}465 $$ $$ TIC \approx ₹6{,}06{,}929.30 \text{ per year} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functions of Management Applied to Materials | Function | Material Activity | Tools/Techniques | Output/Outcome | KTU Exam Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Planning** | Demand forecasting, MRP, budgeting | EOQ, ROP, MPS, BOM | Material plan, budget | High (4–6 marks) | | **Organizing** | Setting up stores, departments, workflows | ABC analysis, layout design, SOPs | Structure & authority chart | Medium (3–4 marks) | | **Staffing** | Recruitment of purchase/stores staff | Manpower planning, training | Skilled workforce | Low (2–3 marks) | | **Directing** | Leading, motivating, communicating | Leadership styles, incentives | Executed plans | Low (2–3 marks) | | **Controlling** | Monitoring costs, inventory, quality | Variance analysis, ITR, audit | Corrective actions, KPIs | High (5–7 marks) | | **Coordinating** | Linking purchase, stores, production | ERP, inter-departmental meetings | Seamless flow | Medium (3 marks) | ### 3.4 Derivation of Reorder Level (ROL) **Step 1: Identify Maximum Lead Time Demand** During lead time, consumption continues at its maximum rate: $$ ROL = \text{Maximum Lead Time} \times \text{Maximum Daily Usage} $$ **Step 2: Incorporate Safety Stock for uncertainty** $$ ROL = \text{Max. LT} \times \text{Max. Usage} + \text{Safety Stock} $$ **Step 3: Average case formula** $$ ROL = \text{Average Lead Time} \times \text{Average Usage} + \text{Safety Stock} $$ This ROL is a *planning* output used in the *controlling* function to trigger purchase orders. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Management Functions Applied to Materials ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Top Management
Strategic Vision] --> B[PLANNING
Demand Forecast, MRP, Budget] B --> C[ORGANIZING
Dept Structure, Workflow, Layout] C --> D[STAFFING
Recruit, Train Materials Team] D --> E[DIRECTING
Lead, Motivate, Communicate] E --> F[CONTROLLING
Inventory, Cost, Quality Control] F --> G[COORDINATING
Purchase-Store-Production Link] G --> H[Material Flow
Right Item, Qty, Quality, Time, Place, Cost] H --> I[Feedback Loop
Variance Analysis & Reporting] I --> B J[Suppliers] --> K[Purchase Dept] K --> L[Stores / Warehouse] L --> M[Production Dept] M --> N[Finished Goods] N --> O[Customers] H -.-> K H -.-> L H -.-> M ``` ### 4.2 Material Planning & Control Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Demand Forecast] --> P2[Material Requirement Planning MRP] P2 --> P3[Bill of Materials BOM] P3 --> P4[Inventory Status Check] P4 --> P5{Stock Available?} P5 -->|Yes| P6[Issue to Production] P5 -->|No| P7[Calculate Reorder Quantity] P7 --> P8[Apply EOQ Model] P8 --> P9[Generate Purchase Order] P9 --> P10[Vendor Selection] P10 --> P11[Receive Materials] P11 --> P12[Quality Inspection] P12 --> P13[Store in Warehouse] P13 --> P6 P6 --> P14[Production Use] P14 --> P15[Variance Analysis] P15 --> P16[Feedback to Planning] P16 --> P1 ``` ### 4.3 Inter-Departmental Coordination Structure ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TopMgmt[Top Management] TMG[Strategic Decisions] end subgraph MatDept[Materials Management Department] PUR[Purchase Department] STO[Stores Department] IC[Inventory Control] QC[Quality Control] end subgraph OtherDept[Other Departments] PROD[Production] FIN[Finance] SALES[Sales / Marketing] ENG[Engineering / R and D] end TMG --> MatDept PUR <--> STO PUR <--> IC STO <--> QC IC <--> FIN PUR --> PROD STO --> PROD SALES --> IC ENG --> PUR FIN --> PUR ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Insight:** The three Mermaid diagrams collectively illustrate how the **five functions of management** form a closed-loop, cyclical system. The feedback arrow in Diagram 4.1 represents the *controlling* function feeding corrective signals back to *planning* — a hallmark of effective management. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the six R's of materials management. Why is it considered the *controlling* function's goal? **Model Answer:** The **6R's of Materials Management** are: 1. **Right Quality** 2. **Right Quantity** 3. **Right Time** 4. **Right Place** 5. **Right Price** 6. **Right Source (Supplier)** These six R's define the objectives of the *controlling* function because controlling ensures that material flow meets the pre-set standards (planned quality, quantity, cost). Variance from any R triggers corrective action. Thus, achieving 6R compliance is the success metric of controlling. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any three functions of management as applied to materials, with one example of each. **Model Answer:** | Function | Example Applied to Materials | |---|---| | **Planning** | Forecasting annual demand for raw materials using historical data | | **Organizing** | Establishing a separate Purchase Department with defined hierarchy | | **Controlling** | Setting reorder level and conducting periodic physical stock verification | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the five functions of management as applied to materials management in a manufacturing industry. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A factory uses 8,000 units of a component annually. The ordering cost is ₹150 per order and the holding cost is ₹8 per unit per year. The unit price is ₹40. Calculate: (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) (ii) Number of orders per year (iii) Time between two orders (assume 300 working days) (iv) Total annual inventory cost **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a): The five functions of management as applied to materials are: **1. Planning:** - Involves forecasting demand, preparing material budgets, MRP schedules - Decides *what*, *how much*, and *when* to procure - Tools: Demand forecasting, EOQ, MRP, BOM - *Example:* A steel plant planning annual coal requirements **2. Organizing:** - Establishing the materials management department - Defining roles — Purchase Manager, Storekeeper, Inventory Controller - Designing warehouse layout, classifying stores (ABC analysis) - *Example:* Creating SOPs for goods receipt **3. Staffing:** - Recruitment of qualified purchase officers, storekeepers - Training on ERP systems, safety, vendor management - Performance appraisal using KPIs - *Example:* Hiring a SAP-MM consultant **4. Directing:** - Leading and motivating the materials team - Communicating purchase policies, inventory targets - Resolving conflicts between purchase and production - *Example:* Daily stand-up meetings in stores **5. Controlling:** - Monitoring inventory levels vs. ROL - Variance analysis (standard vs. actual consumption) - Periodic audits, ABC analysis, cost control - *Example:* Reviewing ITR quarterly and taking corrective action > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often list only 4 functions (omitting Staffing or Coordinating). **Always write 5–6 functions** to score full marks. Avoid generic definitions — *anchor each function with a material-specific example* to earn higher cognitive-level marks. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b): **Given Data:** - $D = 8{,}000$ units/year - $S = ₹150$ per order - $H = ₹8$ per unit/year - $P = ₹40$ per unit - Working days = 300 **(i) EOQ Calculation:** $$ EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 150}{8}} $$ $$ EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2{,}400{,}000}{8}} = \sqrt{300{,}000} $$ $$ EOQ \approx 547.72 \approx 548 \text{ units} $$ **(ii) Number of orders per year:** $$ N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{8{,}000}{548} \approx 14.6 \text{ orders/year} $$ **(iii) Time between orders:** $$ T_{BO} = \frac{300}{14.6} \approx 20.55 \text{ days} $$ **(iv) Total Annual Inventory Cost:** $$ TIC = D \cdot P + \frac{D \cdot S}{EOQ} + \frac{EOQ \cdot H}{2} $$ $$ TIC = (8{,}000 \times 40) + \frac{8{,}000 \times 150}{548} + \frac{548 \times 8}{2} $$ $$ TIC = 320{,}000 + 2{,}189.78 + 2{,}192 $$ $$ \boxed{TIC \approx ₹3{,}24{,}381.78 \text{ per year}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** Discuss the role of the **'Controlling'** function in materials management. List and briefly explain any four inventory control techniques used in industrial practice. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The annual demand for a spare part is 6,000 units. The cost of placing one order is ₹250 and the inventory carrying cost is 20% of the unit cost. If the unit cost of the spare is ₹100, determine: (i) EOQ (ii) Total ordering cost per year (iii) Total carrying cost per year (iv) Minimum total cost **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a): **Role of Controlling Function in Materials Management:** Controlling ensures that actual material performance conforms to planned material performance. It is the **regulatory function** that closes the management loop. Its roles include: - Maintaining optimum inventory levels - Minimizing stock-outs and overstocking - Reducing material wastage and pilferage - Ensuring cost discipline - Providing feedback for future planning **Four Inventory Control Techniques:** 1. **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity):** Determines the optimal order size that minimizes combined ordering and holding costs. 2. **ABC Analysis (Always Better Control):** Classifies inventory into A (10–20% items, 70–80% value), B (30% items, 15–25% value), C (50% items, 5–10% value) for prioritized control. 3. **FSN Analysis (Fast/Slow/Non-moving):** Categorizes inventory by consumption rate. Fast-moving items are reordered frequently; non-moving items are flagged for disposal. 4. **VED Analysis (Vital/Essential/Desirable):** Used for spare parts management — Vital items are tightly controlled; Desirable items have looser control. 5. **SDE Analysis (Scarce/Difficult/Easy):** Based on procurement difficulty. 6. **HML Analysis (High/Medium/Low):** Based on unit price. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write ABC analysis as a "classification" without explaining *how* the cumulative percentage drives the classification. **Always show the cumulative % computation logic** to score the 7 marks. --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b): **Given Data:** - $D = 6{,}000$ units/year - $S = ₹250$ per order - $H = 20\% \text{ of } ₹100 = ₹20$ per unit/year - $P = ₹100$ per unit **(i) EOQ Calculation:** $$ EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6{,}000 \times 250}{20}} = \sqrt{\frac{3{,}000{,}000}{20}} = \sqrt{150{,}000} $$ $$ \boxed{EOQ \approx 387.30 \approx 388 \text{ units}} $$ **(ii) Total Ordering Cost per Year:** $$ TOC = \frac{D \cdot S}{EOQ} = \frac{6{,}000 \times 250}{388} \approx ₹3{,}865.98 $$ **(iii) Total Carrying Cost per Year:** $$ TCC = \frac{EOQ \cdot H}{2} = \frac{388 \times 20}{2} = ₹3{,}880 $$ **(iv) Minimum Total Inventory Cost:** At EOQ, ordering cost equals carrying cost: $$ TIC_{\min} = D \cdot P + \sqrt{2 D S H} $$ $$ TIC_{\min} = (6{,}000 \times 100) + \sqrt{2 \times 6{,}000 \times 250 \times 20} $$ $$ TIC_{\min} = 600{,}000 + \sqrt{60{,}000{,}000} $$ $$ TIC_{\min} = 600{,}000 + 7{,}745.97 $$ $$ \boxed{TIC_{\min} \approx ₹6{,}07{,}745.97 \text{ per year}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification Principle:** At optimum EOQ, Total Ordering Cost ≈ Total Carrying Cost. In the above, ₹3,866 ≈ ₹3,880 ✓ (minor rounding difference). Examiners award bonus marks for showing this verification step. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** - ✅ **Five functions of management:** Planning, Organizing, Staffing, Directing, Controlling (plus Coordinating in modern texts) - ✅ **6R's of Materials:** Right Quality, Quantity, Time, Place, Price, Source - ✅ **EOQ Formula:** $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ — derived by minimizing TVC - ✅ **ROL Formula:** $ROL = \text{Max. Lead Time} \times \text{Max. Usage} + \text{Safety Stock}$ - ✅ **Safety Stock:** Buffer for demand and lead-time uncertainty - ✅ **ITR:** $\frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ — higher is better - ✅ **ABC Analysis:** A-items = tight control; C-items = loose control - ✅ **FSN/VED/SDE/HML:** All are inventory classification techniques - ✅ **TIC Equation:** $TIC = DP + \frac{DS}{Q} + \frac{QH}{2}$ - ✅ **At EOQ:** Ordering Cost = Carrying Cost (key verification principle) - ✅ **Lead Time Components:** Processing + Supplier + Transit time - ✅ **Make-or-Buy Break-Even:** $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Setup Cost}}{\text{Buy Price} - \text{Variable Make Cost}}$ - ✅ **Variance Analysis:** Material Cost Variance = $(SQ \times SP) - (AQ \times AP)$ - ✅ **Always use material-specific examples** in 14-mark answers (not generic management definitions) - ✅ **Mention coordination** between purchase, stores, and production for full marks in modern KTU papers - ✅ **Unit consistency** — keep demand annual, EOQ in units, costs in currency

topics.title: Addressing the Conflicting Objectives in Materials Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Addressing the Conflicting Objectives in Materials Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conflicting Objectives in Materials Management** refer to the inherent, structurally opposing goals pursued by different functional departments (Production, Finance, Purchasing, Marketing, Warehousing) within an organization, each of which seeks to optimize its own sub-system at the cost of the overall organizational efficiency.

The phenomenon of conflicting objectives arises because **Materials Management** is a cross-functional discipline. No single department "owns" the materials function entirely. Therefore, each stakeholder optimizes its local Key Performance Indicator (KPI), producing systemic sub-optimization unless reconciled through a coordinated, integrated materials management framework.

### 1.2 The Core Conflicting Functions

| Department | Primary Objective | Materials Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Production** | Uninterrupted manufacturing | High inventory levels, large buffer stocks |
| **Finance** | Liquidity & low capital lock-up | Minimum inventory, high inventory turnover |
| **Purchasing** | Low unit cost & vendor goodwill | Bulk procurement, large lot sizes, EOQ maximization |
| **Marketing/Sales** | High service level, customer delight | High finished-goods inventory, wide SKU range |
| **Warehousing** | Low space & handling cost | Small lot sizes, frequent deliveries |
| **Quality** | Zero defects | Vendor inspection, AQL buffers, safety stock |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Family Kitchen:** Imagine five members of a family trying to manage the kitchen together. The **cook** (Production) wants a fully stocked fridge to cook any recipe anytime. The **accountant** (Finance) wants to buy only what's needed today to save money. The **bulk buyer** (Purchasing) wants to buy 25 kg of rice at wholesale discount. The **host** (Marketing) wants extra snacks ready for surprise guests. The **cleaner** (Warehousing) wants a small, organized fridge. None of them are *wrong* individually — but together, the kitchen either overflows or runs empty. The solution is a *coordinated plan* (integrated materials management) where trade-offs are explicitly balanced.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in Reconciliation

The following **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** are universally used to balance conflicting objectives:

- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** = $\dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold}}{\text{Average Inventory Value}}$
- **Customer Service Level (CSL)** = $\dfrac{\text{Number of orders met on time}}{\text{Total number of orders}} \times 100\%$
- **Carrying Cost** = typically **15% – 25%** of average inventory value per year
- **Ordering Cost** = cost per purchase order (typically **₹500 – ₹5000**)
- **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** = $\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The reconciliation is achieved through scientific techniques like **EOQ**, **ABC Analysis**, **VED Analysis**, **SDE Analysis**, **FSN Analysis**, **MRP**, **JIT**, and **Just-in-Time replenishment systems**, all of which provide a quantitative common ground for conflicting stakeholders.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Conflicting Objectives Exist — Root-Cause Analysis

The conflicts are not arbitrary; they emerge from **three structural sources**:

1. **Asymmetric Cost Visibility** — Each department only sees its own cost line. Production sees downtime cost; Finance sees capital cost; Purchasing sees order cost.
2. **Differing Time Horizons** — Purchasing plans annually; production plans weekly; finance plans daily.
3. **Misaligned Performance Incentives** — Departmental KPIs reward local optimization.

### 2.2 The Six Major Conflicts and Their Resolution Framework

#### Conflict 1: **Production vs. Finance — Stock Level**

- **Production View:** Large inventory → no stockouts → no downtime → higher machine utilization.
- **Finance View:** Small inventory → less working capital → better ROI.
- **Resolution:** Adopt the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** model, which mathematically identifies the *minimum total cost point* where ordering cost equals carrying cost.

#### Conflict 2: **Purchasing vs. Warehousing — Lot Size**

- **Purchasing View:** Larger lots → fewer orders → lower ordering cost per unit → better vendor terms.
- **Warehousing View:** Smaller lots → less space → less obsolescence → lower carrying cost.
- **Resolution:** Use **EOQ with quantity discounts** and **storage capacity constraints** to arrive at a feasible compromise.

#### Conflict 3: **Production vs. Marketing — Variety vs. Standardization**

- **Production View:** Standardized parts → longer runs → lower setup cost → lower unit cost.
- **Marketing View:** Maximum variety → satisfies every customer → higher market share.
- **Resolution:** Apply **ABC Analysis** — high-value 'A' items get maximum variety, low-value 'C' items are standardized.

#### Conflict 4: **Quality vs. Purchasing — Inspection vs. Vendor Trust**

- **Quality View:** 100% inspection → zero defect inflow.
- **Purchasing View:** Trust vendor → reduce inspection cost → faster receipt.
- **Resolution:** Implement **Vendor Rating** and **Acceptance Sampling (AQL)** plans — statistical compromise.

#### Conflict 5: **Finance vs. Marketing — Credit & Lead Time**

- **Finance View:** Tight credit, slow payment collection.
- **Marketing View:** Liberal credit, fast delivery.
- **Resolution:** **Working Capital Optimization models** balancing Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) against service level.

#### Conflict 6: **Purchasing vs. Production — Make vs. Buy**

- **Purchasing View:** Buy from external specialist → cheaper, better quality.
- **Production View:** Make in-house → control, employment, flexibility.
- **Resolution:** **Make-or-Buy Decision Matrix** using break-even analysis and strategic considerations.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Typical Unit / Range | Conflict Addressed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Units per order | Production vs. Finance |
| **Total Relevant Cost (TRC)** | $TRC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | ₹ / year | Cost trade-off |
| **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = d \cdot L + SS$ | Units | Stockout prevention |
| **Safety Stock (SS)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Units | Service level vs. cost |
| **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{Avg.\ Inv.}$ | Times per year (4–12 typical) | Finance KPI |
| **Days of Inventory** | $DOI = \dfrac{365}{ITR}$ | Days | Finance KPI |
| **Customer Service Level** | $CSL = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Stockout units}}{D}$ | Percentage (95% – 99% target) | Marketing KPI |
| **Carrying Cost Rate (i)** | $H = i \cdot C$ | 15% – 25% of item value | Holding cost |
| **Total Cost of Ownership** | $TCO = P + O + C + Q + S$ | ₹ per unit | Purchasing |
| **Vendor Rating Index** | $VR = 0.4Q + 0.3D + 0.2P + 0.1F$ | 0 – 100 scale | Quality vs. Purchasing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $D$ = Annual Demand (units), $S$ = Ordering Cost per order (₹), $H$ = Holding Cost per unit per year (₹), $Q$ = Order Quantity, $C$ = Unit cost (₹), $z$ = Service level z-score, $\sigma_{LT}$ = Standard deviation of lead time demand, $P$ = Price, $O$ = Ordering, $Q$ = Quality, $S$ = Service, $F$ = Financial stability.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **manufacturing industries** (automotive, electronics, FMCG), reconciliation of conflicting objectives directly impacts:

- **Cash Flow** — Every 10% reduction in average inventory releases capital.
- **Manufacturing Lead Time** — Optimal stock reduces bottlenecks.
- **Customer Retention** — High service level builds brand loyalty.
- **Sustainability** — Lower inventory means lower warehousing energy consumption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of EOQ — The Mathematical Foundation of Conflict Resolution

The EOQ model is the *single most important analytical tool* for resolving the **Production vs. Finance** conflict. Below is the complete derivation as expected in KTU university examinations.

**Step 1: Define the two competing cost functions.**

Let $Q$ be the order quantity in units. Annual demand is $D$ units. The number of orders placed per year is $\dfrac{D}{Q}$.

The **Annual Ordering Cost** ($OC$) is proportional to the number of orders:

$$OC(Q) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

where $S$ is the cost of placing one purchase order.

**Step 2: Define the average inventory.**

If inventory is consumed uniformly between orders, it varies linearly from $Q$ (just after receipt) to $0$ (just before next receipt). The **average inventory** is therefore $\dfrac{Q}{2}$.

The **Annual Carrying (Holding) Cost** ($HC$) is:

$$HC(Q) = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

where $H$ is the holding cost per unit per year.

**Step 3: Define the Total Relevant Cost (ignoring the constant purchase cost $D \cdot P$).**

$$TC(Q) = OC(Q) + HC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}$$

**Step 4: Minimize TC(Q) with respect to Q using calculus.**

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve the first-order condition.**

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}}$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}$$

$$\boxed{EOQ = Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

**Step 6: Verify it is a minimum (second-order test).**

$$\frac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{(always positive)}$$

Therefore, the critical point is a **global minimum**.

**Step 7: Derive the minimum total cost.**

Substituting $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ back into $TC(Q)$:

$$TC_{min} = \frac{D \cdot S}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot H}{2}$$

$$TC_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$

$$\boxed{TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot S \cdot H}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** At the optimal $Q^{*}$, the ordering cost exactly equals the carrying cost. This is the mathematical *equilibrium point* where **Production** (worried about ordering) and **Finance** (worried about carrying) are perfectly balanced. This is the fundamental reason EOQ resolves their conflict.

### 3.2 Derivation of Safety Stock Under Variable Lead Time

The lead-time demand is normally distributed with mean $\mu_{LT}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{LT}$. To meet a target service level corresponding to z-score $z$:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

**Total Reorder Point:**

$$ROP = \mu_{LT} + z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = d \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

where $d$ is the daily demand and $L$ is the lead time in days.

**Trade-off Proof:** If service level increases from 90% to 99%, $z$ jumps from **1.28** to **2.33** — an **82% increase** in safety stock. This mathematically quantifies the **Finance vs. Marketing** trade-off.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Conflict-to-Resolution Mapping

| # | Conflicting Pair | Conflict Type | Primary Tool | Secondary Tool | KPI Impacted |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Production vs. Finance | Stock level | EOQ | Safety Stock | ITR, CSL |
| 2 | Purchasing vs. Warehousing | Lot size | EOQ with discount | Storage constraint | Order cost |
| 3 | Production vs. Marketing | Variety | ABC Analysis | VED Analysis | Service level |
| 4 | Quality vs. Purchasing | Inspection | AQL Sampling | Vendor Rating | Defect rate |
| 5 | Production vs. Purchasing | Make vs. Buy | Break-even | Decision matrix | Cost, flexibility |
| 6 | Finance vs. Marketing | Credit / Lead time | Working capital model | DSO optimization | Cash flow |
| 7 | Production vs. Quality | Reject rate | Pareto analysis | Six Sigma | Yield |
| 8 | All departments | Information silos | ERP / MRP / JIT | TQM | System-wide |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Annual demand for a component is **$D = 10{,}000$ units**. Ordering cost is **$S = ₹200$ per order**. Unit cost is **$C = ₹50$** and carrying cost is **$i = 20\%$** per year. Find EOQ, total cost, number of orders per year, and optimum interval.

**Solution:**

$$H = i \cdot C = 0.20 \times 50 = ₹10 \text{ per unit per year}$$

$$EOQ = Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \text{ units}$$

$$TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 10} = \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000} = ₹6{,}324.56$$

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} = 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders/year}$$

$$T_{0} = \frac{365}{N} = \frac{365}{15.81} \approx 23.08 \text{ days between orders}$$

**Verification of equilibrium:**

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{10{,}000}{632.46} \times 200 = ₹3{,}162.28$$

$$\text{Carrying Cost} = \frac{632.46}{2} \times 10 = ₹3{,}162.28 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equity Check Confirmed:** At EOQ, the **two costs are exactly equal** — this is the mathematical reconciliation of the Production vs. Finance conflict.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conflict Topology — Departmental Stakeholder Map

The diagram below shows the *systemic nature* of the conflicts — every department conflicts with every other on at least one axis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PROD["PRODUCTION DEPT"]:::dept
    FIN["FINANCE DEPT"]:::dept
    PUR["PURCHASING DEPT"]:::dept
    MKT["MARKETING DEPT"]:::dept
    WH["WAREHOUSING DEPT"]:::dept
    QLT["QUALITY DEPT"]:::dept
    MM["INTEGRATED MATERIALS MANAGEMENT"]:::hub

    PROD -- High inventory demand --> FIN
    FIN -- Low inventory preference --> PROD
    PROD -- Long setup runs --> MKT
    MKT -- Wide variety preference --> PROD
    PUR -- Large lot size --> WH
    WH -- Small lot size --> PUR
    PUR -- 100 percent inspection --> QLT
    QLT -- Trust vendor --> PUR
    MKT -- High finished goods --> FIN
    FIN -- Working capital constraint --> MKT

    PROD --> MM
    FIN --> MM
    PUR --> MM
    MKT --> MM
    WH --> MM
    QLT --> MM

    classDef dept fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef hub fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Reconciliation Engine — How EOQ Balances the Two Cost Curves

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Annual Demand D units] --> B[Compute EOQ = sqrt 2DS over H]
    B --> C{Order Quantity Q Decision}
    C -- Q less than EOQ --> D[Many orders, low carrying cost, HIGH ordering cost]
    C -- Q equal to EOQ --> E[OPTIMAL MINIMUM TOTAL COST POINT]
    C -- Q greater than EOQ --> F[Few orders, low ordering cost, HIGH carrying cost]
    D --> G[PRODUCTION IS HAPPY FINANCE IS NOT]
    E --> H[BOTH DEPARTMENTS BALANCED]
    F --> I[FINANCE IS HAPPY PRODUCTION IS NOT]
    G --> J[Reconcile using EOQ]
    I --> J
    J --> H

    classDef opt fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef good fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    class D bad
    class F bad
    class E good
    class H good
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Resolution Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 IDENTIFY conflicting pairs] --> L2[Level 2 QUANTIFY the conflict with KPIs]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 APPLY analytical tool EOQ, ABC, VED, AQL]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 EVALUATE trade-off using cost benefit]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 IMPLEMENT via MRP ERP JIT system]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6 MONITOR with dashboards and feedback]
    L6 -- Continuous improvement --> L1

    classDef lvl fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    class L1,L2,L3,L4,L5,L6 lvl
```

### 4.4 Information Flow Under Conflict vs. Under Integration

| Dimension | Siloed (Conflicted) State | Integrated (Reconciled) State |
|---|---|---|
| Data ownership | Department-private | Shared via ERP |
| Decisions | Locally optimized | Globally optimized |
| Inventory | Either excess or shortage | Near EOQ level |
| Lead time | Long and variable | Short and predictable |
| Cost visibility | Cost line per dept | Total cost of ownership |
| Responsiveness | Slow | Fast |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define "Conflicting Objectives" in Materials Management. Why do they arise?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Conflicting objectives in materials management refer to the opposing goals of various functional departments such as Production, Finance, Purchasing, and Marketing, each of which attempts to optimize its own sub-system at the cost of overall organizational efficiency. They arise because **(1 mark)** materials management is cross-functional with no single owner, **(1 mark)** each department has asymmetric cost visibility and different time horizons, and **(1 mark)** departmental KPIs reward local optimization rather than system-wide efficiency. They are resolved through integrated tools like EOQ, ABC, MRP, and ERP.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q2. State the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) formula and explain its significance in resolving conflicts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Economic Order Quantity formula is **(1 mark)**:

$$EOQ = Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$$

where $D$ is annual demand, $S$ is ordering cost per order, and $H$ is carrying cost per unit per year. Its significance lies in **(1 mark)** mathematically identifying the order quantity that **minimizes total inventory cost** by equating ordering cost with carrying cost. This serves as a **scientific common ground** **(1 mark)** between Production (which fears stockouts) and Finance (which fears capital lock-up), thereby resolving their primary conflict.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **major conflicting objectives** in Materials Management between Production, Finance, Purchasing, and Marketing departments. Discuss how EOQ, ABC, and VED analyses help in resolving these conflicts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company uses **$D = 8{,}000$ units/year** of a raw material. Ordering cost is **$S = ₹400$ per order**, unit cost is **$C = ₹100$**, and carrying cost rate is **$i = 25\%$** per year. Calculate the EOQ, total variable cost, number of orders per year, and the optimum time between orders. Comment on the balance achieved. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

1. **Production vs. Finance (2 marks):** Production demands high buffer stock for uninterrupted operations; Finance demands minimum inventory to free working capital. *Resolution:* **EOQ** mathematically determines the minimum-total-cost point where ordering cost equals carrying cost, satisfying both stakeholders simultaneously.

2. **Purchasing vs. Warehousing (2 marks):** Purchasing prefers large lots for lower per-unit ordering cost and quantity discounts; Warehousing prefers small lots to reduce space, handling, and obsolescence. *Resolution:* **EOQ with quantity discount analysis** and storage-capacity-constrained optimization.

3. **Production vs. Marketing (1.5 marks):** Production wants standardization for long runs; Marketing wants variety to capture diverse customers. *Resolution:* **ABC Analysis** (vary 'A' items, standardize 'C' items) and **VED Analysis** (Vital items get priority).

4. **Quality vs. Purchasing (1.5 marks):** Quality insists on 100% inspection; Purchasing prefers vendor trust and minimal inspection. *Resolution:* **Acceptance Sampling (AQL)** and **Vendor Rating systems** statistically balance defect risk against inspection cost.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 8{,}000$ units/year, $S = ₹400$/order, $C = ₹100$/unit, $i = 25\%$.

**Step 1: Compute H** (1 mark)

$$H = i \cdot C = 0.25 \times 100 = ₹25 \text{ per unit per year}$$

**Step 2: Compute EOQ** (2 marks)

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 400}{25}} = \sqrt{256{,}000} \approx 505.96 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Total Variable Cost** (2 marks)

$$TC = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2} = \frac{8{,}000 \times 400}{505.96} + \frac{505.96 \times 25}{2}$$

$$TC = ₹6{,}324.56 + ₹6{,}324.56 = ₹12{,}649.11$$

**Step 4: Number of orders and time between orders** (1 mark)

$$N = \frac{D}{Q} = \frac{8{,}000}{505.96} \approx 15.81 \text{ orders/year}$$

$$T_{0} = \frac{365}{15.81} \approx 23.08 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5: Comment** (1 mark)

Since ordering cost ($₹6{,}324.56$) exactly equals carrying cost ($₹6{,}324.56$) at $Q = 506$ units, the **Production–Finance conflict is mathematically balanced**. Neither department's cost is being penalized — this is the global optimum of the system.

#### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Discuss the **"Make or Buy" decision** as a conflict between Production and Purchasing. How is the break-even analysis used to resolve it? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **MRP (Material Requirements Planning)**, **JIT (Just-in-Time)**, and **ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning)** systems act as integrative tools to address conflicting objectives. Compare their relative strengths. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Make or Buy (7 marks):**

1. **Nature of conflict (2 marks):** Production wants to *make* in-house to retain control, employment, and flexibility. Purchasing wants to *buy* from external specialists to access expertise, lower capital, and better unit cost. Both have valid economic and strategic grounds, creating a classic make-vs-buy conflict.

2. **Break-even analysis (3 marks):** Let $V$ = annual volume. Cost to make = Fixed Cost + Variable Cost × V. Cost to buy = Purchase price × V. The break-even volume $V_{BE}$ is found by equating the two:

$$V_{BE} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost}_{make} - \text{Fixed Cost}_{buy}}{\text{Variable Cost}_{buy} - \text{Variable Cost}_{make}}$$

Below $V_{BE}$, buying is cheaper; above $V_{BE}$, making is cheaper.

3. **Qualitative factors (2 marks):** Strategic importance, quality control, confidentiality, supplier reliability, and capacity utilization must also be evaluated alongside the break-even number. The Make-or-Buy Decision Matrix integrates both quantitative and qualitative dimensions.

**Part (b) — MRP, JIT, ERP (7 marks):**

1. **MRP (2.5 marks):** A push-based system that uses the Bill of Materials (BOM), Master Production Schedule (MPS), and inventory status to compute exactly *what*, *when*, and *how much* to order. It synchronizes production, purchasing, and finance by giving them a shared plan, resolving scheduling conflicts.

2. **JIT (2.5 marks):** A pull-based system where materials arrive *exactly when needed*, eliminating inventory carrying costs and aligning all departments toward a common zero-stock goal. Resolves the Production-Finance conflict by making waste visible and unacceptable to all.

3. **ERP (2 marks):** A unified software platform integrating *all* functional modules — Production, Finance, Purchasing, Marketing, Warehousing, HR — on a single database. ERP eliminates information silos, the root cause of all conflicting objectives, by enforcing data transparency and cross-functional workflows.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT skip the verification step** in EOQ problems. Always show that ordering cost = carrying cost at $Q^*$. Examiners award the verification mark explicitly.
> - **Do NOT confuse holding cost rate $i$ with holding cost $H$**. $H = i \cdot C$. Mixing them yields wrong EOQ and full mark loss.
> - **In conceptual questions, do NOT just list conflicts** — you must mention the *specific resolution tool* (EOQ, ABC, VED, AQL, etc.) for each. Listing without a tool is considered incomplete.
> - **In make-or-buy, the break-even formula must be derived**, not just stated. Marks are reserved for the algebraic manipulation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Conflicting objectives** arise from cross-functional ownership of the materials function; no single department "owns" the trade-off.
- **Six major conflicts**: Production–Finance (stock level), Purchasing–Warehousing (lot size), Production–Marketing (variety), Quality–Purchasing (inspection), Production–Purchasing (make/buy), Finance–Marketing (credit).
- **EOQ** is the *primary quantitative tool* for resolving the Production–Finance conflict.
- **EOQ Formula:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — derived by minimizing $TC = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{QH}{2}$.
- **Verification identity:** At $Q^{*}$, Ordering Cost $=$ Carrying Cost.
- **Minimum Total Cost:** $TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}$.
- **Safety Stock:** $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ — quantifies the Finance–Marketing service-level trade-off.
- **ABC Analysis** prioritizes variety decisions; **VED** prioritizes criticality; **SDE** prioritizes scarcity.
- **AQL Sampling** statistically balances Quality–Purchasing conflict.
- **Make-or-Buy break-even:** $V_{BE} = \dfrac{FC_{make} - FC_{buy}}{VC_{buy} - VC_{make}}$.
- **JIT, MRP, and ERP** are *integrative systems* that dismantle information silos.
- **Service level z-scores to remember:** 90% → 1.28, 95% → 1.65, 97.5% → 1.96, 99% → 2.33, 99.9% → 3.09.
- **Carrying cost rate** is typically **15% – 25%** of unit value per year; always state assumptions clearly.
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio** is the *Finance department's primary KPI** — higher is better (4–12 typical for manufacturing).
- **Customer Service Level** is the *Marketing department's primary KPI* — typically targeted at **95% – 99%**.
- **Always end numerical solutions** with a "comment" on whether the conflict has been balanced — this is what differentiates a KTU full-marks answer from an average one.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Specifications in Purchasing and Materials Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Role of Specifications in Purchasing and Materials Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specification** is a precise, detailed, and unambiguous written description of the physical, chemical, technical, performance, and functional characteristics of a material, product, component, or service that a buyer requires from a supplier. In the context of **Purchasing and Materials Management**, specifications act as the *technical contract* between the purchasing organization and the supplier, ensuring that what is ordered is exactly what is needed for production, safety, and quality compliance.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a specification is treated as a **strategic procurement document** that translates an organization's engineering, quality, and operational requirements into measurable attributes such as **dimensional tolerances**, **material grade**, **performance ratings**, and **quality benchmarks**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A specification is not the same as a *standard* or a *brand name*. A specification describes *what the product must do or be*, while a standard is a publicly established reference (e.g., **BIS**, **ISO**, **ASTM**), and a brand name is a *proprietary identification*. Engineers must learn to write *performance-based* and *conformance-based* specifications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a pharmacy and ask for *"a tablet for headache."* The pharmacist will ask you a series of questions — strength, active ingredient, brand or generic, time of effect, allergies. Your answers form a **specification**. Without it, you could get the wrong medicine, an overdose, or an ineffective one.

In the same way, a factory cannot simply tell a supplier *"send me some steel"* — it must specify:

- Grade of steel (e.g., **EN8**, **SS304**)
- Tensile strength (e.g., **520 MPa**)
- Dimensional tolerance (e.g., **±0.05 mm**)
- Surface finish (e.g., **Ra 1.6 µm**)
- Certification required (e.g., **ISO 9001:2015**)

Just as a doctor's prescription protects the patient, a **purchase specification** protects the organization from cost overruns, quality failures, and operational breakdowns.

## 1.3 Why Specifications Are Central to Materials Management

| Function | Direct Contribution |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Quality Assurance** | Ensures incoming materials meet engineered tolerances and standards. |
| **Cost Control** | Prevents over-specification (unnecessary cost) and under-specification (failure cost). |
| **Legal Protection** | Acts as a binding technical reference in supplier contracts. |
| **Inventory Standardization** | Reduces variety of stocked items, simplifying **ABC analysis** and **EOQ** calculations. |
| **Supplier Evaluation** | Provides objective benchmarks for vendor rating and acceptance. |
| **Communication Bridge** | Eliminates ambiguity between design, purchase, production, and quality teams. |

> [!NOTE]
> A well-written specification is the **single most powerful document** in materials management because every downstream activity — vendor selection, price negotiation, incoming inspection, inventory storage, and even **disposal** — depends on it.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Classify the types of specifications."* Master all **six types** below.

### 2.1.1 Brand Name Specification
Specifies the product by manufacturer's brand or trade name.

- **Pros:** Simple, assumes proven quality.
- **Cons:** Restricts competition, may raise cost, limits substitutes.
- **Use case:** Specialty items with no equivalent alternatives (e.g., proprietary bearings).

### 2.1.2 Physical/Chemical Specification
Describes the **physical** (dimensions, weight, color, finish) and **chemical** (composition, purity, grade) properties.

- Example: *"Mild Steel bar, 16 mm diameter, Fe 415 grade, IS 1786:2008."*

### 2.1.3 Performance Specification
Defines what the product **must do** under stated conditions, not how it is made.

- Example: *"Motor must deliver 5 kW at 1440 rpm with 90% efficiency at full load."*
- **Advantage:** Encourages supplier innovation, broader vendor base.

### 2.1.4 Conformance Specification
States that the product must **conform to an established standard** (BIS, ISO, ASTM, DIN, JIS).

- Example: *"Bolts conforming to IS 1363 Part 1."*

### 2.1.5 Engineering/Drawing Specification
Supplements text with detailed **engineering drawings**, tolerances, GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing), and assembly schematics.

- Dominant in **OEM manufacturing** and **fabrication industries**.

### 2.1.6 Quality/Inspection Specification
Defines the **inspection method, sampling plan, and acceptance criteria** (e.g., **AQL** — Acceptable Quality Level).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step of the Specification Process

| Step | Action | Why It Matters |
|:----:|:-------|:---------------|
| 1 | **Identify the need** from engineering / production plan | Avoids impulse buying and emergency purchases. |
| 2 | **Draft specification** with input from design, quality, production | Captures multi-disciplinary requirements accurately. |
| 3 | **Review & validate** with cross-functional team | Eliminates contradictions and over-specification. |
| 4 | **Approve & version-control** the specification | Ensures only the latest version is circulated. |
| 5 | **Float to vendors** along with RFQ | Enables accurate, competitive quotations. |
| 6 | **Inspect incoming material** against specification | Closes the quality loop. |

## 2.3 Key Performance Equations in Specification-Driven Procurement

Although specifications are primarily a *qualitative* management tool, several **cost and quality metrics** are mathematically linked to how well specifications are written.

### 2.3.1 Cost of Poor Specification (CoPS)
A simplified engineering economics model used to quantify losses from inadequate specifications:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{reject}} + C_{\text{rework}} + C_{\text{delay}} + C_{\text{warranty}} + C_{\text{downtime}}
$$

Where:

- $C_{\text{reject}}$ = cost of rejected batches
- $C_{\text{rework}}$ = cost of in-house modifications
- $C_{\text{delay}}$ = production downtime cost
- $C_{\text{warranty}}$ = warranty / claim cost
- $C_{\text{downtime}}$ = loss from machine / line stoppage

### 2.3.2 Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) under Specification
A clear specification helps compute the **true life-cycle cost**:

$$
TCO = P_{\text{unit}} + C_{\text{inventory}} + C_{\text{inspection}} + C_{\text{operating}} + C_{\text{disposal}}
$$

### 2.3.3 Acceptance Sampling Linkage
Specifications dictate the **Acceptable Quality Level (AQL)** used in **ISO 2859-1** sampling tables:

$$
LTPD \le \text{Specified AQL} \le \text{Producer's Risk Point}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A tighter specification (lower AQL) increases $C_{\text{inspection}}$ but reduces $C_{\text{reject}}$ and $C_{\text{warranty}}$. The optimum lies at the minimum of $C_{\text{total}}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **automotive industry**, OEM manufacturers like Tata Motors or Maruti Suzuki issue detailed engineering specifications to tier-1 and tier-2 suppliers. A deviation of **0.01 mm** in a crankshaft bearing can cause catastrophic engine failure. In **construction**, BIS specifications for cement (IS 269, IS 8112) and TMT bars (IS 1786) directly determine structural safety. In **pharmaceuticals**, the **Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP)** and **US Pharmacopoeia (USP)** act as binding specifications for every active ingredient.

> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"How do specifications reduce cost?"*, answer using the **TCO framework** — not just purchase price.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Specification Tightness

Many students think *"tighter is always better."* This is mathematically **false**. Let us derive the optimum specification tolerance.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define Cost of Inspection
As specifications tighten, the probability of rejection in **sampling inspection** rises. The inspection cost is:

$$
C_{\text{insp}}(t) = C_{0} \cdot \left( \frac{t_{0}}{t} \right)^{k}
$$

Where:

- $C_{0}$ = reference inspection cost at tolerance $t_{0}$
- $t$ = current specification tolerance (smaller $t$ = tighter spec)
- $k$ = sensitivity exponent, typically $1.0 \le k \le 2.0$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define Cost of Quality Failure
Failure cost (rejection, rework, warranty, downtime) **decreases** as tolerance tightens:

$$
C_{\text{fail}}(t) = \frac{C_{f0} \cdot t}{t_{0}}
$$

Where $C_{f0}$ is the failure cost at the loosest tolerance.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Total Cost Function

$$
C_{\text{total}}(t) = C_{\text{insp}}(t) + C_{\text{fail}}(t) = C_{0} \cdot \left( \frac{t_{0}}{t} \right)^{k} + \frac{C_{f0} \cdot t}{t_{0}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Differentiate and Equate to Zero

$$
\frac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dt} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left[ C_{0} \cdot \left( \frac{t_{0}}{t} \right)^{k} \right] + \frac{d}{dt}\left[ \frac{C_{f0} \cdot t}{t_{0}} \right] = 0
$$

$$
- k \cdot C_{0} \cdot \frac{t_{0}^{k}}{t^{k+1}} + \frac{C_{f0}}{t_{0}} = 0
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Solve for Optimum Tolerance $t^{*}$

$$
k \cdot C_{0} \cdot \frac{t_{0}^{k}}{(t^{*})^{k+1}} = \frac{C_{f0}}{t_{0}}
$$

$$
(t^{*})^{k+1} = \frac{k \cdot C_{0} \cdot t_{0}^{k+1}}{C_{f0}}
$$

$$
\boxed{t^{*} = t_{0} \cdot \left( \frac{k \cdot C_{0}}{C_{f0}} \right)^{\frac{1}{k+1}}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation
- If **failure cost $C_{f0}$ is high** (e.g., aerospace, nuclear) → $t^{*}$ becomes very small → **tighter spec justified**.
- If **inspection cost $C_{0}$ is high** (e.g., low-value consumables) → $t^{*}$ is larger → **looser spec acceptable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **classic KTU long-answer favourite** and demonstrates *why specification writing is an economic decision, not just a technical one.*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Specification Impact on Materials Management Functions

| Materials Management Function | With Clear Specification | Without Specification |
|:------------------------------|:-------------------------|:----------------------|
| **Purchasing** | Competitive, accurate quotations; fewer disputes. | Inflated prices, ambiguity, supplier disputes. |
| **Inventory Control** | Standardization reduces SKUs; **ABC analysis** sharpens. | Variety explosion, high carrying cost, obsolescence. |
| **Quality Control** | Objective AQL-based inspection. | Subjective rejection / acceptance conflicts. |
| **Vendor Development** | Clear benchmark for vendor rating and improvement. | Vendor cannot be evaluated fairly. |
| **Stores & Warehousing** | Predictable storage conditions, stack height, shelf life known. | Damages, mis-stacking, accidents. |
| **Cost Accounting** | Standard costing possible. | Frequent price and usage variances. |
| **Disposal / Scrap** | Specified end-of-life criteria. | Legal and environmental compliance risk. |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A factory purchases steel bushes. The current specification is **±0.10 mm** tolerance, with $C_{0} = \text{₹} 200$ per batch inspection cost and $C_{f0} = \text{₹} 5000$ per batch failure cost. With sensitivity exponent $k = 1.5$, find the **optimum tolerance**.

### Solution

$$
t^{*} = t_{0} \cdot \left( \frac{k \cdot C_{0}}{C_{f0}} \right)^{\frac{1}{k+1}}
$$

Substituting $t_{0} = 0.10$, $k = 1.5$, $C_{0} = 200$, $C_{f0} = 5000$:

$$
t^{*} = 0.10 \cdot \left( \frac{1.5 \times 200}{5000} \right)^{\frac{1}{2.5}}
$$

$$
t^{*} = 0.10 \cdot \left( \frac{300}{5000} \right)^{0.4}
$$

$$
t^{*} = 0.10 \cdot (0.06)^{0.4}
$$

$$
(0.06)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \cdot \ln(0.06)} = e^{0.4 \times (-2.8134)} = e^{-1.1254} = 0.3247
$$

$$
t^{*} = 0.10 \times 0.3247 = 0.03247 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{t^{*} \approx 0.0325 \text{ mm}}
$$

This means the **optimum specification tolerance is ±0.0325 mm**, not ±0.10 mm. Loosening the spec to ±0.10 mm wastes inspection money; tightening it further invites prohibitive inspection costs. This is the **engineering economics of specification writing**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
