# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (CO3, Bloom Level: Remember)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

> **Define the term "Budget" as applied to materials management. List any FOUR objectives of budgetary control.**

**Model Answer:**

A **budget** is a quantitative financial plan, prepared and approved prior to a defined accounting period, that outlines the estimated revenues and expenditures relating to materials acquisition, storage, and usage for achieving organizational objectives.

**Four Objectives of Budgetary Control:**

1. **Planning** — Forces management to plan material requirements scientifically rather than reactively.
2. **Coordination** — Aligns procurement, production, stores, and finance departments toward unified targets.
3. **Cost Control** — Establishes benchmarks against which actual expenditure is measured for variance reduction.
4. **Performance Evaluation** — Provides objective yardsticks for appraising departmental and individual efficiency.

---

### Question 2 (CO3, Bloom Level: Understand)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **Explain the difference between "Material Price Variance" and "Material Usage Variance".**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Material Price Variance (MPV) | Material Usage Variance (MUV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $(SP - AP) \times AQ$ | $(SQ - AQ) \times SP$ |
| **Cause** | Procurement / Purchase department | Production / Shop floor |
| **Responsibility** | Buying officer, vendor selection | Production supervisor, machine efficiency |
| **What it measures** | Deviation in **purchase price** paid | Deviation in **quantity consumed** |
| **Example trigger** | Vendor price hike, market fluctuation | Tool wear, scrap, operator inefficiency |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse AQ and SQ in MUV. **MUV uses AQ consumed (not purchased)** and SP (not AP). Memorizing the formula's symmetry (price on AQ, usage on SP) avoids this trap.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **various types of budgets** used in a manufacturing organization with reference to materials management. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A factory consumes **8,000 kg/year** of a raw material. The ordering cost is **₹400 per order** and the carrying cost is **₹16 per kg/year**. Calculate:
>  (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
>  (ii) Number of orders per year
>  (iii) Time between two consecutive orders
>  (iv) Minimum total annual inventory cost. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a): Types of Budgets (7 marks)

Budgets in a manufacturing organization can be classified along three major dimensions:

**(i) Functional Classification:**
- **Sales Budget** — Forecasts revenue; the starting point of the master budget.
- **Production Budget** — Translates sales forecast into units to be produced.
- **Materials Budget** — Quantifies raw material needs and ₹ allocation.
- **Labour Budget** — Man-hours and wages for direct/indirect workers.
- **Overhead Budget** — Fixed + variable factory overheads.
- **Capital Expenditure Budget** — Long-term asset investments (handling equipment).
- **Cash Budget** — Period-wise inflow and outflow of cash.

**(ii) Time-Horizon Classification:**
- Long-range (3–5 years), Annual, Quarterly, Monthly, and Rolling budgets (continuously updated).

**(iii) Behavioral Classification:**
- **Top-Down**, **Bottom-Up**, **Zero-Based**, and **Incremental** approaches.

For materials management specifically, the **Materials Purchase Budget** is the most critical. It is prepared by multiplying:

$$
\text{Materials Budget} = \sum (\text{Required Quantity} \times \text{Standard Price}) + \text{Safety Stock Allowance}
$$

This budget then drives procurement authorization limits and vendor negotiations.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b): Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 8{,}000 \text{ kg/yr}$
- $S = ₹400/\text{order}$
- $H = ₹16/\text{kg/yr}$

**(i) Economic Order Quantity:**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 400}{16}} = \sqrt{\frac{6{,}400{,}000}{16}} = \sqrt{400{,}000} = 632.46 \text{ kg}
$$

**EOQ ≈ 632.5 kg (or 633 kg rounded).**

**(ii) Number of Orders per Year:**

$$
N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{8{,}000}{632.46} \approx 12.65 \text{ orders/yr}
$$

**(iii) Time Between Orders:**

Assuming 365 working days/year:

$$
T = \frac{365}{N} = \frac{365}{12.65} \approx 28.85 \text{ days}
$$

**Or, in years:** $T = \dfrac{1}{N} = \dfrac{1}{12.65} \approx 0.079 \text{ years} \approx 28.85 \text{ days}$.

**(iv) Minimum Total Annual Inventory Cost:**

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 400 \times 16} = \sqrt{102{,}400{,}000}
$$

$$
TC_{min} = ₹10{,}119.53/\text{yr} \approx ₹10{,}120/\text{yr}
$$

**Audit Check:**
$$
\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{D}{EOQ} \times S = 12.65 \times 400 = ₹5{,}060
$$
$$
\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{EOQ}{2} \times H = \frac{632.46}{2} \times 16 = ₹5{,}059.68
$$
$$
\text{Sum} = ₹10{,}119.68 \approx ₹10{,}120 \checkmark
$$

Both costs are equal — confirms EOQ correctness.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Materials Requirement Planning (MRP)**. With a neat block diagram, describe the **inputs, processing logic, and outputs** of an MRP system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The standard quantity of material required for producing one unit of product is **10 kg** at a standard price of **₹40 per kg**. During a particular month, **1,000 units** were produced using **10,500 kg** of material at an actual price of **₹42 per kg**.
> Calculate:
>  (i) Material Cost Variance
>  (ii) Material Price Variance
>  (iii) Material Usage Variance
> Also state whether each variance is **Favorable or Unfavorable**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a): MRP Concept (7 marks)

**Definition:**
MRP (Material Requirement Planning) is a **computer-based production and inventory control system** used to manage dependent demand materials. It translates the Master Production Schedule into time-phased procurement and production orders.

**Three Primary Inputs:**

1. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — Specifies *what* finished products to make, *when*, and *in what quantity*.
2. **Bill of Materials (BOM)** — A structured listing of all raw materials, components, and sub-assemblies required with their respective quantities.
3. **Inventory Status File** — Real-time record of on-hand inventory, scheduled receipts, and allocated stock.

**Processing Logic:**
MRP engine performs four computational steps:
- **Step 1:** Explode the BOM level-by-level.
- **Step 2:** Calculate Gross Requirements for each material.
- **Step 3:** Subtract on-hand + on-order inventory → Net Requirements.
- **Step 4:** Offset by lead time → Planned Order Releases.

**Outputs:**
- **Planned Order Releases** (purchase/work orders with dates).
- **Exception Reports** (late orders, shortages).
- **Performance Reports** (schedule adherence, inventory turns).

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b): Variance Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Standard Quantity per unit = $10 \text{ kg}$, Units produced = $1{,}000$
- $SQ = 10 \times 1{,}000 = 10{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- $AQ = 10{,}500 \text{ kg}$
- $SP = ₹40/\text{kg}$, $AP = ₹42/\text{kg}$

**(i) Material Cost Variance (MCV):**

$$
MCV = (SQ \times SP) - (AQ \times AP)
$$

$$
MCV = (10{,}000 \times 40) - (10{,}500 \times 42) = 4{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}41{,}000 = -₹41{,}000
$$

**MCV = ₹41,000 Unfavorable (U)**

**(ii) Material Price Variance (MPV):**

$$
MPV = (SP - AP) \times AQ = (40 - 42) \times 10{,}500 = -₹21{,}000
$$

**MPV = ₹21,000 Unfavorable (U)**

**(iii) Material Usage Variance (MUV):**

$$
MUV = (SQ - AQ) \times SP = (10{,}000 - 10{,}500) \times 40 = -₹20{,}000
$$

**MUV = ₹20,000 Unfavorable (U)**

**Verification Check:**

$$
MCV = MPV + MUV = (-21{,}000) + (-20{,}000) = -₹41{,}000 \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Sign Convention:** A negative variance may be favorable (e.g., lower price paid) — students blindly mark all negatives as "U". Read the formula direction.
> 2. **AQ Confusion:** MUV uses **actual quantity used**, not purchased. If the question gives both, use them correctly.
> 3. **Missing F/U Label:** Always write "(F)" or "(U)" explicitly — a variance without sign label loses 1 mark even if the number is correct.
> 4. **No Verification Step:** Examiners reward showing the MCV = MPV + MUV reconciliation — it proves your arithmetic and earns partial credit even if one step has a small slip.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

### Budgeting Essentials

- A **budget** is a quantitative plan expressed in monetary terms for a future period.
- **Master Budget** is the consolidated apex of all functional budgets.
- **Budgetary Control** is a closed-loop: Plan → Execute → Compare → Analyze → Correct.
- **Zero-Based Budgeting** justifies every rupee from scratch; used in petroleum and government sectors.
- **Rolling Budget** continuously extends by one period — ideal for volatile demand.

### Variance Analysis Mastery

- **MPV** = $(SP - AP) \times AQ$ — blame the **buyer**.
- **MUV** = $(SQ - AQ) \times SP$ — blame the **production floor**.
- **MCV** = **MPV + MUV** — total picture.
- Always label variances **(F)** Favorable or **(U)** Unfavorable.

### Materials Planning Pillars

- **5R Principle:** Right Material, Right Quantity, Right Time, Right Place, Right Cost.
- **MRP-I:** Demand-driven dependent requirement planning; three inputs (MPS, BOM, Inventory File).
- **MRP-II:** Extends MRP-I with financial and capacity integration.
- **JIT:** Pull-based, zero-buffer philosophy; Toyota Production System (TPS) origin.
- **EOQ:** $\sqrt{2DS/H}$ — minimum point where ordering cost = holding cost.

### Numerical Problem-Solving Pattern

1. **List given data** with units explicitly.
2. **Identify formula** required.
3. **Show substitution** step (not just final answer).
4. **Calculate** with proper rounding.
5. **Verify** using the symmetry check (Ordering Cost = Holding Cost for EOQ problems).
6. **Interpret** the result in the engineering context.

### Examiner Mindset — What Earns Full Marks

- **Diagrams:** A block diagram of MRP or budgetary control fetches 1–2 extra marks.
- **Units:** Always mention ₹, kg, units/yr — marks are deducted for unitless answers.
- **Definitions:** Quote the formal CIMA-style definition for "budget" — partial credit is generous on theory.
- **Audit Checks:** Showing MPV + MUV = MCV reconciliation is a hallmark of a topper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Aspirants:**
> Module 3 integrates two seemingly distinct domains — **financial control (Budgeting)** and **operational control (Materials Planning)**. The bridge between them is the **Materials Budget**, which is simultaneously a financial document and a logistics plan. Mastering both the theoretical taxonomy (types of budgets) and the analytical formulas (EOQ, variances) ensures full marks on both Part A and Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Purchasing Cycle and Principles of Purchasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Purchasing Cycle and Principles of Purchasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Engineering** and **Supply Chain Management** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the **Purchasing Cycle** is defined as the systematic, sequential set of interlinked business activities performed by an organization to procure the right type, quality, and quantity of materials, services, and capital equipment at the right price, from the right source, at the right time, and at the right place.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: The purchasing cycle (also called the procurement cycle) is a closed-loop, iterative process that begins with the identification of a need and ends with the evaluation of supplier performance, feeding back into future purchasing decisions. It is the operational backbone of the materials management subsystem.

**Principles of Purchasing**, on the other hand, refer to the universally accepted foundational rules or guidelines — popularly known as the **"7 Rs of Purchasing"** — that govern every purchasing decision to ensure economy, efficiency, and continuity of supply.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a grand wedding feast for **500 guests** in Kerala. You cannot randomly order food the day before the event. You need:

- To **identify the menu** (recognition of need).
- To **decide quantities** (food for 500 people, plus buffer).
- To **describe the dishes** specifically (chicken biryani, not just "biryani").
- To **pick the right caterer** (vendor selection).
- To **negotiate price** (₹250 per plate, not ₹400).
- To **place the order** formally (purchase order).
- To **track progress** (is the caterer preparing on time?).
- To **check quality on delivery** (taste and hygiene).
- To **pay the bill** after satisfaction.

The **Purchasing Cycle** is exactly this sequence — just applied to industrial materials, components, and services. The **Principles of Purchasing** are the rules a smart event manager always follows: right quality, right quantity, right time, right source, right price, right place, and right documentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEPET414 Module 3):** Students must be able to draw the purchasing cycle as a flowchart with at least **8 sequential steps** and list/explain the **7 Rs of Purchasing** with at least one industrial example each. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in University Examinations.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally evaluated against the purchasing function in KTU-aligned case studies:

- **Cost of Purchase (CP)**: Total landed cost of procured material in **₹/unit** or **$/unit**.
- **Lead Time (LT)**: Time interval between purchase order release and material receipt, typically measured in **days**.
- **Vendor Fill Rate (VFR)**: Percentage of orders fulfilled on time in full — ideally **≥ 95%**.
- **Purchase Price Variance (PPV)**: Difference between standard and actual purchase price — measure in **₹**.
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)**: A measure of how often inventory is replenished per year, dimensionless.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Purchasing Cycle — Detailed Step-by-Step Logic

The purchasing cycle is a closed-loop process comprising **10 sequential steps**. Below is a granular breakdown of each stage, including the **"Why"** (rationale) and **"How"** (operational mechanism):

### Step 1: Recognition of Need (Materials Requisition)
- **Why?** Purchasing cannot begin until a genuine requirement is identified.
- **How?** Triggered by:
  - **Stock-out signal** when reorder point (ROP) is breached.
  - **Production Planning** order release (MRP output).
  - **Engineering request** for new part numbers.
  - **Maintenance request** for spare parts.
- **Output**: A **Materials Requisition Note (MRN)** — internal document.

### Step 2: Determination of Quantity and Timing
- **Why?** Buying too much blocks working capital; buying too little causes stock-outs.
- **How?** Using:
  - **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** model.
  - **Reorder Point (ROP)** analysis.
  - **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** schedules.
- **Key Output**: Order quantity $Q$ and required-by-date.

### Step 3: Description of Required Item (Specifications)
- **Why?** Ambiguity leads to wrong deliveries and rejections.
- **How?** Detailed specifications are prepared using:
  - **Engineering drawings**.
  - **Material grade codes** (e.g., IS 2062 for structural steel).
  - **Quality standards** (ISO, BIS, ASTM).
- **Output**: Item code, technical specification sheet.

### Step 4: Selection of Source (Vendor Selection)
- **Why?** Quality and reliability depend on choosing the right supplier.
- **How?** Methods include:
  - **Approved Vendor List (AVL)**.
  - **Vendor Rating** based on Quality, Delivery, Price, Service.
  - **Tendering / Quotation Analysis**.
  - **Single Source vs. Multiple Source** decision.

### Step 5: Price Determination
- **Why?** Pricing errors directly erode profit margins.
- **How?** Techniques:
  - **Market survey** and price benchmarking.
  - **Cost analysis** (should-cost modeling).
  - **Historical price trends**.

### Step 6: Negotiation of Terms
- **Why?** To arrive at mutually acceptable commercial and legal terms.
- **How?** Negotiations cover:
  - **Price**, **payment terms**, **delivery schedule**.
  - **Warranty**, **penalties for delays**, **inspection rights**.

### Step 7: Issuance of Purchase Order (PO)
- **Why?** A formal PO is a legally binding contract document.
- **How?** A standardized **Purchase Order** is generated with unique PO number, containing all terms agreed upon.

### Step 8: Follow-Up and Expediting
- **Why?** Suppliers may default on delivery promises.
- **How?** Active follow-up through:
  - **Reminder letters**.
  - **Telephone / email communication**.
  - **Supplier visits**.

### Step 9: Receipt, Inspection, and Acceptance
- **Why?** Ensure materials conform to specifications before payment.
- **How?** Performed in the **Stores Department**:
  - **Goods Receipt Note (GRN)**.
  - **Inspection Report (IR)**.
  - **Acceptance / Rejection / Return (RRR)**.

### Step 10: Invoice Approval and Payment
- **Why?** Completes the financial transaction.
- **How?** Three-way matching — **PO + GRN + Supplier Invoice** — verified before payment release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After Step 10, the cycle **closes the loop** by feeding **vendor performance data** back into Step 4 (Selection of Source) for future evaluations.

---

## 2.2 The 7 Principles (Rs) of Purchasing

These are the universally accepted guiding principles. Each R ensures that purchasing delivers maximum value to the organization.

| # | Principle | Definition | Real-World Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Right Quality** | Material meeting exact specifications and grade standards. | Procuring SS 304 grade steel for a chemical reactor, not SS 202. |
| 2 | **Right Quantity** | Buying the optimal lot size balancing holding vs. ordering cost. | Using EOQ to order 500 bearings instead of 100 or 5000. |
| 3 | **Right Time** | Material available exactly when needed, no stock-out, no excess. | Arrival of clutch plates just-in-time (JIT) for assembly line T+4 hours. |
| 4 | **Right Source** | Reliable vendor with proven track record and capability. | Approved Tier-1 supplier like Bosch or Tata Steel. |
| 5 | **Right Price** | Lowest total cost of ownership (TCO), not just lowest unit price. | Considering freight, insurance, and warranty costs, not just invoice value. |
| 6 | **Right Place** | Delivery to correct location/stores/line-side. | Material delivered to Chassis Assembly Bay, not the Receiving Dock. |
| 7 | **Right Documentation / Records** | All transactions recorded for audit and traceability. | PO file, GRN register, and invoice archival under GST compliance. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **high-yield formulas** frequently tested for this topic.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $$EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$ | Optimal order quantity minimizing total cost. | Units (number) |
| **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $$ROP = d \times L + SS$$ | Stock level at which a new order must be placed. | Units |
| **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** | $$TIC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$$ | Sum of ordering + holding cost. | ₹/year |
| **Vendor Fill Rate (VFR)** | $$VFR = \dfrac{\text{Orders Fulfilled on Time}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$$ | Vendor reliability metric. | **%** |
| **Purchase Price Variance (PPV)** | $$PPV = (SP - AP) \times AQ$$ | Standard vs. Actual price difference. | ₹ |
| **Lead Time Demand Variance** | $$\sigma_{dLT} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_d^2 + d^2 \cdot \sigma_L^2}$$ | Safety stock estimation under variable demand/lead time. | Units |
| **Carrying Cost Rate (CCR)** | $$CCR = \dfrac{C_c}{I_c} \times 100$$ | Annual carrying cost as % of inventory value. | **%** |

Where:
- $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)
- $d$ = Average daily demand
- $L$ = Lead time in days
- $SS$ = Safety stock
- $SP$ = Standard price, $AP$ = Actual price
- $AQ$ = Actual quantity purchased

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of the Purchasing System

The purchasing cycle is not merely academic. In a real **automotive assembly plant** in India:

- A **Maruti Suzuki-style JIT plant** runs thousands of POs daily.
- Any failure in **Step 4 (Vendor Selection)** can halt the entire production line within hours.
- A flaw in **Step 9 (Inspection)** can let defective airbags reach customers, triggering costly recalls.
- The **7 Rs** act as a daily checklist for every Purchase Manager.

In **KTU examination settings**, examiners expect students to interlink these principles with the corresponding cycle steps during 14-mark descriptive answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

The EOQ formula is the mathematical backbone of the "**Right Quantity**" principle. Below is the exhaustive derivation expected in KTU Part B answers.

### Step A — Define Cost Components

The total annual inventory cost $TIC$ has two main components:

1. **Annual Ordering Cost ($OC$)** — increases as order quantity decreases.

$$OC = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

2. **Annual Holding Cost ($HC$)** — increases as order quantity increases.

$$HC = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

Therefore, the total cost is:

$$\begin{aligned}
TIC &= OC + HC \\
TIC &= \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Here, the average inventory is $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ because stock depletes linearly from $Q$ to $0$ over one cycle.

---

### Step B — Optimize Using Calculus

To minimize $TIC$, take the first derivative with respect to $Q$ and set it equal to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d(TIC)}{dQ} &= -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} &= \frac{H}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot D \cdot S &= Q^2 \cdot H
\end{aligned}$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q^2 &= \frac{2DS}{H}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking the square root:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q^{*} = EOQ &= \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step C — Verify It's a Minimum

The second derivative test:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2(TIC)}{dQ^2} &= \frac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0
\end{aligned}$$

Since this is positive for $Q > 0$, the function is convex, confirming a **minimum** at $Q^{*} = EOQ$.

---

### Step D — Optimal Total Cost at EOQ

Substituting $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ back into $TIC$:

$$\begin{aligned}
TIC_{min} &= \frac{DS}{\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}} + \frac{\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} \cdot H}{2} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} \\
&= 2 \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} \\
&= \sqrt{2 D S H}
\end{aligned}$$

This is a high-yield KTU result.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (Typical KTU-style):** An industry consumes **10,000 units/year** of a component. Ordering cost is **₹200/order** and holding cost is **₹10/unit/year**. Find EOQ, number of orders/year, and minimum total cost.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $D = 10{,}000$ units/year
- $S = ₹200$/order
- $H = ₹10$/unit/year

$$\begin{aligned}
EOQ &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{10}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{10}} \\
&= \sqrt{400{,}000} \\
&\approx 632.46 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}$$

Rounding off, **EOQ ≈ 632 units per order**.

Number of orders per year:

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{10{,}000}{632} \approx 15.82 \approx 16 \text{ orders/year}
\end{aligned}$$

Minimum total cost:

$$\begin{aligned}
TIC_{min} &= \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 10} \\
&= \sqrt{40{,}000{,}000} \\
&= ₹6{,}324.56 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the 7 Rs to Cycle Steps

This matrix is a **favourite KTU answer framing tool** for 14-mark questions:

| Cycle Step | Activity | Corresponding Principle of Purchasing |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Recognition of need | (Internal trigger) |
| 2 | Quantity & timing determination | Right Quantity, Right Time |
| 3 | Item description / specifications | Right Quality |
| 4 | Source selection | Right Source |
| 5 | Price determination | Right Price |
| 6 | Negotiation of terms | Right Documentation |
| 7 | Issuance of PO | Right Documentation |
| 8 | Follow-up & expediting | Right Time, Right Place |
| 9 | Receipt & inspection | Right Quality, Right Place |
| 10 | Invoice & payment | Right Documentation, Right Price |
| **Loop closure** | Vendor performance feedback | All 7 Rs (continuous improvement) |

> [!NOTE]
> The above matrix demonstrates that the **Principles of Purchasing are not isolated rules** — they are **embedded at every stage** of the purchasing cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Diagram — The Purchasing Cycle (10-Step Closed Loop)

The following **Mermaid flowchart** illustrates the complete purchasing cycle with all 10 stages and a feedback loop for vendor performance evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start: Material Need Identified]) --> step1[Step 1: Recognition of Need - MRN Issued]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2: Determine Quantity and Timing - EOQ, ROP, MRP]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Describe Item - Specifications, Grade, Drawing]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Select Source - AVL, Vendor Rating, Tendering]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: Determine Price - Market Survey, Cost Analysis]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Negotiate Terms - Price, Delivery, Warranty]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Issue Purchase Order PO - Legally Binding Contract]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Follow Up and Expedite - Track Supplier Progress]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Receipt and Inspection - GRN, Quality Check]
    step9 --> step10[Step 10: Invoice Approval and Payment - Three Way Match]
    step10 --> feedback[Vendor Performance Evaluation and Records]
    feedback --> step4
    step10 --> endNode([End: Transaction Closed and Filed])
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The 7 Principles of Purchasing (7 Rs Hub)

The following block-level functional architecture maps the **7 Rs of Purchasing** as a central hub interacting with the purchasing cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    principleR1[Right Quality] --> centralHub((Purchasing Cycle Activity))
    principleR2[Right Quantity] --> centralHub
    principleR3[Right Time] --> centralHub
    principleR4[Right Source] --> centralHub
    principleR5[Right Price] --> centralHub
    principleR6[Right Place] --> centralHub
    principleR7[Right Documentation] --> centralHub
    centralHub --> outcome1[Operational Efficiency]
    centralHub --> outcome2[Cost Optimization]
    centralHub --> outcome3[Customer Satisfaction]
    centralHub --> outcome4[Audit and Compliance]
```

---

## 4.3 Process Diagram — Vendor Selection Sub-Process (Expanded View of Step 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vendorStart([Enter Vendor Selection Stage]) --> v1[Identify Potential Vendors - Database Search]
    v1 --> v2[Send Request for Quotation RFQ]
    v2 --> v3[Receive and Tabulate Quotations]
    v3 --> v4{Quotation Analysis - Compare on Quality, Price, Delivery}
    v4 --> v5{Negotiation Required?}
    v5 -- Yes --> v6[Negotiate Terms with Vendors]
    v5 -- No --> v7[Select Best Vendor]
    v6 --> v4
    v7 --> v8[Update Approved Vendor List AVL]
    v8 --> vendorEnd([Move to Step 5: Price Finalization])
```

---

## 4.4 Detailed Block Diagram — Purchase Order Flow with Three-Way Matching

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    poA[Purchase Order PO] --> matchBlock{Three Way Matching}
    grn[Goods Receipt Note GRN] --> matchBlock
    invoice[Supplier Invoice] --> matchBlock
    matchBlock -- All Match --> pay[Authorize Payment]
    matchBlock -- Mismatch --> hold[Held for Clarification]
    hold --> resolve[Resolve with Vendor]
    resolve --> matchBlock
    pay --> ledger[Update Accounts Payable Ledger]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six steps involved in the purchasing cycle of an industrial organization.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 each = 3 marks):**

The purchasing cycle of an industrial organization consists of the following sequential steps:

1. **Recognition of Need** — Internal material requisition generated.
2. **Determination of Quantity and Timing** — Using EOQ, ROP, or MRP.
3. **Description of the Required Item** — Specifications, grades, and drawings.
4. **Selection of Source** — Vendor selection from AVL or through tendering.
5. **Price Determination** — Through market survey and cost analysis.
6. **Negotiation of Terms** — Commercial and legal terms agreed upon.
7. **Issuance of Purchase Order** — Legally binding document issued.
8. **Follow-up and Expediting** — Active tracking of supplier progress.
9. **Receipt and Inspection** — Goods Receipt Note and quality check.
10. **Invoice Approval and Payment** — Three-way matching and payment release.

> [!TIP]
> Writing **6 steps with one-line explanations** is sufficient for full 3 marks in Part A.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain any four principles of purchasing with examples.**

**Model Answer (4 principles × 0.75 each = 3 marks):**

The **principles of purchasing**, also called the **7 Rs of Purchasing**, are:

1. **Right Quality** — The material must conform to the specified grade and quality standard. *Example*: Procuring IS 2062 Grade A steel for a pressure vessel.

2. **Right Quantity** — The order must be of optimal economic lot size. *Example*: Using EOQ to decide 800 bearings per order to balance ordering and holding cost.

3. **Right Time** — Material must arrive exactly when needed. *Example*: Components delivered to assembly line just-in-time (JIT) on Day 5 morning.

4. **Right Price** — The procurement cost must yield the lowest total cost of ownership. *Example*: Selecting a vendor at ₹150/unit over ₹140/unit because of lower freight and warranty costs.

*(Other principles: Right Source, Right Place, Right Documentation.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the purchasing cycle of an industrial organization with the help of a neat flowchart.

**Model Answer:**

The purchasing cycle is the systematic sequence of activities undertaken by a firm to procure materials, components, and services in a cost-effective and timely manner. The complete cycle consists of **ten interconnected steps**:

1. **Recognition of Need**: A materials requisition is raised by the production, maintenance, or engineering department when stock falls below the reorder level or when an MRP plan triggers a buy order.

2. **Determination of Quantity and Timing**: The purchase department calculates the optimal order quantity using techniques such as Economic Order Quantity (EOQ), Reorder Point (ROP), or Material Requirements Planning (MRP). Lead time is factored in to determine the right time for placing the order.

3. **Description of Required Item**: Detailed specifications are prepared, including item code, material grade, dimensional tolerances, and applicable standards (IS, BIS, ISO, ASTM). This step is critical to prevent ambiguity.

4. **Selection of Source**: The buyer identifies suitable vendors from the Approved Vendor List (AVL) or through open tendering. Vendors are evaluated on quality, delivery reliability, capacity, financial stability, and past performance.

5. **Price Determination**: Market surveys, historical price trends, and should-cost analyses are used to determine a fair and competitive price.

6. **Negotiation of Terms**: Final commercial and legal terms are negotiated, including price, delivery schedule, payment terms, warranty, inspection rights, and penalty clauses.

7. **Issuance of Purchase Order (PO)**: A formal PO with a unique PO number is issued to the selected vendor, serving as a legally binding contract.

8. **Follow-Up and Expediting**: The purchase department tracks the supplier's progress through reminder letters, emails, and on-site visits to ensure on-time delivery.

9. **Receipt and Inspection**: Materials are received at the stores department, inspected for quality and quantity, and a Goods Receipt Note (GRN) is generated. Rejected lots are returned with a rejection report.

10. **Invoice Approval and Payment**: A three-way match is conducted among the Purchase Order, Goods Receipt Note, and Supplier Invoice. After reconciliation, payment is released as per the agreed credit terms.

Finally, **vendor performance is evaluated** and fed back into the source selection process for continuous improvement — thus closing the loop.

*Flowchart: Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the complete schematic representation.*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the principles of purchasing. How do they help in achieving purchasing objectives?

**Model Answer:**

The principles of purchasing, popularly known as the **7 Rs**, provide the foundational rules that govern every procurement decision. They ensure that the purchasing function contributes optimally to organizational objectives such as cost reduction, quality assurance, and uninterrupted production.

The **7 Principles** are:

1. **Right Quality**: Material must meet exact specifications. This principle prevents rejection, rework, and warranty claims.

2. **Right Quantity**: Order size must be optimal, balancing ordering cost against holding cost. Implemented through EOQ.

3. **Right Time**: Material must arrive exactly when required, enabling smooth production planning and avoiding stock-outs or excess inventory.

4. **Right Source**: The supplier must be reliable and capable. Vendor rating systems and Approved Vendor Lists enforce this principle.

5. **Right Price**: The procurement cost should reflect the lowest Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), not just the lowest invoice price.

6. **Right Place**: Delivery must reach the correct internal location — stores, line-side, or production bay — to avoid internal delays.

7. **Right Documentation**: All transactions must be properly recorded for legal compliance, audit, and traceability.

**How they achieve purchasing objectives:**

- They **minimize total cost** through optimal quantity and price decisions.
- They **ensure quality** by enforcing specifications and source approval.
- They **improve continuity of supply** by selecting reliable sources.
- They **support financial discipline** through documentation and audit trails.
- They **enhance customer satisfaction** indirectly by ensuring uninterrupted production.

> [!NOTE]
> The 7 Rs are not separate from the purchasing cycle — they are embedded in it. For instance, *Right Quality* aligns with Steps 3 and 9, while *Right Time* aligns with Steps 2 and 8.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are the steps involved in the selection of a vendor? Explain with a suitable flowchart.

**Model Answer:**

Vendor selection is a critical step (Step 4) of the purchasing cycle, ensuring that supplies are obtained from the most suitable supplier. The systematic steps are:

1. **Identify Potential Vendors** — Compile a list through market research, trade directories, exhibitions, and existing customer references.

2. **Pre-Qualification** — Assess each vendor's technical capability, financial strength, capacity, quality certifications (ISO 9001), and past performance.

3. **Issue Request for Quotation (RFQ)** — Send detailed RFQ documents specifying quantity, quality, delivery, and payment terms.

4. **Receive and Tabulate Quotations** — Collect responses and prepare a comparative statement on a uniform basis.

5. **Quotation Analysis** — Compare vendors on weighted criteria:
   - Price (30%)
   - Quality (25%)
   - Delivery reliability (25%)
   - Service & warranty (10%)
   - Financial stability (10%)

6. **Negotiation** — Conduct negotiations with the top-ranked vendor(s) on price, delivery, and terms.

7. **Final Selection** — Choose the best vendor based on the composite score and negotiation outcome.

8. **Update AVL** — Add the selected vendor to the Approved Vendor List for future reference.

*Flowchart: Refer to Section 4.3 for the complete Mermaid schematic of vendor selection.*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the EOQ model and derive the formula for optimal order quantity.

**Model Answer:**

The **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** model, developed by Ford Whitman Harris in 1913, identifies the order quantity that minimizes the **total annual inventory cost**, balancing two opposing costs:

- **Ordering Cost** (decreases with larger orders).
- **Holding Cost** (increases with larger orders).

**Assumptions:**
- Constant and known demand.
- Constant lead time.
- Instant replenishment (no stock-outs).
- No quantity discounts.
- Independent demand items.

**Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TIC &= \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2} \\
\frac{d(TIC)}{dQ} &= -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0 \\
Q^2 &= \frac{2DS}{H} \\
EOQ = Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}
\end{aligned}$$

The minimum total cost at EOQ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
TIC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}
\end{aligned}$$

**Significance for Purchasing:**

- Directly operationalizes the **Right Quantity** principle.
- Reduces working capital locked in inventory.
- Provides a scientific basis for purchasing decisions, replacing intuitive ordering.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: Many students forget to state the **assumptions of EOQ** in their answers. Examiners specifically look for assumptions in 7-mark sub-questions. **Loss of 1–2 marks** is common if omitted.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Purchasing Cycle** has **10 standard steps** — memorize them in order.
- The cycle is a **closed loop**, ending with vendor performance feedback into Step 4.
- **7 Rs of Purchasing**: Quality, Quantity, Time, Source, Price, Place, Documentation.
- **EOQ formula**: $\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ — derive cleanly in any 7-mark sub-question.
- **Three-way matching**: PO + GRN + Invoice = Authorization for payment.
- **Vendor Selection** uses weighted scoring models, not just price.
- Always link the **7 Rs to the cycle steps** in 14-mark answers — examiners award **integration marks** for this.
- Use a **flowchart** in every 14-mark cycle question — it visually earns extra presentation marks.
- **Right Price** means **Total Cost of Ownership**, NOT the lowest unit price.
- Carrying cost rate is typically **15–25%** of inventory value in Indian industries.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Value Analysis and Techniques for Materials Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Value Analysis (VA)

### Formal Academic Definition
**Value Analysis (VA)** is a systematic, function-oriented, organized, and creative method of increasing the value of an item, product, or service by understanding its constituent elements and their *function(s)* and thereby *cost*. It is a powerful problem-solving discipline that focuses on **function analysis** to deliver the required functions at the **lowest total cost** without compromising quality, reliability, performance, or safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Lawrence D. Miles):**
> *"Value Analysis is a disciplined procedure directed toward the achievement of necessary functions at the lowest total cost, consistent with the attainment of essential performance characteristics, reliability, quality, and maintainability."*

The mathematical backbone of Value Analysis is the **Value Equation**:

$$\text{Value } (V) = \frac{\text{Function } (F)}{\text{Cost } (C)}$$

Value can be **increased** in four classical ways:

| # | Strategy | Function (F) | Cost (C) | Outcome |
|---|----------|--------------|----------|---------|
| 1 | Improve function, cost same | ↑ | → | Higher Value |
| 2 | Same function, reduce cost | → | ↓ | Higher Value |
| 3 | Improve function, reduce cost | ↑ | ↓ | **Highest Value** |
| 4 | Slightly reduce function, drastically reduce cost | ↓ | ↓↓ | Higher Value |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Homeowner:**
> Imagine you are renovating a house. You need a *function*: a roof that doesn't leak. The traditional approach is to use the most expensive tiles available. **Value Analysis** asks: "What is the *real* function? To keep rain out. Can a fiber-reinforced concrete tile achieve this function at 40% of the cost?" Yes! The function is preserved, cost is reduced, and **Value is increased**. The principle is simple — never pay for *more function than you need*, and never pay for *ornamental costs* that don't serve the essential purpose. This is what every KTU examiner expects you to demonstrate in a 14-mark answer.

---

## 1.2 Techniques for Materials Management

### Formal Definition
**Materials Management techniques** are quantitative, qualitative, and hybrid scientific tools used by inventory professionals to **classify, prioritize, control, and optimize** the storage, movement, and replenishment of materials within an organization.

### Intuitive Overview
Think of a **hospital pharmacy** as a materials management system. The pharmacy has thousands of items — expensive cancer drugs, cheap band-aids, life-saving emergency injectables, and slow-moving creams. Treating *all* items with equal attention would waste effort. The pharmacy uses **classification techniques** (like ABC) to focus attention where it matters most, **ordering techniques** (like EOQ) to decide *how much* to buy, and **control techniques** (like FSN) to decide *when* to act. Each technique answers a different question:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Question Mapping (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **ABC** → "Which items need *tight* control?" (Pareto Principle)
> - **VED** → "Which items are *critical* for operations?"
> - **EOQ** → "*How much* should I order at one time?"
> - **FSN** → "*How fast* do items move?"
> - **SDE** → "How *scarce* is the item in the market?"
> - **GOLF** → *Government/Local/Overseas/Foreign* — sourcing channel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Value Analysis — The Six-Phase Job Plan

Value Analysis follows a **disciplined 6-phase job plan** (also called the **Standard Value Analysis Job Plan**) developed at General Electric by Lawrence D. Miles:

### Phase 1: Information Phase
- Gather all data: drawings, specifications, bills of materials, cost data, supplier data, and user requirements.
- **Goal:** Understand *what* the product is, *what* it does, and *what* it costs.

### Phase 2: Function Phase
- Define the product's functions using a **two-word active verb + measurable noun** format.
- Example: A *drill bit* does not "drill holes" — its function is to "**remove material**" (or "**enforce rotation**" for the chuck).
- Distinguish between:
  - **Basic Function (B)**: The reason the product exists.
  - **Secondary Function (S)**: All other supporting functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Concept:** Identifying the **Basic Function** is the *single most important* step in Value Analysis. Examiners frequently test this with a 3-mark question.

### Phase 3: Creative Phase
- Brainstorm alternatives using techniques like *brainstorming*, *Gordon's method*, *checklists*, and *morphological analysis*.
- The aim is to generate a **large quantity of ideas** without criticism.

### Phase 4: Evaluation Phase
- Screen ideas using weighted scoring against:
  - **Cost reduction potential**
  - **Function improvement potential**
  - **Feasibility of implementation**
  - **Reliability impact**

### Phase 5: Development Phase
- Refine the surviving ideas into **concrete proposals**.
- Prepare sketches, vendor quotes, implementation timelines, and savings estimates.

### Phase 6: Recommendation Phase
- Present the proposal to management with a complete **Value Engineering Change Proposal (VECP)** documenting savings, payback period, and risk analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Value Engineering (VE) vs. Value Analysis (VA):**
> - **VE** is applied at the **design stage** (before production) — *proactive*.
> - **VA** is applied to an **existing product** (after production) — *reactive*.
> - Both use the same 6-phase job plan and value equation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Materials Management Techniques

| # | Technique | Core Formula / Logic | Use Case | Key Metric |
|---|-----------|---------------------|----------|------------|
| 1 | **EOQ (Wilson’s Formula)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}$ | Optimal order quantity | Units per order |
| 2 | **Re-order Level (ROL)** | $ROL = \text{Max Lead Time Demand} = D_L \times L$ | When to place order | Units |
| 3 | **Safety Stock (SS)** | $SS = (D_{max} \times L_{max}) - (D_{avg} \times L_{avg})$ | Buffer against variability | Units |
| 4 | **Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** | $TIC = D \cdot P + \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H$ | Total cost of inventory | Currency/year |
| 5 | **Value Index** | $V = \dfrac{F}{C}$ | Measure of value | Dimensionless |
| 6 | **Cumulative % of Value** | $\text{Cum. \%} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Item Value}}{\text{Total Value}} \times 100$ | ABC classification | Percentage |
| 7 | **Annual Ordering Cost** | $OC = \dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$ | Ordering cost component | Currency/year |
| 8 | **Annual Holding Cost** | $HC = \dfrac{Q}{2} \times H$ | Carrying cost component | Currency/year |
| 9 | **Number of Orders per Year** | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q^*}$ | Order frequency | Orders/year |
| 10 | **Time between Orders (TBO)** | $TBO = \dfrac{\text{Working days}}{N}$ | Order cycle time | Days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Legend (used in all formulas):**
> - $D$ = Annual demand (units/year)
> - $S$ = Ordering cost per order (currency/order)
> - $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (currency/unit/year)
> - $P$ = Purchase price per unit
> - $Q$ = Order quantity (units)
> - $L$ = Lead time (days/weeks)
> - $D_L$ = Daily/weekly demand

### Engineering Utility
The EOQ model is the **bedrock of every modern Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)** system. SAP, Oracle, and Tally all embed Wilson's formula (or its variants like **EOQ with quantity discounts**, **production order quantity (POQ)**, and **joint replenishment**) to automate purchase orders. Mastering these formulas makes you industry-ready in **procurement, supply chain analytics, and operations consulting**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EOQ (Economic Order Quantity) Formula

The EOQ formula is derived by **minimizing the Total Inventory Cost (TIC)** function with respect to order quantity $Q$. Every algebraic step is shown below for full KTU Board credit.

### Step 1: Define the Three Cost Components
The total annual cost of inventory has three components:

$$\text{Annual Purchase Cost } (APC) = D \cdot P$$

$$\text{Annual Ordering Cost } (AOC) = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

$$\text{Annual Holding Cost } (AHC) = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

> The factor $Q/2$ arises because inventory rises linearly from 0 to $Q$ after each order, then falls back to 0 — **average inventory is the area of the triangle divided by the cycle length**, which is $Q/2$.

### Step 2: Write the Total Cost Function
Since $D \cdot P$ is *independent* of $Q$ (purchase cost is fixed regardless of order size), we minimize the **variable total cost (VTC)**:

$$VTC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{H \cdot Q}{2}$$

### Step 3: Differentiate with Respect to Q
Apply the first derivative and set it to zero for an extremum:

$$\frac{d(VTC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for Q
Rearranging the equation:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^2}$$

$$Q^2 = \frac{2 D S}{H}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}$$

### Step 5: Verify it is a Minimum (Second Derivative Test)
$$\frac{d^2(VTC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D S}{Q^3} > 0$$

Since the second derivative is **positive**, the extremum is confirmed as a **minimum**. The optimal cost point is the intersection of the decreasing ordering cost curve and the increasing holding cost curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Insight:** The EOQ is the unique point where the slope of the **Ordering Cost** curve equals the negative slope of the **Holding Cost** curve. To the left of EOQ → ordering dominates; to the right → holding dominates.

### Step 3B: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard)

**Problem:** Annual demand $D = 10{,}000$ units. Ordering cost $S = \text{Rs. } 200/\text{order}$. Holding cost $H = \text{Rs. } 4/\text{unit/year}$. Purchase price $P = \text{Rs. } 50/\text{unit}$.

**Solution:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{4}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{4}} = \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000} = 1000 \text{ units}$$

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10{,}000}{1000} = 10 \text{ orders/year}$$

$$TBO = \frac{300 \text{ working days}}{10} = 30 \text{ days/order}$$

$$AOC = \frac{10{,}000}{1000} \times 200 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000$$

$$AHC = \frac{1000}{2} \times 4 = \text{Rs. } 2{,}000$$

$$TIC = D \cdot P + AOC + AHC = (10{,}000 \times 50) + 2{,}000 + 2{,}000 = \text{Rs. } 5{,}04{,}000$$

---

## 3.2 ABC Analysis — Step-by-Step Numerical Procedure

The **ABC (Always Better Control)** analysis is based on the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)**. The procedural steps are:

### Step 1: List all items with Annual Usage and Unit Cost
Create a table of $n$ items, capturing **Annual Consumption Value (ACV) = Annual Demand × Unit Cost**.

### Step 2: Sort in Descending Order of ACV
Re-arrange items so that the highest-value item is at the top.

### Step 3: Compute Cumulative Value
Add values progressively from top to bottom.

### Step 4: Compute Cumulative Percentage
Divide cumulative value by **total annual consumption value**, then multiply by 100.

### Step 5: Apply Classification Cutoffs
Use the standard KTU cutoffs (which may vary 5% in real exams):

| Class | % of Items | % of Value | Control Level |
|-------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **A** | ~10% | ~70% | **Tight** (rigorous review, frequent counts) |
| **B** | ~20% | ~20% | **Moderate** (periodic review) |
| **C** | ~70% | ~10% | **Loose** (annual review, bulk issues) |

### Worked ABC Example
Suppose 5 items have the following ACV:

| Item | ACV (Rs.) | % of Total | Cumulative % | Class |
|------|-----------|------------|--------------|-------|
| P1 | 50,000 | 50% | 50% | A |
| P2 | 20,000 | 20% | 70% | A |
| P3 | 15,000 | 15% | 85% | B |
| P4 | 10,000 | 10% | 95% | C |
| P5 | 5,000 | 5% | 100% | C |
| **Total** | **100,000** | 100% | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works (Pareto Logic):** In virtually every inventory, a small fraction of items accounts for the bulk of the dollar value. Focusing managerial effort on these items yields the highest ROI on attention.

### Comparison Matrix: ABC vs VED vs SDE vs FSN

| Technique | Classification Basis | Question Answered | Typical Application |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **ABC** | Annual Consumption Value | "How much money is tied up?" | Inventory investment control |
| **VED** | Vital / Essential / Desirable | "How critical is the item for production?" | Spare parts management |
| **SDE** | Scarce / Difficult / Easy | "How hard is it to procure?" | Procurement strategy |
| **FSN** | Fast / Slow / Non-moving | "How fast does stock move?" | Obsolescence control |
| **GOLF** | Government / Local / Overseas / Foreign | "Where does it come from?" | Sourcing decision |
| **HML** | High / Medium / Low unit price | "How expensive per unit?" | Security control |
| **SOS** | Season Off-Season | "When is it demanded?" | Seasonal inventory |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Value Analysis. Mention the Value Equation. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Value Analysis (VA)** is a systematic, function-oriented technique used to increase the value of a product, service, or process by delivering the required functions at the lowest total cost while maintaining quality, reliability, and performance.

It is mathematically expressed as:

$$V = \frac{F}{C}$$

where **V** = Value, **F** = Function (usefulness), and **C** = Cost. Value is improved when we either increase the function for the same cost, reduce cost for the same function, or both. The technique was pioneered by **Lawrence D. Miles at General Electric in 1947**.

---

### Q2. List any three techniques used in Materials Management and state their purpose. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The three key materials management techniques are:

1. **ABC Analysis** — Classifies inventory into A (tight control), B (moderate control), and C (loose control) categories based on the **Pareto Principle (annual consumption value)**. It helps focus managerial effort on high-value items.

2. **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** — Determines the **optimal order quantity** that minimizes the sum of ordering cost and holding cost using Wilson's formula:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}$$

3. **VED Analysis** — Classifies spares as **Vital, Essential, or Desirable** based on the criticality of the item for production continuity. Vital items are stocked in higher quantities and given priority in procurement.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: Value Analysis in Detail `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### (a) Explain the six-phase Job Plan of Value Analysis in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The **Value Analysis Job Plan** is a disciplined, sequential procedure developed by Lawrence D. Miles. The six phases are:

**Phase 1 — Information Phase:**
The VA team collects all relevant data: engineering drawings, specifications, cost breakdowns (material, labour, overhead), supplier information, and end-user requirements. The goal is to fully understand the product, its function, and its cost structure. *Example:* For a study lamp, we gather wattage, materials, manufacturing cost, selling price, and market alternatives.

**Phase 2 — Function Phase:**
Functions are defined using a **two-word active verb + measurable noun** format. A *drill* does not "drill holes" — it **removes material**; the chuck **enforces rotation**. Functions are split into:
- **Basic Function (B)**: The reason the product exists.
- **Secondary Functions (S)**: All aesthetic or supportive functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Trick:** Identifying the Basic Function is the *most important* step. A common student error is to confuse the *use* with the *function*. The function is *what it does*, not *what it is used for*.

**Phase 3 — Creative Phase:**
The team brainstorms alternatives using *brainstorming*, *Gordon technique*, *synectics*, and *morphological analysis*. **No criticism is allowed** at this stage — the goal is quantity of ideas. Lateral thinking is encouraged.

**Phase 4 — Evaluation Phase:**
Each idea is screened for feasibility, cost reduction potential, and impact on function, quality, and reliability. Common tools used are **weighted scoring matrices** and **paired comparison analysis**. Surviving ideas are ranked.

**Phase 5 — Development Phase:**
The best ideas are converted into **detailed proposals** with engineering sketches, vendor quotes, implementation cost, payback period, and risk assessment. Each proposal is documented as a **Value Engineering Change Proposal (VECP)**.

**Phase 6 — Recommendation Phase:**
The final proposal is presented to top management for approval. Upon acceptance, the proposal is implemented, and post-implementation audits verify actual savings. Savings are usually shared between the company, the customer, and the VA team (sharing ratio typically 50:30:20).

---

#### (b) Distinguish between Value Engineering and Value Analysis. Discuss how Value = Function / Cost guides the analyst. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Value Engineering (VE) | Value Analysis (VA) |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Stage of application** | Design / pre-production stage | Existing / post-production product |
| **Nature** | Proactive | Reactive |
| **Objective** | Build value into the design | Extract value from existing product |
| **Information source** | Design intent, simulations | Actual cost data, field data |
| **Cost of changes** | Low (paper changes) | Higher (tooling already exists) |
| **Typical users** | Design engineering teams | Production / cost-reduction teams |
| **Common in** | New product development | Mature product cost reduction |

**Guidance from the Value Equation $V = F/C$:**

The equation directs the analyst to think along **two axes simultaneously**:

1. **Numerator thinking (Function):** Can we enhance the function? Can we add useful features at no extra cost? (e.g., adding a USB port to a study lamp without raising cost).

2. **Denominator thinking (Cost):** Can we reduce material, labour, or overhead? Can we substitute a cheaper material that performs equivalently? (e.g., using ABS plastic instead of die-cast aluminium for a non-structural housing).

The **fourth strategy** (slight reduction in function for a large cost reduction) is also valid when the *excess function* was never valued by the customer. This is called **"trimming unnecessary function"** — a frequent KTU 14-mark question scenario.

---

### Question B: EOQ and ABC Analysis `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

#### (a) Derive the EOQ formula starting from the total cost equation. List the assumptions of the EOQ model. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Total Cost Function:**
The annual variable total cost of inventory is:

$$VTC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D S}{Q}}_{\text{Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{H Q}{2}}_{\text{Holding Cost}}$$

**Step 1: Differentiate with respect to Q:**

$$\frac{d(VTC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}$$

**Step 2: Set the derivative to zero for an extremum:**

$$-\frac{D S}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve for Q:**

$$Q^2 = \frac{2 D S}{H}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}}$$

**Step 4: Verify minimum using second derivative:**

$$\frac{d^2(VTC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D S}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{(positive, hence minimum)}$$

**Assumptions of the EOQ Model:**
1. Demand $D$ is **known and constant** throughout the year.
2. Lead time is **constant and known**.
3. Order quantity $Q$ is **received instantaneously** (no partial deliveries).
4. Ordering cost $S$ is **fixed per order**, independent of quantity.
5. Holding cost $H$ is **linear** with average inventory $Q/2$.
6. **No stock-outs** are permitted.
7. Purchase price $P$ is **constant** (no quantity discounts).
8. The planning horizon is **continuous** (infinite, or year-long).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** These assumptions are *violated* in real life, which is why extensions exist — **EOQ with quantity discounts**, **EOQ with backorders**, **production order quantity (POQ)**, and **stochastic EOQ**.

---

#### (b) The annual demand of a component is 8,000 units. The ordering cost is Rs. 250 per order and the holding cost is Rs. 5 per unit per year. Determine (i) EOQ, (ii) number of orders per year, (iii) time between orders, (iv) total variable cost. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
$D = 8{,}000$ units/year, $S = \text{Rs. } 250/\text{order}$, $H = \text{Rs. } 5/\text{unit/year}$

**(i) Economic Order Quantity:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 250}{5}} = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}000{,}000}{5}} = \sqrt{800{,}000}$$

$$Q^* = 894.43 \approx 894 \text{ units}$$

**(ii) Number of orders per year:**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8{,}000}{894} \approx 8.95 \approx 9 \text{ orders/year}$$

**(iii) Time between orders (TBO):**
Assuming 300 working days per year:

$$TBO = \frac{300}{9} = 33.33 \text{ days/order}$$

**(iv) Total Variable Cost:**

$$VTC = \frac{D S}{Q^*} + \frac{H Q^*}{2}$$

$$VTC = \frac{8{,}000 \times 250}{894} + \frac{5 \times 894}{2}$$

$$VTC = 2237.13 + 2235.00 = \text{Rs. } 4{,}472.13/\text{year}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Trick:** At EOQ, Ordering Cost = Holding Cost. Here both are ~Rs. 2,236, which is a hallmark of a correct answer. The slight 1-rupee difference is due to rounding $Q^*$ from 894.43 to 894.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Mistaking Function for Use:** A pen's function is *to apply ink*, not *to write*. Examiners specifically test this. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** (e.g., $D$ in units/year vs. $D$ in units/month) in EOQ. Check unit consistency **first**.
> 3. **Not sorting items in descending ACV order** before applying ABC. Lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting assumptions of EOQ** in derivation questions. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for assumptions.
> 5. **Confusing Re-order Level with EOQ.** ROL uses *lead time demand*; EOQ uses *annual cost minimization*. They answer *different questions*.
> 6. **Not verifying second derivative** (i.e., not proving EOQ is a minimum). Always include the second-derivative test for full marks.
> 7. **Writing VECP without spelling it out** at least once. Use full form: *Value Engineering Change Proposal*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Value = Function / Cost** — the master equation.
- ✅ **Value Analysis = 6 phases**: Information → Function → Creative → Evaluation → Development → Recommendation.
- ✅ **Value Engineering** is proactive (design stage); **Value Analysis** is reactive (existing product).
- ✅ **Functions** must be written as a **2-word active verb + measurable noun** (e.g., "transmit torque", "contain fluid").
- ✅ **Basic Function** is the *raison d'être* of the product — never pay for *excess function*.
- ✅ **EOQ Formula:** $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$. Optimal when **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost**.
- ✅ **ROL** = Maximum Lead Time Demand. **SS** = Max lead time demand − Average lead time demand.
- ✅ **ABC Analysis** follows the **Pareto Principle** (80/20 rule). A items = ~10% of stock, ~70% of value.
- ✅ **VED** is *criticality-based*; **ABC** is *value-based*; **SDE** is *procurement-difficulty-based*; **FSN** is *movement-velocity-based*.
- ✅ **GOLF** classification: **G**overnment, **O**verseas, **L**ocal, **F**oreign sources.
- ✅ **HML** classification: **H**igh, **M**edium, **L**ow unit price items — used for **security control**.
- ✅ **EOQ Assumptions**: deterministic demand, no stock-outs, no quantity discounts, instantaneous replenishment, linear holding cost.
- ✅ **Value Analysis saves** are usually shared 50:30:20 between *company*, *customer*, and *VA team*.
- ✅ **Just-in-Time (JIT)** philosophy extends EOQ logic to its extreme — minimize inventory to near zero.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Aid for KTU Viva:**
> *"Value Analysis asks WHY, WHAT, and AT WHAT COST — and then finds a cheaper WAY to deliver the WHAT."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Integrated Materials Management (IMM)** is a unified, holistic, and systematic approach to planning, organizing, directing, and controlling all activities concerned with the flow of materials in an organization — from the identification of the need for materials, through their procurement, reception, storage, handling, issue, usage, and final disposal — treating them as a single, interdependent system rather than as a set of disconnected, isolated departmental functions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Snapshot:** IMM is the *coordinated unification* of the traditionally separate functions of *purchasing, inventory control, storekeeping, materials handling, warehousing, traffic and transportation, and materials planning* under a single management umbrella, driven by a common objective of *minimizing total material cost* while *maximizing service levels*.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Patient Care" Model

Imagine a large multi-specialty hospital. Years ago, every department (pathology lab, pharmacy, surgery, billing, reception) worked in silos. The patient had to carry papers from one desk to another, leading to delays, lost reports, repeated tests, and confusion.

Today, a *Central Patient Care Coordinator* integrates all these functions through a single digital record. The moment a patient is admitted, the lab knows, the pharmacy prepares, the surgeon is briefed, and the billing is pre-estimated — all in **one unified flow**.

**Integrated Materials Management works exactly the same way inside a manufacturing or production organization:**

| Hospital Analogy | Manufacturing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient admission & diagnosis | Materials planning & requirement |
| Lab tests & prescriptions | Purchasing & vendor coordination |
| Pharmacy & ward management | Storekeeping & warehousing |
| Surgery & recovery | Production usage of materials |
| Discharge & follow-up | Disposal / scrap management |
| Insurance & billing | Cost control & accounting |

### Key Standard Metrics in IMM

In any IMM system, three standard performance metrics dominate the KTU 2024 assessment framework:

- **Material Cost as a Percentage of Sales = (Total Material Cost / Total Sales Revenue) × 100**
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR) = Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) / Average Inventory Value**
- **Material Availability = (Actual Material Available / Material Required) × 100%**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Whenever a question asks "Explain the need for IMM," examiners expect you to mention the *coordination of traditionally fragmented functions* as the central theme. Memorize the three metrics above — they appear frequently in numerical Part A questions.

### The Three Pillars of IMM

Every Integrated Materials Management system stands on three fundamental pillars:

1. **Pillar 1 — Materials Planning & Control:** Forecasting demand, determining order quantities, and setting safety stock levels.
2. **Pillar 2 — Procurement & Vendor Management:** Sourcing, negotiation, purchase orders, and supplier evaluation.
3. **Pillar 3 — Storage, Handling & Distribution:** Warehousing, inventory accounting, materials handling equipment, and dispatch.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Evolution of the IMM Concept

The concept of Integrated Materials Management evolved through **four historical stages** as engineering practice matured:

1. **Stage 1 — Fragmented Functions (Pre-1950s):** Purchasing, stores, and production worked in isolation. Each department optimized its own cost, often at the expense of the other.
2. **Stage 2 — Coordinated Functions (1950s–1970s):** Recognition that purchasing, stores, and inventory were interlinked. Cross-departmental committees emerged.
3. **Stage 3 — Materials Management (1970s–1990s):** Formal creation of a "Materials Manager" position who owned the entire materials function.
4. **Stage 4 — Integrated Materials Management / Supply Chain (1990s–Present):** End-to-end integration extending beyond the firm to suppliers and customers, supported by ERP systems such as SAP, Oracle, and Microsoft Dynamics.

### Why IMM is Necessary — The Seven Driving Forces

According to the **KTU 2024 PEPET414 syllabus**, the following are the *mandatory reasons* students must know:

- **Rising Material Costs:** Materials typically constitute **50%–70%** of total product cost in manufacturing. Even a **1% reduction** in material cost can boost profit more than a **10% increase** in sales.
- **Capital Lock-up:** Excessive inventory ties up working capital and reduces ROI (Return on Investment).
- **Service Level Pressure:** Production downtime caused by material shortages leads to missed delivery commitments.
- **Globalization of Supply:** Vendors, regulations, and logistics are now international — requiring unified coordination.
- **Information Silos:** Without integration, the same data is re-entered multiple times, causing errors and delays.
- **Just-in-Time (JIT) Demands:** Modern JIT and Lean systems demand flawless coordination between purchasing, stores, and production.
- **Technology Enablement:** ERP and IoT systems have made end-to-end integration practically possible and economically attractive.

### Objectives of IMM

The objectives of IMM form a **hierarchical pyramid**:

- **Primary Objective:** Minimize the *Total Cost of Materials* across the entire value chain.
- **Secondary Objectives:**
  - Ensure uninterrupted supply of materials to production.
  - Maintain optimum inventory levels (not too high, not too low).
  - Procure materials at the *lowest total cost* (price + quality + delivery + service).
  - Maintain material quality and minimize wastage.
  - Develop reliable vendor relationships.
  - Provide efficient material handling, storage, and issue systems.
  - Support organizational profitability and competitiveness.

### Scope / Functions Covered Under IMM

The scope of IMM is **wide** and KTU 2024 expects students to enumerate it precisely:

- **Materials Planning:** Forecasting, MRP (Material Requirements Planning), BOM (Bill of Materials) analysis.
- **Purchasing:** Sourcing, vendor selection, negotiation, purchase order processing.
- **Inventory Control:** EOQ, safety stock, reorder point, ABC analysis, VED analysis.
- **Storekeeping:** Receipt, inspection, binning, issuance, accounting.
- **Warehousing:** Layout design, location strategy, capacity planning.
- **Materials Handling:** Selection of conveyors, cranes, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), forklifts.
- **Transportation & Traffic:** Inbound and outbound logistics optimization.
- **Standardization & Codification:** Reducing material variety through standardization.
- **Disposal & Scrap Management:** Salvage, recycling, and reverse logistics.
- **Materials Accounting & Cost Control:** Inventory valuation (FIFO, LIFO, Weighted Average).

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{Average\ Inventory}$ | Inventory Turnover Ratio | Times per year (4–12 typical) |
| $DOH = \dfrac{365}{ITR}$ | Days of Inventory on Hand | Days (30–90 typical) |
| $Material\ Cost\ Ratio = \dfrac{Total\ Material\ Cost}{Total\ Sales} \times 100$ | Material cost as % of sales | 30%–60% |
| $Service\ Level = \dfrac{Number\ of\ Demands\ Met}{Total\ Number\ of\ Demands} \times 100$ | Customer/material fill rate | 90%–99% target |
| $Carrying\ Cost\ \% = \dfrac{Annual\ Holding\ Cost}{Average\ Inventory\ Value} \times 100$ | Inventory carrying rate | 15%–30% per year |
| $ABC\ Cumulative\ \% = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{Value_i}{Total\ Value} \times 100$ | Cumulative value % in ABC | A-items ~80% value |
| $Stockout\ Probability = 1 - Service\ Level$ | Probability of running out | Should be $\le 5\%$ |
| $Order\ Cycle\ Time = Lead\ Time + Processing\ Time$ | Replenishment time | Days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** When asked to "list the functions of IMM" in a 3-mark question, students often forget *Standardization, Codification, and Disposal/Scrap Management*. Examiners frequently award 1 mark for each *correct* function — losing these is a common mark-deduction point.

### Real-World Utility in Engineering

In modern automotive, aerospace, and electronics manufacturing, IMM is the *backbone* of:
- **Lean Manufacturing** (Toyota Production System).
- **Six Sigma Quality Programs** (variability reduction in supply).
- **Industry 4.0** (IoT-enabled real-time tracking of materials).
- **Green Manufacturing** (reverse logistics, recycling, scrap reduction).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Materials Management" with "Integrated Materials Management." The *key differentiator* is the word **INTEGRATED** — i.e., unified coordination. Always emphasize integration in your answer.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: The Material Cost-to-Profit Sensitivity

A KTU-favourite numerical question type is: *"How much must material cost reduce to achieve a target profit increase?"* Let us derive the governing equation step-by-step.

**Given:**
- $S$ = Annual Sales Revenue (₹)
- $M$ = Annual Material Cost (₹)
- $P$ = Current Annual Profit (₹)
- $\Delta M$ = Required reduction in material cost
- $\Delta P$ = Target increase in profit

**Step 1:** Write the basic profit equation.

$$
P = S - M - O
$$

where $O$ = Other costs (labour, overhead, etc.) assumed constant.

**Step 2:** Express the new profit after material cost reduction.

$$
P_{new} = S - (M - \Delta M) - O
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract the original profit from the new profit.

$$
\Delta P = P_{new} - P = [S - (M - \Delta M) - O] - [S - M - O]
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the bracketed terms. Note that $S$ and $O$ cancel out.

$$
\Delta P = -M + \Delta M + M = \Delta M
$$

**Step 5:** Final insight.

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = \Delta M}
$$

**Interpretation:** A reduction in material cost produces an **equal, one-to-one increase in profit** (assuming sales and other costs remain constant). This is why a **1% reduction in material cost** is more powerful than a **10% increase in sales** — a classic KTU viva question.

**Step 6:** Convert to a percentage. If sales must grow to deliver the same profit gain instead:

$$
\Delta S = \frac{\Delta M}{1 - \text{tax rate}}
$$

**Step 7:** For example, if material cost = ₹50 lakh, profit = ₹10 lakh, sales = ₹100 lakh, and tax = 30%:

$$
\Delta S = \frac{\Delta M}{0.70} = \frac{1,00,000}{0.70} \approx 1,42,857\ \text{₹}
$$

Thus, **₹1 lakh material savings ≈ ₹1.43 lakh additional sales** — a powerful argument for IMM.

---

### Derivation 2: Inventory Turnover Ratio and Days on Hand

**Given:**
- $C$ = Annual Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)
- $I_{avg}$ = Average inventory value

**Step 1:** Define Inventory Turnover Ratio.

$$
ITR = \frac{C}{I_{avg}}
$$

**Step 2:** The dimension of ITR is *times per year* (i.e., how many times the average inventory is "used up and replaced" annually).

**Step 3:** Derive Days of Inventory on Hand (DOH) — the average number of days a unit of material sits in the warehouse.

$$
DOH = \frac{365\ \text{days/year}}{ITR}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute ITR.

$$
DOH = \frac{365 \times I_{avg}}{C}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerical example. If $C$ = ₹24,00,000 per year and $I_{avg}$ = ₹2,00,000:

$$
ITR = \frac{24,00,000}{2,00,000} = 12\ \text{times/year}
$$

$$
DOH = \frac{365}{12} \approx 30.4\ \text{days}
$$

**Interpretation:** Inventory turns over 12 times a year, meaning on average a material item stays in the warehouse for about 30 days.

---

### Derivation 3: Safety Stock Service Level Relationship

**Given:**
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation of demand during lead time
- $z$ = Z-score corresponding to chosen service level

**Step 1:** Standard safety stock formula.

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LTD}
$$

where $\sigma_{LTD}$ = standard deviation of demand during lead time.

**Step 2:** If demand follows a normal distribution and lead time is constant:

$$
\sigma_{LTD} = \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

where $\sigma_d$ = daily demand std. deviation and $L$ = lead time in days.

**Step 3:** Substitute back.

$$
SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute for a 95% service level (where $z = 1.65$), $\sigma_d = 10$ units/day, $L = 9$ days.

$$
SS = 1.65 \times 10 \times \sqrt{9} = 1.65 \times 10 \times 3 = 49.5\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 5:** Order point (ROP).

$$
ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS
$$

For average daily demand $\bar{d} = 50$ units:

$$
ROP = 50 \times 9 + 49.5 = 499.5\ \text{units}
$$

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Integrated Materials Management (IMM). List any four of its primary functions.**

**Model Answer:**

Integrated Materials Management is a unified and coordinated approach to planning, organizing, and controlling all activities related to the flow of materials — from identification of need to final disposal — under a single management framework.

Four primary functions of IMM:

1. **Materials Planning** — forecasting demand and determining order quantities.
2. **Purchasing** — sourcing, vendor selection, and procurement.
3. **Inventory Control** — maintaining optimum stock levels through EOQ, ABC, safety stock.
4. **Storekeeping and Warehousing** — receiving, storing, and issuing materials efficiently.

*(Other acceptable answers: Materials Handling, Transportation, Standardization, Disposal, Materials Accounting.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State any three objectives of Integrated Materials Management.**

**Model Answer:**

1. To ensure **uninterrupted supply** of materials to production at the lowest total cost.
2. To **minimize inventory investment** while maintaining desired service levels.
3. To develop and maintain **reliable vendor relationships** and ensure material quality.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the concept of Integrated Materials Management. How does it differ from the traditional fragmented approach?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Integrated Materials Management (IMM) is a coordinated, unified approach to managing the entire materials flow in an organization, encompassing planning, purchasing, inventory, storekeeping, handling, transportation, and disposal, under a single management umbrella with a common objective of minimizing total material cost while maximizing service levels.

**Differences from the Traditional Fragmented Approach:**

| Aspect | Fragmented Approach | Integrated Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Departmental silos | Unified under Materials Manager |
| Objective | Departmental profit optimization | Total system cost minimization |
| Information flow | Slow, paper-based, re-entered | Real-time, shared ERP database |
| Decision making | Independent, often conflicting | Coordinated, aligned |
| Inventory | Excessive (overstock) | Optimized (JIT, EOQ) |
| Vendor relations | Adversarial | Strategic, long-term |
| Response time | Slow, reactive | Fast, proactive |
| Cost focus | Purchase price only | Total cost of ownership |

**Benefits of IMM:**
