# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Define performance appraisal of materials management. List any four performance metrics used for this purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

Performance appraisal of materials management is a systematic and continuous process of evaluating the efficiency and effectiveness of materials-related activities (such as purchasing, storing, issuing, and handling) against pre-defined standards and benchmarks, with the objective of identifying variances, controlling costs, and improving service levels.

The four important performance metrics are:
1. **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** – measures how often inventory is sold and replaced.
2. **Service Level** – measures the percentage of orders fulfilled on time.
3. **Stockout Frequency** – measures the number of times stockouts occur in a given period.
4. **Material Cost Variance (MCV)** – measures the deviation between standard and actual material cost.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Q: What is vendor rating? Explain the weighted point method of vendor evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**

Vendor rating is a performance appraisal technique used to evaluate and rank suppliers based on multiple parameters such as quality, delivery, price, and service. It helps the organization to select the most suitable vendor for a contract.

In the **Weighted Point Method**, each performance parameter is assigned a weight $w_i$ (such that the sum of all weights = 1) and the vendor is rated on each parameter on a numerical scale (e.g., 0 to 100). The overall vendor score is calculated using the formula:

$$\text{Vendor Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_i$$

The vendor with the highest composite score is selected. This method is widely used because it allows the organization to prioritize parameters based on strategic importance (e.g., higher weight on quality for aerospace components, higher weight on price for commodity items).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks - Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`**
**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various performance metrics used for appraising materials management, with appropriate formulas. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A manufacturing company has the following data: COGS = ₹ 80,00,000; Opening Inventory = ₹ 10,00,000; Closing Inventory = ₹ 6,00,000. Calculate: (i) Average Inventory, (ii) Inventory Turnover Ratio, (iii) Days of Inventory, and (iv) Comment on the inventory management efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Performance Metrics for Materials Management:**

Performance appraisal of materials management is carried out through a structured set of metrics grouped into three categories:

**Category 1: Cost-Based Metrics**

1. **Material Cost Variance (MCV):** Measures the difference between standard and actual material cost.
   $$\text{MCV} = (\text{Standard Cost}) - (\text{Actual Cost})$$

2. **Material Price Variance (MPV):** Measures the impact of price deviation.
   $$\text{MPV} = (\text{SP} - \text{AP}) \times \text{AQ}$$

3. **Material Usage Variance (MUV):** Measures the impact of consumption deviation.
   $$\text{MUV} = (\text{SQ} - \text{AQ}) \times \text{SP}$$

4. **Inventory Carrying Cost:** Annual cost of holding inventory (interest, storage, insurance, obsolescence).
   $$\text{ICC} = \text{Average Inventory} \times \text{Carrying Cost Rate}$$

5. **Ordering Cost:** Cost per purchase order (clerical, communication, transport, inspection).
   $$\text{OC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Annual Ordering Cost}}{\text{Number of Orders}}$$

6. **Material Handling Cost Ratio:**
   $$\text{MHC Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Material Handling Cost}}{\text{Total Production Cost}} \times 100$$

**Category 2: Efficiency-Based Metrics**

7. **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR):**
   $$\text{ITR} = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$$

8. **Days of Inventory (DOI):**
   $$\text{DOI} = \dfrac{365}{\text{ITR}}$$

9. **Inventory Accuracy:**
   $$\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{\text{Items Matching Physical Count}}{\text{Total Items Counted}} \times 100$$

10. **Obsolescence Rate:**
    $$\text{Obsolescence Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Value of Obsolete Stock}}{\text{Total Inventory Value}} \times 100$$

**Category 3: Service-Based Metrics**

11. **Service Level:**
    $$\text{SL} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Met on Time}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100$$

12. **Fill Rate:**
    $$\text{FR} = \dfrac{\text{Units Delivered on Time}}{\text{Total Units Ordered}} \times 100$$

13. **Order Lead Time (OLT):**
    $$\text{OLT} = \text{Order Processing Time} + \text{Supplier Lead Time} + \text{Receiving Time}$$

14. **Stockout Frequency:**
    $$\text{Stockout Freq.} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Stockout Occurrences}}{\text{Period}}$$

15. **Vendor Rating Score:**
    $$\text{Vendor Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot R_i$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- COGS = ₹ 80,00,000
- Opening Inventory = ₹ 10,00,000
- Closing Inventory = ₹ 6,00,000

**Step 1: Average Inventory**
$$\text{Average Inventory} = \dfrac{10{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000}{2} = \dfrac{16{,}00{,}000}{2} = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 2: Inventory Turnover Ratio**
$$\text{ITR} = \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 10 \text{ times/year}$$

**Step 3: Days of Inventory**
$$\text{DOI} = \dfrac{365}{10} = 36.5 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Comments on Efficiency**

An ITR of 10 times/year means the company is selling and replenishing its inventory 10 times annually, which is a **healthy and efficient** turnover for a medium-scale manufacturing company. The DOI of 36.5 days indicates that, on average, an item remains in inventory for about 5 weeks before being consumed. This shows:
- **Adequate liquidity:** Capital is not locked up in inventory.
- **Low obsolescence risk:** Items are moving fast.
- **Reasonable service levels:** Stock is available when needed.

If the industry benchmark is, say, 8-12 times/year, this company is performing **at par with industry standards**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Modified]`**
**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of vendor rating. Describe in detail the weighted point method and the cost ratio method of vendor evaluation with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Two vendors, X and Y, submitted the following data. Quality weight = 0.5, Delivery weight = 0.3, Price weight = 0.2. Rate on 100 scale: X: 80, 70, 90; Y: 75, 90, 80. Determine which vendor is better. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Vendor Rating Concept and Methods:**

**Concept:** Vendor rating (or vendor evaluation) is a sub-system of performance appraisal of materials management that aims to assess the suitability and reliability of suppliers systematically. It enables purchasing managers to make objective decisions about awarding contracts, continuing relationships, or replacing vendors.

**Method 1: Weighted Point Method**

In this method, several performance parameters (quality, delivery, price, service, etc.) are identified, and each is assigned a weight $w_i$ based on its strategic importance. The sum of all weights must equal 1. Each vendor is then rated on each parameter on a numerical scale (commonly 0-100 or 1-10). The composite vendor score is:

$$\text{Vendor Score} = w_1 R_1 + w_2 R_2 + \ldots + w_n R_n$$

**Example:** If a company assigns weights 0.5 to quality and 0.5 to delivery, and rates Vendor A as 90 on quality and 80 on delivery, and Vendor B as 80 on quality and 95 on delivery:
- $\text{Score}_A = 0.5(90) + 0.5(80) = 85$
- $\text{Score}_B = 0.5(80) + 0.5(95) = 87.5$

Vendor B would be preferred.

**Method 2: Cost Ratio Method (Comparative Method)**

In this method, the lowest quote is treated as the benchmark (100%), and other vendors are rated as a percentage of the lowest quote.

$$\text{Vendor Rating} = \dfrac{\text{Lowest Quoted Price}}{\text{Vendor's Quoted Price}} \times 100$$

**Example:** If Vendor A quotes ₹ 100, Vendor B quotes ₹ 120, and Vendor C quotes ₹ 90:
- $\text{Rating}_A = (90 / 100) \times 100 = 90$
- $\text{Rating}_B = (90 / 120) \times 100 = 75$
- $\text{Rating}_C = (90 / 90) \times 100 = 100$

Vendor C is the most competitive on price. This method is simple but only considers price, ignoring quality and delivery — hence it is used in conjunction with other methods.

**Difference between Methods:** The weighted point method is more comprehensive and considers multiple parameters, while the cost ratio method is simpler and primarily used for price-sensitive purchases. In practice, organizations use a **hybrid approach** combining both.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Weight ($w_i$) | Vendor X ($R_X$) | Vendor Y ($R_Y$) |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| Quality | 0.5 | 80 | 75 |
| Delivery | 0.3 | 70 | 90 |
| Price | 0.2 | 90 | 80 |

**Step 1: Verify weight sum**
$$0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \;\; ✓$$

**Step 2: Compute Vendor X Score**

$$\text{Score}_X = (0.5 \times 80) + (0.3 \times 70) + (0.2 \times 90)$$
$$\text{Score}_X = 40.0 + 21.0 + 18.0$$
$$\boxed{\text{Score}_X = 79.0}$$

**Step 3: Compute Vendor Y Score**

$$\text{Score}_Y = (0.5 \times 75) + (0.3 \times 90) + (0.2 \times 80)$$
$$\text{Score}_Y = 37.5 + 27.0 + 16.0$$
$$\boxed{\text{Score}_Y = 80.5}$$

**Step 4: Decision**

Since $\text{Score}_Y = 80.5 > \text{Score}_X = 79.0$, **Vendor Y should be selected**. Although Vendor X is slightly better in quality, Vendor Y's superior delivery performance and reasonable price compensate for the quality difference. Since the weights reflect organizational priorities (quality is most important at 0.5), the margin is small, suggesting the company may also consider negotiating quality improvements with Vendor Y.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
> 
> 1. **Weight Normalization Error:** Students often forget to ensure that the sum of all weights equals 1. If not, normalize them: $w_i^{normalized} = w_i / \sum w_i$. A vendor rating calculated with un-normalized weights will yield an inflated score and lose **2-3 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Variance Sign Convention:** In KTU papers, the convention is **Favorable = Positive, Adverse = Negative**. Reversing this loses marks.
> 
> 3. **MCV Verification:** Always cross-check that $\text{MCV} = \text{MPV} + \text{MUV}$. If it doesn't, you have made a sign error — KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 
> 4. **ITR Denominator:** Some students use only closing inventory instead of average. While both are accepted, using **average inventory** is the standard and preferred method.
> 
> 5. **Vendor Rating Direction:** A higher composite score is better. Students sometimes mistakenly select the vendor with the lower score, losing 1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Units Confusion:** In Days of Inventory formula, the constant is **365** (days in a year). If annual data is given for a 360-day accounting year, the constant changes to 360. KTU sometimes uses 360 to test your attention.
> 
> 7. **Omitting Assumptions:** When solving numerical problems, always state the given data and the formula you are using before substituting. Students who directly substitute lose 1-2 marks for "lack of clarity".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary - Read 3 Times Before Exam:**

- **Definition:** Performance appraisal is the **systematic measurement, evaluation, and monitoring** of materials management activities against standards to enable continuous improvement.

- **Key Principle:** *"You cannot manage what you cannot measure."* Performance appraisal is a **closed-loop, continuous, periodic** activity, not a one-time audit.

- **3 Categories of Metrics:**
  1. **Cost-Based** → MCV, MPV, MUV, Carrying Cost, Ordering Cost
  2. **Efficiency-Based** → ITR, DOI, Inventory Accuracy, Obsolescence Rate
  3. **Service-Based** → Service Level, Fill Rate, Stockout Frequency, Lead Time

- **Master Formula: ITR = COGS / Average Inventory** — the single most tested formula in KTU 2024.

- **DOI = 365 / ITR** — companion to ITR, tells how many days an item sits in inventory.

- **Vendor Rating** uses the weighted point method: $\text{Score} = \sum w_i R_i$, where $\sum w_i = 1$.

- **MCV = MPV + MUV** — always verify this identity in numerical problems.

- **Favorable Variance = Positive value**; **Adverse Variance = Negative value** (KTU sign convention).

- **At EOQ**, ordering cost = carrying cost. This is a favourite KTU theory question.

- **Higher ITR = Better** (capital is not locked up). **Lower DOI = Better**.

- **Service Level > 95%** is the industry benchmark for manufacturing.

- **Vendor rating threshold for contract award** is typically a score above **80-85** out of 100.

- **Materials cost** is often **50-70% of total product cost** in manufacturing, which is why performance appraisal of materials management directly impacts **profitability**.

- **Carrying cost rate** typically ranges from **15% to 30%** of inventory value per year.

- **Performance appraisal cycle:** Set Standards → Measure → Compare → Analyze Variance → Corrective Action → Feedback to Next Cycle.

- **Industry 4.0 connection:** Modern ERP systems automate these calculations in real-time dashboards, but the underlying formulas remain the same as studied in KTU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Management Information System for Materials Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET414 - MATERIAL HANDLING AND MATERIALS MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Management Information System for Materials Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Management Information System (MIS)** is a computer-based, integrated, man–machine system that provides information to support the planning, control, and operational functions of an organisation. When applied to the materials function, it is termed a **Management Information System for Materials Management (MIS-MM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** MIS for Materials Management is a formal system designed to collect, process, store, analyse, and disseminate information related to procurement, inventory, warehousing, material handling, and disposal in a timely and accurate manner to decision-makers at strategic, tactical, and operational levels.

According to **G. S. T. G. K. Aparna** (standard KTU reference) and the **APICS/ASCM Dictionary (16th Edition)**, MIS is the *“formal routine for the collection, processing, storage, and dissemination of data into information needed to support management functions.”*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital ICU Dashboard" View

Imagine a **hospital ICU** monitoring a critical patient:
- **Sensors** (BP cuff, ECG, pulse oximeter) continuously **collect** data.
- The **central monitor (processing unit)** converts raw signals into readable values, trends, and alarms.
- **Nurses and doctors** (decision-makers) view colour-coded screens (outputs) and adjust medication (action).
- **Feedback** from the patient’s response updates the display in real-time.

In the same way, an **MIS for Materials Management** is the *digital ICU dashboard of a factory*:
- Raw data comes from **GRNs, purchase orders, stock registers, bin cards, vendor invoices**.
- The **system** converts these into reports — stock status, ABC analysis, lead-time exceptions, supplier performance KPIs.
- **Managers** use these outputs to decide *when to reorder, how much to buy, which vendor to blacklist*.
- **Feedback loops** refine forecasting, safety stock, and reorder points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** An MIS is **not just a database** — it is a *decision-support system* that transforms raw transactional data into actionable management information. This distinction is a frequently tested 3-mark concept.

---

## 1.3 Salient Features of MIS for Materials Management

- **Systematic & Formal:** Follows defined procedures, formats, and schedules.
- **Integrated:** Links procurement, stores, production planning, finance, and marketing.
- **Subsystem of Overall MIS:** Part of the organisation’s broader information architecture.
- **Computer-Based (but not exclusively):** Modern MIS is digital, though manual registers still exist in SMEs.
- **Information-Oriented:** Converts **data → information → intelligence**.
- **Future-Oriented:** Provides historical, current, and predictive views.
- **User-Friendly:** Outputs are tailored to the literacy level of each user (operator vs. top management).

---

## 1.4 Objectives of MIS-MM

1. To ensure **right material**, in the **right quantity**, of the **right quality**, at the **right time**, at the **right place**, at the **right cost** (6R principle).
2. To provide **real-time visibility** of inventory across multiple locations.
3. To enable **exception reporting** (e.g., stock-out, overstock, delayed deliveries).
4. To support **vendor evaluation and rating**.
5. To generate **statutory and regulatory reports** (e.g., GST, customs, excise).
6. To **reduce decision latency** by providing summarised information to the right level.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** The 6R framework is a *high-yield* concept. Use it verbatim in 7-mark and 14-mark answers to demonstrate conceptual depth.

---

## 1.5 Hierarchical Levels of Management & Information Needs

| Level | Manager Type | Materials Information Required | Time Horizon |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic** | Top Management / MD / Board | Capital investment in warehouses, long-term vendor contracts, ERP selection, make-or-buy policy | 3–10 years |
| **Tactical** | Materials Manager / Purchase Manager | Budgeting, ABC-XYZ classification, supplier development, inventory policy revision | 1–12 months |
| **Operational** | Storekeeper / Purchase Officer | Reorder alerts, GRN, stock receipts, issue slips, bin card updates | Daily / Real-time |

This **three-tier hierarchy** is the foundation on which an MIS is designed.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components / Architecture of MIS-MM

An MIS for materials management consists of **five interlinked sub-systems** (the classic 5-component model). Each is examined in detail below.

### A. Input Subsystem
- **Source documents:** Purchase Requisition (PR), Purchase Order (PO), Goods Received Note (GRN), Material Requisition (MR), Material Return Note (MRN), Bill of Materials (BOM), Inventory Ledger.
- **Data capture modes:** Barcode scanners, RFID, EDI (Electronic Data Interchange), Vendor portals, IoT sensors on bins.
- **Validation rules:** Mandatory fields, range checks, duplicate-invoice check, three-way match (PO–GRN–Invoice).

### B. Processing Subsystem
- **Database Management System (DBMS):** Stores master data (item master, vendor master) and transactional data (PO, receipts, issues).
- **Logic engines:** EOQ calculation, safety stock re-computation, reorder point trigger, ABC ranking, lead-time analysis.
- **Algorithms running in background:** MRP (Material Requirements Planning), forecasting (Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing), lot-sizing (L4L, Periodic Order Quantity).

### C. Output Subsystem
- **Routine reports:** Daily stock statement, pending PO list, vendor delivery performance.
- **Exception reports:** Items below safety stock, slow-moving items, aged inventory (>180 days), vendor quality rejections.
- **Query-based outputs:** Ad-hoc SQL/ERP queries raised by managers.
- **Visual dashboards:** Heat maps of stock-out risk, Pareto charts of consumption, trend lines of lead time.

### D. Feedback Subsystem
- **Corrective action loop:** Reorder point missed → purchase officer places order → GRN updates stock → MIS recalculates next reorder.
- **Performance feedback:** Forecast accuracy (MAPE), inventory turnover ratio, fill rate, vendor OTD (On-Time-Delivery) %.

### E. Control Subsystem
- **Audit trails:** Who created/modified a PO, with timestamp.
- **Authorization matrix:** Segregation of duties (creator ≠ approver).
- **Data security:** Role-based access control (RBAC), encryption, GDPR/IT Act compliance.

---

## 2.2 Information Flow Diagram (Logical View)

The classical **Anthony’s Hierarchy** maps directly to MIS outputs:

$$
\text{Strategic MIS} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Tactical MIS} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Operational MIS}
$$

- **Strategic:** Long-term capacity, supplier base expansion, technology roadmap.
- **Tactical:** Inventory budgets, EOQ revision, vendor rationalisation.
- **Operational:** Daily receipts/issues, stock positions, exception alerts.

---

## 2.3 Subsystems of MIS Specifically for Materials Management

| Sub-system | Function | Typical Outputs |
|---|---|---|
| **Purchasing MIS** | Vendor selection, PO tracking | Vendor rating, Open PO report, Cost savings |
| **Inventory MIS** | Stock monitoring, ABC analysis | Stock status, Aging report, Dead stock |
| **Stores MIS** | Receipts, issues, bin management | Bin card, GRN, Issue slip, Stock variance |
| **Forecasting MIS** | Demand prediction | Forecast vs Actual, MAPE, trend charts |
| **Material Planning MIS (MRP)** | Net requirement calculation | Planned order release, Exception messages |
| **Vendor MIS** | Performance evaluation | Scorecard, Audit reports, Blacklist alerts |
| **Cost MIS** | Material cost control | Price variance, Usage variance, PPV report |
| **Logistics MIS** | In-transit tracking | Shipment status, Freight cost, ETA |

---

## 2.4 Database Design — Key Master & Transaction Files

- **Item Master:** Item code, description, UOM, HSN code, MOQ, lead time, safety stock.
- **Vendor Master:** Vendor code, name, GSTIN, payment terms, rating, approved status.
- **BOM Master:** Finished good code, component code, quantity per assembly.
- **PO Transaction File:** PO number, date, vendor, item, qty, rate, delivery date.
- **Stock Transaction File:** Item, location, batch, qty, movement type (receipt/issue/transfer).
- **Vendor Performance File:** PO date, promised date, actual date, accepted qty, rejected qty.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Numerical Sub-Parts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **commonly asked in Part B** as sub-parts (7-mark numericals) where students are expected to compute a metric and *then* explain how it would be reported in an MIS dashboard.

| # | Metric / Formula | Equation | Unit | When Used in MIS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** | $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units / order | Determines lot size in PO suggestion report |
| 2 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = D \times L + SS$ | units | Triggers “Reorder Alert” exception |
| 3 | **Safety Stock (SS)** | $SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{DL}$ | units | Sets minimum buffer in Stock Status report |
| 4 | **Average Inventory** | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{EOQ}{2} + SS$ | units | Used in Inventory Turnover computation |
| 5 | **Inventory Turnover Ratio** | $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{\bar{I}}$ | times/year | KPI in Materials Manager dashboard |
| 6 | **Total Annual Cost (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | ₹/year | Justifies EOQ in Cost MIS |
| 7 | **Vendor On-Time-Delivery (OTD) %** | $OTD\% = \dfrac{\text{OTD POs}}{\text{Total POs}} \times 100$ | % | Vendor scorecard in Vendor MIS |
| 8 | **Vendor Rejection % (QR)** | $QR\% = \dfrac{\text{Rejected Qty}}{\text{Inspected Qty}} \times 100$ | % | Vendor quality KPI |
| 9 | **MAPE (Forecast Accuracy)** | $MAPE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{A_t} \times 100$ | % | Forecasting MIS performance |
| 10 | **Fill Rate (Service Level)** | $FR = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Stockout Units}}{\text{Demand Units}}$ | ratio | Customer service dashboard |
| 11 | **Carrying Cost %** | $CC\% = \dfrac{\text{Annual Holding Cost}}{\text{Avg Inventory Value}} \times 100$ | % | Cost MIS |
| 12 | **Order Lead Time (OLT)** | $OLT = \dfrac{\sum (GRN\ Date - PO\ Date)}{N}$ | days | Vendor scorecard |

Where: $D$ = Annual demand, $S$ = Ordering cost per order, $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year, $L$ = Lead time, $Z$ = Service-level z-score, $\sigma_{DL}$ = Std. deviation of demand during lead time, $A_t$ = Actual demand, $F_t$ = Forecast demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions on MIS, the model answer usually expects (a) a numerical/analytical sub-part using the above formulas, followed by (b) a descriptive sub-part explaining how the result populates a specific MIS report.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Domains

- **Manufacturing Plants (e.g., HMT, BHEL, Maruti Suzuki):** MIS-MM drives **just-in-time** and **lean production** by signalling Kanban replenishment.
- **Construction (L&T, Afcons):** MIS tracks cement, steel, and aggregate consumption against BOQ (Bill of Quantities).
- **Automotive Supply Chains:** Tier-1 vendors share real-time MIS dashboards with OEMs for **Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)**.
- **Pharma & FMCG:** MIS supports **FEFO (First-Expiry-First-Out)** and **batch traceability** mandated by regulatory bodies.
- **E-commerce Warehouses (Amazon, Flipkart):** MIS is the backbone of **pick-pack-ship** operations and dynamic slotting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are the **most-asked analytical sub-parts** when an MIS question is paired with a numerical. The derivation of **EOQ** and **Total Cost minimisation** is mandatory in the KTU Production Engineering syllabus.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Wilson’s Formula

The EOQ is the order quantity that **minimises the total annual inventory cost**, assuming constant demand, constant lead time, instantaneous replenishment, and no stock-outs.

### Step 1 — Define the Total Annual Cost (TC)

$$
TC = \text{Ordering Cost} + \text{Holding Cost} + \text{Purchasing Cost}
$$

For decision-making on lot size, purchasing cost is constant (independent of $Q$), so we minimise:

$$
TC = \dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H
$$

**Textual Explanation:** $D/Q$ gives the *number of orders per year*; multiplied by $S$, this is the annual ordering cost. $Q/2$ is the *average inventory*; multiplied by $H$, this is the annual holding cost.

### Step 2 — Differentiate TC with respect to Q and set to zero

$$
\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0
$$

**Textual Explanation:** The first term is the derivative of the ordering cost (negative because cost decreases as $Q$ grows); the second is the derivative of the holding cost (positive because cost increases with $Q$).

### Step 3 — Solve for Q

$$
\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} = \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** At the optimum, the slopes of the two cost curves are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign.

$$
Q^2 = \dfrac{2DS}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,EOQ = Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}\,}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify the Minimum (Second-Order Test)

$$
\dfrac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{(since all parameters are positive)}
$$

Hence $TC$ is **convex** in $Q$, confirming a minimum at $Q^{*}$.

### Step 5 — Minimum Total Cost

$$
TC_{min} = \dfrac{D}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} \cdot S + \dfrac{\sqrt{2DS/H}}{2} \cdot H
$$

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2DSH}
$$

**Textual Explanation:** At the EOQ, ordering cost equals holding cost — a classic *equal-cost crossing* principle useful in cost-mis dashboard visualisations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing firm consumes **10,000 units/year** of a raw material. Ordering cost is **₹200/order**, holding cost is **₹8/unit/year**, and lead time is **15 days**. The standard deviation of demand during lead time is 50 units. The firm desires a **95 % service level** (Z = 1.65). The MIS must suggest the optimal order quantity, reorder point, and annual total cost at EOQ. Assume 300 working days/year.

**Step 1 — Calculate EOQ**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{8}} = \sqrt{500{,}000} \approx 707\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily Demand**

$$
d = \dfrac{D}{\text{working days}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{300} \approx 33.33\ \text{units/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Lead-Time Demand**

$$
D_L = d \times L = 33.33 \times 15 = 500\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 4 — Safety Stock**

$$
SS = Z \cdot \sigma_{DL} = 1.65 \times 50 = 82.5 \approx 83\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 5 — Reorder Point**

$$
ROP = D_L + SS = 500 + 83 = 583\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Cost at EOQ**

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200 \times 8} = \sqrt{32{,}000{,}000} = ₹5{,}657
$$

**Step 7 — Number of Orders per Year**

$$
N = \dfrac{D}{EOQ} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{707} \approx 14.14 \approx 14\ \text{orders/year}
$$

**Step 8 — Time Between Orders (TBO)**

$$
TBO = \dfrac{EOQ}{d} = \dfrac{707}{33.33} \approx 21.2\ \text{days}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Metric Surfaces in MIS Reports

| Computed Metric | Threshold | MIS Report It Populates | Trigger / Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| EOQ = 707 | ± 5 % | **PO Suggestion Report** | Auto-generate draft PR |
| ROP = 583 | Below 583 | **Reorder Alert (Exception)** | Email to Purchase Officer |
| SS = 83 | Below 83 | **Stock Status — Red Flag** | Manager review |
| TC = ₹5,657 | ± 10 % variance | **Cost MIS Dashboard** | Variance analysis |
| ITR (assumed avg stock 437) | < 6 | **Inventory Health KPI** | Slow-moving review |
| Vendor OTD < 90 % | < 90 % | **Vendor Scorecard (Red)** | Vendor development / de-list |

---

## 3.4 Vendor Performance Scorecard — A Typical MIS Output

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** *(a) Compute the composite vendor score using the formula below; (b) Design the vendor performance MIS report explaining each KPI, threshold, and corrective action.*

$$
\text{Composite Score} = w_1 \cdot OTD\% + w_2 \cdot QR_{inv}\% + w_3 \cdot \dfrac{1}{Price\ Variance\%} + w_4 \cdot Lead\ Time\ Adherence\%
$$

Where weights $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$ (e.g., 0.35, 0.30, 0.20, 0.15).

A vendor with composite score **< 60 %** is auto-flagged for **vendor development or de-listing** by the MIS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of MIS for Materials Management

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **five-component MIS architecture** (Input → Process → Output → Feedback → Control) specifically contextualised for the materials function.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT[External Environment]
        VENDOR[Vendor / Supplier]
        MKT[Market / Customer Demand]
        REG[Regulatory Bodies - GST - Customs]
    end

    subgraph IN[Input Subsystem]
        PR[Purchase Requisition PR]
        PO[Purchase Order PO]
        GRN[Goods Received Note GRN]
        MR[Material Requisition MR]
        BOM[Bill of Materials BOM]
    end

    subgraph DB[Database DBMS]
        IM[Item Master]
        VM[Vendor Master]
        ST[Stock Ledger]
        VT[Vendor Transaction Log]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Processing Subsystem]
        EOQ_ENG[EOQ Engine]
        ROP_ENG[Reorder Point Engine]
        ABC_ENG[ABC XYZ Classifier]
        MRP_ENG[MRP Netting Engine]
        FCST[Forecasting Module]
        VEN_SCR[Vendor Scoring Engine]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Subsystem]
        R1[Daily Stock Status Report]
        R2[Reorder Exception Alert]
        R3[ABC Analysis Report]
        R4[Vendor Scorecard]
        R5[MRP Planned Orders]
        R6[Cost Variance Dashboard]
        R7[Statutory GST Customs Report]
    end

    subgraph DEC[Decision Makers]
        OP[Storekeeper]
        POFF[Purchase Officer]
        MM[Materials Manager]
        TOP[Top Management Board]
    end

    subgraph FB[Feedback Subsystem]
        FA[Actual vs Forecast Variance]
        FB2[OTD Feedback to Vendor]
        FC[Reorder Point Recalibration]
    end

    subgraph CTL[Control Subsystem]
        AUD[Audit Trail Logs]
        RBAC[Role Based Access Control]
        SOX[SOX IT Act Compliance]
    end

    VENDOR --> GRN
    MKT --> PR
    MKT --> FCST
    REG --> R7

    PR --> PO
    BOM --> MRP_ENG
    PR --> ST
    GRN --> ST
    MR --> ST
    PO --> VT

    IM --> EOQ_ENG
    IM --> ROP_ENG
    IM --> ABC_ENG
    ST --> ROP_ENG
    VM --> VEN_SCR
    VT --> VEN_SCR
    FCST --> ROP_ENG
    FCST --> MRP_ENG

    EOQ_ENG --> R1
    ROP_ENG --> R2
    ABC_ENG --> R3
    VEN_SCR --> R4
    MRP_ENG --> R5
    ST --> R1
    ST --> R6
    R7 --> REG

    R1 --> OP
    R2 --> POFF
    R3 --> MM
    R4 --> MM
    R5 --> POFF
    R6 --> TOP
    R7 --> TOP

    FA --> FCST
    FB2 --> VM
    FC --> ROP_ENG

    AUD --> CTL_BOX[Control Layer]
    RBAC --> CTL_BOX
    SOX --> CTL_BOX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The diagram flows from **External Sources → Input → DBMS → Processing Engines → Reports → Decision Makers → Feedback → Control**. The Control layer envelops the entire system (shown as a guardrail) — a common KTU visual expected in 14-mark answers.

---

## 4.2 Information Pyramid — Anthony’s Hierarchy Applied to Materials MIS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Strategic Level - 3 to 10 years - Make or Buy - ERP Selection - Warehouse Expansion] --> B[Tactical Level - 1 to 12 months - ABC XYZ Reclassification - Budget Revision - Vendor Rationalisation]
    B --> C[Operational Level - Daily Real time - GRN - Stock Issues - Bin Card - Reorder Alerts]
    C --> B
    B --> A
```

- **Vertical flow:** Higher levels summarise, aggregate, and roll up data from lower levels.
- **Upward arrows:** Transactional data flowing up.
- **Downward arrows:** Policies, budgets, and targets cascading down.

erDiagram

    ITEM_MASTER ||--o{ ABC_CLASS : "classified as"

    ITEM_MASTER {

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Define Management Information System for Materials Management. List any four characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

A Management Information System for Materials Management (MIS-MM) is a **computer-based, integrated, formal system** that collects, processes, stores, and disseminates information related to procurement, inventory, warehousing, and material handling to support decision-making at strategic, tactical, and operational levels.

**Four Characteristics:**
1. **Systematic & Routine:** Operates on predefined schedules (daily/weekly/monthly).
2. **Integrated:** Connects purchase, stores, production planning, and finance.
3. **Subsystem Nature:** Part of the organisation’s overall MIS architecture.
4. **User-Oriented Outputs:** Reports are tailored to managerial hierarchy.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between Data, Information, and Intelligence with a materials-management example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Term | Definition | Materials Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Raw, unprocessed facts and figures | PO #2024-0187 dated 12-Mar-2024, vendor V-014, qty 500 units of Bolt M10 |
| **Information** | Processed, contextualised data that has meaning | “Vendor V-014 delivered only 450 of 500 ordered M10 bolts, with 2-day delay, on 18-Mar-2024.” |
| **Intelligence** | Actionable insight derived from information by analysis | “Vendor V-014’s OTD has dropped to 78 % over 6 months; flag for vendor development and reduce next PO allocation by 30 %.” |

**Key Point:** MIS converts *Data → Information → Intelligence*, a 3-step transformation that is **frequently tested as a 3-mark question**.

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE guidelines**, every Module question in Part B carries an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B** from the same module. Each question has sub-parts typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **five-component architecture of an MIS for Materials Management**. Describe the role of each component in the context of an automobile manufacturing plant.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The annual demand for a critical spare part is **24,000 units**. Ordering cost is **₹150 per order**, and holding cost is **₹6 per unit per year**. Lead time is **10 days**, and the firm works 240 days/year. Calculate:
- (i) EOQ
- (ii) Reorder Point (assume no safety stock)
- (iii) Total annual cost at EOQ
- (iv) Number of orders per year and Time Between Orders.

**Show how each computed value is reflected as a column in the company’s MIS Stock Status Report.**

#### Model Answer — (a)

The five components are:

1. **Input Subsystem:** Captures PR, PO, GRN, MR, BOM, and vendor invoices. *In an auto plant, RF terminals at the receiving bay scan bar-coded crates and auto-populate the GRN module in SAP MM.*
2. **Processing Subsystem:** DBMS + logic engines (EOQ, ROP, ABC, MRP, Forecasting). *SAP runs MRP nightly, nets demand against on-hand + scheduled receipts, and generates planned orders.*
3. **Output Subsystem:** Routine, exception, and query reports + dashboards. *The Materials Manager views a Power BI dashboard showing ROP breaches and slow-moving parts.*
4. **Feedback Subsystem:** Corrective loop on forecast, lead time, and policy. *Actual consumption is fed back to update the next forecast cycle.*
5. **Control Subsystem:** Audit trail, RBAC, and statutory compliance. *PO creation rights are restricted to Purchase Officers; approval is via senior manager in segregation-of-duties mode.*

*(A hand-drawn block diagram with arrows showing data flow earns 2 marks.)*

#### Model Answer — (b)

**Step 1 — EOQ:**

$$
EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150}{6}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} \approx 1{,}095\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily Demand:**

$$
d = \dfrac{24{,}000}{240} = 100\ \text{units/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Reorder Point (no SS):**

$$
ROP = 100 \times 10 = 1{,}000\ \text{units}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Annual Cost at EOQ:**

$$
TC_{min} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150 \times 6} = \sqrt{43{,}200{,}000} = ₹6{,}570
$$

**Step 5 — Number of Orders per Year:**

$$
N = \dfrac{24{,}000}{1{,}095} \approx 22\ \text{orders/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Time Between Orders:**

$$
TBO = \dfrac{1{,}095}{100} = 10.95\ \text{days}
$$

**MIS Report Reflection:**

| Column in MIS Stock Status | Source Formula | Computed Value |
|---|---|---|
| Reorder Level (units) | ROP | 1,000 |
| Order Quantity (units) | EOQ | 1,095 |
| Expected Orders / Year | N | 22 |
| Min. Total Inventory Cost (₹) | TC | 6,570 |
| Cycle Time (days) | TBO | 11 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three levels of management information** with reference to Materials Management. State at least **four MIS reports generated at each level**, and outline their decision-making utility.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company has 1,000 line items in its stores. Annual consumption value is tabulated below. Perform **ABC analysis**, and recommend the **control policy** and **MIS reporting frequency** for each class.

| Item Group | No. of Items | Annual Consumption (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| A1 | 50 | 60 |
| A2 | 100 | 25 |
| B  | 200 | 10 |
| C  | 650 | 5 |
| **Total** | **1,000** | **100** |

#### Model Answer — (a)

**Three Levels:**

1. **Strategic MIS (Top Management):** Long-range, 3–10 years. *Reports:* (i) Make-or-Buy feasibility, (ii) Warehouse expansion plan, (iii) ERP capital budget, (iv) Long-term vendor contracts. *Utility:* Investment decisions, supply-chain re-engineering.

2. **Tactical MIS (Middle / Materials Manager):** 1–12 months. *Reports:* (i) ABC-XYZ reclassification, (ii) Vendor rating scorecard, (iii) Inventory budget vs. actual, (iv) EOQ revision report. *Utility:* Periodic policy tweaks, supplier development.

3. **Operational MIS (Executives / Storekeepers):** Daily / real-time. *Reports:* (i) Daily stock statement, (ii) Reorder exception list, (iii) GRN register, (iv) Bin card. *Utility:* Immediate replenishment and issue decisions.

#### Model Answer — (b)

**Step 1 — Compute % Contribution and Classify:**

| Class | Items | % of Total Items | Value (₹ L) | % of Total Value | Cum % of Value | Control Policy | MIS Reporting Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | 50 | 5 % | 60 | 60 % | 60 % | Tight, perpetual, daily review | **Daily** |
| A2 | 100 | 10 % | 25 | 25 % | 85 % | Tight, periodic review, weekly | **Weekly** |
| B | 200 | 20 % | 10 | 10 % | 95 % | Moderate, two-bin system, fortnightly | **Fortnightly** |
| C | 650 | 65 % | 5 | 5 % | 100 % | Loose, bulk ordering, quarterly | **Quarterly** |

**Step 2 — Pareto Verification:** ~5 % of items account for 60 % of value (A1), confirming the **80/20 principle** that ABC analysis is built upon.

**Step 3 — MIS Implementation:**
- A1 → **Perpetual Inventory System**, **continuous review**, **MRP-driven**, **daily dashboard**.
- A2 → **Periodic Review System**, **weekly review**.
- B → **Two-bin system**, **monthly cycle count**.
- C → **No formal review**, **annual physical count**, **bulk procurement**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing MIS with ERP:** Students often write *"MIS is a software like SAP/Oracle."* Examiner deducts marks because **MIS is a system concept, not a product**; SAP is a tool that *implements* the MIS.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram:** A 14-mark question without a block diagram or ER diagram is marked down by **2–3 marks** even if the text is correct.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Always write *units/year*, *₹/order*, *days* explicitly. The examiner expects dimensional discipline.
> 4. **No link to MIS dashboard:** When a numerical is asked, students compute EOQ/ROP and stop. **Always state which MIS report column the value populates** — this is the differentiating factor for 90 %+ scoring.
> 5. **Mixing ABC & XYZ:** XYZ classification is based on **demand variability**; ABC is based on **annual consumption value**. Examiners deduct if these are confused.
> 6. **Missing control layer:** Forgetting the Control subsystem (audit, RBAC) in the architecture diagram loses 1 mark consistently.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this section the night before the exam for a 5-minute high-density revision.**

### Core Definitions to Memorise Verbatim
- **MIS:** Formal system for collection, processing, storage, and dissemination of data into information for management decision-making.
- **MIS-MM:** MIS applied to procurement, inventory, stores, and material handling functions.
- **6R Principle:** Right material, Right quantity, Right quality, Right time, Right place, Right cost.
- **Anthony’s Hierarchy:** Strategic → Tactical → Operational.
- **ABC Principle:** ~20 % of items account for ~80 % of value (Pareto).

### Five-Component Architecture (Always in this Order)
1. **Input** — source documents (PR, PO, GRN, MR, BOM).
2. **Processing** — DBMS, EOQ/ROP/MRP engines, forecasting.
3. **Output** — routine + exception + dashboard reports.
4. **Feedback** — variance, vendor OTD, policy recalibration.
5. **Control** — audit trail, RBAC, statutory compliance.

### Must-Know Formulas
- **EOQ** = $\sqrt{2DS/H}$
- **ROP** = $D \times L + SS$
- **SS** = $Z \cdot \sigma_{DL}$
- **TC min** = $\sqrt{2DSH}$
- **Inventory Turnover** = COGS / Avg Inventory
- **MAPE** = $\frac{1}{n}\sum \vert A_t - F_t \vert / A_t \times 100$

### Sub-systems of MIS-MM
Purchasing MIS, Inventory MIS, Stores MIS, Forecasting MIS, MRP MIS, Vendor MIS, Cost MIS, Logistics MIS.

### Hierarchical Information Flow
- **Operational:** daily, real-time, transactional.
- **Tactical:** monthly/quarterly, aggregated, exception-based.
- **Strategic:** annual/long-term, summarised, investment-oriented.

### Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- Treating MIS as a single software tool.
- Drawing architecture without a control layer.
- Numerical answer without units and MIS report linkage.
- Confusing ABC and XYZ.
- Missing the feedback arrow in the architecture diagram.
- Skipping the ER diagram in database design questions.

### Last-Minute Mnemonics
- **“I P O F C”** — Input, Processing, Output, Feedback, Control.
- **“6R”** — Right material, quantity, quality, time, place, cost.
- **“STO”** — Strategic, Tactical, Operational.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, allocate **7 minutes to drawing the architecture diagram + ER diagram**, **5 minutes for numerical computation with units**, and **3 minutes for the MIS report linkage paragraph**. This time distribution mirrors the marks distribution and is the same formula top-scorers in KTU follow.
